item.title: [All-In Podcast] Bill Maris: How Google Could Crush AI Competitors, Why Small Funds Win, and AI's Atari Stage
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最隐秘的悖论：Bill Maris 用"反规模"对冲了他亲手发明的"规模信仰"

最让人意外的，不是他说"小基金跑赢大基金"，而是说这话的人，恰恰是当年在 Google 用机器学习证明"数据规模可以决定 VC 收益"的那个人。

他在 GV 时期做的事，是把 VC 这门"手艺活"变成"算力问题"——百万次模拟、回测、最优组合构造。那是典型的 Google 式思维：用规模碾压人类直觉。

但他今天回来说的是反话：基金一旦超过 7.5 亿美金，回报曲线出现"discontinuous compression"——不是平滑下滑，是"断崖式塌陷"。

这背后藏着一个他没明说的哲学转向：计算可以优化决策，但不能优化注意力。算法可以告诉你"投哪家"，但无法替你打那个深夜电话、无法替你坐在创始人对面让他相信自己。基金规模一旦突破某个临界点，LP 的资本效率和 GP 的注意力效率开始反向运动——这是任何模型回测不出来的，因为这是人类带宽的物理极限。

所以他不是反对 Google 式规模思维，他是发现了规模思维的领域边界。

二、跨领域连接：Zork → PlayStation 类比背后的真正主张是"反对模型崇拜"

他用 Zork（1980 年代命令行文字游戏）类比当下的 ChatGPT，看似只是个段子。但仔细看他下一句——"我不投更大的模型，我投的是控制器、物理引擎和 GPU"——这是一个极其反共识的判断。

把它翻译成更狠的话：他认为当下所有 LLM 公司，本质上都还在卖"更好的剧本"，而游戏产业的历史早已证明——好剧本从来不是游戏好玩的原因。Atari 到 PS5 的真正跃迁，发生在显卡、物理引擎、手柄反馈、网络协议这些"非内容层"。

如果他对，那意味着 OpenAI、Anthropic 的估值逻辑里，有一部分是基于"内容（模型本身）会持续是核心壁垒"的假设——而这个假设在游戏史里被反复证伪过。

这就直接连接到他下一段关于 Google 的"杀手锏"：Google 可以把 token 价格砍 80%。为什么 Google 敢这么干？因为他们也认为模型不是壁垒，分发+算力+成本才是壁垒。这跟游戏机厂商当年的逻辑一模一样：硬件平台一旦确立，软件就是耗材。

三、最尖锐的隐藏指控：他在场内骂了"延迟 IPO"是一种系统性的"代际财富抽吸"

这段访谈里最反共识、也最少被注意的观察，是他对"公益叙事 + 长期私有化"的双重批判。

表面上是在批 OpenAI、Anthropic、SpaceX。实际上他指控的是一种新型的金融结构性掠夺：

  1. 公司以"benefit humanity"包装，把价值创造曲线的 80% 留在私有市场；
  2. 当估值已经透支未来十年，才去敲钟；
  3. 而被动指数基金、401k、ETF 因为规则被改写（"S&P 500 rules are being broken"），被迫接盘；
  4. 最后是普通退休账户当 bag holder（接盘侠）。

这个论述真正狠的地方在于：他用一句话把"公益叙事"和"散户接盘"拧成了同一件事——当你越是宣称自己是为人类服务，你越应该早点上市；不上市就意味着这套人类叙事只供精英分享。

跨领域类比一下：这跟 17 世纪东印度公司、20 世纪初的信托垄断、2008 年的次贷打包，是同构的。每一次都是"创新叙事 + 私有化早期价值 + 公共市场接最后一棒"。

而 Bill 给出的小基金哲学——400M 规模、6 只基金、全部 top decile——其实是他对这套结构的另类抵抗：与其期待巨型基金后期接力跑赢公开市场，不如把赌注押在早期、退出节奏由真实流动性而非锁仓游戏决定的位置上。

这才是他真正在说的事。

📝 转录文本

引言：从离场到回归

After saying he was out, now Bill Maris is returning to the investing world. The founding CEO of Google Ventures has raised $150 million for his new fund called Section 32.

With a smaller fund, I have the advantage to be very selective in the companies that I invest in, the people that I hire. We're going to invest for a financial return. Any other metric is impossible to measure and therefore won't succeed. Think of the change that has happened just in the last hundred years and what's about to happen in the next hundred years with the advent of AI. The world is going to change by orders of magnitude.

自我介绍

Thank you very much for that warm welcome. I am Bill Maris. I'm the founder of Section 32. Prior to that, I was the founder and CEO of Google Ventures. I was also Google's vice president of special projects where I incubated Waymo and Google X, Calico, and many other projects as well. And before that I founded a web hosting and data center company, which we're going to talk a little bit about.

Today I think I'm going to talk to you about a few of the lessons I've learned on these interesting experiences I've had in life. So, we'll start. We're going to have four lessons I'm going to talk about.

第一课：1997 年，一个钥匙孔里的未来

We're going to go back to 1997 to start when I was a fresh college graduate. I had a degree in neuroscience, and I found myself on Wall Street. Somehow managed to land a job there, but I was miserable having to wear a suit and trudge to work in the heat.

But one good thing came of that, which was: I looked in the closet of the office one day and I saw a server, and I asked, "Well, what is this thing beneath our jackets?" And they said, "Well, that's where our email and websites live."

And as can happen to many of us, I had a moment where I felt like I was bathed in the light of inspiration. And I thought — I think I've glimpsed the future. I think I can maybe make a business out of this, because if you can have our website and email in your closet, how many websites and emails could I put in my closet?

So I immediately quit my job, because I had kind of glimpsed through a keyhole, and through that keyhole I thought I saw the internet and I saw a data center.

1997 年的"先进数据中心"

In 1997, a state-of-the-art data center looked almost exactly like this. We had three servers — a small, medium, and large. Business grew. We eventually had five servers.

And this isn't a data center at all. This was my apartment where I founded the company with credit cards. The servers lived in one room. The work happened in the other room. And it would get very hot in that room. And this was in Vermont. So I open the windows and then it would get very cold. So cold, in fact, that by noon, if you had a glass of water in your desk, it would ice over.

You may think though, this isn't so bad. But actually, this was also my apartment as well. This was the bed. You may look at that and think, well, you've got a mattress and a nice pillow and then look at that nice blanket — but this is a rug I got from Home Depot to keep myself warm on those nights.

雷暴中的屋顶

And one day there was a thunderstorm. The roof started to leak. And I knew I needed to do something because water and computers and servers don't mix well. So I called the landlord and said, "The roof's leaking." The landlord said, "Well, that happens sometimes."

But I knew that I needed to do something. So, when you don't know what to do, you go to Home Depot. I got a bucket of tar and a mop and I went up on the roof. And there was lightning and there was rain. And I went up there and I tarred the roof.

And I did not glimpse the future in that case, because I didn't know when you're tarring the roof that you should start at the far corner and work towards the door rather than the reverse. And I tarred myself into a corner. But the choice that I faced was either the servers get electrocuted or perhaps I get electrocuted. But as an entrepreneur, I was willing to take that risk. Which, you know, news flash, I survived. My shoes though are still stuck on that roof in Vermont.

第二课：要看见未来，有时你得有点疯

Which takes me to lesson two, which is: to see the future, sometimes you need to be a little bit insane. It may appear to those around you that you are tarring the roof in a thunderstorm.

And to that point, I'm going to share a few slides here that a friend named Stuart Butterfield was kind enough to share with me.

就职典礼的对比

Here's the inauguration, 1989. And there's someone taking a picture. That makes sense. It's probably a film camera. And 2005 is not very different. There's still someone back there taking a picture.

And then let's go just four years later to another inauguration. And if we look closely, it's quite a bit different — because now everybody's got a camera. Everybody's got a camera. And this was kind of before cameras were mushed into cell phones. It was kind of around that time it was starting to happen.

But that's not the most interesting thing about this photo. Because in this crowd is someone who — to his friends, I'm sure seemed insane — who also did glimpse the future. If we look closely, this gentleman has decided to, I don't know, live stream or record the inauguration on his laptop.

He knew something that those around him didn't know. Which is one of the things that I've always looked for in entrepreneurs: they know a secret about the future that most of us don't believe.

第三课：别和计算机科学对赌

Let's fast forward to 2007. I find myself somehow at Google. And a challenge was given to me. The challenge was: Google needs a venture fund. We were starting to make some investments. We didn't have a coherent strategy. There were no budgets. I had to figure out what to do.

So I first found a friend, Rich Miner, who's the co-founder of Android. And he became my partner in crime as we conceptualized what could Google Ventures be. We went up and down Sand Hill Road and we talked to everyone — anyone that was willing to talk to us and have a conversation, we were willing to talk to, to see what we could learn.

用数据和"机器学习"做 VC

We came up with a plan. Our plan was to obtain all the data of venture that we could find. And being Google, you can imagine it was a lot of data — historical data, you name it.

Then we decided we would, as step two, use AI. But at that time, Google would not let us use the term AI. And this persisted for many years. "Bill, AI is science fiction. It is a hundred years away if it's ever going to happen. Let's stick to machine learning. By the way, when you say AI, it freaks people out. So stop freaking people out."

So we had to call it machine learning. And we used machine learning to do two things: design the ideal portfolio construction by running millions and millions of simulations and back testing and all of the things you can imagine that data scientists would do — and to determine what the ideal fund size would be.

And people were excited. Here's a headline from TechCrunch at the time. And people inside of Google were also pretty excited. This is one of the senior execs I later learned had this to say. And I have to admit it seemed crazy. The plan seemed crazy at the time.

业绩验证

But let's look at how it turned out. So, over this time period, 2009 to 2018, top quartile VC returns looked like this, and top decile looked like this. Using publicly available information, I'm not sharing any non-public proprietary Google information — we would estimate Google Ventures returns at about 4.1x. And I adhered more closely to the strategy, and the investments that I led turned out like this.

Which takes me to lesson three: don't bet against computer science. I've seen it happen many many times in many many fields. If you apply the right kind of computer science at the right time to the right problem, you will get to the right answers. I would not bet against it, even if it looks like you're tarring the roof in a thunderstorm.

第四课：小基金跑赢大基金

So, let's fast forward to 2017. I decided to start my own fund. And again, those around me said, "You're insane. Why would you do that? You're in the warm womb of Google. Lunch is free and massages are aplenty."

But after the idea sunk in, the advice turned into: "Raise as much money as possible. That's the right way to run a fund. You'll get a big management fee, you'll be happy, things are going to work out really well for you."

And I thought about that relative to everything I had done up to that point. And I decided to not take that advice.

Section 32 的实绩

Over the course of my time at Section 32, we've had six funds. We've invested in companies like CrowdStrike and Cohere and Coinbase. All six of those funds have averaged about $400 million in size and all are performing in their top decile. And to the extent there is DPI to measure, that's the only measure as far as I'm concerned in venture that counts — DPI.

数学背后的真相

Which takes me to lesson four — this will be heresy to some: small funds outperform large funds. This is simply the math. This is not an opinion I'm trying to convince you of.

There are many reasons for this. Smaller funds — you can have more focus. I've already managed a multi-billion dollar fund with hundreds of employees; it's distracting. You cannot give the attention to founders that I would like to give.

If we look at top decile performance of DPI: funds smaller than $750 million, average return of 4.76x. Funds larger than a billion: 2.42x. Funds below $750 million across that time period represented 95% of top decile performers, with discontinuous return compression above $750 million.

一个思想实验

Why is this? There's a lot of reasons. Use your own numbers, but I'll just do a little thought experiment.

If you have a $500 million fund and let's say on average these days you can own 10% of a company, you need $5 billion of exits to get your money back. Let's just remind ourselves that the 75th percentile of venture loses money, and there is persistence of performance of the top quartile.

So if you need $5 billion to get your money back, and if you want to be in this business for the long term, let's say you set your goal at 3x — you need to return $15 billion of exit value in your companies.

Now, if you have a $7 billion fund, and we do the same math, you've got to return $210 billion. $7 billion × 3x is $21 billion — actually 70 × 3x is $210 billion, which exceeds the total venture-backed M&A and IPO exit value in most years. This year may be an exception, but that is something I'm looking forward to talking about when we sit down.

For those of you, we've crunched the numbers, we've done all the math. Those are Bill's four lessons for today. I hope that they're somewhat useful. There's a lot of stories behind all this and I'm looking forward to talking about them for a few minutes with the guys. Thanks so much.

圆桌对话

老朋友：Climate Corp 的第一单

Host: You guys are old friends.

Bill: Yes, we are. We go way back.

Host: Well, Bill's — um, when he started Google Ventures, I was the first ex-Google company you invested in.

Bill: That's correct.

Host: And how did it go?

Bill: Climate Corp — a billion dollar exit to Monsanto.

Host: What was your multiple? What was the return?

Bill: Oh, I don't know.

Host: It was actually good for you guys.

Bill: It was — it was quite good. Yes.

Host: Yeah. You guys were in the B and the C.

Bill: Yeah, it was — one billion dollars was a lot of money then.

Host: That back then, that was a good deal. Now, that would have been the C round. Now it's like an A round.

Bill: Yeah, that would have been your A round.

Host: Now we're going to do it again with — Oh, so now we're going to do it again with Oh.

大基金 vs 小基金：杠铃策略可行吗？

Host: So, you know, I just want to juxtapose what you said with what Thomas shared. They've got a very large kind of capital base that they invest, and they're investing significantly in these later stage rounds of these well-proven companies where, you know, the data he shared is that you can get significant multiples to get to that next phase. You're more likely to go from a billion to 10 billion and then you're more likely to go from 10 billion to 100 and 100 to a trillion, trillion to whatever.

Doesn't that justify an alternative strategy to what you're saying — of having smaller funds focused on venture that you can maybe barbell it, have smaller vehicles focused on venture and then very large vehicles that bet on the sure things that have that durability and that compounding advantage — and you can kind of have the two together both be 3x return?

Bill: So my observation on that would be:

One — I haven't seen the data science to support that second conclusion of the latest stage companies. That can be an ongoing trend other than this one moment — this weird moment in time with these multi-trillion dollar exits that are coming. That would be kind of observation one.

Two — would be: at a certain point, and this is not a negative, it's just an observation, if you're an RIA and you're, you know, collecting assets, that is not venture. Venture as I practice it at least is a different craft where you are making concentrated bets of your time and capital on entrepreneurs and helping them build a business. And there's nothing wrong with late-stage investing.

对"公益叙事 + 长期私有化"的批判

Bill: However, I also have an objection to companies that wrap themselves up in public benefit language and then keep the value creation to themselves and an elite group of investors through a big part of the curve, and then say, "Well, we're here to benefit humanity." Well, what humanity needs is money. So it might be better to go public sooner, because we'll see how these multi-trillion dollar IPOs go.

Google 的"代币武器化"剧本

Bill: However, if I'm Google — and I don't speak for Google — and I decide to arbitrarily cut the cost of tokens to 80%, I'm going to cut them — what happens to the business models of OpenAI and Anthropic at that point?

Host: What happens? Tell us actually what — yeah, what does happen?

Bill: Well, if you're a company and you can go to Google and Gemini and you can pay 80% less for that basically identical product, why wouldn't you do that? And then the compression and the pressure on those other businesses goes super critical.

Host: What are the chances that — you don't think that that fall — that might happen?

Bill: If I were Google, that's what I'd do.

Host: Walk us through the scenario where Google decides — with their war chest, with their money printing machine — "You know what, their margin is my opportunity. I'm going to give tokens out 20 cents on the dollar. Every time they lower their price, I lower our price." What happens on the playing field? Walk us through that. Would that not be the rational thing for —

Bill: It's clear they're going to do it.

Host: It may not be a margin though — they may be burning investor cash sort of like an Uber type model. Grab market share, growth — capital as a weapon, tokens as a weapon.

Bill: Tokens as a weapon. Grab market share, grab an install base on consumer and enterprise. But fundamentally at some point you got to have cash generation.

Host: So that's 100% possible.

Bill: It's 100%. Look, it's been said before — a trillion-for-spend commitments on $60 billion of revenue. And now you're going to go to the public and hope that retail is going to pick that up.

公司延迟上市与"散户接盘"

Host: Yeah. Tell us about companies staying private longer and how unfair that is to the bottom half of society who don't get to participate.

Bill: Speak for those 99% who are mostly not us, right? So those 401(k)s, those retirement plans — to get into those companies now, which are getting bizarre exceptions to S&P 500 rules, all of the rules are being broken. The passive funds, the ETFs are going to have to pick them up. And where do you think we are on that curve of value creation? Could they go 3x from here? Sure. But —

Host: So just to say it as plainly as possible: we're going to force overpriced products on the 401(k) holders of America who didn't get to participate early. This is your position — that this profoundly creates more wealth creation for the people who don't need it, and it makes the people's retirement accounts the bag holders.

Bill: There's a lot of risk in that. And my objection is: don't say you're doing this for the benefit of humanity and do the other thing.

Host: Make the public's retirement accounts the bag holders —

Bill: Or just say this is how we're running our business, and this isn't for the benefit of humanity.

风险投资的双峰分布

Host: Bill, do you think — what happens to venture? I asked Thomas this question. When these dollars get distributed, there's going to be a handful of funds that have ginormous returns. I mean just unbelievably excessive. Founders Fund 2, you know, is going to print a hundred billion dollar return on $200 million of invested capital. But that's one fund in isolation.

Bill: Right.

Host: And there'll be a few — your funds when you were at GV are going to print an enormous upside. And so if you don't look closely though, beyond the averages, venture is going to look incredible. If you look past the averages, venture is still going to look extremely bimodal — a handful of winners and a ton of losers. How does that play out?

Bill: I mean, one — that's how venture is, right? 75% of funds lose money. But two — in order for Founders Fund, or pick any fund, to get that $100 billion out, they have to sell that stock to someone else. Otherwise, it's just on paper. So who's the buyer for that? Is it retail? You've got to make a business case in the public market that can show that this business is worth the discounted value of its future cash flows. And so whether it's SpaceX or Anthropic or so forth — like, can that case be made? We'll see, six months after or so. I know they're playing with the lockups to kind of drag that out. But we'll see what the public market thinks of that.

投资主题：AI 的 Atari 阶段

Host: Okay. So we have this one set of companies, and then there's everything else. What do you like in the everything else bucket as a venture investor?

Bill: So I'm going to make an analogy to the gaming industry. We all get asked and we all think about — well, what does the future look like when AI is everywhere? And there's doomers on one side and utopians on the other.

Host: Zork. That's Zork.

Bill: I'm going to get to that. Just bear with me 30 seconds. It's probably not as bad or as great as everyone says.

So let's look at the gaming industry. I used to play this game Zork. There's one called Planetfall back in the 80s, and it was very brittle. It was turn-response, turn-response. "Grab the lamp." "Oh, I didn't — it's a lantern. I should have said lantern." "Go north." And you wait for the computer to respond.

Let's show the most sophisticated retail-available AI system out there today on the next slide. And tell me how different it looks. So what's happened to the gaming industry from the 80s to today is going to happen in AI — but in the next like 5 years. So that will be compressed in terms of how quickly that change happens.

But we would all agree games are better today than they were then. They're photorealistic. You can inhabit them, and they're moving very quickly. On the AI side, there will be ambient computing. There will be — the problems that Zork had will be solved for AI. Lack of memory, lack of consistency, session resets, and so forth.

How did we get there? To answer your question, I don't plan on investing in kind of larger models, right? Just like it wasn't better stories that would make better games. It was controllers and physics engines and GPUs. And those are the parts of the AI cycle that I'm interested in — which is all the platforms that need to be built to —

Host: Machinery.

Bill: Correct. That is going to make this reality real in the next 5 years. And it's not just bigger models. I think we're at the Atari command-line stage of AI, and we're going to get to the, you know, PlayStation 10 stage in the next 5 years.

生命科学与计算生物学

Host: You also used to do a lot of stuff in life sciences.

Bill: Yeah — not as much anymore. My interest in life science — I founded Calico and been very interested in that space, and we were investors in Flatiron and Veer and lots of other companies. I'm very interested in that space because it has a dual benefit of helping people and also —

Host: Doing good, doing well.

Bill: Correct. However, the therapeutic space that requires human clinical trials is a specialist investment area that we're not spending a lot of time on. I'm very interested in computational biology, and in those areas.

Host: It seems, if you just look on X, that there's a renaissance happening in human health. I don't know if that's true — whether it's cures for pancreatic cancer, cancer vaccines, peptides — obviously there's just an explosion, and a lot of it seems to come back to computation. But this class of winners so far is not really computationally driven. It was just really good science 10 years ago. And so do you think that we're about to see this massive —

Bill: I hope so. I started Calico, and again it was like fringe science — longevity at the time — and now we're investors in NewLimit, which is Blake Byers and Brian Armstrong's company, and a number of other companies in that space, which doesn't seem so crazy anymore.

However, because of human biology and the FDA — if you find a compound and you think you've got something, that's like 5% of the work. There's still all kinds of titrating and safety testing that needs to go on. And so I don't think it's going to go quite as exponential as we would all like it to. However, if we can achieve a realistic simulation of a human cell in silico, then you will see that accelerate as well. We're not quite there yet.

美中科研环境对比

Host: Generally we're seeing — some might say — a flight of capital to India and China right now. Are you seeing that the biotech path to market is faster if you invest in firms that are based offshore?

Bill: I think the US has always indexed on human safety over speed to market, and that has cost us in some ways. However, some other countries are indexed in the opposite direction, which costs lives. And so there's a balance there.

But there are certainly research going on in China and other places — experiments and cloning and all sorts of things — that as far as I know aren't happening here. So yes, and I think the gutting of the CDC and the NIH and the anti-science vibe that now pervades this country has driven a lot of mind share elsewhere as funding is drying up for basic research.

Host: I mean, China's got their own paperclip model now. They're recruiting some of the best scientists from Europe and India, and they're all immigrating to China.

Bill: Yeah, to go do work. And that used to be a scientific pool that we used to access and we used to recruit —

Host: And we're losing.

Bill: We really need the neurological reserves here. And this business with —

Host: Brain trust would be another way to say that as well. But the pushing out of H1B holders — there's so much happening now that it's causing — it's just easier to go elsewhere. That's not good for science.

Deep Tech 与 AI 加持

Host: What's your view on what's been called deep tech for the last decade — these traditionally long investment cycle, capital-intensive, high-risk — like Elon is one of the few entrepreneurs that has successfully tackled deep tech business models with SpaceX and Tesla. Is this becoming a more tractable area for entrepreneurs to activate, and for investors to invest in, because of AI enablement and physics engines?

Bill: Absolutely. Because things are moving so much faster.

Host: What areas like that are you focused on investing in?

Bill: Human biology and healthcare — that's probably the largest TAM in the world. So super interested in that. And then all of the others I mentioned that kind of underlay the AI revolution — which are the physics engines and the controllers and the GPUs and everything that is going to take to get us there.

Sacks 提问：风险投资本身的未来

Host: Has — Google — I want to bring Sacks and Friedberg in before we run out of time if it's possible. Sacks —

Sacks: I'm curious your thoughts on the venture capital business. You did five Craft funds or four.

Host: Well, we've done four venture and two growth.

Sacks: I'm assuming you're going to be going back into the venture business. But I'm curious your take on — when you started in venture, and when we started as entrepreneurs 25-30 years ago, this was a much different playing field. What are your plans, based on Bill's look at this — and do you believe in the $500 million fund sweet spot, or do you think you need to become Andreessen Horowitz when you go back to the private sector?

Sacks: Well, I don't think we need to become Andreessen Horowitz. Look, I think fund size determines fund strategy. The size of your fund — because you're going to divide your fund size by 20 to 25 names to achieve some portfolio diversification and construction — that'll determine your check size, and that sort of determines where you play in the market.

The thing that's spinning through my head after Thomas's presentation is: you know, are you better off just focusing on what used to be called, I don't know, late venture / early growth. You're writing $50 million checks. You just kind of wait for the breakouts, as opposed to playing in this really noisy super early stage game.

Bill 的反驳：扭曲的激励结构

Bill: Well, I think the problem with that — yeah — is we have to look at the incentive structure of venture.

So a $5 billion venture fund that returns 1.01x gets to say that they are in the 75th percentile, and can raise their next fund. And no one at the Stanford endowment is going to get in trouble for writing that check. They need to put $200 or $500 million into a fund multiple times. So I understand that dynamic.

So now let's look at the GP dynamic. Well, if I have a $5 billion fund and I return 1.01x, I'm going to make more money than Bill with his $500 million fund that returns 3x. Okay, so that's also a strange incentive.

So now let's look at the entrepreneur side. I am researcher X from OpenAI. I'm going to start a company. Bill says, "I'll give you $20 million at $100 million valuation. I want to buy 20% of your company." Giant Fund Y says, "Well, we have this giant fund. We need to put $250 million in." And then entrepreneur says, "Well, but my company's valuation is 100." "No, your valuation is now $4 billion, and we'll give you $250 million for a percent of your company."

They're going to take that deal every day — unless you're a seasoned entrepreneur who has kind of been down the road and knows the pitfalls of that.

And so the incentives are broken in all those ways. And the pendulum will swing back. So I don't think just staying late-stage and waiting to sniper at larger companies will be a long-term — the data would suggest that's not going to work in the long term.

Host: Okay, let's thank Bill. Amazing job. Thanks guys.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最隐秘的悖论：Bill Maris 用"反规模"对冲了他亲手发明的"规模信仰"
最让人意外的，不是他说"小基金跑赢大基金"，而是**说这话的人，恰恰是当年在 Google 用机器学习证明"数据规模可以决定 VC 收益"的那个人**。
他在 GV 时期做的事，是把 VC 这门"手艺活"变成"算力问题"——百万次模拟、回测、最优组合构造。那是典型的 Google 式思维：**用规模碾压人类直觉**。
但他今天回来说的是反话：基金一旦超过 7.5 亿美金，**回报曲线出现"discontinuous compression"**——不是平滑下滑，是"断崖式塌陷"。
这背后藏着一个他没明说的哲学转向：**计算可以优化决策，但不能优化注意力**。算法可以告诉你"投哪家"，但无法替你打那个深夜电话、无法替你坐在创始人对面让他相信自己。基金规模一旦突破某个临界点，**LP 的资本效率和 GP 的注意力效率开始反向运动**——这是任何模型回测不出来的，因为这是人类带宽的物理极限。
所以他不是反对 Google 式规模思维，他是发现了规模思维的**领域边界**。
## 二、跨领域连

item.title: [Business of Sport] The Ruthless World of Red Bull Racing | Jean-Éric Vergne
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：Red Bull 的"残忍"其实是一种风险定价机制

大多数人把 Helmut Marko 的高压管理当作性格缺陷或文化粗暴，但仔细看 Vergne 的叙述会发现一个隐藏的结构：Red Bull 不是在培养车手，而是在用最低成本对"心理韧性"进行金融式压力测试。

注意那个被忽视的细节——即便 Vergne 拿下两个杆位、以 40 秒优势赢下双冠，Marko 的第一句话仍是"为什么没有最快圈"。这不是吹毛求疵，这是人为制造信息不对称下的负反馈：让你永远无法用客观成绩兑换心理安全感。这种机制在金融领域有个对应物——压力测试中的"逆周期对冲"：在系统看起来最稳健时施加最大冲击，因为真正的崩溃永远不发生在你以为脆弱的时候。

Red Bull 之所以能跑出 Vettel 和 Verstappen，不是因为他们筛选出了"最强车手"，而是因为他们在车手到达 F1 的真正高压环境之前，就把脆弱的人提前淘汰了。Kimi Antonelli 在 Mercedes 茁壮成长这个对照案例尤其精彩——Vergne 说得很对：同一个人在两个系统里，是"世界冠军"还是"失败者"，并不取决于天赋，而取决于环境给予的反馈函数。

二、跨领域连接：Vergne 的职业轨迹是一份完美的"反脆弱"教科书

把 Vergne 的故事抽象出来，你会得到一个出乎意料的发现——他真正的胜利不是 F1，而是 F1 之后。

他被 Red Bull 抛弃后做了三件事，每一件单独看都像降级，合起来却是 Nassim Taleb 笔下"反脆弱"的完美执行： 1. 自降身价去 LMP2（自掏腰包），重建作为团队玩家的口碑——这是品牌重置（rebranding），而不是降级； 2. 买下一支 Formula E 车队，控制自己的命运——这是从"被招募者"切换为"系统设计者"； 3. 进入 Stellantis 生态，把短期车手合同换成长期厂商关系——这是从"零工经济"升级为"股权经济"。

最有意思的跨领域对照是：他对待职业生涯的方式，跟他后来投资 Nvidia 的方式一模一样。两者的共同逻辑是——不要在已经被定价的赛道里争夺剩余价值（F1 后期、被分析师覆盖的成熟股），而要进入还未被市场定价的早期系统（Formula E 第一年、2010 年代的 GPU 算力）。他自己可能没意识到，但他的车手思维和投资思维已经是同一套操作系统。

三、最具系统性意味的隐藏真相：他对社交媒体的"违和厌恶"，恰恰是一个领先信号

Vergne 在采访里有一段几乎被对话者强行扭转的发言——他说"希望生活在没有社交媒体的世界"，对方立刻反驳"这就像说所有食物都不好"。表面看是普通的代际审美差异，但放在他整个职业弧线里看，这个细节惊人地重要：

社交媒体崛起后，运动员从"表演者"变成了"内容生产者"，而 Vergne 的整个成长系统（Red Bull、Ferrari、耐力赛）都把他训练成了纯粹的表演者。他作为"从不微笑的法国人"被淘汰出 F1，部分正是因为他没有意识到——在 Netflix、Drive to Survive、Instagram 主导的新生态里，"是否能赢"已经不再是唯一指标，"是否能被叙述"同样重要。Daniel Ricciardo 总是微笑的真正回报，要十年之后才显现。

这里的系统性洞察是：Vergne 的职业拐点（被 Red Bull 抛弃）和体育商业模式的拐点（Liberty 收购 F1）发生在同一时刻。如果他晚出生五年，以同样的成绩进入 F1，他的商业价值可能完全不同。这不是个人失败，这是生态位错配——他是用旧版操作系统在跑新版应用程序的最后一代车手之一。

而这恰恰让他的故事比 Verstappen 更值得研究：胜利者教你如何在系统里赢，但 Vergne 教你的是——如何在系统抛弃你之后，自己造一个新系统。

📝 转录文本

引言：Red Bull 的法则

"with Red Bull is either you win or you disappear. You always have that pressure to perform and to win. Doesn't matter if it was your rookie or not, you had to win."

The first year I did with Red Bull was my worst year. I had any podiums and before the last race, Helmut Marko called me and said, if I was not going to do a podium, I was fired. He knew exactly what he was doing. Constantly under pressure. Even when you win, he was putting you under pressure.

First year in Formula One, it was like $200,000 earnings. Then I had to pay my super license. I had to pay the fines. I ended up negative in the first year.

It's not like I was in F1 in today's world. I mean when I was in F1 actually it was not a sport that many people was watching. Netflix didn't exist, social media was just starting, I had maybe 4,000, 5,000 followers.

I guess the image that I had in F1 didn't work out, no factories wanted me and I came to the hard realization that I had to change something, I had to change my image, I had to do it all over again to convince people that I was actually good.

开场寒暄

Host: Jean, it is so great to have you in the studio. Thank you so much for joining us today.

JEV: Welcome, thank you.

Host: Have you done many of these out of interest?

JEV: Not many, no. I mean, you know, I think I haven't replied to you for one year and I really apologize. No, I don't do that many. I can count them on one hand.

Host: He hasn't replied to you for a year.

Host 2: I didn't actually think it was that bad. That's savage. I've sent 52 messages, never give up.

JEV: I agree. Persistence. You go, bro.

Host: Because I was having a look beforehand. It's always interesting to see what kind of conversations have been had before. And I actually only saw one. I saw one that you had done, which to me is always great because we love to have the kind of conversations that maybe haven't been had a lot. You have a really interesting career across motorsport but then also doing stuff alongside it.

卡丁车起步与早期生涯

Host: Just from a motorsport perspective, can you give people a quick overview of your journey around motorsport?

JEV: Yeah sure. I was born on a karting track literally speaking. My first babysit was a karting seat. So coming back from school, I had to do my homework, but basically I was driving every night at my parents' go-karts near Paris.

So done karting until I was 16. I was okay, but not very successful in karting. I didn't win any world championship or European championship. The best I've done was French champion and vice European champion.

Went to single seater, did the Formula 4, French Formula 4, which I won — thanks to that I got within the Red Bull family during the same time as Daniel Ricciardo. Then I did two years of Formula Renault 2.0, one year of Formula 3, British Formula 3 — that was my first year living in England. And then World Series and then Formula 1.

So I won a lot of championships before F1, which was quite important because back then — I mean, still now with Red Bull — is either you win or you disappear. So you always have that pressure to perform and to win. It doesn't matter if it was your rookie or not, you had to win. So luckily I did.

I went to F1 with Toro Rosso also. Did three years. My first two were with Ricciardo as a teammate. The third one was with Kvyat. And that last year went pretty well on the sporting side. I mean, I beat Kvyat, but didn't go very well politically speaking with Red Bull because when Vettel left Red Bull Racing to go to Ferrari, it was only logical that they would take the best driver into also — and they took Kvyat. So yeah, that didn't make much sense actually. He didn't even do the full season; he got demoted after only six months in Red Bull Racing. But unfortunately, that was the end for me in Formula One. So it was a rather short and not very successful one.

And this is when I joined Formula E, so now 10 years ago, and I won two championships in Formula E. It's been a pretty good run so far. That's the best summary.

赛车运动的"金钱门槛"

Host: You know, there's two sports that we come across where there's a really interesting trajectory at the earliest stage. It's tennis and Formula One. What it actually takes for you to be able to make it to the top in those sports seems to be hugely dependent on financial exposure — being able to support a career to even get the chance to sit in that seat. What was your journey like?

JEV: Well, in karting, I'd say it was okay because my parents having a karting track, I was associated with Sodikart, who were the partner with my parents' family. But even that — I had to earn my place to be in the top team and to have the season paid by the factory. Once I almost got demoted and it was quite... I remember my parents being quite stressed. Obviously, when you're 14, 15 years old, you have absolutely no idea of the money being put behind you and how much it costs to do a full season in go-kart.

Host: Can I ask on that? Tennis players say it takes a million dollars to get someone to be a professional tennis player in training. When you look at your parents having a karting track and the money that goes behind those early stages, is racing an accessible sport if you don't have money?

JEV: No, it's not. It's very difficult. Everybody knows it's too expensive. We're all trying to make efforts to change the rules. There are new championships now in go-kart where it's the same engine, same go-kart for everybody, so that it reduces the price — but it's still very expensive. And now the seasons in karting are completely out of hand. I've heard like some kids, the parents spending more than half a million in just one season in go-kart. It's absolutely mental.

加入 Red Bull 青年车手计划

Host: We spoke to Claire Williams. She talked to us about that period of Formula One — let's say from 2010 to before the Liberty era — about running Williams and being in a situation where you had to sell race seats if you want. So when you're a driver fighting for those 20 positions, is that quite a difficult thing when you're coming through and aspiring to that, knowing that your talent may not be the thing that gets you in that seat?

JEV: Well, in that sense, I got very lucky to be coming in the Red Bull Junior team because Red Bull never cared about money. The only thing they cared about was they want the future talent — the future Sebastian Vettel, the future Max Verstappen. In that sense, it was good. All I had to do was to deliver, to win championships. And I knew already when I signed with them that if I delivered, I was going to go to Formula One. That had nothing to do with money.

But Red Bull didn't make life easy. Let's say the season in Formula Three was — I don't remember the deals they had because we never knew how much they were paying — but let's say it was 800,000. They made sure that my parents, through my sponsors, always paid a part. They didn't want to make my family life easy. So my parents — we still had to pay like 100,000 a year, which was difficult. I mean, it was not impossible — it's always easier to find 100,000 through sponsorship than it was to find a million — but they never wanted to make my life easy.

"Ruthless"：Red Bull 的高压文化

Host: What's the Red Bull family like as a racing entity?

JEV: Ruthless. Many people are going to talk bad about Red Bull, about the way they handle their drivers and everything. Yeah, for sure, it's not easy. But let me give you an example.

The first year I did with Red Bull was my worst year. I was teammate with Ricciardo. It was my very first year in proper single-seater. It was Ricciardo's second year, and of course, he completely destroyed me that year. I hadn't had any podiums, and before the last race, Helmut Marko called me and said: if I was not going to do a podium, I was fired. Luckily I did a podium.

The year after was okay. I won the championship the year after I was in British Formula 3. And this is when things started to get quite serious — faster car, you're getting closer to Formula One, you're starting to be a reserve driver for the F1 team. That year I was in Monaco — I was on pole, I stalled, not once but twice. First race I finished second. Second race I started on pole, I stalled, I came back second, but I didn't win the race. And I remember all my life on the way back home, the guy called me — I was on loudspeaker — he destroyed me like there was no tomorrow: "You're a piece of [expletive], I expected better from you, this is unacceptable coming from you, I don't accept this anymore." And I was leading the championship already.

The race after, the weekend after was in Hockenheim. I did the two poles. I won the two races by a huge margin — like 40 seconds. It was a lot. And he calls me, and he didn't say congratulations or anything. He said: "Why didn't you have fastest lap?" I didn't have time to explain. He said: "I'm disappointed. I still expect better from you." And he hung up.

I didn't have time to explain that the fastest lap was done by a guy that broke his front wing, put new tires and did it on the last lap with low fuel. Didn't even have time. But he didn't care. He knew exactly what he was doing — constantly under pressure. Even when you win, he was putting you under pressure.

For some drivers, it's bad, because it can really break you. But for some drivers, it can literally make you — because when you arrive to Formula 1, for sure the pressure is going to be way higher than it is in Formula 3, and he wants to prepare you. So I kind of understood that. Oh yeah, I got fired or sworn at by him once.

Red Bull 模式 vs Mercedes 模式

Host: This is the most exclusive sport. We're guilty in modern sport now a little bit of sometimes being over-sensitive and protecting some of the talent. But the reality is it's going to be hugely competitive. And if it's too much for a driver, then they're probably not going to be right for that environment.

JEV: Yeah, but that's Red Bull's way. But it doesn't necessarily mean that it will work for all the drivers. There are some drivers that were in the Red Bull family that could have been world champion but that didn't work through the system.

Because when I look at, for example, Kimi Antonelli — if he was in the Red Bull family, he would have never made it to Formula One. Or he would have been gone after one year. In Formula Two, he hasn't won anything. In the first year in F1, he was not that great. But the way Mercedes and Toto Wolff were with him was maximum support, maximum love — everything that Red Bull would have never given you. And it turned out to work out pretty well. He's doing an amazing season so far, really impressive. But if he was with Red Bull, I don't think he would have been where he is today.

F1 时代的真实薪水

Host: Can I ask, when you chart the kind of years in your career and scaling up to Formula One, what was the first big year financially for you? When does the money start to hit?

JEV: It never really hit. The first year in Formula One was like $200,000.

Host: What? For the first year?

JEV: Yeah, for the first year. And then I had to pay my super license, I had to pay the fines. I ended up negative in the first year.

Host: Wow. What?

JEV: Yeah. With Red Bull, it was also ruthless on the end. Some drivers don't make a lot of money in F1, so I didn't make a lot. Honestly, I didn't.

Host: All the years in Formula One?

JEV: I did only three years in Formula One.

Host: Yeah, but all three. That's still going good though, right? Three years, it's over 50 races.

JEV: Yeah, I did more than 50 races, but it was honestly the same contract for all the drivers. For the first three years, it was like 200, 500, 700 thousand dollars.

Host: Wow. Was there commercial uplift on the side, opportunities?

JEV: No, because on top of it, it's not like I was in F1 in today's world. When I was in F1, it was not a sport that many people were watching. Netflix did not exist yet — the Netflix show was not existing. Social media was just starting. I had maybe 4,000, 5,000 followers as an F1 driver. So yeah, I was not in F1 in the good time, that's for sure.

Toro Rosso 到 Red Bull Racing 的政治学

Host: When you made it into the Toro Rosso seat, is it very much: come in here, perform here, Red Bull's the destination?

JEV: Yep. So if you then don't achieve that... it's over. Which was my case in the end. I didn't go to Red Bull Racing and that was the end.

Host: Well, as you said in the previous interview, you were talking about the mentality of finishing in different places in the grid — but actually you wish you'd been happier with finishing sixth or in eighth position and understanding what success could have looked like in the cars that you were driving. Why do you think you weren't able to recognize that when you were in the car?

JEV: It comes from your background. When you used to win a lot of races, a lot of championships, and when finishing second was a disaster — when you have that mentality for so many years and you come in F1 and you finish eighth and the whole team is clapping and happy, you say: "Am I in a madhouse? Why are those guys happy about it?"

I just wish I had a better understanding back then that being eighth in a team like Toro Rosso was actually a great result and that it should have been celebrated more. It also gave me that sticker of "the French guy never being happy" — which in the end, I was not, for sure, I was not happy to finish eighth. But I had a teammate next to me that was smiling no matter what — if he was being beaten, if he was crashing or if he was finishing eighth. And I think people liked him way more for that reason than me.

Host: He lives a way nicer life, doesn't he?

JEV: Sorry?

Host: Beaten, getting crashed, and he's always smiling. He's got it. I'm just like, that's a nice life to live, isn't it?

JEV: Oh, yeah. But again, that's your difference in personalities, right?

Helmut Marko 的电话与被放弃的瞬间

Host: 100%. But then, you mentioned it's not what Formula One is now. It was a massively different environment for you. One thing Drive to Survive did amazingly well, it also gave a really interesting view into the team principal side — the relationships in the garage. From your perspective, when you're in there, in a Red Bull family, what was that like from the operating side and the relationships that you had with a team principal? Are you in conversation continually with a Christian Horner, or are you speaking purely on Toro Rosso standards?

JEV: No, the big boss at the end of the day was Helmut Marko. He was the one making the shots — no one else, really. Even at the level of drivers, it's like the owner of a football club picking a team. He was the shot maker, 100%. It wasn't Christian, it wasn't Franz Tost. He's the one that gave me the call to tell me basically: "Fuck off."

Host: When he said basically "fuck off", what did he actually say? How did he do it?

JEV: Helmut Marko — Stivert — is very cold, so: "Jev, we're not going to continue with you next year." I cannot do the Austrian accent, I'm sorry. I tried, but I'm bad. "Yeah, we're not continuing. We need to make way for young drivers."

And I was like, "okay." I kind of understood the reasoning behind it. But what I got very angry at: I asked him a very clear question. I said: "Okay, look, I'm doing a good season. If I keep doing a good season and if Vettel is leaving, am I going to go to Red Bull?" He said: "Vettel is not going away." I said: "But if Vettel is going away, is it me or Kvyat that you're going to put in the Red Bull car?" "I'm going to put the best driver — the driver that has the most points, the most experience. So yeah, it's going to be you."

Okay, so I kind of went in the second half of the season knowing that there was still this opportunity. And in Japan, during the Grand Prix, when Vettel announced on Friday night that he was leaving to Ferrari, I was like: "Okay, then I have a good opportunity." I came back from very strong races in Singapore and everything. So I was like, "okay, tomorrow we're gonna have an interesting conversation." And I woke up in the morning with the news that Kvyat was signed by Red Bull.

This is when I lost it with them. It doesn't make any sense — why? Some people within the Red Bull team, higher people, never understood that choice. A lot of people in the paddock as well.

Host: Do you know why it was?

JEV: To this day, I have no absolute… I don't know why, and I don't care, frankly. It's not going to change anything, understanding why.

Host: The young driver that you gave way for would end up being a young Max Verstappen.

JEV: Yeah. Honestly, I have no problem that I gave way for him. I would have had a problem that I gave way for a young driver that would have done one year, be bad and disappear.

Host: Had you seen him much in the juniors?

JEV: Yeah, I've seen him.

Host: Was he so obviously good so early?

JEV: Yeah, he was.

没有经纪人的世界

Host: So if you find out that you're not going to continue in Red Bull, what starts to happen? How do you look at opportunities that could be on the table for you?

JEV: Yeah, obviously. The other difficult thing with Red Bull is that when you sign a contract, you have no rights of having a manager. So I never had a manager — it was always Red Bull.

Host: What?

JEV: Yeah, you don't have a manager. When I signed my contract with the Red Bull junior team, I didn't have the right to have a manager. So obviously, when you do your whole life with Red Bull, you don't really get to speak to any other teams, to any other managers — you don't really know what's going on outside this Red Bull world.

I remember speaking to Alain Prost, to Checo Perez. There was this manager called Julian Jakobi, who I'd heard of but never really spoken to. So I spoke to him during the second part of the season to sign a deal with him to help me out to find a new drive. I signed with him — I'm actually still with him more than 10 years down the line.

But there was no seat available in F1. There was nothing. There was a seat in Renault, but I needed to bring like 15 or 20 millions.

Host: Okay, so that's it. Wow.

JEV: Yeah, so I was very good in their eyes for 20 millions.

Host: Was that common?

JEV: Unfortunately, yeah, back then it was. The teams were struggling financially. Not all the teams had a lot of money. Some teams — as Claire Williams must have told you — it wasn't so difficult for Williams. They benefited from paying drivers for many years for a lot of cash.

F1 的十年变迁

Host: How do you look at that whole development of the sport? Because in 10 years, it's ridiculous. I can't believe it's been 10 years, but you look at how far it's come.

JEV: They've done a brilliant job. Nothing bad to say on what they've done. It's frankly amazing. The value of the teams — I don't know — quadrupled, quintupled, 10x. It went through the roof. No more paying drivers. I don't know if all the teams are making money, but all the teams are able to get pretty good sponsorship, pretty good visibility. It's a completely different world now.

进入 Formula E：从零开始

Host: And so then we have the move to Formula E. Talk us through that decision, how you thought about it and the entrance there — because you also own part of the team.

JEV: Formula E was a difficult choice because it was a completely new championship. You're in F1, you see yourself in the best category. You don't even look what's done. One of my managers called me and said — it was after Abu Dhabi — "Obviously there is no seats in F1 for next year, I'm sorry." I was like, "okay, so what's next?" He said, "There is this championship Formula E, I can maybe get you a seat in." I was like, "okay, why not." I had nothing else to do.

He was able to negotiate a contract for one race with Andretti. I was like, "okay, how much they're paying?" They're paying for your flights — that's it.

Host: Only for one race?

JEV: Yes. And I was like, you know what, I got nothing else to lose. I've lost everything already. Obviously with the F1 years, it's not like I had a lot of cash. I had nothing. So I was like, okay, let's do it. "So I said one race — you have to leave tomorrow." I was like okay, fine. No simulator, no testing the car, nothing at all. Okay, let's go see how it works.

Honestly, I arrived there and it was such a nice atmosphere — drivers I knew, very good drivers, good teams. It was buzzing. There were parties also going on during the race weekend. They were mental. I had never seen that in motorsport. I was like, wow, that's pretty cool. I like Formula E, it's nice.

Michael Andretti, I will always remember, before the qualifying, said: "Jev, if you're doing the top 10, I'm gonna sign you for the rest of the season. So good luck for quali and we speak after." I did quali, I did the pole by sweet ends — obviously was not expecting that. This is when my manager started — I started to see the effect that my manager could have. Obviously, he signed a pretty good deal for the rest of the season thanks to that pole. I could have won the race, but the suspension broke in the last lap. Anyway, my first impressions were made in FE and I was off to a good start.

Host: What was the money comparison to F1?

JEV: The deal I had was already more than the first year I had in F1.

Host: Wow, for the rest of the season.

JEV: Yeah.

Host: You must have been a big name for them, so that's a really good scoop for Formula E as it starts out — to be able to bring a Formula One driver.

JEV: And fine, as you said, I didn't have a seat. I think Formula E helped to bring Formula One drivers to the championship financially to the teams. So there was an incentive to get known names in the championship.

从内燃机到电动车的怪异感

Host: I mean, you kind of just glossed over the fact that you just walked out of Formula One and then got a pole in Formula E. But I'd imagine the cars are pretty different.

JEV: Completely different. So weird. Very weird. I remember actually there was a Bourdais on that race, and I saw him after I did the pole. I'm not gonna say what he told me and I'm not gonna say what I told him back, but since that day it's fair to say that he didn't like me very much.

But you go from full combustion to electric. That was very weird. The first time I go out of the pit, not hearing the noise — the wind for the first time, it's very weird. It's like a buzz, so weird. Then you have a steering wheel, two pedals, but it's battery, it's power. Just to drive the car fast over one lap, it's very similar. It doesn't matter what you put in the car — obviously you're still gonna have some differences but not that many. The biggest difference was in the race — obviously having to save energy and recover the energy. That's the biggest difference that you can have to Formula One or to any other series.

Ferrari 储备车手与忍耐力赛事

Host: Am I right in saying that you also did some test driving for Ferrari?

JEV: Yes. So that same weekend I got the call from Ferrari saying that I was going to be the reserve driver. So that was nice. I did two years with Ferrari. But frankly, to go to almost all the Formula One weekends drinking coffee, knowing that you're never gonna drive… I could see that I had some — obviously I was in the paddock, I was able to talk to some of the teams. There was some interest, but the interest fades away after a certain time. So staying in the F1 paddock just to watch your friends race and drink coffee all day long — I didn't want to continue doing this. So I stopped after two years the Ferrari and I wanted to do also endurance racing.

Host: What was that like as a team when you get inside it and you started to see how Ferrari operated compared to what you'd lived in for so many years in Red Bull?

JEV: Quite different. But in a way not so different — because Red Bull is a British team, but I was with the Italian team, Toro Rosso. So to go from Toro Rosso to Ferrari was actually quite similar. The factory was very close — one was Maranello, the other one, Faenza, is like, I don't know, less than a hundred kilometers away. More or less the same people. I saw a lot of people in Ferrari that went to Toro Rosso back then. It's just the political pressure that there is in the Ferrari team that is pretty heavy.

Host: You feel that even as a test driver?

JEV: Oh, yeah.

Host: How does that show? Is that in meetings?

JEV: It shows in meetings. It shows when the big boss is coming to the team, to the race. People panic. I don't know why, but they were panicking back then.

触底反弹：重塑形象

Host: That's quite interesting — so you could do that with Ferrari but you could also then compete in Formula E.

JEV: Yes. And I wanted to go to endurance racing, 24 Hours of Le Mans. I did the test with Toyota. They — it's fair to say — they didn't like me very much.

Host: Why?

JEV: Sorry?

Host: Why?

JEV: I don't know. For, I guess, the image that I had in F1, and it didn't work out.

So I came to the realization after F1, and one year down Formula E — so the first race went pretty well, but during two years, I haven't won a single race in Formula E. I didn't have a contract in Hypercar. Back then it was LMP1. No factories wanted me. And I came to the hard realization that I had to change something. I had to change my image. I had to do it all over again to convince people that I was actually good — because the image of a Formula One driver wanting to prove himself in endurance racing, not being really a team player, wanting everything for himself…

Host: Do you think that was a fair image?

JEV: Yeah, it was. It was a fair image. Yes and no. I understand the image, but it's not necessarily what I wanted it to be. But I can understand that, for example, Toyota had that image of me.

Host: But when you look back now, would you have done anything differently to not have that image?

JEV: No, no, no. In F1, I would have done things differently, and maybe I would still be in F1. But the two years after my exit in F1 were pretty hard. I think I touched the bottom rock solid.

So two things happened. The first one was I decided to go to LMP2 — where the young drivers and pay drivers were. I went to a small team where I actually needed to pay for the travels and everything. When you come from F1 and you go to LMP2 and you pay for yourself — for the travels — I didn't have a sponsor. It was out of my own pocket. I was like: "You know what, I'm gonna do it. I'm gonna show the world of endurance that I can be a team player, that I can be performing well in those cars and prototype cars, that I'm not going to crash during a course of 24 hours race or during the other endurance racing."

Then I got to be friends with Russian driver Roman Rusinov, who had the best team in LMP2 — it was called G-Drive. That guy hated me for a reason I don't know, because I never spoke to him. But in Japan, we had dinner together and after the dinner, he said: "Actually, Jev is a pretty cool guy." Then we started discussing and he offered me a contract for the year after. We won the championship, we won the 24 Hours of Le Mans together in LMP2. I did two-three years with him. We were very successful. My image completely changed also in the paddock. This is when I got the contract in Hypercar with Peugeot.

So it was a difficult moment that I needed to pass by and to basically go down to earth and start working again.

买下一支 Formula E 车队

Host: Was it learning to play the game as a political element to these organizations? And maybe if you go in as the fiery character or have a particular way of being that doesn't fit with the organization's way…

JEV: In endurance it was the case, but in Formula E it was not the case. Formula E I did something completely, dramatically different.

I went to that team Virgin Racing — the second year Formula E. That could not have gone any more wrong. I had a massive fight with the team principal, team owner. He wanted to put me out of a car because I was sick and wanted to use another driver. I'm not going to go into details. In its own, it could be a one-hour show just explaining what happened.

But bottom line, halfway in the season, I realized: okay, it didn't work out with the first team, it's clearly not working out with that team. My image is going through a sink — no one thinks I can win a championship in Formula E, no one thinks I can win a race. I have to do something about it.

This is when I talked to one of my long-term sponsors. I told him: "We have to buy a Formula E team. We're going to do our own team, our own way." I knew exactly what was wrong in that particular team. I knew that there was a deal to be done with Stellantis, with DS, where I could bring DS in the team as a manufacturer to get their powertrain. We were able to do that with a team that was called Aguri. It was back then the worst team on the grid. We worked out through everything. This is why I became partner with the Chinese — with CMC and Seca Sports.

Host: How much did it cost to buy the team?

JEV: Back then it was not much, and it was a good deal. But I was not doing it for the money. I was doing it for controlling which people I was going to put in the team. I wanted a good team — non-political, only result driven and no politically driven — which was the case in all the teams I had been with and which was frankly a nightmare.

So I remember in the contract that I had with the Chinese, I had the hand — so they had their hand on the marketing and on the business side, I didn't want to hear about it. I said I want only to control the technical side of things. The first meeting we had was: "Okay, we have a million for marketing for doing activation on the races and everything." I said: "Hold on, that million you don't use it for marketing — you give it to us for the engineering side." They accepted.

For the first year, we haven't done anything — but it was all going into hiring good engineers, into having a good simulator. The team we built around ourselves was the best team I've ever been with. So we won our first race the first year, and then we won three championships in a row.

Host: And you won two, right?

JEV: Yes.

Host: The only driver to win multiple, is that right?

JEV: Yes. And I went to hire the driver that actually beat me, Antonio Félix da Costa. For me, it was a way to get back to who I wanted to be in the championship, and I don't think I could have done it differently in a different team.

Host: Was there a conflict ever being a team owner — part team owner — and a driver?

JEV: No, never. Actually, it worked out pretty on the opposite side because for once, the team principal had the driver that knew the interest, and I also knew what a team wanted from a driver. So actually it gave me a different perspective as to what a driver has to bring to a team. It's not only on the sporting side, it's also on the people side. And it's all about people — how much they're willing to work for you, how much they're willing to sacrifice. Because it's not easy for them as well. We're traveling the world, they have family, they don't see their kids — it's not easy to go from testing to simulator to racing and always give their best.

Formula E 的成长与挑战

Host: How do you look at the growth of Formula E? It's been — what are we in, 12, 13 seasons?

JEV: 12 seasons now.

Host: It's had not a huge amount of time to establish itself but it's done well in that time. But it's also got some challenges, right? Trying to scale and grow. So where do you view the sport today?

JEV: I'd say the first five years had a growth trajectory that was pretty phenomenal. Then COVID happened, and the years following COVID has been pretty rough for the championship. They brought a car, the Gen 3, that I don't think was a good car. I've always been quite outspoken about that car. It was not very good looking, performance-wise not very good, and I think people could not really get interested into that kind of racing that we were having with this car.

But now they changed a lot. They bring in the Gen 4 — different car which is a beast. I've driven it, I've tested it. It's a completely different ballpark and it's gonna bring the championship into a different dimension. I'm pretty convinced of that.

And just to answer a very quick question — let's say if Formula E was a listed company, I would be buying a lot of stock right now. Because I think the next few years are going to be amazing for the championship.

Host: Do you think Formula One shifting to hybrid and leveraging more electric is a good thing for Formula E, or do you think it makes no difference?

JEV: I think it's a good thing. I think it's a good thing because at least Formula E — the fans and the sponsors involved with us — they know what we're here for. It's a 100% electric championship. And Formula One there is no clear message. Why do they go 50% electric? Why do they have the super clipping down the straight line? Why do we have this kind of overtakings? The fans of Formula One don't understand it. The fans of Formula E watching Formula One understand. As I actually with all the drivers talking about Formula E — Ariana, it's like a Formula E on steroids. People talk more about the championship, about Formula E. So I actually think it was a positive thing.

收入与奖金

Host: When you are a world champion, do you get a bonus?

JEV: Yeah, you get like prize money.

Host: So there are financial incentives at the top which are really meaningful. I'm just looking at this respectfully going — where's the money? 200, 500, 700. It's like for Formula One. And then Formula E — how does it work for drivers to make enough to travel the world, live a good life? It's just hard to get your head around. Has that changed as well? Formula E drivers paid a lot more now? I'm so sorry for being basic.

JEV: No problem. I think it's public knowledge what the Formula E drivers earn. For sure, not as much as Formula 1. The top drivers are between 1 to 3.

Host: 1 to 3 million.

JEV: When you take into consideration the bonuses and everything. Then the Formula E drivers also have — can do another championship like the endurance championship. Some drivers also have reserve or simulator roles with some F1 teams. So I don't know for sure, but I would say top earners within Formula E outside of F1 are between 1, 2, 3, 3.5.

Host: And then do they have brand deals in the same way? Are there big brands wanting to partner with you and you could do sponsorships?

JEV: It's more difficult in FE. And I'm not gonna lie, I think the visibility of the championship has not been where it should have been the last few years. But I'm very hopeful that in the coming year with the new cars, the new regulation…

Host: Is that purely because of the cars or is it a packaging problem? I've never seen it on any platform. Maybe I'm not in the right platforms, but it ain't on my socials. It's not in short form done well. Maybe it's not packaged right.

JEV: I think it's a bit of both. The product is fixed with the new car next year. And the packaging also is going to be fixed. I've known now pretty much all the people working at Formula E. There are amazing people coming actually from F1, from Liberty. And don't forget that the owners are also Liberty. So they've done it with F1. They know what they've done good, and they know they can do it also with Formula E.

边际收益与心理战

Host: How have you as a driver approached that kind of marginal gains mentality? When we spoke to Nico Rosberg, he talked about trying to shave every second off — to the extent where he changed the color of the paint on his helmet because black paint was 80 grams lighter than the metallic paint. Just to get those extra seconds. How deep did you go?

JEV: I wouldn't say to that extent because that's pretty much in your head. 80 grams is not going to make a single difference. Everything you can do mentally speaking, in your head, it's going to have a much bigger impact. For some drivers like Nico, knowing that 80 grams was going to make like a thousand or two thousand of a second difference and that it helps him — then for sure he was ready to do it. I know I'm not wired that way.

Host: What did you do to retain coolness, focus, and just stay in your own head and not get lost in the madness of it all? You're sitting in the car, it's about to be race time — what do you tell yourself?

JEV: Actually nothing. It's weird. It's not long ago, I've had that question many times and I never know what to answer. Once I was on the grid and I remembered that question and said, okay, what am I thinking now?

Host: Dude, make it up. Say: "I think of my wife and kids, how much I love them, how special they are." You said you want a brand image rehabilitation thing. Okay, I'm going to learn from you. Next time I come up with a good answer.

JEV: What are you thinking? Yeah, thank you very much.

Le Mans：世界最美赛事

Host: You mentioned endurance racing, Le Mans. Can you just tell people a little bit about what it's like when you compete on those disciplines compared to the ones we talked about?

JEV: Well, Le Mans is to me the best race in the world. Number one, first it's history. The track itself. The fighting that we have over the course of that race. The amount of fans watching the race, coming to the race. It's just so special.

Have you ever been to Le Mans?

Host: No.

JEV: Okay. If you go, if you have the chance to go, you will not ask the question why. You will know. You will feel it. It's insane. It's hard to describe, honestly.

To tell you about Le Mans — so it was six or seven years ago when I wanted to take over that Formula E team. The first thing I did was to partner with Stellantis and DS, because I wanted to be part of that family because I knew they had a program coming three or four years down the line to come to Hypercar with Peugeot. I had this in the back of my mind for a very long time to be able to race for Stellantis brands. Before it was DS, now I'm racing for Citroën, but still it's for Stellantis. To be in the best Formula E team and to be also in the best hypercar team with Peugeot.

I hope that next year with the new Formula E and the updates that we're bringing with the Peugeot, I'm going to be in the situation where I can both win the World Championship in FE and the 24 Hours of Le Mans in Peugeot. That's been my dream ever since I left F1. That was my target. I tried to do everything. I try to direct everything in career choice to be in that situation and for this to happen one day — to be in the Le Mans situation, to be in the situation where I can win for Stellantis both in Formula E and in Le Mans.

耐力赛的"团队心法"

Host: Well, you mentioned the team dynamic you don't get elsewhere. For people who may not understand, explain what happens in those moments, because it is so different to other motorsport.

JEV: So first of all, you're three drivers per car. Your three drivers compared to Formula One are not drivers that you need to kill. Because when you're teammates in F1, you need to kill your teammate — that it's either him or you anyway. But in endurance, it's different. You need to help them. You don't want one lagging. You want three killers with you that are your best friends, and that you know you're gonna go until the end of the night together fighting, protecting your car and helping each other.

That's something you don't really have in motorsport — to be with teammates that you actually want to help, or that you actually want to be super performant together. Because the stronger your two other teammates are, the stronger you're going to be as a team. So that is the first thing that is completely different from any other category.

Then it's all the preparation, all the work. There's a lot more going on in Le Mans than in the championship and in F1. The amount of endurance testing that we do during the winter — when it's freezing cold, you're alone on the track driving for three hours in a row. You know the pain and suffering that we are going through — mechanics, engineers, drivers. Let's be honest, driving at night in Paul Ricard in the winter alone in the fog or rain — it's not fun.

But we're doing this for one race, one purpose only. So when you arrive to that race, we all know all the difficult moments that we've had to get to where we are on the grid. It's like you're super pumped up before the race. You have so much emotion coming and you can feel it in the garage through the team principal, engineers, mechanics. It's pretty special.

Host: Is it difficult to shift from that individual mentality to the team mentality?

JEV: Not for me. Maybe for some drivers it is, but I actually never found it difficult.

Host: So cool. Because you know that — three drivers.

JEV: No, because at the end of the day, I want to win. And you understand really quickly that if you want to win in that kind of championship, you need to have two strong teammates and to help one another.

投资、对冲基金与商业起步

Host: Jev, tell us about what you've been doing away from the track. When you were driving, were other drivers talking about starting businesses, investing? Was business part of the language?

JEV: When I was driving — it's only recently that we started to talk a lot about business and investing with some drivers. So the best teammate I've had in that perspective was Stoffel Vandoorne, who was also a Formula One driver. We spent pretty much all our time speaking about business and investment than racing. I think 10% of our time spent is talk about racing, the other 90% is business.

We've done a few investments together. We started a hedge fund together as well. I can't speak too much about it, but we spend a lot of time developing a strategy tool that is working pretty well but still early stage. We're putting a lot of efforts into it and hopefully it's gonna turn out to be a good one.

I'd say that I understand every sportsman have not facilities but have a pretty good entourage. We get to meet a lot of people through the course of our career, and it's a question of grabbing the opportunities when they come in front of you.

Some sportsmen will say: "No, I'm fully focused on my career. I don't want to know. I don't want to do anything else" — which is fair. I respect that a lot. There are some athletes that say: "I want to see the real opportunities right now that are in front of me." And in general, when you have that mentality as an athlete, your career tends to end pretty soon. Because the moment you shift your focus from being successful at what you do to trying to be successful at something else, it doesn't work anymore. Because you're fighting guys that put 100% of their focus into winning and into beating you. And the moment you let that focus down a little bit is the moment you're over.

So I would say I'm in between the two. At the moment I do a lot of investment, but with smart people, with people I trust. While I'm still in my career, I'm more in the learning phase, trying to meet people, trying to understand that world by doing also investment. And when I finish my career, I would have very good opportunities, very good contacts, and a much better knowledge than what I had a couple of years ago.

资产配置与 Nvidia 的运气

Host: How do you think about setting an investing budget? When you think about your overall asset allocation, often it's like save a third, spend a third, invest a third.

JEV: A little bit different. I have a family, two kids. So first thing is my family — how much do I need in a year for my family to be comfortable? Once I know that number, all the rest is investment. All the rest.

Host: What has been the best investment for you?

JEV: Stock market. Nvidia. I bought it at 12. I made like, I don't know, more than a thousand percent, I think. I got lucky to try and understand the data center business and the compute power that was needed and the thirst for those big companies to need those data centers.

I'm going to be fair — I'm not Warren Buffett. I got lucky as well. I invested in a couple of companies, but I was lucky. I'm not going to pretend saying I knew Nvidia was going to be there — because that wouldn't be right.

Host: You did restaurants as well, famously told to be the worst investment ever.

JEV: Yes. Well, I wouldn't say I've done an investment. It was one of my good friends and also therefore a business partner who lives in Dubai, lived in Dubai forever. I supported him in the first restaurant that he opened. I basically just invested my money with him. I think he's done a fantastic job in opening those restaurants and selling them as well.

Host: And you made money from them.

JEV: Yes. He made the money for me. So thank you very much to him.

对银行家和理财顾问的不信任

Host: Did you do all this on your own? No one was telling you to do it, no one was telling you to be interested or curious about this?

JEV: No, I had someone in my early career that was supposed to tell me that, but I ended up in court with him. So I have a pretty bad opinion about bankers, money managers, basically everybody trying to sell you something and get a fee from it. They in general never outperform the market. They're not that smart either. They're just very good sellers.

I think I got lucky to get screwed by bankers, money managers quite early on. Lost quite a bit. Luckily it was a long time ago. It was a lot for me back then. But it was the best thing that ever happened to me — to get screwed by those people quite early on. Because it made me realize who they were and how much of a — I live in a world of sharks, but obviously the world of finance and investment is not a world of business either.

Host: There's a big challenge for athletes now that we see — they just don't necessarily know who to trust. Because everyone knows that they've got a lot of liquidity. There's probably a lot of cash that they can put into things. But at the same time, who are the right people when they're pitching them to follow into this? As you said, as you joked with us earlier, you had to leave education relatively early. While you may bring some really good experiences and skill sets from being a professional sports person, there's an education gap, there's a knowledge gap that you look to bridge.

JEV: I think you have to be honest being an athlete that there are a lot of things that you don't know and that you have to be willing to learn. If you're not willing to learn, you're gonna get screwed 100%.

退役之后的方向

Host: Where do you see the most value that you bring to these types of organizations, if it's not knowledge of a particular industry?

JEV: I'm not yet valuable to the point where I want to be — because as I said before, I have a career that I need to take care of. I represent a brand and I cannot be seen to leave that on the side doing something else. So as of today, I'm 100% focused in bringing the best result I can for the manufacturer I represent. But I think in the future, I will have a lot to bring — not only by the contacts that I have, but also by the mentality that I acquired after so many either failures or success. Failure or success is driven the same way in any sport or in any ventures. Whatever you learn in sport, this is something you can put into any business venture.

Host: So your future from a business perspective, do you see it being a split of investments and involvements in motorsport and businesses outside of it?

JEV: I think when I stop racing, I'm gonna leave motorsport for good. Do something completely different for a couple of years. Learn something else.

Host: And do you still love it?

JEV: Yeah, I love it. But I will go back to motorsport. It's my love, it's what I know the best. But have a break. It's a good break in the sense that, you know, trying to learn some other skills, see some other worlds and come back with a fresher vision. I think it's very important. Toto Wolff has done that.

对社交媒体的复杂态度

Host: How important is social media for you today and for drivers, do you think? To what extent is it a helpful addition — monetization, brand deals — versus a negative detrimental: pressure, judgment, criticism from media and press?

JEV: I'm not the best driver on social media.

Host: Do you do it actively?

JEV: No, I'm not doing it much. I know I should do it more, but it's not my, as we say in England, it's not my cup of tea. I wish I could live in a world where social media was not existing. Let's put it that way.

Host: Why?

JEV: I fully — sorry, why?

Host: Why?
