item.title: [a16z] Why AI Feels Like the Internet in 1997 | Benedict Evans on a16z
item.dc:creator: a16z
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽略的悖论：这次"和以前一样"，恰恰因为它"完全不同"

Benedict 全场最反直觉的论断，藏在结尾的 IBM 1950 年代广告里——"一台 IBM 计算器，给你额外 150 个工程师"。他用这张旧广告解构了整场 AI 革命的修辞：每一次"前所未有的技术革命"都长得一模一样，包括"它前所未有"这句话本身。 这是一个递归的认知陷阱——我们用"AI 是独一无二的"来论证它的颠覆性，却忘了这正是 PC、互联网、移动互联网当年的同款修辞。真正的预测价值不在于"AI 多特殊"，而在于承认它会和以前一样陷入混乱、淘汰、再均衡，然后在 20 年后变得无聊到没人记得它曾经稀奇。

跨领域连接：基础模型公司正在重演电信运营商的命运——而它们浑然不觉

这是整段对话最锋利的一刀，但被埋在了大量数据里。Benedict 把基础模型行业映射到 2009 年的移动运营商：建造了人类最复杂的全球基础设施 → 流量增长 1500-2000 倍 → 改变了每个人的生活 → 自己没赚到钱。 所有"酷的东西"被苹果、Google、Netflix 拿走了。他甚至自嘲——他曾在一家"申请了银行牌照、以为自己会做移动银行"的电信公司工作。

这个类比令人后背发凉的地方在于：Anthropic 押注 coding、OpenAI 想"做 15 件事建立产品价值"，本质上就是 2008 年的 Vodafone 想做移动银行。 当基础设施层试图向上爬到应用层抓取价值时，几乎从未成功过——因为应用层的逻辑（行业 know-how、用户洞察、网络效应）和基础设施层的逻辑（规模、效率、资本密度）是两套语言。芯片厂商没抓到、ISP 没抓到、电信运营商没抓到。Nvidia 现在的高毛利，可能是电信运营商 2000 年代初的高 ARPU——回头看是"巅峰前的最后辉煌"。

一个被很少人注意的认知结构性转折：问题正在"离开科技行业"

Benedict 引用了自己当年的一篇旧文——"Netflix 不是科技公司"——来揭示一个正在发生的范式迁移：AI 真正重要的问题，正在从旧金山搬到洛杉矶、纽约、伦敦、孟买。 这不是"AI 落地难"的陈词滥调，而是一个更深的观察：

当 AI 进入律所、咨询、广告、好莱坞，判断一个 AI 产品是否成功的能力，将从"懂技术"变成"懂这个行业里那些没被写下来的事"。 麦肯锡咨询师的核心价值，是被允许进入公司去问那些"不该问的人"——那些"奖金 KPI 故意和公司战略相反"的人。这些隐性知识 (tacit knowledge) 无法进入训练数据，因为它从未被文档化。

这指向一个更深的哲学问题：LLM 擅长输出"任何人都会做的答案"，但人类付高价雇佣的，恰恰是"别人不会做的答案"。 律师、医生、咨询师、艺术家的全部价值，建立在"non-average"之上。这创造了一个奇妙的市场分层——AI 把"均值"的市场压成零边际成本，而把"差异化判断"的市场推向溢价。这就是为什么会计师 50 年来工作内容完全变了，但客户买的东西没怎么变——任务被自动化了，但"判断"作为最终交付物没变。

最让我意外的子观察：Benedict 暗示了一个新型阶级分化的开端——那些能"指挥 AI"的人和那些"被 AI 替代"的人之间，分野不是技术能力，而是行业默会知识的深度。 这和工业革命时"懂机器的工程师 vs 操作工"的分化不同，更像是"懂如何让组织运转的人 vs 懂如何完成任务的人"。

📝 转录文本

开场

Agentic coding went from being kind of useful to really changing everything. It was going to be magic. And in 20 years time, we'll just say, "Well, of course that's how it is. Computer's always done that."

Every year, Silicon Valley awaits Benedict Evans presentation. Former A16Z partner, author of one of the industry's most read newsletter, The Mind Behind AI eats the world.

We are in this extreme scarcity. Like, we can't spend $10 trillion a year on AI infrastructure cuz there isn't $10 trillion a year there to spend on it.

The big part of your thesis is this idea that models are going to end up as commodities. I don't think foundation models are a product. I don't think a chatbot is a product. I think the value will be further up.

Explain the reasoning that they're a bit in what they look like.

There's like three or four building blocks you can put on the table. One of them is...

Benedict. Welcome back to the Acing Z podcast.

Thank you.

回顾过去一年：发生了什么变化

Last time you were here, we were discussing the first iteration of your presentation AI use the world. Uh you know, you since wrote it almost a, you know, year and a half ago. At at this point we're get you always begin your presentation with your you know what are the big questions but I'm curious this time first before getting into the what are questions going forward I want you to reflect on what have we learned since you originally uh made the presentation what's played out um and let's reflect back on what's changed in the last year

so I think we have much more of a sense of diverging product strategy we have much more a s of a sense of kind of competitive tension that goes beyond just make a bigger model faster with more about more more compute. Um we've had several iterations of open AI strategy in particular from sort of everything all at once yesterday to oops no maybe we should double down on coding.

Um clearly agentic coding started working and so all the focus in tech has kind of narrowed in massively onto that as something that has absolute product market fit in the sense that like the customers are pulling it out of your hands. Um and um and of course that comes with the supply crunch around capacity and price imbalance imbalance of supply demand capacity capex pricing that we see at the moment.

Um so that's kind of the big shift like we had a moment of like this is kind of sort of working and kind of exciting but we're not quite sure what we're going to do with it to like right it works for coding um will it work for anything else like yes almost certainly but that's what's working right now and so that's become we've got this kind of much narrower focus.

Um otherwise um you know the chartman numbers keep coming up, the models keep getting bigger, the capex keeps growing, the usage keeps growing, people using this more. But most of the sort of fundamental questions you might have had two or three years ago didn't really have answers. Like we don't know if there'll be a winner in the models. We don't know if they can capture value up the stack. We don't know how much the models can do. Um we don't see a way that consumers will use this daily rather than weekly with the technology we have right now. So all all of those questions are still open.

为什么 Coding 率先跑通

Yeah. And just on on the on the coding, how could could we have figured could we have foreseen that that would have been the the the use case that really would have taken off or what's sort of a reflection on that?

Well, um you deterministically you could have said, well look who's messing about with this stuff? Software developers. What are software developers going to try and make work software development? Um so you know at a very kind of simplistic naive level well yeah the stuff that should work is software develop first is software development just as like kind of I often compare this moment to like the internet in like 9798 but it's also like the PCs in the early 80s or the late '7s. It's incredibly exciting but it's not quite clear what it's for and it doesn't quite work yet and clearly the first thing that people did with PCs was make computers. Um, and the first thing that people are doing with LLMs, in a sense, LLMs are computers, is to make more compute.

Um, and so that's not terribly surprising. I think the shift is been at the beginning of this year clearly that agentic coding went from being kind of useful to really changing everything. And I'm not sure you could have you clearly there were people who were going to say, well, this is going to be able to do absolutely anything. And so they will say, well, yes, look, I told you. Um, but I don't think anyone kind of kind of could have deterministically predicted exactly when that was going to happen and that it was going to be coding it would work first.

对工程师团队的影响：我们还不知道

And and what have we learned about sort of uh, you know, say more about what this means for engineers, junior engineers, senior engineers, sort of the the jobs discussion, how teams are organized, uh, etc. What have we learned so far?

I don't think we've learned anything. I mean, you know, this this didn't this didn't this didn't work six months ago.

Yeah.

and everyone is scrambling around trying to work out what it means. And you know, you can get very very into the noise and the detail and what did somebody say at a party yesterday. So, oh my god, that's how it's all going to work. Um, you know, it's going to take a couple of years for this all to settle down. You know, if nothing else because of the pricing, you know, we've got this enormous crunch between the demand and the supply and hence the pricing.

Um, so we don't know what, you know, what a team is going to look like. I think people are asking new questions around, you know, the sort of the obvious one of, you know, do you hire junior people and if so, what are they doing and why were you hiring junior people in the past and were you actually hiring to do the thing that they did or were you hiring them to do something else? And so if you automate away a class of stuff that used to get done by people, then what will happen? And that's sort of becomes much more real now in software development because you actually are automating a bunch of stuff that used to be done by people. So those questions are kind of now rather than theoretical. But I don't think anybody can possibly say they kind of know what the market structure is going to look like or what the career of a software engineer is going to be in three years time. I think it would be you'd be insane to think that you could know that yet.

OpenAI 的战略演变

Yeah. the talk about uh open AI uh talk about what's most uh surprised you or how have you kind of made sense of their sort of strategy development and and the questions that they have going forward.

Well, you know, it's always been such a such a a tranquil drama-free environment. So, you know, it's and you know, obviously they've had the issue with with with Fiji Simo having to take a medical leave um which kind of shuffled things up a bit.

Look, clearly the second half, last quarter of last year, this their question was right, well, the models are the models, but what else? And how do we get people to do other stuff with this? You know, ask chat GP GPT for 15 ideas for what we could do to build value on top of infrastructure, and then we'll do all of them. It's almost literally what what it looked like. And then um um Anthropic with having less capital raised said, "No, we're going to focus on coding." And they got coding working. Um whether that was like a deliberate strategy or kind of they stumbled into it is you know for other people to say but like clearly that worked but the question kind of still remains.

So mobile didn't need to wait for um the internet or cellular networks like mobile data. Mobile internet didn't need to wait for it kind of needed to wait for cellular data but it didn't need to wait for like the internet to happen and the internet didn't need to wait for PCs and PCs didn't need to wait for consumer electronics and semiconductors and so on. So you've always got this accelerating adoption and you know when when when your boss my old boss Mark Andre was working on Netscape there were like doubledigit millions of PCs on the entire planet. I think the second point is like at the early stage of any of these shifts it's not really clear how it's going to work and nothing works. So you know like I'm just about old enough to remember this. I'm not sure how how how old you are, but like, you know, anyone in their 30s doesn't really remember a time when it was completely normal that you'd be working and then everything on the screen would just freeze and you just have to crawl under your desk and unplug the computer and then pray that like some of what you done in the last hour might still be there. That just doesn't happen anymore. And go back to, you know, the 80s and like you bought a sound card. Well, that's $300. You won't have sound on your computer. 用新技术做"新事"，而不是"旧事的新版"

It it seems like yeah, one of the questions we're now asking is what sort of follows is um sort of what was in unreasonably expensive to do before that now is is possible and may you know is it so I don't something crazy like rebuilding YouTube from scratch or rewriting Linux from scratch or

yeah it's funny I mean you know the other the the paired fallacy of course is the new thing comes along and says well we're going to build the new thing with the the old thing with the new thing um of course we're going to we're going to build office with open source we're going to rebuild it on the web And you know, it turns out like, you know, guess what? Look at Google Docs. It's got like 20% of the market because that's actually not the point.

What's interesting is to do something else, is to do something new. Um, and it's to shift that level of abstraction and and and to kind of spot problems that have never existed. I mean, you know, it's the experience you get sitting in pitches all day at a venture firm is there's some stuff where you think, well, that sounds kind sounds kind of useful. and the stuff where you think I'm not quite sure that why that would work. But there are some things that like kind of fill a hole in the universe and like as soon as somebody explains it to you, you think wow why did never nobody do that before? Why did no one see that that thing existed and that's you know where you know part of the fun part of looking at startups is and that's what people will do with this. people will suddenly work out a way that you could turn that that will work out that that problem existed and no one including the people who have that problem no one realized that problem existed and then they'll go out and make a thing to solve it

this is also incidentally going back to an earlier point this is why I don't see the I think that this is the problem with the idea the model will do the whole thing and if you kind of you kind of go back and think about all the pictures you've seen since you joined S6C how many of them were things where people in the industry knew that was a problem like quite often the answer is actually No, actually no one in the industry thought that was a problem and it was actually took like two years to explain to them and persuade them that that problem actually existed at all and that this new thing would fix that for them. And that's kind of the problem with the idea that you know you know middle manager in finance is going to use this tool to solve this big global industry problem like no because no one knew that industry problem was there let alone could could work out the right way to build a tool to solve it.

SAS 行业的未来格局

Does this imply a less consolidated SAS environment than than before AI? Maybe less bundling or single behemoths like the Microsoft Enterprise.

Gosh, way to bring me back down to earth. Like is a SAS industry going to be less consolidated, Benedict? That's all great, but like tell us about the stocks.

Um, what are the kind of building blocks that we can put down here? So, obviously, it's going to be way cheaper and quicker to build software. Obviously, there's going to be a bunch of stuff you could do with software that you just couldn't do before at all. Um, and so there will be more competition. there and of course this comes with a new margin structure but as per our conversation earlier we don't really know what that margin structure is going to look like

um are you going to go to you know outcomebased pricing it's really hard to like tie each button press in a piece of enterprise software to P&L sometimes you can in Salesforce or something there's an awful lot of software it would be really hard to say well you know the the work I did today did this to DPS therefore this is what we should pay for it um this is what we should pay for that piece of software I don't think that makes sense and long Anyway, but what does the pricing structure look like over time versus now? Um, there will be more competition. It will be easier to build stuff and quicker to build stuff.

框架一：企业软件的三个桶

The way that I sort of thought about I suppose this is there's maybe kind of two framings to think about this that are kind of useful. One of them is to say that if you think about the sort of enterprise software fleet today, you've got like three buckets. You've got like your big iron horizontal systems, so SAP and workday and your CRM and your capital management software and your payroll management software and so on. And then you've got um vertical software and typical big US company has like three to 400 SAS apps and then like another thousand apps that they built bought or built themselves internally running on prem and then in the middle you've got this kind of fuzzy improvised space of Excel and email and the shared file system and so

and stuff kind of moves back and forth between those and like in principle every SAS app is doing something that you could have done in SAP or you could have done in Excel like you could have like managed your graduate recruiting in workday, but at a certain point like if you're friends conversation the other day, like if you're PWC um and you hire however many thousand graduates every year to train to be accountants, um you probably got a piece of dedicated software that you built for yourself or maybe you hired Accenture to build and you probably hate it, but anyway, you've got this piece of dedicated hiring software or you bought something. If you are a company that hires five graduates a year, you're doing that in email and a shared Google sheet, like cuz why would you buy software for that?

And then like then there's a space in the middle. Do you do it in workday? Do you do in Excel? Do you do it in a dedicated app? And now you add chat EVC to that. Do you do that in an LLM? Is there an LLM tool that means you can do that in Salesforce where you couldn't do it before? Or you can do it in your vertical app that you couldn't do before? Um, do you use the LM to build yourself a tool for that? Just as you might have a company department that runs on a 10 10meg Excel spreadsheet that someone built 15 years ago. No one knows it works. No one know how it works, but they're still using that. So it kind of it's it arrives within this kind of broad fragmented complicated landscape and it's another set of options for how you would do that task.

框架二：LLM 在栈的顶部还是底部？

So this is kind of one framing to think about to think about it. I think the other framing to think about this is does the yellow lamb go at the top of the stack or the bottom of the stack. So on the one hand bottom of the stack is a feature inside Salesforce. So you're in Salesforce look at the history with this customer. Look at the context of every other sales call we've done. look at our business objectives and suggest an email or suggest me what I should do here and what I should say on the call to call the customer. So, it's a feature. It's a button that's controlled and has tooling and guardrails and everything else that are driven by that particular use case.

The other way to look at it is the example I gave earlier which is you know go look at Salesforce and workday and all of our email and Google Analytics and and and then synthesize something that you couldn't have done before.

So the the the the tension in both cases is where do you put the probabilistic software that can make mistakes and where do you put the deterministic system software that can't answer these kind of questions. So where do you put the database and where do you put the LLM and is it like which is at the top and which is at the bottom. The answer is probably both depending on on what you what you what you're doing and where it goes.

Um all of which is a long way of saying like what does this do to software and the answer is more software like way more software. I mean all software companies exist to solve problems created by other software companies. That was the joke in in security. Like all security software is exist to solve problems created by other security software. And like clearly that's that's what we went through with SAS. Like SAS gave us an order of magnitude, two orders of magnitude more software. Um and we should probably expect that with this.

Um what that gets to you to the SAS apocalypse is all the investors are kind of looking at all these companies and saying well we don't really know which of these companies are going to get screwed by all of this. Some of them must be like obviously there must be some you know go through the end of this and you know x% of all the SAS companies that are out there are going to get wiped out by this but you don't know which ones so you probably shouldn't derate the whole thing by 50%. But clearly you're going to like go I'm not sure I'm going to be long software at the moment until I have some idea of what the hell's going on.

隐性知识与组织复杂性

You you said in your talk with Ben Thompson that software is someone sat down and designed a workflow and said this is the right way of doing this from now on. But you also said that a process grows out of the way just a business runs. Does that just take time or do you think we need more experimentation, iteration from these vertical AI startups to get this the right shape of software for the future?

Well, in a sense, I mean maybe kind of a an interesting turn on this is this is both what what strategy consultants do and software companies do is they kind of look at what's going on inside a company and say, well, this is a crap way of doing it. This would be a better way of doing it. It would achieve your objectives better. And a software company kind of encodes that in software. and you know a strategy consultancy kind of encodes that in you know workflows and or charts and processes and training and you know objectives and you know maybe tells them to buy some software to do that thing or maybe now increasingly maybe builds them that software as well.

Um I think um another thing to talk about here is how much of what's done inside an organization is implicit and not documented and not in the training data and not something that anybody in that company could actually kind of sit down and draw you an eflow chart of and explain to you. Um that's what that's a big chunk of the value of Bane BCG McKenzie is that they have license to come into a company and talk to everyone and talk to the people you're not allowed to talk to that are in a different org and not get fired and to go and work out how this actually works as opposed to how it's supposed to work and why it is that people aren't doing the strategy because actually guess what their bonus targets depend on them not doing the strategy and work all of that out and be a team that's ready to come in from the outside and give you the answer and then you can blame them or have that kind of that that pre-baked solution.

Um that's not you know you that's that that's these are sort of problems in organizational management and how people function and how people can explain what they do that are very hard to write down and very hard to kind of bake into a claude skill and say there you are like make a PowerPoint. Um, and so there's a sort of broader how does this always work challenge here of how do you get people to use these technologies? Um, how do people adopt new tools? How do you work out how to help people adopt new tools and work out what new things you would do with them? Which is also what happened with cloud and the web and mobile and the internet and PCs and spreadsheets and so on.

AI 原生软件与新的界面形态

To that end, do do you think there's some kind of co-evolution between AI native software and new types of interfaces? for example, new customer service AI platforms that might not have had as much human facing UI or system of record software being built without a front end at all because its primary user will be AI agents querying it directly.

So I think these are kind of interesting ideas. They're things I struggle to have a strong opinion on because you know they're not kind of not deep deep into the weeds of of how enterprise infrastructure gets bought. I I wonder how new some of these questions are.

Um I remember um Chris Dixon saying like 10 15 years ago that you know APIs is a new BD and software wouldn't need come. Software companies could just you know open up your APIs and like well like what's old is new. Um you know you don't need an API anymore. You just have an MCP server and like people will just plug in the agent will just plug into that.

Um I don't know. I think the challenge with a lot of this stuff is that all the the decisions are really exception handling. Like the question is always what can you not automate? What requires someone to make a decision um and some judgment and have an opinion about it because maybe that hasn't been written down or that didn't happen before or doesn't look quite the way it happened before.

Um I think there's a sort of you know there are various ways of kind of think about separating out what gets automated and what doesn't. Um the way that I used in the deck was to talk about what's a task versus what's what's a job. We often the tasks that are used to accomplish the job might change without the job itself changing very much or without the thing that the job is selling to the client changing very much. Like if you think about what accountants did 50 years ago and what accountants do today. Um they do spend almost none of their time doing the same things but like to the client it's kind of the same thing. Um it just gets done in a completely different way with a whole bunch of different tasks.

Um, and one of the sort of the more sort of either profound or kind of abstract ways to think about this is where is it that you want the ab the average? Where is it that what you want is the way that everybody will do this? That's the way everyone would do it. That's what anyone would say. That's what anyone would make. That's what any associate would make. That's what anybody would give me. That's the answer anyone would give. Versus where is that not what you want? Where is it that you want the answer to a new question or a different answer or a different idea? Um because LLMs are are going to be very good at anything where you can describe how people do it and where what you want is the way anybody would do that and not so good at where you can't really explain why you did it like that and where you're doing it differently to the way people would normally do it.

CapEx 的物理上限

Various people including you know uh CEO of Google said that the risk of underinvesting is riskier than overinvesting. Is there any level of capex where that stops being true and are we getting there now?

Well, there's a financial gravity problem in that um Microsoft, Meta and Google are all on in line to spend over 50% of revenue on capex this year. And you know we think of telecoms as being capital intensive. telecom spend instead of 15 to 20% of revenue on capex. Um and so you know $700 billion is the guidance from the big four companies this year. Well you know telecoms is 300, mobile is 200, total telecoms is 300. Oil and gas depending on which definition and which bits of it you're counting is anything from 700 billion to a trillion dollars. I think from memory depends which exactly who you ask.

Um, so $700 billion a year is not an impossibly large amount of money as of what big global infrastructure costs. It's just a lot of money. Clearly, like those companies could not spend 1.5 trillion next year or if they did, they'd have to borrow it and they certainly couldn't sustain that level of spending um for any length of time.

Um, and so there's a certain point at which like that growth has to slow down because like there isn't any more money. Um now clearly you can talk about ROI and your ability to produce returns from that investment. Um and you know clearly the capital markets are willing to fund that up to a point but like pick a number at random like we can't spend $10 trillion a year on inf AI infrastructure because there isn't $10 trillion a year there to spend on it. So there's a finite there are kind of like laws of physics caps on the amount of money um that's available.

I'd hesitate to say something more tangible than that at the moment. I mean, I kind of almost go back to what I said at the beginning that like we've got a bunch we've got a bunch of multiples. So, um there's far more demand than supply. On the other hand, the efficiency is increasing massively. Um we don't know what the next model will be. We don't know where edge or open source come in yet, when edge and open source come in yet. And meanwhile, you're always chasing the next model. And so this is kind of the line that runs across all of it is is the model is only relevant for 3 to six months, six to nine months, whatever you want to say. And the model costs how many billion dollars. Um, and how much infrastructure do you need to do that? Um, I don't think that mass is really shaken out yet.

I mean, obviously you can, you know, there's a bunch of very clever semiconductor analysts who spend lots of time trying to put numbers on this. It is kind of like trying to put numbers on bandwidth, internet bandwidths in the late 90s. like you kind of know what the rows in the spreadsheet are, but you don't really know where the values are. All you can really say is, well, look, it can't be you. There's there's clearly physical limits on this.

Um I think you know another way to answer the question is like if you're Google or Meta or Microsoft um to some extent Amazon some extent Apple this is sort of an existential problem and you have a sort of you know a FOMO problem in that um so on the one hand your returns on the investment at the moment are hugely positive. Um, on the other, you can't let other people get away with this without you participating because then your company's gone and you are you don't want to end up like Microsoft in the 2000s or IBM in the '90s or indeed Meta in the 2010s where they are kind of continually getting shafted by Apple. Um, so if this is the future of compute, then you need to be participating in it. Um but obviously at the same time the CFO is sitting there saying well yeah that's great but um how much participation are we talking about here and I don't think we you know it's clearly at a certain point that curve is going to have to taper off cuz like there's nowhere else it can go.

Token Maxing 和 ROI 评估

Do you think there's going to be a reckoning around token maxing? Uh is it possible that companies have been overshooting AI usage and when they do proper ROI studies they'll pull back?

Well, obviously, you know, you've had people like using the most expensive model to dick around on the internet. Um, which is kind of what happened with mobile, you know, in 2010. Like, you know, you you got a $10,000 bill and you would have said, "Wait, wait, I thought this was a flat rate bundle." Like, what happened? Um, so there's like you've got you've got you obviously you've got a bunch of of like silly/mateful stories.

Um, I think there's also a a point of like I think but what what maybe what's slightly more interesting as a question is um and clearly there's going to be a point in which as I've said se several times we're at a moment of kind of massive dise equilibrium and the pricing has got to get back into alignment with the cost and the usage has got to get it into alignment with the pricing and the ROI. The challenge is it's a bit tricky at this early stage. It's quite hard to know what the ROI is. It's it's rather like giving everybody the internet in the late '9s and saying okay go off be more productive

and if you look at like there's a survey from deote there's also a survey from the fed that's in my presentation where if you go and ask CFOs where have you seen seen the benefits and most of the benefits so far have been stuff that's pretty hard to measure so like better analytics better customer support um more productivity you can make more slides more quickly you can do the analysis more quickly it's kind of tough to put a financial value on that it has a financial value but It's not the same as saying, well, we made this new thing with AI and it had this revenue or it saved us this much money. Those things just obviously those things take longer. It's harder to build a new revenue line than to give this to everybody and have them use it to make spreadsheets more quickly. So, there's a little bit of like, well, well, how long does this take?

I think the other answer to problem here, of course, is consumer surplus, which is to say that um which is kind of what happened with Excel in that, you know, guess what? you know, if if a DCF takes you a week, then you probably only do one or two DCFs. And if a DCF takes you 10 seconds, then you do 50 DCFs, but you probably can't charge any more money for that. Um, so some of what happens is that, um, these things become competitive necessities and everybody has to buy it, use it. Um, but the cost saving or the productivity gain that you get from it just kind of gets competed away. So you don't get to charge more for it, you I mean, if you're, you know, if you're at McKenzie and, you know, doing that or or Bane or BCG and doing that piece of analysis used to take a week and now it takes a day, um, you probably do five times more analysis and charge your customer the same and like your cost base hasn't changed either. So, like, you know, which is exactly the way to think about, you know, what happened with with with with investment banks and financial analysis. You just went did way more analysis with probably fewer people and charged customers the same amount of money.

给基础模型公司的建议

part of your your big part of your thesis is this idea that models are going to end up as commodities and yet the you know the layer that's raising the most money uh you know in the in the fastest time in history is these foundation model companies. Um so given that what advice might you have for them either either collectively or we can pick on someone individually um in order to in order to adapt?

It's not that I know that they're going to become commodities. my position is more more now well like hey here is a here is a chain of argument that says that deterministically it looks like these things will be commodities and explain to me why they won't be um and that's as far as I would commit to that

um I think the you know the the raising all this money I kind of go back to my point about mobile which again has no predictive value but it's worthwhile observation is that the mobile industry is very big and spends a lot of money on infrastructure and isn't very profitable and all the cool stuff is done by somebody else and then you do you know well what's the return on capital and the answer is well it depends which mark whether you're in America or Europe or or India or China um but meanwhile like that was a worthwhile thing to do and it produced a return for somebody but then it didn't ended up not controlling the whole thing and other people ended up getting more value from that um than they did

um you know I don't have the number in my head what is Google's net income last year was what $50 billion or something what was net income for you know the total telecoms industry I should really subscribe to Bloomberg then I could just answer these questions instantly. Um but like you're a pretty safe bet that Google, Meta, Amazon, um Microsoft, Apple produce more profits than the entire telecoms industry.

Um so this is a um is a puzzle is you're build you're driving the frontier forward. You're kind of caught in this trap that you have to keep competing because otherwise they'll do it and you'll fall behind. You've also got this thing that we haven't talked about at all which is you know hey aren't we just building AGI like we're going to build God in a box which you know some people they do believe although it's kind of hard to it's hard to analyze but maybe

um so you know carry you're going to carry on building this stuff but the practical question is well how do you get things that people want to use that aren't software that aren't that aren't software development I mean that's a good business um is that the only business there's you know pick a number of that many hundreds of billions millions of dollars it is to make the software industry more productive great then what I mean that's you know that's worth a trillion dollars maybe but but then what like how do you expand this into the rest of the economy into everybody else which is why you get these conversations about you know partnering private equity partnering with consultancies where you know exactly as we've been discussing guess what it's actually quite hard to work out what to do with this stuff if you're actually running a real company um so you go to Bane BCG Mackenzie or Infosys and cognizant and IBM app Accenture or private equity shelters. So there is this sort of sense of like on the one hand you so I'm sort of trying to work out the answer as I speak but like on the one hand you're building this big these bigger and bigger models and you've kind of feel like you've got to keep doing it but on the other hand yes but what are people doing with it?

Why do most people look at chat GPT and not really think of anything to do with it today?

结语：每 20 年的"魔法"循环

Last question. Is there anything from the presentation that you want to make sure uh listeners leave with?

The thing that I I used last year and I I used again is an IBM ad I found from the early 50s which has got a picture of a sea of of engineers all holding up slide rules and it's an IBM ad and it says you know an IBM electronic calculator gives you 150 extra engineers and that's like how many pictures have you seen at A6Z where that was the pitch.

Um, and we kind of remember like we go through these waves of these these fundamental technology changes every 10 or 15 or 20 years and they're all amazing and change everything and are completely unlike anything that's happened before. And so AI is amazing and transformative and completely unlike anything that's happened before. Mobile was quite a big deal too and so was the internet and so were PCs and so was computing. Those were all also very big deals where it was hard to tell what was going to happen.

And so we should sort of presume as a base case, okay, well, we're going to go through that again. And you know, that will produce a bunch of things that ruin people's lives and it will put a bunch of people out of work. Um, and you know, there'll be a bunch of stuff that we're not very happy about. Um, and there'll be a bunch of stuff that we all think is great. And then in 20 years time, we'll kind of forget that there was a world when computers couldn't do that.

Um, I mean, here we are. We've been on this call for an hour and our computers didn't crash and we're streaming HD video to each other and it's like, well, of course that worked. In fact, I'm also doing it with my iPhone. So, my iPhone is streaming to my Mac over Wi-Fi streaming video here and like it just works like it's magic and we don't notice it anymore. And I think that's really my kind of oneline description of how all of this is going to end up. It's going to be magic and in 20 years time we'll just say, well, of course that's how it is. Computers have always done that.

Yeah, that's a great place to great place to wrap. The presentation is called AI Essets the World. It's on Benedict Evans website. It is excellent. Uh there's a lot more that we didn't get to, so definitely go check it out. Benedict, this has been a great conversation. Thanks so much for coming to the podcast.

Thanks. Great to chat.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个被忽略的悖论：这次"和以前一样"，恰恰因为它"完全不同"
Benedict 全场最反直觉的论断，藏在结尾的 IBM 1950 年代广告里——"一台 IBM 计算器，给你额外 150 个工程师"。他用这张旧广告解构了整场 AI 革命的修辞：**每一次"前所未有的技术革命"都长得一模一样，包括"它前所未有"这句话本身。** 这是一个递归的认知陷阱——我们用"AI 是独一无二的"来论证它的颠覆性，却忘了这正是 PC、互联网、移动互联网当年的同款修辞。真正的预测价值不在于"AI 多特殊"，而在于承认它**会和以前一样陷入混乱、淘汰、再均衡**，然后在 20 年后变得无聊到没人记得它曾经稀奇。
## 跨领域连接：基础模型公司正在重演电信运营商的命运——而它们浑然不觉
这是整段对话最锋利的一刀，但被埋在了大量数据里。Benedict 把基础模型行业映射到 2009 年的移动运营商：**建造了人类最复杂的全球基础设施 → 流量增长 1500-2000 倍 → 改变了每个人的生活 → 自己没赚到钱。** 所有"酷的东西"被苹果、Google、Netflix 拿走了。他甚至自嘲——他曾在

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Danger of UBI – Alex Imas and Phil Trammell
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最意外的观察：UBI 不是经济问题，而是主权赎回问题

表面看，UBI 的争论围绕"钱够不够、激励对不对"。但 Imas 在开头那句话——"我们现在被赋予了可以转化为收入的劳动"——暴露了一个被几乎所有讨论者忽略的底层结构：劳动不只是收入来源，它是公民对国家的议价筹码。

这就是为什么 UBI 和"任何政府再分配项目"在本质上不同：领失业救济的人仍然拥有"重返劳动市场"的退出选项（exit option），而 AGI 时代依赖 UBI 的人没有退出选项。这在博弈论里有一个名字——叫做"hostage equilibrium"（人质均衡）。

跨领域连接：这与核威慑理论中的"second strike capability"结构同构。冷战时美苏之所以稳定，不是因为彼此信任，而是因为双方都保留了"被毁灭后仍能反击"的能力。一个失去劳动议价权的公民，相当于失去了二次打击能力的国家——表面和平，实则任人宰割。

二、隐藏的系统性真相：所有"温和方案"都在同一个滑坡上

注意对话中一个未被点破的模式——每一种替代方案都被对方用同一种攻击模式打回：

  • UBI → "选举官员可以撤销"
  • 负所得税 → "选举官员可以撤销"
  • 财富税 → "0.5% 没有政治稳定均衡，必然上涨"
  • 全民基本资本 → "indexing 不可能，政客会挑公司"

这不是四个独立的问题，而是同一个元问题的四张面孔：任何依赖"政治自我克制"的机制都会被政治本身侵蚀。这与生物学里的 Muller's Ratchet（穆勒齿轮） 惊人相似——无性繁殖物种的有害突变只能积累不能逆转，因为没有重组机制清除它们。政治再分配制度一旦启动，"加码"是单向齿轮，"退回"几乎不可能（看看美国所得税从战时临时税涨到 40% 的轨迹）。

真正的洞见：争论不应是"选哪种方案最好"，而是"哪种方案自带回滚机制"。而对话中没人提到的答案恰恰是——产权。产权之所以稳定，不是因为它"公平"，而是因为剥夺它需要可见的暴力，这制造了政治成本。

三、跨领域连接：这场对话其实在讨论"宪法物理学"

社保私有化、全民基本资本、David Autor 的"主权财富基金给每个人发股票"——它们看起来是经济政策，但真正的设计目标是把一项福利从"立法可撤销"变成"立法不可撤销"。

这与密码学里的"承诺方案"（commitment scheme）完全同构：你不是在传递信息，你是在让自己无法反悔。私有化社保的精妙之处不是收益率，而是它把"政府的承诺"转化成"个人的资产"——撤销它需要的不是一次投票，而是一次征收。

最反直觉的推论：从这个角度看，"低效但不可撤销"的制度可能优于"高效但可撤销"的制度。这解释了一个长期困扰经济学家的现象——为什么各国都偏爱明显次优的实物福利（食物券、公共住房、医保）而非现金转移？因为实物福利自带宪法锁：你很难在政治上撤销人们的医院。

所以当 Imas 担心 UBI 时，他真正担心的不是经济学，是他直觉到 UBI 缺少这把锁。而这把锁的学名，可能叫做"产权式福利"——一个在政治哲学、密码学、生物进化里反复出现的同一个深层结构。

📝 转录文本

一、UBI 的政治经济隐忧

Imas：UBI, the — for example, the I I I I worry a lot about like the political economy implications. Like for example, like if people are just kind of dependent on a check, it really matters who's in power. Like right now we're endowed with labor that can turn into — that can turn into income. When that is no longer the case and we are now at the mercy of the of the elected official for like basic needs, right? So that to me feels like a power sharing arrangement that's really dangerous.

主持人：But wouldn't that be true of any sort of government redistribution program?

二、全民基本资本（Universal Basic Capital）作为替代方案

Imas：So something like universal basic capital where you have like an ownership share and you have property rights for capital, then you just — you just you're just you're a normal shareholder. You're just a normal person.

主持人：And but this goes back to the question of indexing — because if indexing is hard, then universal basic capital is hard.

Imas：That's the — that that's the problem with universal basic capital is targeting, right? What do you target to put into people's portfolios?

主持人：Like what if Anthropic goes to zero, but some random robotics company takes all the Anthropic stock?

Imas：Exactly, exactly. So that's the risk of universal basic capital.

三、负所得税与"floor being wet"问题

With a negative income tax, you have the same sort of issues that with UBI. Where where like, you know, somebody comes into power and says like — "this is, we're not going to do that anymore" — and people can't work and then, you know, you you have the issue of the floor being wet.

四、财富税的滑坡困境

主持人：One concern with the wealth tax is that, you know, you — there's no political — politically sustainable equilibrium at like 0.5% wealth tax. And you know, I mean, this happened with the income tax before, right? It starts low, it's like for war or something and then it is slowly and slowly escalates until the marginal tax rate in the US is probably on the order of — income tax rate is like 40% or something. Um and in certain states upwards of 50%.

Um with a capital tax, is there a reason to worry — would would that distort investment because people would just be like, "why would I invest in Anthropic or Intel? The government's is going to take larger and larger shares of it and dilute my share."

五、区分"如何征收"与"如何分配"

Imas：Well, well, hold on. So, um I think it's worth separating like how the revenue is raised, like what's taxed, and then how it's distributed. It could be that the government hands out shares of Anthropic profit to everyone by a broad-based tax [clears throat] and then buying Anthropic profit.

主持人：Yeah, okay.

Imas：Which would probably be the right thing to do. I mean, hopefully some like populist proposal doesn't interfere with that and and like expropriate some like particular company that everyone happens to know about.

六、消费税融资的股票再分配方案

主持人：But how — So you're you're suggesting there could be a tax that is some sort of optimal tax — it's we're taxing externalities or we're taxing land or we're — I guess we'd probably need to tax something other than just those two things, but that tax pays —

Imas：Yeah. Work consumption.

主持人：Okay. So a consumption tax like a European value-added tax type thing that allows the government to go buy a bunch of stocks and then they just distribute those stocks to everybody.

Imas：That's David Autor's.

主持人：Yeah, yeah.

Imas：I mean, that's not going to be that different from just like redistributing the stocks, but it'll be a little different.

七、与社保私有化提案的对照

主持人：Yeah. That's what social security — That was the proposal for social security, but that was privatizing social security, right? So it's like you have — you turn like this sort of weird — like not weird, but it's been working — it's worked so far, but you know, there's questions for how long it's going to keep working. Like basically privatizing social security was giving everybody a basket of stocks.

Imas：Right.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最意外的观察：UBI 不是经济问题，而是**主权赎回**问题
表面看，UBI 的争论围绕"钱够不够、激励对不对"。但 Imas 在开头那句话——"我们现在被赋予了可以转化为收入的劳动"——暴露了一个被几乎所有讨论者忽略的底层结构：**劳动不只是收入来源，它是公民对国家的议价筹码**。
这就是为什么 UBI 和"任何政府再分配项目"在本质上不同：领失业救济的人仍然拥有"重返劳动市场"的退出选项（exit option），而 AGI 时代依赖 UBI 的人**没有退出选项**。这在博弈论里有一个名字——叫做"hostage equilibrium"（人质均衡）。
跨领域连接：这与**核威慑理论中的"second strike capability"**结构同构。冷战时美苏之所以稳定，不是因为彼此信任，而是因为双方都保留了"被毁灭后仍能反击"的能力。一个失去劳动议价权的公民，相当于失去了二次打击能力的国家——表面和平，实则任人宰割。
## 二、隐藏的系统性真相：所有"温和方案"都在同一个滑坡上
注意对话中一个未被点破的模式——每一种替代方案都被对方用**同一种攻击模式**

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1436期】回来了！宏观风险回归？SPACEX估值，参与吗？✨20260607#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：下跌不是风险释放，而是"杠杆情绪"的卸妆

这期内容最耐人寻味的地方，并不在于宏观数据或估值本身，而在于主播无意中揭示了一个"情绪通胀"现象：当一个人觉得"今天账户不涨10%就对不起自己"的时候，市场其实已经进入了一种心理高估值状态——这种估值不会出现在任何PE模型里，但它决定了下跌的剧烈程度。

非农数据本身只是"火柴"，真正的"汽油"是韩国和美国散户加杠杆堆出来的预期厚度。换句话说：这不是基本面的回调，而是预期曲线的瘦身。这给我们一个意外启发——在AI时代，市场的"波动率"本质上正在从"信息驱动"转向"情绪杠杆驱动"，VIX的瞬间40%跳升不再是危机信号，而更像是社交媒体时代的"集体情绪心电图"。

跨领域连接：SpaceX估值困境 = 演员转型导演的身份折价

主播对XAI估值的分析藏着一个绝妙的洞察，但他没有把它点透——XAI把算力租给Anthropic和Google，反而让自己的估值下降了。这不是简单的"商业模式问题"，这是一个身份认同的市值定价问题。

把它翻译到其他领域你会发现惊人的对称： - 一个米其林三星大厨如果开始给别的餐厅供应预制菜，他个人品牌的估值会暴跌——即使营收翻倍 - 一个顶级演员如果开始接广告代言，业内地位会下降——即使现金流改善 - 一个研究型科学家如果转去做工业咨询，学术声誉会折价——即使影响力扩大

XAI正在经历的，是从"造物主"（模型公司高估值倍数）到"水电工"（NeoCloud低估值倍数）的身份滑落。市场对"创造者"和"提供者"给出的乘数是数量级差异的。这就是为什么"算力过剩反而是利空"——它暴露了你不是你声称的那个角色。

隐藏的系统真相：美联储已经被锁在"政治正确的牢笼"里

主播提了一句很关键但容易被忽略的话：美联储"不能因为非农数据强劲就加息，因为这政治不正确"。这句话往深了挖，揭示了一个结构性悖论：

美联储的双重使命（充分就业 + 物价稳定）在AI生产力革命的背景下，已经悄悄变成了单一使命——它只能在"通胀失控"时出手，但不敢在"就业过强"时出手。

这意味着什么？意味着如果AI真的带来生产力跃升（同时压低通胀 + 制造就业波动），美联储的政策工具箱实际上已经被部分焊死。它现在更像一个只能踩刹车不能踩油门、还只能在仪表盘红灯亮起后才能动手的驾驶员。

这与9月份53%加息概率被主播判定"几乎不可能"形成呼应——市场定价的是数据，但美联储行为的真实约束是政治叙事。投资者如果只盯着CPI预测加息路径，就漏掉了一半的方程式：美联储能做什么 ≠ 美联储被允许做什么。

这也解释了一个反常识结论：在AI时代持有美股的真正护城河，可能不是基本面，而是政策制定者越来越窄的腾挪空间。

📝 转录文本

开场：度假归来与9%回撤的心态

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场分析小陶。逃课了一周多了，终于回来了。那去哪儿了？其实我在视频里面和大家讲过，我去度假了。去了哪里？去了希腊，去了雅典，去了圣托里尼，然后去了距离雅典4小时车程的一个迈托尔拉，都非常的漂亮。我在视频的最后和大家分享几张图，非常推荐大家可以去看一下、去玩一下。

那在过去的这一周，我自己的账户也出现了9%的这个下跌。平时在视频里边我和大家分享过，我认为最合理的仓位是我们能够拿得住、睡得着的仓位，也就是说，明天无论发生了什么，我都可以接受的这种仓位。那过去一周9%的这个下跌，我能不能接受？当然可以接受。虽然把我YTD的这个涨幅擦掉了非常多。

所以今天正好借着录这期视频，在整理资料的时候，我也从各个角度对于当下的市场去做了一个审视，去补了一下过去一周所缺失的一些课。那补完之后录这期视频和大家分享一下，我对于当下市场的这个看法。那过去一周出现的这个下跌，接下去我们应该怎么去面对？好吗？那就废话不多说了，直接开始。

在开始之前，老规矩，大家别忘了留言分享加点赞。

一、过去一周资产价格走势回顾

先来快速地回顾一下过去这个资产价格的走势。

上周五因为大非农数据（稍后会非常详细地去讲一下上周五的大非农数据，包括本周的CPI数据，那延展出去就是美联储接下去的一些政策和行为），所以上周五因为大非农数据出来之后，大幅超出市场的这个预期，美元指数出现了0.7%的这个上涨，美债收益率都出现了上涨，TLT出现了下跌。

过去的一周，美元指数1.1%的这个上涨，美债收益率全面上涨，TLT出现了下跌。

那过去一周三大指数都出现了下跌，下跌的幅度不小，特别是纳指下跌了4.7%，标普下跌了2.6%，主要的这个DF全部是来自于周五的这个下跌。那随着美元指数的一个上涨，黄金也出现了下跌3.3%，原油指数出现下跌，VIX上升了40%，当日上升40%。这种上升幅度非常非常的大。如果我们回过头去看历史的话，基本上除了有一些比较大幅度的下跌，VIX上到22、25都是一个买点，因为长期来说，如果我们在一个牛市里的话，VIX通常可以保持在14到16之间。

那么IGV在星期五也出现了4.21%的下跌，上周五下跌的幅度也不小。相对来说，罗素2000下跌的幅度反而会好一些，周五下跌了3.5%，上一周下跌了2.9%。那主要还是因为上周五的一个下跌集中在芯片板块，整个芯片板块下跌了10.4%，那也把科技板块带下来，下跌了6.7%。

其实从市场参与度去看的话，标普500的参与度并没有那么的糟糕，那么上周五标普500还是有很多板块都出现了上涨——必需品消费、公共事业、REITs、医疗板块和保险板块。

那随着科技板块的下跌、保险科技板块的下跌、芯片板块的下跌，前七大科技股下跌3.6%，等权指数只下跌1.4%。我记得在最新一期视频里面，我和大家聊到，我说我的期待是接下去RSP等权指数将会跑赢前七大科技股，录那期视频的时候，前七大科技股的一个涨幅YTD应该是和RSP是相同的。那到截止到上周五的话，这两者之间的一个涨幅差距再次进行拉开，等权指数已经跑赢了前七大科技股将近7%。

二、市场参与度：纳指偏弱、标普中性偏乐观

然后我们来看一下市场的参与度，和我预期有一些不一样的是，上周五指数下跌的幅度非常的大，但是市场的参与度并没有出现非常大幅度的下跌。

  • 罗素2000：20均线还是在51%，50均线55%，处在一个中性的位置。
  • 标普500：20均线59%，50均线53%，处在一个中性相对偏乐观的位置。
  • 纳指：相对是比较的弱，38%和43%，处在一个相对中性偏悲观的位置。

所以在现在当下的这个阶段，我们回到前面的这个话题：接下去是前七大科技股能够跑赢RSP还是RSP能够跑赢前七大科技股？其实从概率这个角度去看，我认为前七大科技股将会跑赢RSP，就是和前段时间又反过来了。前段时间是好不容易那么前七大科技股的一个涨幅追上了RSP，但事实上的参与度告诉我们，现在当下，标普500反而是在一个中性偏乐观的位置，而纳指是在一个中性偏悲观的位置。那接下去如果说纳指出现一个追涨的话，七大科技股可能会再次跑赢SPY。

三、估值分析：标普500与纳指仍然中性偏便宜

我们来看一下估值。标普500的估值，我这边看的估值都是向前看的Forward PE，或者未来一年的一个盈利增速。

标普500：向前看的市盈率Forward PE，现在截止到上周五是20.4倍。那今年4月份、3月底的4月初的时候是在19倍不到一些，上年的2025年的4月份是在18倍。现在标普500是不是在一个非常贵的位置？绝对不是。现在标普500，我认为是在一个中性偏便宜的一个位置。那虽然标普500的这个指数在非常高的位置，但是从估值这个角度去看，我认为标普500现在仍然是便宜的，和上年最高点23倍还有非常大的这个距离。

NDX：其实在上周五下跌之前，NDX可能处在一个中性的位置，在25倍左右。那随着上周五的这个下跌，NDX这个估值来到了23.5倍。23.5倍，我认为是处在一个中性偏悲观或者中性偏低的这个位置。所以虽然纳指只是从最高点可能下跌了5%、6%左右，就历史最高啊下跌的幅度并不大，但是现在这个估值仍然没有非常的贵，或者根本就不在一个贵的位置，在一个相对偏中性便宜的位置。

芯片板块：我这边有红色这条线——红色这条线是上周三6月3日那一天，芯片板块的估值去到了29倍。29倍其实已经非常接近于上年10月份的一个最高点，所以上周三芯片板块已经去到了一个非常高的位置。但是随着上周五的这个下跌，芯片板块的估值在上周五回到了25.1倍。25.1倍，我认为是处在一个相对比较偏中性的位置；如果再严谨一些的话，可能在中性偏贵的一个位置。我这边画了一个上区间和下区间，中性偏上区间可能在25倍左右，中性偏下区间可能是在22.5倍左右。所以现在芯片板块的这个估值处在一个中性偏贵的一个位置，但是绝对没有上周三处在一个非常高的位置。

所以现在芯片板块比起其他两个指数的话相对贵一些，那纳指来说NDX或者说标普500现在处在一个中性偏便宜的这个位置。

四、看市场的层级框架

我自己一般看市场的话，我首先回顾一下那么现在当下的一个市场到底估值处在什么的一个位置：

  1. 估值 — 如果估值没有非常贵的话，那只要关注一下基本面。
  2. 基本面 — 短期之内首先估值有，基本面是最重要的，那伴随着基本面的就是估值。
  3. 宏观 — 看完了估值，看完了基本面，然后我们继续看一下宏观方面。宏观方面只是短期之内可能一些噪音，那除非有一个非常大，比如说美联储宏观上的这个转向，那对于市场的这个影响可能是可持续性的，或者这个持续的时间会相对比较长一些。
  4. 市场参与度 — 然后再往一个更小的一个区间，就看一下市场的一个参与度。
  5. 个股日K线 — 再更小的一个区间就是你拿一个标的出来，拿一个指数出来去看一下日K线，现在是不是在一个超卖的位置。

如果说这些区间或者这几个角度出现一个共振的话，那这将会大大增加我们赚钱的这个概率。

所以最上层的这个逻辑：现在估值，芯片板块可能在一个中性偏贵的一个位置，然后其他两个指数都在一个中性偏便宜的这个位置，那基本面没有非常大的问题的一个情况下，在现在当下市场的这个点位，虽然离最高点并没有下跌非常大的这个幅度，但是我认为从估值这个角度去看，仍然现在持有美股这边就非常的舒适。

其实也就是好比类似于回到一周之前。那么一周之前——呃，过去的这一周——我自己的账户下跌了9%，那我要看的是：OK，基本面有没有改变？并没有改变。那无非对我来说，基本面没有改变，整个估值出现了下跌，那就是我手里所持有的这些个股，其实变得更加的便宜了，其他没有任何的改变。那接下去就需要关注宏观这一块有没有一些风险。

五、宏观风险：上周五大非农数据解读

然后我们就回到宏观这一块。上周五大非农数据，我看了一下大非农数据，非常详细的数据，我看了，我这边不多做展开。看完之后可以做一个总结是：5月份的大非农数据非常的强势。彭博他们这边做了一个总结，他们说的是5月份的就业报告表现极其强劲。我个人同意这个观点，就业的一个总增长远超预期，那主要是集中在休闲和酒店行业，然后医疗保健，这地方的一个政府部门的一个非农增速非常的快。

针对这一份非农报告，市场当然给出了这个反馈了，但我个人首先周五市场的这个下跌——虽然那天我还是在度假的一个过程之中——那一天的一个下跌，我个人认为大非农数据本身它只是一个契机而已，它只是一个引，它只是一个火柴而已。

那更主要的是短期之内，我说短期之内市场的情绪面非常非常的高涨。那么很多人可能加了很多的一些杠杆进去，无论是美国这边的投资者，还是韩国那边的投资者，加了非常多的杠杆在市场里面。所以短期之内那个情绪面非常的高，就感觉好像今天我自己的账户不涨10%、不涨15%就对不起自己一样。那么情绪面非常的高，短期之内情绪面顶得非常的高，所以在这种情况下，一旦出现1%——就指数出现1%、2%的这种下跌——就会引起踩踏。这种踩踏是多头踩踏多头，因为很多人无论是因为止损也好，还是因为恐慌也好，短期之内都选择离场。

站在当下，非农数据并不会成为美联储转向的一个首要的衡量指标。首要的衡量指标仍然是通胀。即使接下去今年美联储有可能会出现加息的话，也不会因为非农数据的强劲使得美联储加息。能够使得美联储加息一定是通胀数据，他们不满意。

六、Nick解读：从降息偏好走向中性、再走向加息？

好吧，所以回到上周五的非农数据这一块。Nick也上周五非农数据出来之后，Nick发表了他的这个观点。

首先我们知道4月份美联储他们那个公告里边保持了一个我们说降息的一个偏好在里边，或降息的偏好，它叫做easing bias。那接下去Nick说到美联储的内部已经不会再去讨论是不是会保持一个中性的——叫做neutral bias，neutral bias——他们这一点已经毫无争论了，就是接下去一定会就这一次这个美联储会，6月份嘛，这一次的美联储会，美联储一定会把这一个降息的偏好变成一个中性的一个偏好。

然后甚至于会讨论在7月份的时候有没有可能会把自己的偏好变成一个tightening bias，变成一个加息偏好。一旦有了加息偏好，下一步就是把2025年的那几次降息——那几次降息它叫做insurance cut，它是用来做保险的降息——什么保险的降息？就是当时的一个降息是为了防止劳动力市场变得更加的糟糕，但是如果劳动力市场没有变得更加的糟糕，是不是应该把当时所用的这种保险式的降息给加回来？

虽然上周五的大非农数据出来之后，美债收益率出现了上涨，那么市场对于降息的这个预期、有加息的这个预期吧，也出现了这个上调，但是美联储的这个货币政策仍然会把这个焦点放在CPI身上。

因为我们想一下，如果单纯的只是因为非农数据一个月增长在18万或者说20万，甚至比20万还要高，就会使得美联储进行加息吗？或者说美联储的这个加息就是为了去抑制劳动力市场的这个增长，然后使得失业率出现上涨吗？这是美联储不会去做的，因为这是一个政治不正确，基本上背后不会有任何的这个支持，联储就地方的联储成员也不会支持这样的一个决定。18万或者20万、甚至25万，它不足以使得美联储去加息。能够使得美联储去加息的话，一定是会因为CPI的数据他们不满意，或者CPI数据出现大幅度的反弹。

七、CPI预期与历史对比

那所以焦点应该还是在CPI数据身上。那本周三我们将会看到上一个月的CPI数据，现在市场的预期环比增速是在0.5%和0.3%，彭博他们的预期是在0.4%和0.2%。那么所以如果上周五是因为宏观数据出现了——美股出现了下跌，那这一周如果说CPI数据好于市场预期的话，起码会给市场稍微缓一缓。

另外这边我们注意一下，核心CPI市场预期是在2.9%，年化同比增速是在2.9%，彭博的预期也是在2.9%。那上一个月5月底所公布的核心的一个PCE数据现在是在3.3%。

所以我们想一下，现在我们很多观众可能担心接下去美联储会加息嘛，当然美联储是有可能接下去会出现加息，市场现在也已经开始进行定价。但是我们想一下，当下和22年去做对比的话，或者和21年底去做对比的话，当时21年的年中CPI的数据就已经来到了5%，而我们现在PCE的数据只有3.3%，核心CPI的数据只有2.9%。离美联储2%的这个目标的确还有1%的这个距离，但即使现在美联储开启、再次开启这个加息的周期，它能够加多少？对吧，最多加个50个基点可能已经非常大了，他们不可能再去加个75、加再去加个100个基点，没有这个必要，因为现在的通胀数据没有那么的糟糕。

八、市场加息预期：为什么9月加息几乎不可能

最后我们来看一下市场对于加息的这个预期。

这边9月16日，也就是3个月之后吧，市场预期有53%的可能性，美联储会进行25个基点的这个加息。但我认为这个可能性是过高，或者几乎不可能美联储会在9月份加息，为什么？是因为这时间不够。

因为首先如果9月份要加息的话，那势必意味着6月份美联储就先要从公告里边走——一定会，当然我认为这个事情会发生，就是先把降息这个偏号变成中性的偏号，对吧？先从cutting bias，然后变成一个neutral bias。然后7月份就像前面那个时候说的，7月份最早在7月份要从一个中性偏好变成一个加息偏好。

那从加息偏好——中性偏好变到加息偏好之后，理论上来说还会有一次美联储会议告诉你"我们下一次要进行加息了"。美联储的加息或者降息它是一个非常缓慢的一个过程，他一定会提前去为你铺这个路。所以我认为这铺路的一个过程，单拿6月份和7月份的这两次会议，时间不够、时间不够美联储会去对9月份会在9月份进行第一次一个加息。

所以最早可能也要等到今年的10月28日那一次会议或者12月9日那一次会议。可能如果一定要加息的话，那在这两个会议里面的话，加息的概率会更高，甚至于可能会拖到12月份。

如果说有必要加息的话，那在这个过程里边他们将会看到很多次大非农数据，包括失业率数据，还会看到最重要的是会看到很多次的CPI数据。在上一次鲍威尔最后的那一次议息——那么最后一次的美联储会议里面——他有聊到，其实美联储他们宁愿去看看什么？看今年的下半年，特别是9月份、8、9、10、11月那4个月的CPI数据。为什么？因为这4个月CPI数据，美联储的一个期待是同比增速将会出现下降，因为关税一次性的这个影响已经在身后了。但是如果说这4个月数据没有出现下降的话，那是有可能在12月份再次开启或者再次打开这个加息。

所以现在市场的这个定价，我认为52%你可以认为合理，也可以认为不合理，但是我认为概率非常非常的低美联储会在9月份开启加息。那12月份今年的12月份，美联储现在市场预计美联储会有120%的可能性会加息一次，也就是说会加息1.2次了，市场预计。那我认为这个加息的概率可能也过高了。If anything的话，今年美联储会在12月份加息一次最多了。

那这一次如果说再次打开加息周期的话，会不会复制2022年美股的这个下跌？我认为整个环境并不一样：首先估值这个环境也不一样，然后其实另外一点最重要的是CPI或者通胀所在的这个位置环境并不一样。

所以宏观这边一步一步去看，暂时来说，我认为并不需要放太多的一个焦点在宏观上面，或者说宏观和当下美股的一个基本面和估值来说，这一个影响还没有非常的大，和2022年去做对比的话还差了非常非常的远。那在AI基本面持续落地的情况下，估值又没有非常贵的这种情况下，甚至于标普500和NDX它是在便宜的、相对便宜的这个位置，那宏观的这个风险又没有严重到像2022年这样，那短期之内的这些下跌我认为仍然是一个买点。

九、本周关注：CPI与Oracle、Adobe财报

本周除了CPI数据之外，还会有两家公司发布财报，分别是周三的Oracle和周四的Adobe，那这两家公司我们也可以关注一下。

十、SpaceX估值拆解：1.4T vs 市场预期1.75T–2T

另外一个非常重磅的IPO，就是SpaceX，6月12日将会进行上市。我在上一期的会员视频里面有非常详细的去拆解了一下SpaceX的估值方法，包括最后的这个结果。那我们来做一个更新。

第一块业务：Space（发射业务） 首先是他们第一块业务Space。第一块业务我是根据Rocket Lab二级市场的这个标的去给了一个估值。当时我假设Rocket Lab的一个估值——因为Rocket Lab的一个市值在100个billion点，因为Rocket Lab的一个营收占了SpaceX的营收差不多6分之1左右，在Space这一块的营收差不多6分之1——当时给的Space这一块的业务是600个billion。那现在Rocket Lab差不多70个billion了，那600个billion打一个7折，Space这一块的业务差不多是420个billion。

第二块业务：Connectivity / Starlink 第二块业务Connectivity就是他们Starlink这一块的业务。Starlink是非常的赚钱，整个25年他们operating income是4.4个billion。我当时给了他200倍的这个PE，其实非常非常的贵，几乎是估值是拉满了，200倍的这个PE，那差不多是800个billion。

第三块业务：XAI 最后一款XAI。那XAI当时被SpaceX所收购的话，收购的一个价格是在250个billion。那为什么我现在只给他200个billion呢？

正是因为XAI他们把部分的算力——差不多现在加一起的话0.5个GW，其中0.3个GW卖给了Anthropic，然后其中0.15个GW卖给了谷歌——那加一起的话差不多0.5个GW卖给了其他公司。那从理论上来说，就从数据上来说，接下去他们的财报会非常的好看，那么因为单单卖给Anthropic这一部分的一个营收，就可能就可以使得XAI这一块的营收翻一倍。

那么但是我们要知道为什么XAI能够给到2500亿美金的这个估值，是因为Grok，是因为他们AI这一块的业务，是因为他们那个大模型——因为他们自己的这个模型才能够给到这么高的一个估值。而如果说XAI它变成一家NeoCloud，它变成一个自己去造数据中心，然后把这个数据中心以出租的这个方式把这个算力去卖给其他公司的话，NeoCloud这个估值和模型公司这一类的估值是没有办法去做对比的。作为一家NeoCloud来说，NeoCloud这个估值就会比大模型的这个估值要低很多。

那所以XAI——现在为什么XAI愿意把自己的算力去出租给这两家公司？因为自己本身并不需要那么多的算力。自己本身在现在当下算力是一个非常紧缺的一个资源的情况下，XAI还能够把自己算力给出租给其他公司，就代表他们自己内部的这个模型根本不需要那么多的算力。而当时SpaceX收购XAI的时候，XAI是作为一家模型公司，它是作为一家AI公司，那么如果说今天你是变成NeoCloud的话，那你就不是一家AI公司。

所以从我个人的一个理解去看的话，正是因为XAI和Anthropic、然后和谷歌签了合约之后，我反而认为它那个估值是下降了，并没有上升，虽然它接下去的一个营收会比以前要好很多。

估值汇总 好吧，所以在XAI这一块，我认为现在的估值是在200个billion，然后2000亿，然后加上前面的420个billion是Space这一块，然后再加上800个billion Starlink这一块，全部加一起的话，420加上800，然后再加上200的话，1.4个trillion。1.4个trillion这个估值我认为已经非常非常的高了。

对吧，因为我这里面给了一个假设——其实最主要的，因为我知道第三方那些机构，包括华尔街的一些机构，包括晨星morningstar，他们给SpaceX这个估值是在0.8个trillion，800个billion吧。那我这边1.4，他们给了800个billion，差距在哪？其实差距我认为主要的差距是来自于对于Starlink这一块的估值。Starlink我给了200倍的PE，你可以给100倍的PE，你如果给100倍的PE的话，那瞬间就少了400个billion，那瞬间就从1.4T变成了1T了。

好么，但无论如何，如果SpaceX以1.75个trillion或者是两个trillion的这个价格上市的话，或者IPO的话，那我认为这个价格是非常的高估。

十一、嘉盛集团 SpaceX 上市前暗盘交易（产品介绍）

在那么重要的时期，forex.com嘉盛集团这家老牌的场外交易平台推出了一款全新的交易产品——SpaceX上市前的暗盘交易，让有兴趣预测SpaceX上市估值的大家可以在其正式上市前对其潜在的估值进行投机交易。

原理 SpaceX上市前暗盘交易的一个原理是支持我们预判该公司上市当日收盘时整体市值并且进行交易，提前布局SpaceX估值的一个总市值，并且根据自己对于市场的这个判断进行多空的这个交易。

那前面我和大家分享了，我认为1.75T到2T的这个估值是过贵了，最多可能在1.4T，而且1.4T里边背后也给了非常乐观的一些预测，但是说实话，上市交易的那一天到底这个市值会落在哪里，我个人并没有非常大的这个把握。但是大家如果感兴趣的话可以进行参与。

风险提示与产品性质 但是第一点，我这边要强调一下，这一个SpaceX上市前的一个暗盘交易，它属于是一个价差合约的一个交易品类，仅仅是给投资者预判SpaceX完成上市后可能达成的市值规模。

结算方式 第二点，这个产品是怎么结算的？SpaceX正式完成IPO之后，所有的持仓头寸将按照其上市首个交易日收盘时候的一个官方市值进行结算。

与实际股票的区别 第三点，这个价差交易是不是等同于买卖SpaceX个股的一个股票？完全不一样。那么参与这一次的交易的话，并非购入SpaceX这个实体股票，投资者也没有办法享有任何股东权益、投票权、股票分红，都没有。价差合约的一个交易仅适用于企业上市前压注其预估的一个市值。

所以这边我要提醒一下大家，价差合约的这个交易风险非常的高——这第一点。第二点，随机性很强，因为你毕竟是预测当天上市之后的一个估值到底停留在哪里。

如何找到该产品 好吧，那么在了解了这些风险的情况下，如果还决定去尝试交易的话，那怎么去找到这个产品？登录嘉盛集团的一个账号之后，直接搜索"SpaceX上市前暗盘交易"，也可以在流行市场专区内找到该品类。如果没有嘉盛集团账号的话，我会把开户的这个链接放在置顶的一个留言里边，那大家如果感兴趣的话，可以去了解一下嘉盛集团。

收尾

那今天这期内容就到这边，我们下期见。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反直觉的观察：下跌不是风险释放，而是"杠杆情绪"的卸妆
这期内容最耐人寻味的地方，并不在于宏观数据或估值本身，而在于主播无意中揭示了一个**"情绪通胀"现象**：当一个人觉得"今天账户不涨10%就对不起自己"的时候，市场其实已经进入了一种**心理高估值状态**——这种估值不会出现在任何PE模型里，但它决定了下跌的剧烈程度。
非农数据本身只是"火柴"，真正的"汽油"是韩国和美国散户加杠杆堆出来的预期厚度。换句话说：**这不是基本面的回调，而是预期曲线的瘦身**。这给我们一个意外启发——在AI时代，市场的"波动率"本质上正在从"信息驱动"转向"情绪杠杆驱动"，VIX的瞬间40%跳升不再是危机信号，而更像是**社交媒体时代的"集体情绪心电图"**。
## 跨领域连接：SpaceX估值困境 = 演员转型导演的身份折价
主播对XAI估值的分析藏着一个绝妙的洞察，但他没有把它点透——**XAI把算力租给Anthropic和Google，反而让自己的估值下降了**。这不是简单的"商业模式问题"，这是一个**身份认同的市值定价**问题。
把它翻译到其他领域你会发现惊人的对称： -

item.title: [Zhang Xiaojun Podcast] 144. 对阳萌的4小时访谈：消费电子死与生、第三类公司、端侧模型、产品方法、游戏模式
item.dc:creator: Zhang Xiaojun Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：阳萌的"非英雄主义"恰恰是他最深的护城河

整场访谈最让人意外的不是Anker要做芯片、做机器人，而是阳萌反复强调的一种"反创业偶像"姿态——他不是乔布斯型的暴君，不是马斯克型的赌徒，甚至明确说自己"不是最酷的人，不是话最狠的人"。他用 1/3/5/7 星把消费者切片，把自己定位成"中庸"的5分玩家。这看似平庸，但暗藏一个被大众严重低估的真相：

在长价值链行业里，"中庸"是稀缺品。

互联网产品的价值链短、节奏快，所以崇拜天才一击的个人英雄。而消费电子有200个角色、6000人协作，复杂度按"平方密度"上升——这种系统里，过于极端的CEO反而是熵增源。阳萌选了一种"无聊但稳定"的人格，让组织能跑长链条。这就像航空业不需要战斗机飞行员当机长——它要的是不出错的稳定，而不是英雄式的拉杆动作。

跨领域连接：这一点惊人地像《长尾理论》里的Costco模型——平庸的SKU选择者反而能跑出极致的会员忠诚度，因为他们的"无聊"被复利成了信任。

二、被掩盖的核心叙事：Anker的"芯片梦"不是技术升维，而是对冯·诺依曼架构的哲学反叛

表面看，Anker做端侧 NPU 芯片是为了让耳机降噪更好。这种叙述太工程师化了，遮蔽了一个更刺激的命题——

阳萌在访谈中其实悄悄做了一次计算机架构的形而上批判：冯·诺依曼架构（存储+计算分离）的本质，是"分治法"时代的产物——把大问题拆小，每次只取一小块知识到CPU。但在端到端大模型时代，每次推理都要把"整个左脑"搬到"右脑"，这在能耗上是荒诞的。

他给出的隐喻极其精妙：人脑从不搬运参数，因为存储即计算。

这意味着 Anker 真正赌的不是"做出一颗芯片"，而是"赌冯·诺依曼范式在端侧 AI 时代会终结"。这是一个被几乎所有消费电子从业者忽略的判断——它把 Anker 从"硬件公司"重新定位成了"后冯·诺依曼时代的端侧计算原住民"。

跨领域连接：这与生物学中的"具身认知"（embodied cognition）理论惊人吻合——大脑不是中央处理器，而是分布式神经网络。Anker的"百万级 muscle neuron + 千亿级 sensor neuron + 万亿级 brain"分层架构，本质上是对生物体的逆向工程，而不是对计算机的延伸。

三、最隐蔽的"系统反身性"：Anker的"游戏难度论"暴露了它真正的危险所在

阳萌反复说：Anker选了"Easy模式"开局，现在要切到"Hard模式"。这听起来励志，但稍加分析就会发现这是一个结构性悖论：

  • Easy模式让Anker积累了渠道、品牌、现金流这三件武器；
  • 但Easy模式也让它没经历过深度技术苦战——这是大疆从Day 1就在淬炼的核心肌群；
  • 而当它现在主动切到Hard模式（芯片、AI、机器人），它面对的对手都是"出生即Hell模式"的玩家。

这构成一个深层的系统性张力：Anker要用"5星品质"的肌肉记忆，去打"7星突破"的战争。它的组织 DNA 是"持续微创新+稳健交付"，但 7 系产品要求的是"基于第一性原理的反共识跃迁"。两种文化的内在冲突，比外部竞争更致命。

最意外的观察：阳萌自己其实非常清楚这一点——这就是为什么他用了一整段去讲"使命愿景价值观"。他不是在做公司宣传，而是在做组织基因的二次改造。他打掉的不是产品线，而是那批"易模式中赢家"的胜利惯性。这是一种罕见的、CEO对自身组织的"反身性手术"。

跨领域连接：这与生态学里的"R选择 vs K选择"非常像——Anker前10年是R选择（快速繁殖、铺品类），现在要切K选择（少量高投入、深耕）。但生物史告诉我们：完成 R→K 转换的物种，存活率极低。 阳萌的真正赌注，不在芯片或机器人，而在能否让Anker完成这次进化论意义上的"选择压力切换"。

一个额外的"啊哈"：他不经意说出的"中国式管理者悖论"

阳萌讲了一个未被展开的洞察——台湾很多公司40岁上市后就停在了40岁。

这不是一句闲笔。它暴露了一个所有华人创业者必须面对的隐秘命题：财富自由的时刻，恰恰是组织失去"再创业冲动"的时刻。阳萌让"创造者拿7、股东拿3"的分配模型，不是慷慨，而是一种精心设计的反熵机制——他在用结构防止 Anker 重蹈"40岁停滞"的命运。

这才是真正的"系统性洞见"：分配比例不是道德问题，是组织生存学问题。

五、15年三阶段：1357人群论与5星品牌

If you divide the development history of your company into some stages in 15 years, how would you divide it?

I think the previous 10 years is a stage. In the past decade, we have been from one category to many other categories, from one channel to many channels, from one country to many other countries. It's actually a very natural process of extension.

1/3/5/7 星人群框架

First, we will divide all the user groups into four groups. We call them the first class, third class, fifth class, and seventh class.

  • 1星：The first class is the type of people who only want to spend the least money and buy basic things that are affordable.
  • 3星：The third class will buy the best cost-effective product.
  • 5星：5-star wants good, stable performance, good quality, willing to pay a certain price.
  • 7星：Finally, 7-star wants the most expensive and best thing.

For example, let's say milk tea. The cheapest milk tea is a cup of milk ice tea for 6 yuan. This is 1-star. The best price is 10-12 yuan. Then there are the stable ones, like the $15-$16 one, like the one from Hushang Auntie. The best quality tea is probably $20.

Let's take another category as an example. For example, cars. The cheapest four-wheeled car is a $40,000 QQ. So the car from the first generation is $40,000. The most expensive and powerful car is basically the Mercedes-Benz, BMW, Audi. The military price is around $400,000. So you find that the first generation is 40,000 yuan, the second generation is 400,000 yuan. In the middle, when it comes to the era of oil cars, you find that Biadi is about 100,000 yuan. Our Dazhong and Fengtian are basically at 150,000 to 160,000 yuan. Many times they use the same engine, the same nuclear plant engine. They sell 100,000 in Biadi and 16,000 or 70,000 in Dazhong. Is it because everyone buys it? Right? Of course. Then how would you view the people who buy Biadi? Right? In other words, how would you view the people who buy Biadi?

So you'll find it very interesting. When you look at the cross-segmental and abstract view, the people are divided into four groups based on their different consumption habits. Right, 1, 3, 5, 7.

So in the previous 10 years of our company, what kind of brand or product do you think we do? I think many of your products are high-end and mid-high-end. I got this impression in the last few years. When did it start?

Actually, in the first 10 years, many people would say Anke is an overseas Xiaomi. Right? If you put it that way, then we are a... Because Xiaomi, as you all know, is known for its cost-effectiveness. So it's actually a three-segment brand. At least before Mr. Lei's high-endization, it was like this. Right? So many people would say when we were an overseas Xiaomi, Anke was actually a three-segment brand.

Because you know when you buy something on Amazon, you will definitely choose the new and good stuff. Right? For a brand, of course, you have a choice. 为什么海外先红？

I have a question here. Because everyone used to say that Anke is a company that is not very well-known in China but is very well-known overseas. Is this your subjective choice? Which year did you make this choice?

This is definitely a subjective choice. Because we started to choose Amazon as a channel. So countries like the United States, Japan, and Europe are actually the best in development.

中国的亚马逊后来应该已经关掉了。所以当我们今天从这个渠道长出来的时候, 其实我们的那些国家就会长得更坏一些。

— Why is it so late? Many people may think that you think China is too twisted. So I don't twist it. I just do overseas. I just do overseas quietly. In fact, China's consumer development did start after 1718. Because it's very simple. If you go to Taobao to buy a power bank, the earliest price may be 30 or 50 yuan. Until 1718, you see everyone's 这对更好的产品的追求才会越来越冒出来。然后这个时候一个相对5系的产品在中国才开始会有市场。

So you actually made a product positioning as a 5G product, but at that time it may not match the Chinese market. The first step it matched was the European, American and Japanese markets. So maybe this is also a subjective choice. In fact, these positioning were all proposed when I was analyzing the summary of the regulation.

选择品类的"血海"逻辑

At that time, you only know you want to do the product well, right? Then you find a good product on the network, which is actually a 5G product. So this is a big part of the channel you grew up with. You first chose Amazon. Why does this look like a blue sea? Is it? In that era, why did you find this business opportunity and didn't find it? That point must be a very blue sea. Then I have to go to the store to buy things all day long. Because I'm a person who is very lazy to go to offline stores. So I basically buy everything online. When I first went to the US to study, I would go to all kinds of places to buy things in 2003. But after a few years, you'll find that you're only going to buy things on Amazon. 创造者三层框架：怎么想、怎么做、怎么花

Looking back, for a company that makes mechanical products, or in other words, for a company that is extremely innovative, Actually, you have to answer a few questions. The first one is how you can do it. How your company or your people can do it to create a creative innovation today.

How you think about things is the first point. You are willing to not think about what others do, not think about what you did in the past. Finally, thinking and doing are probably related to things. You will find that there is actually another layer at the bottom. In the difficult process of doing things, how do you get rid of yourself? How do you deal with yourself? Your emotions, your difficulties. Your fear of the unknown. Yes.

So behind this, we say that this layer, the self-sufficiency layer, requires several qualities. First, we say long-termism. You can see the future, right? Second, you can endure the short-term benefits for long-term benefits. So we add this up and call it long-termism. In addition, we have self-improvement, self-reflection. Self-reflection is the first part of the problem we see. Self-improvement is the problem we solve. It sounds like a big lie, but you will find it's actually very difficult to do. 九、做芯片：从分治法到端到端

You've been doing a lot of things recently, but it feels like you're not done yet. For example, you released a new chip. How did you come up with this decision?

This was done three years ago, right? It's actually back to what you said. The values guide us to make the decision to make a chip. We have a good way of studying in the industry. So in 2021, we established a team to study. We started with In the sound field, we started by using some small models to solve the problem.

Because you know, in the process of sound reduction, there are usually several steps. The first one is called beamforming. It's to calculate where the sound should come from through a few pixels. The second step is to reduce background noise. The third one is to do sound reduction. The fourth step is to enhance life.

Ten years ago, these four steps were probably done through some mathematical models, right? Through some programs. In 2021, when we started doing it, we had turned it into some deep learning models. Some paper problems started to be solved with deep learning models. In 2023, we actually saw the chat GPT. And you especially believe that the future should be an era of "end-to-end solution" problems.

This is also a abstract. In fact, the first 80 years of computers were an era of solving problems using division methods. It was like dividing a big problem into many small ones, then dividing it down again and again. For example, when I was doing search, I could divide a search into many words. In fact, search itself is a word problem in natural language processing. Then the search problem is divided into many words, then divided down again and again. Then a group of people go in to solve each problem today. So you find that in the whole search engine, it may take a team of hundreds or thousands of people to solve these problems.

But when the GPT-1 was released, you could see very clearly that in the future, we shouldn't use the division method to solve the problem. We should actually start from the division method to the end-to-end. The end-to-end method, you find that it is actually the first thing to learn from the data. Second, after learning the basic model, we can enhance it in the real-world process. Data learning and strengthening learning are the ways we can learn in the future.

So this is something that you can see happening very clearly. In the field of bioengineering, we have to throw away the old programming codes and small models and change them into a big model. Of course, the size of this is completely different from the size of today's language model. When this model is running on a portable device like an earphone, you can actually... Our hardware conditions at that time, the best chip could run a model with a few hundred K parameters. There would be a huge signal. It means that this model can't run on an earphone.

Then you look down again, and you find out why. Basically, because of the sound application, it needs to be cut into many time frames per second, and then continuously process the sound of each time frame. Without processing a time frame, you need to encode dozens of tokens, and then complete the model's all parameters. This challenge made it possible to move all the parameters of the model from the memory to the NPU at every second. And this move itself was the biggest advantage.

So our challenge was to make a model that could do a few megabytes, and the performance was much better, a single-core model. But we found that it couldn't run on the earpiece chip. Because this model needs to be moved constantly in the process of hundreds of milliseconds of push. If it's moved too much, the power consumption will be high. So in the end, we can only reduce it to a small model of a few hundred kms, 300kms or 500kms. At this time, its performance will be greatly affected.

So back to values. At this time, you know you want to make a model ……

冯·诺依曼架构与"存算一体"

The general structure of the phantom 1.0 is a word called "divide and store". You put the program and the code into the memory and then you do the calculation in the CPU. You will find that the general structure of the phantom 1.0 and the method of solving the problem is called the division method. There are also software called program and data produced by the division method. These three actually constitute a very stable triangle.

When you go through to solve a problem, you write down the code and program, and then the other data. At any given time, you only need to run a small part of the program. So when you're computing, when you're constructing, you separate the computer and the processor, and then you put the program and code in the memory, and then you just put a small part of it in the CPU every second.

But you realize that in the era of the end-to-end, — At this point, you are still using a phonoid architecture. When you find that you have your parameters stored in your memory, and every time you need to put all the parameters into your CPU to calculate, the natural efficiency will be very low.

For example, it's a bit like you have all your knowledge stored in your left brain. In the past, when we were talking about the division method, every time we solved a problem, there would only be a small part of knowledge that was used. We would just move that part to our right brain to calculate. At that time, it seemed reasonable.

But today, because your knowledge or the way you solve problems is no longer very detailed, but it is a whole, a large model. At this time, when you solve every problem, you actually need to think about the whole person. or a large amount of energy in the left brain, and move it to the right brain to calculate, do you think it becomes unreasonable? It's a bit low-key. Right?

So, look at what the human brain does. The human brain is one. Right. In fact, the human brain is your storage today, all your model parameters, in your nervous system. And the same nervous system also plays the role of calculation. So, in this way, your brain doesn't move any parameters during calculation. In this way, naturally, you get a higher number.

So, if we turn a single-celled phonoid into a single-celled brain, the power consumption will drop significantly. Then we can run a big-spectrum model on a small device like the headset. How big is this model? Today, we only have less than 2 million spectra. But for a model that can run in a headset, it's already huge.

第一个落地：耳机降噪

Why did you put it on your earphones? Is this your first product? It's still coming back to the point of pain for consumers today. Today, let's say you use the best earphones today. For example, when you call a place in a big city, or when you go to the airport, right? Will the people on the other side of the phone hear your background report from the airport? They can still hear it. Or when you're filming a video, there's always someone who comes up and you think their background noise is very noisy, right? And in the end, you can't hear their voice clearly.

So today you'll find that, essentially, in terms of sound, we're talking about separating sound from background noise, today's headphones, right? And then the level they can reach, in fact, is a relatively ideal state, there's still a big gap.

What are the standards of the 7-series products you define in this category? The best 7-series headphones today should be AirPods Pro, right? Some people will say Sony or Bose. If you buy all of their best headphones and test the call quality in a noisy environment, you will find that it is actually very challenging. As I said, the background noise will often not be filtered. If it is filtered, you will find that its life will become very poor and even unnatural. So how can we make life very clear and natural while effectively filtering background noise? you will need a big model to do this.

Going back to our own experience, we realized that the communication was not good enough. To solve the communication problem, we had to use the model that was mentioned earlier, the model with millions of parameters. To run such a model, you have to have a complex structure in your earphones. ……

It was a very natural choice. This didn't happen repeatedly in the company. Because today, when your values and vision changed, you thought this is what you should do. So when our team proposed that we would run a big model and we needed a chip like this, we said we would make this chip. So we actually found a whole market. It's the strongest company in that field. We developed a chip with them.

To be honest, this process is very difficult because there's nothing like this in history. So what is the model like? What are the requirements for this chip? How do you define and optimize the chip? 十一、真正的智能家居：可调节 vs 可自调

It's actually a big part of what you've pre-designed. It's always been that way. So today's so-called smart home is essentially a home that can be adjusted and predicted.

So we say that all products can be divided into three stages. The first stage is called the unadjustable stage. For example, your house is very weak. It actually has nothing to adjust. And then the second stage is that it has more than ten buttons. It suddenly becomes adjustable. But it's not intelligent. The third stage is that he can adjust himself. He can feel what you're doing and adjust it.

Let me give you another example. For example, a chair. An unadjustable chair is what we're doing today. An adjustable one is like a human body. Today, you may have more than ten places to adjust. You find that you're not good at adjusting. Secondly, you don't think about adjusting it. When your back hurts, you might think about it. So this is the stage where we call it "adjustable". ……

So you asked why it seems that in 2026, our homes are not yet real intelligent. The modeling ability is not enough. Not smart enough. There is no modeling on them. Right.

You actually want to achieve real intelligence, it needs three abilities. That is, perception, planning, and control. If you want it to be intelligent, it must have perception ability. It must be able to plan itself, right? And it must be able to control itself.

So today, actually, and there needs to be hardware below. Right. The sensor and the model capabilities should be like the products in our homes.
