Steven: Setbacks in 2022 contributed to our biggest changes. We shrank to fewer categories, then dug deep into deep technology and extreme innovation. In 2023 we established a lab to do deep tech — the storage-compute integrated chip we just released is one outcome.

Another aspect — changes in mission, vision, values. The 1357 concept came out in 2020 when we seriously started thinking about strategy.

Going from 5-series to 7-series is a very difficult process. At the most basic level, 5-series — everyone can do a great job by improving quality, doing micro-innovation. Our values at that time: "be reasonable, pursue excellence, grow together."

Three "transcendences": - Surpass yesterday's self - Surpass the best peers - Surpass the most essential needs of consumers

The first two are on a deterministic road. But "surpass essential needs" requires breaking away from existing tracks, taking a completely different path that no one has walked — some have died on it. You must dare to see this road, and your entire organization must dare to walk it.

For a company that has walked a certain path for 10 years — such a mental change isn't just "the strategy has changed." Your whole organization, the atmosphere, what everyone believes — must shift. How can thousands of people pass through? It's something we've been exploring for the past few years. Painful, but I think we've gained some good results today.

极致创新者的三层结构

Steven: To make 7-series products is to do extreme innovation. Looking at successful companies — Steve Jobs, Musk — and our own: their characteristics boil down to three points:

  1. How you think: Don't follow what others do. Don't start from what you did in the past. Go back to user value. Study first principles (essence of physics). Build the framework layer by layer. You'll find a missing piece — this missing piece is generally what no one has done.

  2. How you do things: Dare to take huge risks. Try your best to get it done.

  3. How you deal with yourself: In this difficult process — your emotions, difficulties, fear of the unknown.

The third layer requires: - Long-termism: see the future situation, bear short-term pain for long-term interests. - Self-awareness and self-evolution: First find the problem within yourself. Change yourself to solve problems. Sounds like nonsense but very hard. Today, when something goes wrong — do I look for the cause within myself first, or outside? - Continuous learning — we put this together as "growing together."

This framework is quite stable to describe creators around the world — the only way to create.

充电产品的 7 系：Prime

Steven: Some categories are relatively easy to make 7-series. Charging, for example. In 2020, the most expensive charger on the market — a power bank — was only $50–60. We had to do it. When we launched 7-series we thought: make it really extreme. In this field we have the strongest R&D. The team makes progress every year. The most extreme product in the industry.

Our Anker charging line is called Prime — this is the 7-series product line. Average price over $100. A stable 7-series position.

But for other categories like headphones, the 7-series is already occupied by Apple AirPods. You have to be as good as them or better — a very long, very difficult process. We spent several years on it. By this year, when this show airs, I believe the product we released should be done.

与爱普生 CEO 的对话再谈

Xiaojun: You raised a few questions — how many categories to enter, why, which not to enter. How do you make these choices today?

Steven: Honestly, most categories today were inherited. Let me tell an interesting true story.

We make UV printers. The nozzle is provided by Epson. In February this year, Epson's CEO came to visit. He introduced their technology first — Epson made Romax technology, derived 1, 2, 3 categories from it. When it came to me, I said: we did charging, audio-video, home automation. He sincerely said: "Yang-san, you're so awesome, you do so many categories."

I said: combining my understanding today, I must be very ashamed. The more ideal way: today you have a good underlying technology, and based on it you expand to 1, 2, or 3 categories.

做芯片的故事：存算一体

Xiaojun: You've done a lot of things for me recently. I feel very uneasy. For example, you released a chip. How was this decision made?

Steven: Made three years ago. The value guided us. We're in the audio-video field — we need good acoustic algorithms. In 2021 we established our acoustic algorithm team.

Noise reduction has several steps: 1. Beamforming — figure out where the sound comes from 2. Reduce background noise 3. Echo cancellation 4. Voice enhancement

Ten years ago these were mathematical models, programs. In 2021 we started converting to deep-learning models on sub-problems.

By 2023, ChatGPT came out. We firmly believed: the future is an era of end-to-end problem solving.

The first 80 years of computers were the divide-and-conquer era — divide a big problem into many small problems, divide down further. Search was natural language processing, divided into sub-problems, divided further. A team of thousands inside.

After ChatGPT, we should no longer use divide-and-conquer. We move from divide-and-conquer to end-to-end. End-to-end essentially is: first learn from data, then reinforce in the real world. Data learning + reinforcement learning constitutes the paradigm of future learning.

Applied to acoustics: throw away all programs and small models of divide-and-conquer; replace with a large end-to-end model. (Large here is on a completely different scale than language models.)

When you run this model in wearables like headphones — our hardware conditions at the time could run a model with hundreds of K parameters. Huge power consumption. The model can't run in the headset.

Why? Sound is divided into many time slices per second. Process each slice — encode into dozens of tokens, pass through all parameters of the model. Every second, every frame — move all model parameters from memory to NPU once. This move accounts for the largest power consumption.

So our challenge: we can make a few-megabyte, significantly-better end-to-end model — but it actually cannot run in the headset chip. Because hundreds of inferences per second × parameter moves × too much movement = power consumption too high. We could only downgrade to 300K or 500K. Performance greatly affected.

冯·诺依曼架构与"脑"的类比

Steven: Back to values: you want to make a bigger model to solve the problem end-to-end. Today's chips can't run it because they have to keep moving data. So the very natural method is — can I stop moving data?

If you go back, the architecture of computers in the first 80 years is the von Neumann architecture — separation of storage and calculation. Put program and code in memory, do calculation in CPU. von Neumann + divide-and-conquer + software files (programs and data) — these three form a stable triangle. At any point in time you only need to run a small part of the program.

But in the end-to-end era, this stable triangle should be broken. The problem-solving method is no longer divide-and-conquer; it's data learning, reinforcement learning, end-to-end. What you produce is not millions of lines of code, but a neural network. Even with MoE (Mixture of Experts), activated parts represent millions or billions of parameters.

With von Neumann architecture, every calculation requires pouring all parameters from memory to CPU. Natural efficiency is very low.

Analogy: you store all knowledge in your left brain. In divide-and-conquer, every time you solve a problem, only a small part of knowledge/code is used — move that part to right brain and calculate. Seemed reasonable.

But today your knowledge is no longer broken into fine pieces; it's a whole — an end-to-end model. When solving each problem, you need to move the entire left brain (or a large part) to the right brain to calculate. Starting to become unreasonable, inefficient.

Look at the human brain: storage is your model parameters in your neurons. The same neurons play the role of calculation. The brain doesn't carry any parameters when calculating — naturally more efficient.

So we separate from von Neumann's separated storage-calculation, convert to a storage-calculation integrated architecture — like a brain. Power consumption significantly reduced. Now we can run a model with large parameters on small devices like headphones.

这颗芯片的具体场景

Xiaojun: How big are the parameters of your model?

Steven: Less than 2 million. But for a model running inside a headset, this is already huge.

Xiaojun: Why headphones first?

Steven: Start from consumer pain points. Use the best headphones today — make a call in a noisy place, at the airport — the other side hears your voice with the airport background. Or in video conferences — someone comes up, background noise is noisy, vocals unclear.

The best 7-series headphones today should be AirPods Pro. Some say Sony or Bose. Test call effects in noisy environments — actually very challenging. Background noise often not filtered; if forcibly filtered, vocals become incomplete, unnatural.

To effectively filter background noise while keeping voice clear and natural — you must need a large model. Million parameters. Must run inside the headset. Must have storage-calculation integrated architecture.

Look around — no one in the world had made such a chip. We searched the market for the strongest company in that field and customized with them.

Project started in August 2023. Today April–May 2026. Three years. The product equipped with this chip is finally on the market.

Effect: in any noisy environment, accurately separate your vocals from ambient noise. Plus soft call — you sit and speak softly; the other party hears clearly. Soft calls + extreme noise reduction — this essentially represents the first application of a new problem-solving paradigm.

未来：从知觉到大脑

Xiaojun: Will this chip be used in other scenarios in the future?

Steven: Call noise reduction is one scenario. When you can run a million-parameter model in a very limited power-consumption environment — many more features.

We've seen small-parameter models like Qwen 7B — highly intelligent. In the future, different scales are needed: - Super brain in cloud: trillions to tens of trillions of parameters, solving the hardest problems - Brain on terminal: billions to tens of billions of parameters, real-time on-site - Perception/control organs: millions to tens of millions of parameters

Like humans: the brain is a deep neural network. Cerebellum is different magnitude. Eyes, ears, muscles — controlled by deep neural networks one by one. Eyes have optic nerves that process visible light; only a small amount sent to brain. Brain doesn't control every cell directly — muscles have nerve cells that control specific movement.

So in the future, different sensing/control organs should match the complexity of their problems with appropriately-scaled neural networks. Eye nerves, ear nerves vs brain nerves — differ by orders of magnitude.

(One school says: don't dismantle these nerves individually; collect all sensor raw data and import to a large model. We don't think it's the best way long-term — frequencies and complexities differ.)

So these small models should run on independent hardware in different places. Including the embodied brain — billions of neural parameters. Should also run on storage-calculation chips.

Currently our chip can only run nervous system. But we believe along this road, it will reach billions or tens of billions of parameters — carrying a brain.

自用 vs 外卖：苹果模式

Xiaojun: The storage-calculation chip — is it for your own use or for everyone?

Steven: For our own use. Completely customized from our model — operators and structures adapted to our model. Our own "little CUDA." If I sold this chip to others, I'd essentially be giving them the model — I'd become a chip company.

Look at Apple's A-series, M-series; Huawei's Kirin series — used only in their own hardware. From a closed-loop perspective, this generates the best user value (best performance) and best business value. Selling the chip alone vs putting it in your own device — the latter generates much greater value.

Inference side is more suitable for storage-calculation. Different model scales. Trillion-level — savings hard to achieve. We start with millions, then scale to billions and tens of billions over the next few years. Inference future is competitively storage-calculation.

Our chip total: 4 trillion parameters. Different types. Maximum single model around 2 trillion.

Long-term vision: a trillion-level super-brain at home, costing a few thousand dollars to deploy. In each embodied robot and complex device — billion-parameter brains. In various devices — large numbers of perception/control neurons.

"智能家居"其实是"可调家居"

Steven: We always talk about "smart home." Are our things smart?

For example, your toilet has more than a dozen buttons. Smart? Not smart. It's a function — actually adjustability. Including today's "smart home systems" — set in advance, always like that. So today's so-called smart home is essentially adjustable, preset furniture.

Products have three stages: 1. Non-adjustable 2. Adjustable (a dozen buttons grew suddenly — but not smart) 3. Self-adjusting — sense what you're doing, adjust by itself

Analogy: a stool. Non-adjustable stool → ergonomic chair with a dozen adjustments (but you can't adjust well, don't think to adjust until your back hurts) → truly smart stool that senses you, retreats when you come, pushes forward, lays back when you play games.

So the home in the future should be: perceive what you do, plan, control itself.

Xiaojun: Why isn't our home really smart in 2026?

Steven: No model on them. To achieve true intelligence requires three abilities: perception, planning, control. Plus hardware below: sensors, controllers. Plus software/models above.

These earliest matured in self-driving cars — perception/planning/control is the typical three-step process of autonomous driving. After maturing inside cars, it's time to overflow — sensors below, model capabilities above — to all products in our homes. After remaking, products become truly smart.

All non-adjustable or human-adjusted consumer products can be remade.

AI 时代的核心技术

Steven: This will make us rethink: what should our underlying core technology be?

The story I told about Epson — that company has a history driven by core technology. We didn't have that. But today, we clearly see such a core technology: perception, planning, control — from chip to model to application stack. When polished and mature, this becomes the company's core technology.

Why don't others have it? Logically everyone can. But from chip to model — many obstacles. First cognitive challenge. Plus very hard work to do it.

第一款 AI 时代的产品

Xiaojun: Is this headset your first product for the AI era?

Steven: You may not know much about our security system. We're the second-largest security system in the world. Looking at the high-end market (>$500 systems), we have over half the market share.

Security comes equipped with a local large model — we built a sub-2B model running on our security base station. It processes all your videos locally. Your videos don't have to leave home. You don't have to look for videos — you can ask via text. "Is my daughter back?" "Where's the cat?" "Where's the dog?" AI really falling on your family.

Our company has nearly 300 AI-related engineers — 260–270. Started recruiting AI in 2017 for security. Need AI for person detection, motion detection.

Xiaojun: Yingqi (Chairman of Step Star) told me the last generation of AI companies all fought security and were defeated by domestic security giants. How did you develop overseas?

Steven: We do consumer-grade security — not industrial-grade like Hikvision/Dahua. In consumer there are giants too — Amazon acquired Ring. They rely on Amazon's cloud, do 3-series at low price — security where all data is uploaded to cloud.

As a Chinese company, we want sensitivity, and to differentiate from Ring. So we make security that doesn't call cloud. Place a base station in user's home: - Long-distance WiFi connection — can install cameras far away, still send data back - Local storage - Local AI analysis

Launched 2018. Successful, at least from market results.

AI Native 是不是第一性？

Xiaojun: Looking at observations from the past, how do you understand AI? What changes will AI bring to the consumer electronics industry?

Steven: Customers don't care about AI. Customers care about better experience.

The clear path: AI empowers your devices. Devices today need adjusting — in the future they really adapt to you. It knows what you're doing, adjusts itself. You use it almost without feeling.

Xiaojun: It becomes a living thing.

Steven: A service entity. But not "alive" — embodied robots and dogs won't have independent will. A self-adjusting chair as a living thing? A bit exaggerated.

We'll add AI to existing hardware. Like 10 years ago — IoT was emerging, everyone said all hardware should be redone. But what happened over 10 years: those with solid scenes — companies that can evolve — beat those who only had IoT tech. The side with scenes wins more.

So AI native is not the first principle. Customers really care: how do I feel more comfortable, efficient, happier?

To change a category needs three things: 1. Leading technology 2. Real scenes — productization capability + understanding 3. Good global business capability

For us, business is strong. In areas needing change, we lack technology. So 2021–2022 — we still lack tech. There's no perfect company. So past few years — AI team grew, chip team built. Today many things need doing that no one has done yet.

My main purpose coming to this show: tell our vision so more people join to make this vision reality.

AI 与具身智能投入

Xiaojun: How much investment are you willing to make in AI chips, embodied intelligence?

Steven: Hard to give a total. From specific problems we need to solve. We most likely won't train trillion-level models. Today's training: from millions to tens of millions parameters; embodied — billion-level. Investment probably hundreds of millions.

AI broadly hard to count. Over 200 engineers × hundreds of thousands per capita = much, much money.

机器人业务

Xiaojun: Tell us about your robot business.

Steven: Robots — three forms: 1. 2D planar: sweepers, lawn mowers (2.5D: pool robots). First stage. 2. 3D mobile/interactive: robot dogs, pets. Second stage. 3. Operating robots: humanoid. Final crown jewel.

Stages 1, 2, 3 — we're doing.

Humanoid: pre-research. The tech stack hasn't converged at all. Brain state, body, waist joints — everyone's solutions completely different. Not yet productizable.

Dogs (Stage 2): each company's forms are close. Hardware tech stack relatively converged. Agent runs on a model — basically consistent approaches. Should be ready for productization. Started a team last year.

What role does a dog play in our life today? Pets — emotional value. But when dogs first entered families they were caretakers — functional value. Today's pet dogs lost functional value largely.

We make security systems in millions of 7-series homes worldwide. Problem: your home is full of cameras and alarms, but if someone breaks in, very little you can do.

When you have a guard dog — security detects intruder → dog drives them away → closed loop of real user value.

User research: many 7-series users are willing to spend a considerable amount for a family guard dog. We're sure it can be sold in large quantities. Creates particularly clear customer value.

浅海战略与超级品类

Xiaojun: Why shift from shallow sea to deep sea — has the attitude changed?

Steven: "Deep" doesn't mean category complexity. Deep represents category scale — over $50B annual sales. Like Texas Hold'em blind size — millions to tens of millions RMB (shallow) vs 500M-1B (deep).

Watch dogs / security — total market most likely under $50B globally. Still shallow sea. Shallow sea strategy continues.

Will you go into deep sea? Two visible future deep seas: 1. Humanoid robots — tech stack hasn't converged. Dog products extend our tech into that field. Hope to reach the convergence point. 2. Smart glasses — the 4th personal interactive terminal. Three waves of giants competing: phone makers, internet giants, model companies. They want to grab the entry point. To enter you must beat all three waves. So we won't do smart glasses today.

Deep sea, if entered, most likely not by current company. Spin off — like Huawei's mobile business spun off from operator business.

Xiaojun: Is this something you must do?

Steven: Not 100% must. But when time is right, when we have the ability, with the right leader — we're willing to challenge.

产品方法论

Xiaojun: Summarize your product methodology in concise language.

Steven: Complex system behind. If one sentence: look at segmented user groups; create unique value.

Going back to Steve Jobs returning to Apple in 1996 — facing dozens of products. He divided users into two groups (professional vs ordinary), two scenes (mobile vs fixed). Four product slots. Each niche — unique value.

For all product people, if only one principle: segmented people in definite scenes, create unique value.

Xiaojun: Have you experienced product failure? Win rate?

Steven: After 2022 contraction, we never reduced categories again. More cautious now. Clearer principles:

Start from both ends: - People: users who already bought your product, basic understanding and trust - Technology: underlying tech stack, common and extreme innovation

Best category to enter: high overlap with existing users + high overlap with tech stack. Different people + different tech = high challenge.

把公司当作产品

Xiaojun: Are you the genius-product-manager type?

Steven: My product sense is good. But consumer electronics is endless — 10 years ago new products, today new, 10 years later new. I'm a long-term person.

What product to make that can be used forever? Today I regard this company as my product. A platform where many young product/tech creators continue to create. The platform itself is a product. Difficult and challenging — far bigger than making a specific product.

Realized in 2020 strategic discussions — how many categories we've done, will do, in how many fields.

Three (now four) fields: - Charging & energy storage - Audio-video - Home automation - Health (incubating)

Each field — 10–20 categories normal. Total maybe 40–60 categories. The third-category company.

长价值链的难度

Steven: Consumer electronics — long value chain. 200 different positions. With limited time, money, opportunities you must polish most to "basically usable." Difficulty rises non-linearly with chain length — every dot communicates with a patch of dots.

Look at hardware startup success rate. Internet era of smart hardware — 10 years ago — many invested companies, very few alive and well today.

In AI era, creators face choices. Option 1: build value chain from scratch. Option 2: join a mature platform like ours with precipitated intelligence. Then solve distribution — distribute most creation fruits to creators themselves.

好产品经理

Xiaojun: What's a good product manager?

Steven: A good PM is first dedicated to delivering customer value — not broad customer value. Must focus on specific group + specific scene + unique value.

Opposite of Internet products. Taobao, Douyin — not selective, all groups. Small/medium hardware — must select people and scenes.

We have lessons from failures. Background, academic credentials, even interview process — not enough valid proof.

Why values? To test creativity. Most effective: 1. First-principle thinking — your past products, what made you different, derivation 2. Strive for perfection — overcome huge difficulties 3. Long-termism + self-awareness + self-evolution + continuous learning ("growing together")

These are the qualities of best creators globally. When you want to do something 0 to 1 — you need these.

创业者为什么来安克？

Xiaojun: If they can start their own business, why come to Anker?

Steven: Consumer electronics — long value chain. Building it means probability of success is challenging.

After AI, will this change? Not easily — there are people in the value chain. So our platform may be a better way. Anker is already a global consumer brand. Global channels help commercialize good products.

上市的回顾

Xiaojun: If you hadn't done financing/capitalization, what would Anker be today?

Steven: Financing and listing give external pressure. But we're not a group driven by external pressure. Dongping and I would still reach today's path.

Xiaojun: Bad effects on retention if not listed?

Steven: Good question. Bonus distribution — we already distribute large part of current profits to employees. But after listing, share realization distributes profits of next 5–10 years. Big attraction.

When we listed, China regulation: unlisted company ≤ 200 shareholders. We had over 170 employee shareholders that year.

Some left after enough money — reconsidered life. But bigger ratio: not about money, but whether this place can keep him growing and happy.

Interesting story: a finance/economics classmate. After IPO felt he had enough. Resigned. Went to Central Academy of Drama to study screenwriting. Worked as screenwriter for years. Then came back. Found screenwriting wasn't fun. Missed us, came back.

上市选择

Xiaojun: Now for AI hardware companies, profitable companies — when should one consider listing?

Steven: Two perspectives.

Employee perspective: almost always want IPO. Without listing, hard to liquidate shares.

Founder perspective: different. Taiwan has many 80-year-old chairmen of listed companies. Went public at 40. Company still here decades later. But didn't grow big. Family won't take over. Colleagues can't pick up. Even though you enjoyed ringing the bell at 40, when you're older you still have to pay for this company.

Listed and locked in for 10/20/30 years — give the company a long-term path.

Cannot the second generation? If company is on stable smooth road, maybe a second generation or professional manager can. But if path of change (category changing, AI wave) — second generation or professional manager really can do this well?

Xiaojun: What are the benefits of not being listed?

Steven: Without listing, more exit options — sell to a larger third-category company. Look at P&G, Texas Instruments — growth via M&A bringing new categories. China's current environment isn't particularly suitable for M&A; everyone still thinks IPO is better.

When we listed, we thought clearly: we can use systematic approach across many categories, become a third-category company. Listing was an intermediate process — relatively certain.

Xiaojun: Why isn't DJI listed?

Steven: Ask Wang Tao himself.

For employees in unlisted company — we issued stock options to first wave; they got rich and free. For the next wave, no individual liquidation. So we drew an equity pool for new categories. When new category passes investment period and generates profits, per listed-company rules we buy back employee shares — give good returns.

The best creators want opportunity first — challenge something difficult and never done. Then growth — when company supports them, helps them grow. Then rewards. And finally meaning — you also want a place to brag at dinners, talk to children, feel parents are proud.

A good company provides creators: opportunity, growth, reward, meaning. The best company sustains this 2, 3, 4, 5 times — turning him into a powerful person. If he starts a business again, higher success rate.

护城河三层

Xiaojun: What's the moat of a consumer electronics company today?

Steven: Three layers:

Layer 1 — unique leading technology. Only you can do it at this stage. Effective moat. But technology hits diminishing marginal benefits — perception is analog with an upper limit. When tech reaches perception limit, no room to advance; others catch up.

Examples: - Apple's screen — more than 10 years ago, everyone thought iPhone's screen was better. Today? Apple itself coined "Retina" — beyond a resolution, normal eyes can't distinguish. - Chargers — make 140W small as a card. Twice smaller still makes sense? Smaller still — doesn't make much sense.

Technology surplus gets wiped out over long enough time. First principle.

Layer 2 — brand. Trust in users' hearts. The emotion when users think of you. Very hard to build, easy to lose. Consumers may need many positive experiences to trust your brand — but you may do wrong once or twice and lose trust forever.

Layer 3 — mission, vision, values, and a group of people who truly believe them. Look at Nokia, Sony, Philips at certain points — brands still trusted, but probability of choosing them isn't high. Final question: does this company still have a group of people maintaining extreme innovation, confident in first principles, seeking perfection and growing together? If this group stays for 10, 20, 30 years — that's the longest moat.

Anker 三层板分别在哪？

Steven: Test by our own example. Each layer is from 0 to 1, gradually approaching.

Tech (Layer 1): Since 2023 with the lab. Storage-calculation chip is one result. Maybe 0.2–0.3. Huge space remaining.

Brand (Layer 2): 15 years of hard work. Anker's global brand trust is quite high.

Example: February this year released household energy-storage products. Customer price $10,000. No offline stores. Just released — buy on official website. Imagine — $10K spend online, sight unseen. First month: 1,000+ customers placed orders online. Trusted brand.

Brand is our longest board — maybe 0.6–0.7.

Mission/values (Layer 3): When we started transforming in 2020, proportion and concentration of "right people" was quite low. Past few years, painful change. Many old colleagues left after IPO; we brought in many creators. Quantitative to qualitative change. Past two years faster. Because we speak out — what kind of company Anker wants to be, what environment we provide creators, what rewards.

But just talking about vision sounds empty. Eventually products speak. Examples from last year: - UV printer — first consumer-grade in this category. Industrial-equipment-sized device shrunk to suitcase size on a table. - Storage-calculation chip + headset using it.

These are real creator outputs.

I hope when I'm on this show again in 2 years — let our products speak; let our creators in each field stand on stage proudly telling their own creation stories. That opportunity should be theirs, not mine.

文化：层层授权

Xiaojun: Is your company bottom-up or top-down culture?

Steven: Hierarchical authorization culture. Each layer makes its own decisions, delegating maximally.

CEO decides: what the company looks like, strategic/competency/organizational/talent frameworks, return design, which big macro categories. Business department decides: specifically how to do in that category. Product line decides: specific product details.

Many opportunities to be independent across three tiers. Best growth for creators is being independent.

中国土壤里长出的全球品牌

Xiaojun: As a Shenzhen-grown company, why did you build a good brand especially in European and American markets?

Steven: Brand legacy is trust with customers. Every decision should accumulate user trust. The first principle.

Tested by big decisions. Last year we recalled millions of power banks globally. Economic loss hundreds of millions. You could choose not to recall. Result: many users face overheating, even burning risk. We started from user value — just do it. After repeated discussion and debate.

When this happens at every level, output is a trustworthy brand. Whenever we find a problem violating principles, we correct it. Probabilistically — when we work hard most of the time to earn trust of most users — eventually we created a trustworthy brand.

AI 中台

Xiaojun: You established an AI middle platform. Do you spend a lot of time on AI? How do you learn?

Steven: My background is in AI. After this big wave, I firmly believe in its future. Past six months — much time spent here.

Started September last year. Internal tokens (not consumer service tokens) — by airing should exceed 10 trillion. About 150 billion daily internally. Rising rapidly. 6,000 people → ~25M tokens/person/day → ~500–600M tokens/month.

Top models: ~1/3. Mid models: ~40–50%. Entry models: ~20%.

Used not just for code — every functional line in the company uses AI today.

Not opening various accounts on various platforms. Built a middle platform — connected various models, frontend provides chat, local command line, web page, various access. Everyone in company uses AI on same platform.

OpenClaw / real-time orchestration: tells him a requirement, compiles a process. Challenge: temporary, unstable, doesn't fully understand enterprise data.

So enterprise-level applications should be largely pre-programmed agents — integrating business process and data into the intelligent agent, deterministically generating expected value. Huge difference from personal AI use today.

Xiaojun: Was there management objection to all-employees unlimited tokens?

Steven: Many asked how we measure output. "Because you believe, you see." Up to now, not strictly measured what tokens produced. We believe future organizations must be AI-enhanced organizations. Start early.

This year's spending: several hundred million.

Xiaojun: What's the upper limit?

Steven: Matched to value. If we create good customer value and earn well, no upper limit.

AI Native 组织

Xiaojun: What's your view on AI native organizations?

Steven: What's an AI native organization? Like AI native hardware — there must be unchanging things. Think clearly about those, then how AI changes them.

For organizations, what's unchanged: 1. User value — make users happy and satisfied 2. Value chain — to create user value, you go through these points one by one. Hardware: user research → product definition → tech R&D → quality → manufacturing → marketing → sales → service. Even with AI, these value points still exist. 3. Scrums — small collaborative teams delivering each point.

So: - Scrums won't disappear. They will shrink. Software: traditional Scrum 8–10 roles → AI integrates roles → 1 person plays several → smaller team. Hardware: harder for AI to suddenly let an electrical engineer draw structure diagrams or do embedded code — physical world experience required. Hardware Scrums change less or maintain size. - Internal Scrum operation changes — AI defines goals, dismantles projects/OKRs, helps manage and complete. Higher efficiency. - Cross-Scrum collaboration becomes more efficient.

Xiaojun: You're transforming now. How's progress?

Steven: After half a year, clear view of the path. Hopefully complete transformation by year-end. Great if achieved.

中层管理与初级岗

Xiaojun: Some think middle managers become more important; some think they'll disappear.

Steven: Jack Dorsey's famous article: company controlled by world model, eliminate middle managers. Honestly — today's model context length is unlikely capable.

Context length analysis: a project needs N tokens. A Scrum running several projects — more. A department with several Scrums — more. Whole company — over 1 billion, tens of billions, possibly trillions. Today's model: context over 1M tokens — capabilities lost. You can say "compress," but what's left? This is a value choice — models can't do it well yet.

Conway's law: organizational shape determines output. Who defines organizational form? Not CEO alone — managers cultivate organization layer by layer.

So middle managers won't disappear due to AI — may become more powerful.

What disappears: hope the last to disappear are repeated boring jobs.

Junior positions: Many think AI eliminates juniors → no seniors in future. False assumption — that seniors must grow from juniors.

Counter-example: construction. Junior — building walls, plastering, operational. Senior — architectural design. Ancient times: architects did rise from frontline craftsmen (10 years building walls). Modern: architectural design is its own category — don't need to build walls first.

Future: in a field, you can use AI to deliver complete solutions for customers — skip traditional juniors. School admissions today: students with zero programming experience use AI to support business and deliver complete systems.

So I'm not worried — no senior talent in future.

N 倍人 / 大新人 vs 小新人

Xiaojun: Will organizations shrink? Will fewer people earn 10× more?

Steven: We define N-time efficiency worker (NEW) — N-times effectiveness individuals.

Two types: - Big NEW — those building AI agents. Understanding business — data, process, methods — precipitating into intelligent bodies. They make the axes. - Small NEW — those efficiently using AI agents.

Future organization: small number of big NEW + many small NEW. Value created N× — value distribution should match.

Consumer electronics: value created by people making products. Companies that succeed continuously — value distribution respects value creation. More value to employees — to creators.

We say in company: creators get 7, shareholders get 3.

Why not 8:2 or 9:1?

We define total residual value: income minus expenses = what's left to divide between employees and shareholders. First-class companies usually >30% total residual.

Of that 30%, how much to employees, how much to shareholders? Final net profit only 3–5 points — not a good company. Shareholder value almost 10 points — basic threshold. So 7:3 emerges.

收入数字

Xiaojun: How much cash return can creators expect?

Steven: We do calculate. Average. Recent years — as 7-series ratio rises, customer value rises, total value rises. Gross margin: 1.1–1.5 points/year. Shareholder share unchanged. Extra goes to employees.

Employees joining since Jan 1 2023: 2024 income vs 2023 — average up 20%. 2025 vs 2024 — also 20%.

Million-yuan-annual income count: 2024 had ~500 people of 5,000. 2025 with 6,000 people — grew to 800. Hope to exceed 1,000 this year. Ideal: best/most innovative companies in China — per capita over million.

AI 转型的痛点

Xiaojun: How was the AI transformation? Painful?

Steven: Three parts: 1. All staff use AI via our middle platform, building agents on existing capabilities — huge work. Internet companies' data is naturally digital; hardware companies' data mostly isn't. Sweep data into system gradually. 2. Build orchestrated agents based on data. Cross-domain agents must connect. Product agent and R&D agent must speak the same language. Establish per-domain first, then connect — huge workload. 3. Organization changes. Break Scrums, recompose to fully use AI.

People change is hardest. People must believe AI won't replace them — AI makes them stronger.

Three returns: 1. People embrace AI 2. Abilities precipitate into agents; agents connect 3. Organization changes

Started September last year. Inspired by Speckit — process intelligence defining what PMs do, how to develop, test. Adds intelligence/limitations on model — runs smoothly.

So in other fields, when you build that intelligence — your organization becomes AI-based.

We believe abstraction. See something beautiful; abstract underlying principles; diverge concretely into other fields.

焦虑吗？

Xiaojun: Are you anxious in the AI era? Many around me say they're very anxious.

Steven: A little. Changing dramatically, faster and faster. But our habit: ask what's constant. Customer value unchanged. Value chain unchanged. These need creators. If you do these unchanged things better — facing any change, you should be the better one.

Rational way to overcome emotional. Doesn't mean no emotion inside — rationality and sensibility coexist.

AI Coding

Xiaojun: AI coding very popular. Impact on companies like yours?

Steven: Big software OK team — PM, engineer, algorithm engineer, embedded, test — 1,000–1,500 people combined.

Helping everyone catch up with AI is the company's responsibility. Don't expect colleagues to learn on their own. Empower: 1. Build our own software intelligent agents (Speckit-like adapted to our product areas and data structure) 2. Help colleagues use these agents

People who've worked 5–10 years — internal motivation to change is one thing; organization creating atmosphere/environment is another. We empower colleagues to catch the AI bus, not be left behind.

Xiaojun: Worried about being left behind?

Steven: Not worried. We understand and embrace AI; today our colleagues can be empowered. Among consumer electronics companies, I think we're at the forefront. When a bear comes running — you don't need to be fastest; faster than peers.

不看竞争看用户

Xiaojun: Are competitors' AI actions concerning?

Steven: Not particularly trying to understand what others are doing. Originally I'm an I person.

There's a book Infinite Games. Prologue: 2004 Microsoft vs Apple on MP3 players. Apple's iPod successful; Microsoft wants same. Author talked to both: - Microsoft exec: "Look how beautiful our player is, functions better than Apple." - Apple exec: "Our users still have some dissatisfaction; we wish to solve user dissatisfaction."

Hardware industry — you can see who laughed last. Common basic truth.

First principle test: staring at competition, you're always the last one — you escape as a faster horse, not a real car. Don't stare at competition all day. Think about users' most extreme needs. Look at frontier tech — where can it create? Take care of unmet needs, develop ultimate tech, create products to satisfy. Faster and steadier you go, most likely won't fail.

Took over 10 years to really understand this.

When young you compare with people around. At my age, thought through clearly — clarify first principles, then done.

第一性的演化

Xiaojun: When did you realize first principles?

Steven: In 2023 when we changed mission and vision. It's not how flashy — we always say we step firmly and strive for perfection in execution. Striving for ultimate means you don't have ultimate yet.

15 years ago doing e-commerce — first principle hasn't changed: create customer value. But the way changed. Then: in complex categories make improved products, polish through customer feedback. Today: extreme innovative customer value in many complex categories. Completely different first nature.

不是最炫的人

Xiaojun: When you were a child, was there anyone who aspired to be fancy?

Steven: I never seem to be the coolest. Definitely not the one saying cruelest things, wearing brightest clothes, looking most fancy.

Dongping and I — every day work solidly, do better than yesterday. Stand firm on one step, work hard to reach next. Engineer-style — solve one problem, then more complex, then most complex.

Xiaojun: Are you a gambler? Extreme left or right?

Steven: Define left and right. If 10M to invest, scale 1–5: - 5 (extreme left): leverage, borrow money - 4: full investment - 3: 6–7M - 2: 3–4M - 1: 0

I'm always middle. Won't go all in, won't not invest. Will invest more than half. Long enough rounds, with rising knowledge and team — probability of success rises. Middle leads to success — especially a successful third-category company.

大疆比较

Xiaojun: What kind of company is DJI?

Steven: Tens of categories. Third-category company. Fundamentally different from us:

  • DJI chose hell mode Day 1. Hard category. First few years painful. Underlying tech accumulated + high-value-aligned people → expand to other categories easier.
  • We chose easy mode. Easy in front, harder to step up. Past few years making up lessons — playing the difficult game.

Xiaojun: Insta360?

Steven: I think JK is more like DJI than us.

Xiaojun: Two types of companies in Shenzhen — first hard or first easy?

Steven: Basically a 0/1 choice (with degree — 0.2/0.3 vs 0.8/0.9). But standard between difficult and easy must exist.

Xiaojun: If you and DJI on same battlefield — who wins?

Steven: Three barriers: 1. Advanced tech 2. User trust in category 3. First-class enough, striving for perfection

We're growing in all three. Rapidly.

个人变化

Xiaojun: Personal changes in past 5 years switching from easy to difficult mode?

Steven: Easy mode — do one or two things well, live good life. Difficult mode — almost can't make mistakes in any field. Talking about a company today requires complete understanding + actual practice.

Past few years — stumbling. Self-awareness, self-evolution. Realize truths. Start meditating. Painful — meditation helps.

Most painful: when we want to do a platform empowering creators across categories — but can't defeat outside unicorns. Very painful. Then realize: not unsolvable — the company is missing a key link. After adding key links, past 2–3 years getting better.

Solution at that time: cultural values. With clear strategy + organizational capability accumulation + people allocation (willingness to distribute money) — logically should work. Then why not?

Tools and methods are for people to use. What kind of person are you today, what behaviors. When this link added — turning defeat into victory.

使命愿景与前沿实验室

Xiaojun: Relationship between mission/vision/values and your cutting-edge lab?

Steven: 2023 two things: dig deep root tech + change MVV to attract creators.

A creative person — has first thoughts, others think "this person is so weird, suggests unusual directions, very demanding, KPI-curl, long-termist." In many companies feels out of place, experience not good.

In our environment advocating these things, with more such people — work makes him happier. When more like this — changes those not like this. People change by environment.

After company atmosphere changed — when good creators come in, atmosphere changes, others change too.

Xiaojun: Any details reflecting change?

Steven: I'm abstract. Don't walk around company every day looking at details.

Xiaojun: How many hours daily there?

Steven: Within 8 hours. Conscious of controlling time in company. As a third-category company — if I push everything, won't grow into third category. Third category relies on system and creators running, not me pushing.

一天

Xiaojun: A typical Yang Meng day?

Steven: Get up around 8 AM. Company between 9–10 AM. Half to two-thirds of day in meetings. After 6 PM leave. At night I read. Look here and there. Many fields. Unpopular things — politics, history, military, food.

Read many specifics, abstract further — closely related laws behind.

Xiaojun: Recent realization?

Steven: Productivity revolutions — every revolution creates higher value. Who's it allocated to first? Military and political — the watermelon cutter takes first. Bourgeois revolution etc — same. Later revolutions distribute more evenly.

AI revolution: huge value created. Watermelon cutter is model makers. Plus enterprise AI users. Sam Altman talks universal income society — government taxes value into hands, distributes to everyone. Complete closed loop.

In our company: don't default that AI-improved efficiency profits go to me. We say creators 7, shareholders 3 — proportions agreed before. Short-term seems can share with yourself; long-term — value must circulate. Distribute value to those who create.

书与电影

Xiaojun: Recent interesting book or movie?

Steven: Don't read much now. Books' information density isn't high enough — hard to find dense ones.

Movies — barely remember what I watched. Re-watch every 6 months — like new. When attention focused on present/future, selectively forgets.

Xiaojun: What memory are you sensitive to?

Steven: Abstract structural memory continuous and firm. Specific photographic — not.

Xiaojun: When did this come into being?

Steven: College entrance exam — you'd better learn abstraction. Knowledge points and questions highly repetitive. Abstract into a few types — like undressing a cow, complete quickly. Approach to abstraction came from college entrance exam.

Continued habit — step back from specifics, find essential laws.

If a person or org has only abstract — not good. With abstract people + concrete people — like Dongping (opposite MBTI) + me — 1+1 better and better.

Joke in company: without me — still better, more challenging, more fun. Without Dongping — would die.

第三类公司会不会死

Xiaojun: Why would Anker die?

Steven: Successful third-category companies don't die — when winning many categories with continuously good people. Death: people forget previous mission/vision; no longer adhere to values. Then company gradually dies.

Xiaojun: Any signs?

Steven: Haven't become a successful third-category company yet, let alone die.

Xiaojun: If not catching AI paradigm — will Anker die?

Steven: AI to Anker means: user value, business value, employee value. - AI makes surrounding products truly intelligent. Can't provide such products → company dies. - AI makes organization efficiency rise. Lag → organization dies. - People growth speed and ability lag → people die.

Today must embrace AI.

安克的"宝宝/宠物/猪"模型

Xiaojun: Anker as a company — how many years to survive?

Steven: Founder analogy: - Extreme left: raising a baby. Dies → painful. - Extreme right: raising a pig. Work — eat meat at New Year. - Middle: raising a horse / pet. Partner. Won't sell normally — but if offered a price you can't refuse, might.

Dongping and I — really raising a baby. Hope this company outlives us. Why work hard to transform — hope outlives us.

工程师为什么选安克而非大厂

Xiaojun: Creators — why not go to big factories or big-model companies?

Steven: Not competing with big-model companies for people. Honestly — in big-model companies, those making models earn high incomes but their job-crisis sense is strong. Many think: high salary for 1–2 years; after AI evolves, no job either.

Our characteristic: contact with physical world → slow iteration → long, long cycle for human growth.

School admissions: people choose big internet at 27–28 — high income, stand alone. But you find big internet at 35: face issues — 20-something next wave grows up.

Underlying principle: person matures with battles fought. First few battles green. By 5–10 — experienced. By 15–20 — mature. Maturity highly correlated with battle count.

Internet: every battle fast — product released, improvement in months. A feature even shorter. Entering big internet — first 4–5 years fight 10+ times. Mature. Value rises. By 50–100 battles, doesn't change much. People behind come up → 35 crisis.

Hardware: define a product → make → 9 months to 1 year normal. Complex products 1.5–3 years. One war = 1–2 years. AI speeds up but not to 3 months. Long period → continue growing → value rises → most likely no worry about job.

Lower-limit thinking. Honestly — those breaking upper limit are very, very small proportion. Most people must consider their own lower limit. AI in big models can absorb limited talent. Many creative people in the world — they deserve opportunities to create.

消费电子是怎样的战役

Xiaojun: What kind of battle are we fighting?

Steven: Don't think with war mentality. Most essential — create value for customers.

I think electronic products are the platform creating the most incremental value in visible future (besides models, virtual things).

Xiaojun: Hardware as extension of large models?

Steven: Models = brain of perception/planning/control. Hardware = hands, eyes, actions, body. Can't just have brain — must have body. Body will evolve greatly with brain. Model companies shouldn't be able to cover hardware. Models hierarchical naturally — trillion models, billions, tens of billions, millions. Distributed in hardware layer by layer, collaborating.

Xiaojun: OpenAI and Doubao made phones. Entering hardware?

Steven: What OpenAI's phone leaks reveal — not just occupying entry. Through entry, record/collect users' data. Phones carry most personal-related data. OpenAI wants your data, memories, so model can provide best service. Phone makers — most likely unwilling to open data.

Xiaojun: Shenzhen hardware companies training particularly large models?

Steven: Phone makers make their own large models. Embodied companies need own models — usually billion-level (3B, 7B), more like cerebellum models.

推荐一本书

Xiaojun: Recommend a book?

Steven: The Leadership Pipeline by Ram Charan. Head of GE leadership development. Decades of experience.

Divides company into 6 different leadership ladders. What people on each should and shouldn't do. Huge guide for life.

道：语言即世界

Xiaojun: Our studio is called "Language is the World Studio." Hearing this name — what are you thinking?

Steven: Language is very interesting. Language is consistent with Tao. Translation: TAO. - T = task (the task you have to complete today) - A = action (each word you say — actions one by one) - O = observations (this action changes the world — your observations)

Every single word you speak has no meaning by itself — or may have many meanings. Precise meaning comes from being added behind the bunch of words spoken before. Creates new meaning.

Text and language naturally let you observe under a task — how each token affects state, and what next token is needed. From mission to state-deduce-next-action, action-deduce-next-state.

Robotics — two models: - VLA: from previous state to next action - World model: from action to next state

Two models because no language-like data exists letting you observe O→A and A→O simultaneously. Language is under task — A and O are an effective whole. Language is consistent with Tao.

Future robots: with data + clear definition of A↔O bidirectional cycle → train one Tao-consistent model. Foundation for future embodied robots.

星际穿越

Xiaojun: Why do you like Interstellar?

Steven: Human nature glorious facing huge challenges. Negative human nature, but also very positive. Beautiful display of glorious humanity.

Xiaojun: In today's AI revolution moment — still feel like the film?

Steven: Still believe in human nature. No matter how big changes — good side of human nature + those creators — bring us safely to next era.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、"易模式陷阱"——杨萌真正的对手不是 DJI，而是 15 年前那个"聪明"的自己
杨萌坦言他选了 easy mode，DJI 选了 hell mode。表面看这是路径选择题，但有一个反讽极少被人注意到：**前 11 年的顺风顺水，恰恰是 2022 年痛苦的根源。** 一家公司的"舒适债"和金融债一样会累积复利。当 DJI 在最初几年用最痛苦的方式补齐底层技术债时，Anker 则用最舒适的方式累积了 15 年"我不需要核心技术"的肌肉记忆。
所以 2022 年那场危机的本质不是产品线扩张失败，而是 easy mode 惯性对企业精神肌肉的慢性侵蚀。杨萌真正在搏斗的对手，是那个 2011 年做出"先卖货、后做产品"决策的自己。
## 二、妈妈要回 300 万但不要股份——一次教科书级的"反向尽调"
这是整段访谈里最被低估的细节。妈妈作为天使投资人投了 300 万，最后只拿回本金、放弃所有股权。常规叙事会把它解读成"母爱"，但更精确的描述是：**这是一次反向尽职调查**。她在测试一件事——你这事到底是做给我看的（亲情债），还是真的属于你？她抽走"亲情锚点"，让杨萌必须独自面

item.title: [All-In Podcast] Why Secondary Markets Are Eating the IPO | All-In Liquidity Secondary Markets Panel
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的真相：二级市场不是 IPO 的替代品，而是 IPO 的"反向腐蚀剂"

所有人都在讨论"为什么二级市场吃掉了 IPO"，但真正反直觉的观察是：二级市场的繁荣正在让那些选择留在私募市场的公司变得更脆弱，而非更强大。

Gavin 引用扎克伯格的话点破了一个鲜少被讨论的悖论——私募公司的 CEO 是"最娇嫩的花"，所有董事会成员都把他当作家人对待。这种"被宠爱"的代价是什么？是信息腐败。私募投资人不敢提尖锐问题，因为问了就拿不到下一轮的份额。HTML5 vs Apps 的故事不是技术判断错误，而是缺乏对抗性反馈的系统性结果。

这里藏着一个让人意外的连接：二级市场越发达，留在私募市场的成本反而越高。因为当员工和早期投资人可以通过二级市场套现时，那个原本驱动 CEO 接受公开市场审视的"流动性压力"消失了。CEO 可以同时享受私募市场的"宠爱"和公开市场的"流动性"——这是一种道德风险结构。SpaceX 私募 24 年的"成就"，可能正是这种结构的产物，而非证明。

二、跨领域连接：私募二级市场正在重演中世纪威尼斯的"公债"逻辑

这一点没人在台上说出来，但数据已经摆在那里——secondary 从折价 80 美分到溢价 106 美分，这不是市场效率的提升，而是稀缺性的金融化。

让我做一个跨领域跳跃：这与 13 世纪威尼斯的 Monte Vecchio（强制公债二级市场）惊人相似。当时威尼斯贵族被强制持有政府债券，但创造了一个流动性极强的二级市场，使得"持有政府债权"这件事本身变成了一种身份资产。身份+流动性+稀缺性的组合，使价格脱离基本面。

今天的 SpaceX、Anthropic、OpenAI 股份正在变成同一类东西——它们不是单纯的金融资产，而是"我能拿到"这件事本身的社会信号。Schwab 把 4600 万散户接入这个市场，并不是在做"民主化"，而是在把稀缺性的社会消费扩大化。当 Brad 在 CNBC 警告散户"不要 yolo"时，他在对抗的不是金融判断错误，而是一种古老的社会资本逻辑。

更让人不安的连接：14 个 SpaceX IPO 日杠杆 ETF这个细节——这与 1929 年投资信托嵌套杠杆的结构几乎同构。每次"民主化"叙事被工具化包装时，杠杆就开始累积。

三、惊人观察：风险投资行业正在静悄悄地分裂成两个物种

表面上 Brad 在讲 DPI、讲卖出策略，但底层正在发生的是风险投资作为一个行业类别正在物种分化。

注意 Gavin 那句被快速带过的话："没有暴露在万亿美元公司里的 VC 开始做'不自然的事'……写一堆 Neolab 期权式投注。"这不是行业评论，这是生态位坍缩的信号。

我的预测：未来 5 年风险投资会分裂成两个完全不同的物种—— - "指数物种"：持有 3-5 家万亿级私募怪兽的少数 GP，他们的工作本质上变成了"二级市场做市商+私募指数管理者"，与 Gavin 描述的公募基金趋同 - "期权物种"：没有这些怪兽暴露的 VC，被迫转向越来越离散的赌注（核聚变、neolab、机器人狗），他们的工作本质上变成了"科技品类的看涨期权制造商"

这两个物种的 LP 体验、决策逻辑、招聘画像、监管暴露都将完全不同。今天我们用"VC"这一个词描述的，已经不再是同一个行业。

最反直觉的推论：Jason 谈 zipline 时无意中泄露了第三个物种的雏形——"barbell 重构"——这意味着传统中期成长投资正在被两端挤压消失。早期种子 + 晚期 secondary，中间的 Series B-D 正在成为风险投资中的"中产阶级"，正在被结构性消灭。这与社会层面的中产阶级被挤压形成了一个让人发寒的同构现象——资本市场和社会结构的"哑铃化"是同一个深层动力学的两个表现。

📝 转录文本

开场：二级市场为何"吃掉" IPO

Everybody wants access to these private markets. Joining us right now to discuss all of this is Kelly Rodriguez. He's a Forge CEO.

We see a world where the private market opens up and is accessible to any US and global investor. There's 19 companies in the private market AI basket. These companies have grown on average 300%.

Please join us in welcoming Gavin Baker, managing partner and CIO of Atrades. The ROI on AI has empirically, factually, unambiguously been positive. Investing is the search for truth.

We welcome in Brad Gerstner.

It's good to be back with you. You have a program called Invest America.

I think we have a historic moment right now to get everybody into the game of capitalism.

Do we have a few slides from Brad to kick this off?

数据铺垫：二级市场已超越 2021 峰值

You know, let's start like old times. This panel — I actually was backstage. I said, "Gavin, do you know we're talking about secondaries?" He's like, "What do you mean?"

And I said, "Okay, so here, let's just set this up for everybody. The room's full of people who are allocators. People are looking for distributions."

So this is secondary markets over the course of the last decade. This is the amount of money going into VC each year, the amount of money coming out of VC each year. The red line represents the net effect of that.

So Chamath, we're in like 5 years where a lot more is going in than's coming out. But the secondary market is at record volume. So this is — you know, I call these companies quasi-public companies. These are these later-stage companies. There's buying and selling that's going on every day.

Look at that, Jason. Relative to the 21 peak — we thought that was crazy at the end of 21. We're double that now in terms of secondary transactions.

This is the amount of employee secondary. So this is people buying into Anduril, Anthropic, SpaceX. Now represents 31% of all primary venture activity — is buying into these secondaries in 2025.

Secondaries are now competing with IPOs and acquisitions as the principal way that these guys are exiting. So, I thought that was a decent setup to start the conversation this morning, just to level set how important secondaries have become.

And then the final one is: secondaries over the last couple years were trading at a discount to market. So if we wanted to sell shares in one of our companies to buyers out there, they were willing to give us 80 cents on the dollar in order for us to get liquid so that we could send DPI back to our LPs. Today it's at 106 — so a premium in the market as a coupon.

And this doesn't include some of the wild west of SPVs that have been unraveled recently.

People charging 10% load-in fees, double carry, and a lot of gray market, off-market stuff.

员工视角：纸上百万富翁的现实困境

This is also having a profound impact, Gavin, on employees at these companies that I want to hear about, because you've seen it up close and personal with SpaceX and they have a very orderly process here.

So why don't we start there? What impact is this having on the employees, Gavin? And then on the market, how orderly is this? And who are the buyers? Are the buyers the sucker at the table? Are these family offices, high-net-worth individuals who keep hearing us talk about Anthropic or SpaceX or Anduril and they just say "I have to own the name" and they're not discerning?

So Gavin, maybe you can start about the impact on the SpaceX employees you saw firsthand, etc.

Well, maybe broadening it beyond SpaceX — I do just think if companies are going to be staying private longer, this is absolutely necessary. There are a lot of people who are very wealthy on paper but actually cash poor. And if you're making tremendous sacrifices because you work for a company that you really believe in and you're contributing a lot to that company — it's hard if you can't buy a nice house for your family. It's hard if you can't afford to do nice things,

especially in year seven, eight or nine of working at the company and you tell your spouse "we're worth 10 million on paper, 30 million on paper" and you don't own your home.

Yeah. Or year 15. And so I think this is necessary and important. And whether it is good or bad, I think it is very clear that companies are going to stay private for longer.

核心辩论：为什么公司选择留在私募市场？

What's the reason to stay private longer? Truly?

I don't think there is actually a good reason to stay private longer here.

And I completely agree with you too.

Why has it happened? Founders don't want — let's just call it what it is. Founders don't want to be under a microscope. They want to build and enjoy life and have it easier than being on the public market microscoped.

Yeah. I think there is a perception that life as a private company is easier and you have more freedom and you can think long term. I don't agree with this.

I always think about Mark Zuckerberg's commentary that had he been public — so just, you know, Facebook. I won't call it a near-death experience but long ago, it's difficult to believe — but I don't… 2011, 2012, Facebook did not believe in apps, they believed in something called HTML 6 —

and HTML 5 —

HTML 5, yes. You were the actual —

It was the cataclysmic debate. And it was me and Bret Taylor. Me versus Bret. I was like "apps, I want to go build a phone." Bret was like "HTML 5." Zuck picked Bret. Spent the next three years unwinding that decision.

Absolutely. And Mark Zuckerberg basically — the idea was, you know, the iPhone comes out and initially there was not a big app ecosystem and there was a thought that "hey, there's no need for apps. You're just going to use the web browser on your phone." And HTML 5 was a way of making websites look mobile native —

dynamic.

Yeah. And this seemed like kind of the future to a lot of very smart people including Google, Facebook. But it was not the future. It was wrong.

And what Mark Zuckerberg has said, I think several times in public, is he profoundly believes that had he been a public company when there was this internal debate —

And the detail was: actually I went to Zuck and I said "I need a billion dollars to build this phone, and we are in this moment in 2010 where we can have the third leg of the stool. There's Android, there's iPhone, neither have really taken off yet."

And he's like, "We don't have a billion."

And I said, "But the public markets will give us a billion."

And he said, "No." But then we went public a year later —

but that year made all the difference.

Made all the difference. And he said that had I had the constant pressure testing from public market investors…

私募 CEO 的"最娇嫩花朵"困境

There's a dynamic — I was talking to another CEO here this morning. When you're the CEO of a private company, you are the most special flower to all of your investors. [laughter] You are as important to your board members, particularly if you're really successful — you know, maybe as the board members' families or parents. The board members think about you a lot.

Once you're public, you're one of thousands of companies. And that's its own dynamic.

But the consequence of this is that private investors are often selling to management teams. And at some level that can mean telling management teams what they need to hear, because you want to be able to keep participating in the rounds.

Once a company's public, you can buy or sell as you wish. And this means that investors feel freer to give companies management teams — and Zuckerberg said, "Had I been public, had I been getting rigorous, detailed questions from really smart public equity investors, I think I would have…"

By the way, the second unwritten story of that, which has never been said — he called me. He's like, "Hey man, what the f*** is going on over there?" And I was like, "Yeah, I know." Cuz I had just left. And then we wrote a deck and I walked over to Zuck and I'm like, "Here's the deck of what you need to do."

Well, this is a key point, I think, Gavin. When you're private, you do not get clean information as the CEO and the management team — because people want access, and once you give the truth or you ask the hard questions, you might lose access.

100%.

The sycophantic nature of private markets is real.

Now, an exceptional CEO —

Elon —

seeks out negative feedback, looking for that. But not many — and actively discards. But not many CEOs maybe are wired that way.

And by the way, I do think we have to give Brad credit. That was a very good deck you said back in 2012.

No, cuz he did a second one.

When he did the second one, he did the open letter to Zuckerberg at the end of — was that at the end of 22?

October 22.

Why don't you call it? "Get Fit." What was the time — "Time to Get Fit"?

"Time to Get Fit." Those are two very impactful.

Kelly Rodriguez 视角：公私 CEO 之别 与 Schwab 交易

Okay. So look, you're hearing the bulls on going public. But Kelly, take the red team — the other side — because you're on the other side. You built a private business, you sold it to Schwab. So clearly one of the largest financial institutions now is going to ram its way into this market.

But then you're seeing a lot of pushback. Anthropic is like, "Hey, dissolve these SPVs." OpenAI, I think, was saying today: "now dissolve these SPVs." Should we dissolve the SPVs? Where are they coming from? And why are you on the right side of history?

And have you had to dissolve any of the ones on your marketplace?

No.

Look, I think that first of all, being a private company CEO for most of my career and then being a public company CEO for three years — I recognize the job is incredibly different. It's much less fun.

Hold on. What do you mean when you say much less fun?

Turning into an investment manager primarily as a public company CEO is a very different job than being a visionary, product-first, first-principles business. When you become a public company CEO, everything changes.

And I would say in the world we're in now, the kind of capital you can raise — the kind of capital that was represented in the very last discussion — allows you to extend your private life. SpaceX private company for 24 years.

But the reality is these SPVs that are now emerging — because these companies are getting so big — is because a market's trying to happen. And a company like SpaceX has done this extraordinarily well. They've run essentially liquidity programs for almost a decade because there's so much pent-up interest in both being an investor and getting liquidity for some of the reasons that Gavin was mentioning.

So I think what we see now is the next phase of this. This Schwab deal with Forge basically says to the world: this is a real asset class. It's more than just secondaries. We're going to put these companies' equity into fund products, into very well-managed, regulated SPV structures — because they do serve a purpose in the market.

怎么说服下一个 Elon？

Yeah, but how do you convince Elon specifically to give you access to that when he wants to do it himself and he has a team and every six months he runs it himself? How do you get access to that? What's your pitch to the next Elon?

Here's the pitch. The pitch is: you're going to go from being a private company eventually to a public company. What Schwab represents is 46 million investors and 12 trillion. This will change capital access and the way that you distribute your shares moving from private to public.

How did that work when you pitched them on that?

Well, I'll tell you. We got our first SPVs on SpaceX in 2018 and 2019.

Was he okay with it?

Absolutely. Totally permissioned. And then as we got closer to the IPO, we said, "Guess what? We've got 30 million retail investors that would like to have a $50,000 slice of SpaceX." And he went out publicly and talked about having broad-based distribution —

at the IPO price.

At the IPO price. And Schwab was named one of the IPO allocations.

Beautiful. I do think this is actually a very effective pitch. I think a lot of these CEOs are a little bit ambivalent about — and I think they understand that maybe the institutions who are investing in these private rounds may represent unions, may represent retirement plans — but I do think they like the idea of democratizing access. And if they're building something that they think is great, giving ordinary Americans an opportunity to participate, I actually think that's a very appealing story to a lot of these CEOs

because they're capitalists and they understand the power of equity.

"Going Direct" 运动与责任

So Brad, what is the downside then — cuz you're part of the "go direct" movement now? BG2 pod officially fifth bestie. Gavin officially sixth bestie. [laughter] We officially made you sixth bestie today.

But does that mean I'm definitively behind Brad? Cuz that's the real news.

I can — you're standing behind Brad. You're just giving him that big bear hug right behind him.

So are you saying I'm the big spoon?

You're the big spoon now [laughter] in the side draw with the extra spoons.

But Brad, it's getting very weird very quick.

In all seriousness, with great power comes great responsibility. Sometimes the enthusiasm people can have can exceed reality.

Correct.

Going direct — you've become more measured. I've noticed as your profile has gone up.

I think all of us have to make sure people don't blindly follow trades.

You were talking stuff down on CNBC a couple of times saying, "Hey, I don't think the average American needs to be in some of these companies. There's time."

I get worried at this point in the market stage, particularly on CNBC where you're talking to retail investors at home.

I was one of those retail guys looking up to everybody on this stage, trusting everybody on this stage. And when people are telling you to yolo into right double-fee structure SPVs and all this, you know, like — it's time to be careful, to do your work, to be thoughtful. We're in this because we want this to be durable democratization for a long time.

We want to build trust among those who feel left out and left behind in capitalism. We all think that we need to go public sooner. It is destabilizing when you're creating trillions of dollars in private value and 80% of America think it's a scam where they're left out and left behind.

And then they come rushing in —

and they could be not so good cards, right?

So all I'm saying — they asked the question on CNBC last week: if you had $100,000 of fresh capital and you were sitting at home, is today the day that you would shove it all into the market? And I said, no. I think about it in sizes. We just had two of the biggest months in the last 10 years in the public markets. They've been big months. So if I had a stack of a hundred, I may put 30 to work today. I'm never going to pick the bottom, I'm never going to pick the top, but I certainly wouldn't be putting it all to work.

And I'd say the same thing about late-stage privates. People who are yolo-ing into this stuff, and then they feel really disappointed. They're like, "Hold on a second. I bought the SpaceX IPO and it didn't go up 3x."

VC 的卖出策略：被忽略的另一半

Let me ask you then — do you view this as exit liquidity for you? Like, would you shape your portfolio and returns and increasingly say, "You know what? I don't know when this guy's going to go public. Let me just pump the stuff out. Let me get the distribution and let me send it to my LPs and just call it a day"?

We are selling into this.

You're selling into this, right? So I have LPs in this room who say, "Listen, we invested in your VC5 or VC6 7 or 8 years ago. If you can go sell a slice of that at 4 or 5x and we get DPI and it's priced really high, then go sell some of it."

And we often don't talk about this in Venture-land. Half of what we do is in the public markets. Gavin and I get up every morning and we think to ourselves: should we buy today or should we sell today? Venture capitalists don't think about the sell part. They think about the buy part.

So if we're going to stay private for longer and we're going to have trillion-dollar private companies and Databricks at $200 billion — you got to think about: is today a day we should be selling some and returning it to our investors?

Doesn't it create though, as Jason said, these very complicated personality dynamics where maybe you get shut out of a new company, maybe you get shut out of an incremental round, and you know there's bad blood because you're a credible investor? And there's this signaling risk. Whereas in the privates, if you and Gavin decide to sell, nobody knows.

Well, in the private, nobody knows.

Exactly. The public — they don't know until our 13F comes out. Okay.

But in the private market, it's always a conversation between me and the founder to say, "Listen, we're going to sell 30% of our position." They never like it, Chamath. They're always like, "We wish you wouldn't do that." They don't want it known, etc. But my job as a fiduciary to the LPs is to do that.

早期 VC 的"第三条路"

It does feel, Gavin, like we have crossed over for early-stage venture to a point in which there is a third way. Either your company had M&A — and we saw in the presentation yesterday that during the wrath of Lina there was no M&A and they just froze the market; now it's coming back. IPOs — we did have some freezing of that market for certain periods.

But this third way is now fantastic. I can tell you as the earliest of the early, we are now pari passu selling into every chance we get. Because our average investment is at 10 to $20 million valuations. When they hit 500 million, I tell the founder: "You're going to start selling at 500 million. I'm going to sell right alongside you so that I can invest in the next 'you' coming into the market." Everybody's fine with it.

But I can tell you six or seven years ago when I did this with a company, they begged me to not participate when they hit peak ZIRP 2021. They begged me: "Jay-Cal, you have to be loyal to us, you can't sell pari passu." And I said, "You guys are clearing 40 million of the $110 million round. I'm just asking to be next to you, same amount."

二级市场基础设施：从影子市场到平台化

Can I ask Kelly a question — how do you systematize this so that it's like an exchange? So like, if we just want to hit the bid, we can do it? What I don't like about the secondary markets is, you know, I ask my CFO, he calls five guys, then my fund CFO, she calls like four — it's like ticket brokers. We get a bunch of bids. None of it makes any sense. And I'm already dealing with, as Brad said, the agita from the CEO. It's got to be easier than this.

Yeah. Look, 10 years ago, we said there needs to be infrastructure to pull this off. This can't just be a big shadow market. We're sort of in this tipping point now where we spent the last 3 years building this brand new platform so that a company could plug into it the same way they could list on an exchange and say "we're going to offer liquidity." And furthermore, if you're a VC and you're on that cap structure for 10 years and you want to offer LP liquidity, you can do it.

To be specific — what do you mean? We would be plugging into Schwab's 30 million humans?

There's a platform. We brought a platform with about 3 million investors and now we're going to add 46 million investors to them.

But wait — are those accredited investors? Do they need to be? Because we just had the chair of the SEC on.

So today, if you are trading individual shares, whether it's in an SPV or direct on a cap table, you're accredited. However, there are products coming to market — we can talk about this in detail later — that have 60 companies, including SpaceX, that are listed products for unaccredited $500 minimums. And that capital for those funds will be the underlying —

closed-end funds.

These are interval funds.

Interval funds. He's got one out now.

I think Naval just did USVC as one of these.

The closed-end funds are a very different bet because you're betting on FOMO. Because if you look at the underlying value of some of the assets in those closed-end funds, they have no bearing to reality of what those underlying shares are actually worth. So price discovery is another key component of this structural shift.

But to answer your question specifically — if a VC's LPs want to recycle or want to get liquid, then a platform like this will allow them to recycle that capital and put it back into the next vintage fund if they want.

DPI 的真相：均值 vs 中位数

I have a question for you based on this. When these returns come out, the mean return in venture is going to look incredible. The median return is still going to be sh*t. So walk us through how people will sort through that and the reality of what's going to happen in the next year.

Well, so I think there's two very important things.

One, I observe: if you were a venture firm and you do not have material exposure to one of these trillion-dollar-plus companies that you had many, many chances to buy into — not only are your returns not going to be good, but you're not going to have DPI on a relative basis. And there's exceptions — you know, great series A firms, they may not have this, but their returns are still amazing with great DPI.

So I am beginning to see venture firms who don't have exposure to one of these companies behave in strange ways, because I think they're starting to feel a little bit of franchise risk — because their DPI and their returns are going to go from "hey, top quintile, top decile."

So they're doing unnatural acts. They're writing what I see as call options, like a bunch of these neolabs. "Well, I need a story. I've done something." And maybe some of these call options pay off, but I do think they're engaging at some level —

they're chasing it.

They're chasing in gambling terms. Whereas the people who have exposure to this are being a lot more disciplined because they know they're in a great position.

长线公募与私募市场的对接

I think another very important dynamic is going to happen in the world of long-only mutual funds and crossover funds. So Long Only Mutual Funds — my former employer, Fidelity, amazing place, love it. Bailey Gifford, Capital Research, Wellington —

T. Rowe.

They all can, per SEC rules, allocate up to 15% of their funds into privates. And these are the biggest pools of capital in the world. They dwarf sovereign wealth funds.

But, you know, most firms — because they don't want to get in trouble with the SEC — they say, "Hey, we're going to cap it at 3% or 5% or 7%." It was very public. Bailey Gifford was forced to sell SpaceX last year for regulatory reasons.

And what's going to happen as these companies go public? All of these long-only mutual funds are by and large finding it hard to participate in private markets right now because they're at the limits of their self-imposed 3%, 5% —

When a company goes public and the lockup expires, it moves out of that bucket. Nice.

So this is going to be hundreds of billions of dollars of new late-stage demand that is coming back to the market after kind of being out of the market for a while.

That's a lot of dry powder. There's a lot of dry powder.

The net trade is up. The marginal trade is up.

Founders are going to be in the catbird seat. People are going to be looking to put money to work.

监管演进：合格投资人测试与代币化
