## 一、"白领大屠杀"的悖论：劳动力市场正在演出一场"集体共谋的幻觉"
这段对话里最容易被忽略的一句话，其实是 Imas 提到的"协调装置"（coordination device）——企业之所以裁员，并不是因为 AI 真的取代了人，而是因为**不裁员就会被市场解读为"没跟上 AI 浪潮"**。这是一个极其反直觉的发现：裁员本身不是生产力的结果，而是一种**信号成本**。企业宁可让自己变得更糟，也要避免被资本市场打上"AI 落后者"的标签。

item.title: [Business of Sport] Should the Olympics Pay Athletes?
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：IOC 的"不付钱"其实是一种选票经济学，而非道德立场

表面上看，Kirsty Coventry 拒绝向运动员支付奖金，是在守护"奥林匹克纯粹性"。但视频里藏着一个真正惊人的揭示——这套"不付钱"逻辑的真正受益者不是奥运精神，而是IOC 主席的连任票仓。

每个国家奥委会一票，与 FIFA 同构。主席想连任，就必须把那 20 亿美元/年的现金流分发给小国联合会，换取它们的忠诚票。一旦把钱直接发给运动员，主席就失去了"政治分赃"的弹药库。 所以"我不相信付钱给运动员"这句话，本质上不是体育哲学，而是一位现任执政者在保护自己的选举机器。

这跟 Charlie 那个英国"先到先得"选举制的比喻完美咬合：在野党永远支持比例代表制，执政党永远反对它。Kirsty 现在就是那个"已经赢了的执政党"。

二、跨领域连接：奥运 vs. LIV/Enhanced Games，本质是"垄断者第一次面对真正的竞争"

视频里最被低估的一句话是 Charlie 说的——"They've never really had genuine competition at this level."

把这件事放到更大的产业史里看，你会发现一个清晰的模式： - 音乐产业 在 Napster 出现前，唱片公司可以任意压低艺人版税； - 出租车牌照 在 Uber 出现前，司机几乎没有议价权； - 传统高尔夫巡回赛 在 LIV 入场前，球员奖金天花板被 PGA 控制； - 奥运会 在 Enhanced Games（增强人类运动会）出现前，运动员根本没有外部选项。

Enhanced Games 出价"破世界纪录 100 万美元"看起来像个噱头，但它在结构上扮演的角色，和 LIV 之于 PGA、Uber 之于出租车工会一模一样——它不需要赢，它只需要存在，就重新定价了 IOC 的劳动力市场。 Kirsty 这句话最致命的，不是激怒了运动员，而是让"叛逃"在道德上变得正当："反正你不付我钱，我去拿那笔钱凭什么有负罪感？"

三、最隐蔽的系统性真相：奥运是一台"把分散现金流虹吸成集中现金流"的机器

Charlie 几乎说出了这一点，但没说透。我帮他把它说完——

奥运的商业模式不是"创造价值"，而是重新分配价值的时间结构。 赞助商每四年把营销预算集中砸向奥运，意味着这些钱不会再流向世界田联、世界泳联、各国年度赛事。IOC 等于在每四年的"虫洞"里，把原本应该均匀分布在四年内、分散到各项目巡回赛的钱抽吸到一个 16 天的窗口里。

然后它说：这笔钱我留着。

这才是矛盾的真正几何形状：不是"该不该付奖金"，而是"IOC 是否有权同时垄断聚光灯又拒绝向被它聚光的人付费"。 这跟平台经济里 Uber 司机、YouTube 创作者、外卖骑手面对的是同一个结构性问题——平台聚合了所有注意力，但把"我们不是雇主"当作护身符。

额外的"啊哈"：5 亿美元现金储备 + 0 奖金 = 一个非营利组织正在以营利方式囤积现金

IOC 坐拥 50 亿美元现金储备。一个声称"我们是运动促进组织"的非营利机构，囤现金的速度比很多对冲基金都快。Matt Richards 拿奥运金牌时年收入 1.5 万英镑，而 IOC 的现金储备够给每个夏奥会运动员每人发 45 万美元——一次性付清。

这不是"付不起"的问题。这是"不想付"的问题。 一旦你看清这个数字差，所有关于"奥运纯粹性"的修辞都会变成另一种声音——一种听起来很像 19 世纪贵族解释为什么不该给煤矿工人涨工资的声音。

📝 转录文本

开场：Kirsty Coventry 又来了

A: One of our favorite breakdown contributors, IOC President Kirsty Coventry, has been back at it.

B: Oh yes. She the the gift that keeps on giving.

Um, Kirsty Coventry, the head of the IOC, the president of the IOC, the lady who was so brilliant and competent that she actually beat Sebastian Co to that job um having never had a senior executive job in her life before. And if you remember, if viewers and listeners remember early on in our tenure as breakdown co-hosts, after she in a press conference when she was asked a question that she didn't know the answer to, she said that she was going to fire her press officer because they hadn't predicted the question.

Um, so since then we've coined this phrase "pleading a Kirsty," which is when you haven't got a clue what you're talking about, just fire a junior member of staff. Um, and I can tell viewers and listeners as the ever diminishing group of Charlie's staff demonstrates that we've got a few things wrong and been a bit ignorant at times recently. But, um, so yes, Kirsty has continued on her merry way.

Um, and her most recent contribution to the sports industry discourse has been to put her size — don't know what she's a swimmer probably quite large size eight or n size eight or nine feet. Size eight or nine feet right in another mess. Um, and Charlie is going to outline what that mess is.

那句引爆争议的话

Charlie: It's a very simple quote actually, unlike the uh the Bible I just read out. It's very simply says, "I don't believe in paying athletes."

I come from — so good, isn't it? So good. This is the president of the IOC now. And yeah, anyway, sorry, let me get — "I don't believe in paying athletes. I come from a small country. I came from a sport that doesn't necessarily pay athletes very well, and I still don't think we should be paying athletes at the Olympic Games."

Now, this was said in an interview in Eden Park in New Zealand. Uh, it was captured on kind of a video — went all over Twitter. It was a short conversation and it needed more context which was later provided. She gave clarity and you know she said she opposes prize money specifically for just the medals rather than broader athlete support. So, she's basically saying she doesn't want it to only benefit a small number of athletes.

A: I mean, a slightly more defensible position.

Charlie: That was her clarifying quote, right? So basically having put her foot in it by saying that she doesn't want to pay athletes — and we'll come on to the wise and wheres of that in a moment. Then there a big storm blew up with lots of athletes going "sorry what — um Kirsty you you really really don't want to pay us. Okay we thought before there was a conversation going on but you've just literally said there's no point in the conversation I don't want to pay athletes."

She then came out with a clarifying quote where she put a bit attempted — under heavy guidance from those communications officials that she hadn't yet fired. She attempted to put a little bit more context around it, but what it does is it brings a spotlight back onto um — Kirsty makes you laugh so much. I just blithering in conflict. I just can't believe it. And also like you're looking at a com strategies, you know, just your — Yeah. I mean, it's even, you know, when Key Hodkinson comes out, she goes, "Eh, this is going to age well."

You know, I mean, you've got your kind of athletes who are massively impacted by this, but —

IOC 的财务规模：为什么这是一个"活生生"的议题

A: Because it is a live topic, right? Let's just revisit why it's a live topic.

It's a live topic because the IOC over a 4-year cycle, as they call it — so that's the gap between the two main summer games, which also includes a winter games halfway through that cycle. Over that cycle, the IOC makes around about $8 billion.

Okay. Now, obviously, they have some head office costs and so on and so forth. It's not — so the money doesn't just appear out of thin air and they have a commercial department who helps make that money. Yeah, it's the whole thing is totally legitimate. But nonetheless, 8 billion, which if you then divide it by the number of years in which it's earned is 2 billion a year.

And I think I said on a recent um breakdown that — just put a finger in the air and say of that money, how much of it is attributable to basically like the main summer Olympic sports — mostly being athletics, but also being Kirsty's own home sports, swimming and one or two other of the big sports which really command the vast majority of the attention at the summer Olympic games. The summer Olympic games in turn being miles bigger than the Winter Olympics.

Okay. So, we just put a finger in the air and said, "Well, let's just call it half." We're probably being a bit conservative there. It's probably more than that, but let's just call it half. So, let's just say that $1 billion a year is being generated by the athletics and the swimming, the main summer Olympic sports. And currently, they are giving no prize money to those athletes who then create those special moments at the Olympics.

类比：网球大满贯的奖金争议

Now, I said some weeks ago we were discussing um the putitive strike by the tennis players at the French Open. Well, what happened to that by the way? Anyway, that's a story for another day. Um, funny, I seem to watch Arena Sabalenka an awful lot on TV for somebody who's meant to be on strike.

But we were discussing it in that context because the tennis players were saying "we are currently given about 17.5% of Grand Slam tournaments revenues and we want to see that being more like 25%." And I discussed why I felt the Grand Slam organizers should hold firm on that and say "you're already unbelievably well paid and these days even down to 100 in the world earning 600–700,000 bucks a year." You know how much further down that do we owe people a living — you know it's arguably not a lot further than that.

Um, so I'm not somebody who is one of these people that — okay, the athletes basically deserve everything and everyone else can go hang — because it's absolutely true that the IOC does have a duty to the broader Olympic movement. It does have um all sorts of federations in parts of the world which doesn't necessarily produce Olympic champions. So where they put money to try and help foster athletic development — and so it that's all absolutely fair and Kirsty could have made those points I think with a bit of clarity.

But really, instead of asking from going from 17.5% to 25%, the athletes — the runners and the swimmers and co — basically currently in the position saying "can we just have something? Can we just have something please? Can we have something which helps us through the years after a summer Olympics, cuz we don't earn much in our year-to-year sport. This is our one moment in the sun. This is what we're all aiming for and we're very happy to come to Olympics. We want to be at the Olympics, but this is the moment when the event that we're taking part in pulls in vast amounts of revenue and currently we get zero."

"体育场是谁付的钱"这个红鲱鱼

B: Kirsty tried to weaponize that in part of that conversation as well by saying "if they want to play in nice stadiums or feature in nice stadiums and be part of these amazing events, that's what a lot of the money goes towards."

A: But don't forget — those stadiums aren't paid for by the IOC. They're paid for by the host city, which again is which is why I'm kind of, you know, going in here. Sorry, Kirsty. I just I hate to keep on picking you up on your own blithering incompetence. But that is just a total red herring.

B: Their comeback will be that they distribute 90% of that revenue to international federations and governing bodies. Now, while that may be well and good, there is very little system of accountability within those systems as to how the athletes then get paid from there. So to put it — so you don't have a situation where your athletes are then really benefiting from that. That will go into so many different areas.

So you're still getting a group of people expected to dedicate an entire life — well a young life — to featuring in a games that will leverage them as brands. Their name, image, likeness will be completely exposed by the organization and they will receive no money for competing in it.

实际数字：金牌得主到底拿多少？

Now the money that they may receive — World Athletics led the charge on this in Paris in 2024. They said that they will pay, and they did pay, $50,000 to a gold medal in athletics. Now, that's good. It's a good start. But the realities again of what $50,000 does for you in the context of how much you've had to put into your training and career to that date is very, very small.

Matt Richards, British swimmer, gold medalist, brilliant guy. Had him on the show. I picked out a comment that he said when I spoke with him a few months ago. He said:

"I was on £15,000 when I won gold."

And you have to be — and when he says I was on £15,000 that is the allowance that was being given to him by the British Olympic Association.

"I was on £15,000 when I won gold and you have to be a multi-gold medal winner in multiple years to get to the top band of funding which is £28,000."

So — that's what Adam Peaty level — this is 23 years old double Olympic gold medalist. He'll be going again in LA. He's been very vocal actually since — he's actually very prominent this week, super smart, really like he's properly invested in all of this and there's no inflammatory comments coming out around it. It is very much baked in the theory.

历史视角：奥运早已不是 1900 年代的奥运

And look to me what we have to look at is you can't live off the legacy of the values that Olympics was built on in the early 1900s, because I actually had a look you know and saw how the revenue generating commercial entity of the Olympics has changed throughout that time.

In 1950, the budget for the Olympic Games was $10 million. Okay, which is the equivalent of $140 million today.

From 70s/80s onwards, you started to build a commercial powerhouse. And the minute you build a commercial powerhouse — and I mean the level of production that goes into it, the stadia that goes into it, the sponsorship, all of this — the minute you create that, you have to start paying the key contributors to that, which is still the athletes.

Now, whatever that percentage is to make that make sense, fine. You guys go and have that discussion. But with that being zero in a sport where this is the pinnacle of your athletic prowess —

Ryder Cup 比较 vs. 现实差异

This is — and people have thrown this back recently. They said, "Yeah, but the Ryder Cup, we have this issue every time." It's like, "Ah, but it's different because do you know what McIlroy and DeChambeau and all of that lot are getting alongside it? For $5 million paychecks pretty much every time they turn up on a golf course or at least win a golf tournament, right?"

So this for the athletes is their opportunity to make money. That's their cycle. And outside of that cycle, it's few and far between.

时机问题：Enhanced Games 的阴影

What happens — and the reason Charlie sorry is finally why I find this even more extraordinary — is a timing perspective. Because what did we talk about last week and trying to defend the purity of athletics? We talked about the Enhanced Games.

Now the Enhanced Games happened, Kirsty makes these comments. If I'm an athlete and I'm looking at this — and she's sitting here, the pinnacle of my elite performance — and these guys are sitting here going "I'll pay you a million dollars to break a world record, 250 grand to win an event, and some and some." I'm starting to see this come a little bit closer together if I'm an athlete who is sitting here going "I cannot make this work under current circumstances." And that's the threat.

Charlie 的"如果我是 IOC 公关"反事实

Charlie: Yeah. Let me try and imagine myself to be in the unfortunate position of being one of Kirsty's communications people. Um, I'm just asking you to just imagine that for a moment. So you're in some office at IOC Central knowing that if any point in time your boss makes a mistake, you're probably going to get fired. And in any case, even if it isn't your mistake, she's going to go and say something stupid anyway.

But nonetheless, you are basically having to give advice. So I think this is what I would say. I would say:

"I don't think it's appropriate for the Olympics to itself pay prize money because this is the pinnacle of the sport and we want people to come here for the greater glory. And furthermore, if we put all the prize money into that one event, those athletes who happen to be injured at that particular time are basically losing out on that money for the entire cycle. And that's causally disproportionate — just one mistake in one event and suddenly they don't earn anything.

So instead, what we're going to do is we're going to distribute portions of the IOC's earnings to the governing bodies of those sports and make sure that they spread that as prize money in the intervening years in between our summer Olympics, to make sure that our athletes are able to put bread on the table during that period, to make sure they're able to come back as good as they possibly can be."

I think that would be a totally logical thing — if they said right okay who have we got here, we've got the IAAF, we've got athletics — sorry, is it called World Athletics? — so we are going to work with them on how that is apportioned throughout the cycle. We're going to work with these various different bodies on how they use IOC money to make sure the athletes are earning okay in between the Olympic Games. I think that would be perfectly logical and reasonable thing to do, and they could then still keep the Olympics itself as something which is unpaid.

商业真空的迷思

I think what is not tenable is for them to say "we are the one" — because you got to remember that the Olympic Games does not exist in a commercial vacuum. So when they go and attract a commercial sponsor to get involved, the commercial partners get involved with them — that is a commercial partner who's apportioning marketing budget to the Olympics that they then will not have to apportion to World Athletics or the various different other bodies. Right?

So, they're sucking in all of the money which might otherwise be spread more evenly throughout the 4-year cycle on different events and different circuits and all this type of stuff. And they're basically then saying, having sucked it all in, "no, we're going to keep that all for ourselves."

真正的根源：投票机制

Now, there is one last thing I want to say on this Charlie, which is that part of what drives behind this is the voting mechanism for the IOC. So rather like with FIFA — and the reason frankly why Kirsty is in that position rather than Sebastian Coe — is that each individual Olympic federation, each individual country, gets a vote. And the way in which FIFA presidents and IOC presidents keep their presidency going is basically by doling out goodies — financial goodies — to these individual federations. And dare I say it, sometimes those things might end up not always in —

B: The places where they should be.

A: Yeah. Putting it very politely.

B: Being political. Yeah. Thank you very much.

A: Um, so effectively that funding model is what works for the president, the current president of the IOC, whoever that president might be. It's rather like in UK politics where we have a first-past-the-post voting system. When a political party is out of power, it always says, "well, we should have proportional representation, we should have proportional voting." And then the moment they get into power, they say, "well, I tell you what, this first-past-the-post system ain't half great, is it?" Right?

And it's a bit like that here where if you were to say to Kirsty, "I tell you what, of your two billion a year, we suggest that you take a billion of that and hand it out to the governing bodies of these sports to make sure the athletes are okay" — there'll be a bit of her thinking, "well, how am I going to get the votes of all those small countries who are relying on me to push a few million quid in their direction to, I'm sure, do some very good things, because that's how I get re-elected, right? If I go to them and say I'm pulling that money out to give it to the athletes, that's going to cause a big problem for me when somebody comes along and challenges me for the presidency of the IOC."

B: So once again, the power mechanism is completely out of touch with the outcomes.

与网球的对比：奥运运动员"没什么可失去的"

A: So the downside I saw — the downside which you've just alighted on — just be clear on the jeopardy. Because there's a bit of these things where you can say — and I say this with Grand Slam tournaments say "look, what are they going to do, right?" And the thing here is, as you've outlined in the case of tennis players, they're earning 20–25 million bucks a year, a big chunk of which is coming from the Grand Slam tennis tournaments. The "what are they going to do" question is very opposite — is Arena Sabalenka really going to give up three or four million because she feels so strongly about prize money? As it turns out, no, she won't. Right.

Now, when it comes to the Olympics and the athletes, particularly those who are already big stars who may well already have won a gold medal — right? So, they've already scratched that itch. And then the Enhanced Games comes along and says, "Tell you what, since Kirsty says she doesn't want to give you any money, you kind of haven't got a lot to lose here. You already — you'll always already be an Olympic gold medalist. How about if you come and earn some money?"

I would be very tempted — which is just what you're saying.

B: Exactly. And whether it's the Enhanced Games or anything else, it leaves them wide open. And it comes back to I think a point we made the other day. They've never really had genuine competition at this level.

But I'm also looking at it and I saw they sit on 5 billion in cash reserves. So it's not like the cash isn't there.

A: No, it's not.

收尾：支付与体育商业的根本问题

B: Um, it's not. So we talk a lot about this, don't we, Charlie? I mean this is coming up a lot — as in just pay in general sport, pay and sport cost. Because I tell you the reason why it comes up a lot Charlie is because it's a critical element of running a sports business — right? Which is that in any sports business that you're running, a large chunk of your cost base and therefore your business model is basically how much do we pay the athletes.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：IOC 的"不付钱"其实是一种**选票经济学**，而非道德立场
表面上看，Kirsty Coventry 拒绝向运动员支付奖金，是在守护"奥林匹克纯粹性"。但视频里藏着一个真正惊人的揭示——这套"不付钱"逻辑的真正受益者不是奥运精神，而是**IOC 主席的连任票仓**。
每个国家奥委会一票，与 FIFA 同构。主席想连任，就必须把那 20 亿美元/年的现金流分发给小国联合会，换取它们的忠诚票。**一旦把钱直接发给运动员，主席就失去了"政治分赃"的弹药库。** 所以"我不相信付钱给运动员"这句话，本质上不是体育哲学，而是**一位现任执政者在保护自己的选举机器**。
这跟 Charlie 那个英国"先到先得"选举制的比喻完美咬合：在野党永远支持比例代表制，执政党永远反对它。Kirsty 现在就是那个"已经赢了的执政党"。
## 二、跨领域连接：奥运 vs. LIV/Enhanced Games，本质是**"垄断者第一次面对真正的竞争"**
视频里最被低估的一句话是 Charlie 说的——"They've never really had genuine

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Strategy Sold Bitcoin. Polymarket Can't Agree on When
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、表面是"披露日 vs 交易日"的争议，本质是预测市场把自己活成了一家"私设法院"

大多数人把这次 Polymarket 争议看作合约条款写得不严谨的小事故。但更深一层的真相是：预测市场正在不知不觉中扮演司法系统的角色——它需要对"事实何时发生"这种本属于法庭和监管者的认定问题做出裁决。当 UMA 上仅 9 个人的投票权就能决定数千万美元的归属，这已经不是"去中心化预言机"，而是一个没有上诉机制、没有证据规则、没有回避制度的小型陪审团。

讽刺的是：加密世界诞生于对"中心化机构武断裁决"的反抗，最终却复刻出一个更不透明的版本——传统法院至少有公开判例、回避制度和上诉权。这是一个加密原教旨主义者大概率从未在 KPI 里量化过的"治理回归"。

二、一个出人意料的观察：预测市场最有价值的产品，根本不是赌博，而是它的"副产品"

嘉宾说出了一句几乎所有平台运营者都不愿大声讲的话——"我从不在 Polymarket 下注，但我每天看它。"

这句话的真正含义是：Polymarket 在商业上靠赌徒维生，但在社会价值上靠不下注的人维生。换句话说，它的核心用户（散户赌徒）正在为它的高价值用户（分析师、记者、对冲基金、政策研究者）补贴一个免费的实时民意/概率数据库。

这和报纸 vs 广告主的古典模式高度同构：读者以为自己是客户，其实是产品；真正的客户是广告主。预测市场里：赌徒以为自己在交易概率，其实他们生产的是概率数据，真正的"客户"是看盘但不下注的人。 一旦你这样看，Polymarket 不是"金融科技公司"，它是一家伪装成赌场的彭博终端。

三、跨领域连接：塔木德、合约法、预言机——同一种"语义战争"在三千年里打了三遍

嘉宾不经意间提到自己上过希伯来学校"能正反两面辩塔木德"，紧接着说律师是"靠抠字眼吃饭"。这看似随口一句，实际是整集播客最深的一刀。

预测市场争议的底层结构其实是一个反复重演的人类难题：当事实复杂、语言有限时，谁来定义"发生了"？ - 塔木德拉比争论"安息日开关电灯算不算点火" - 普通法法官争论"Zelensky 那件衣服算不算 suit" - UMA 投票者争论"Strategy 卖出的'日期'是交易日还是披露日"

这三件事本质完全相同：人类在用离散的符号系统去切割一个连续的现实，而每一次切割都需要某个"权威解释者"。区块链以为可以用代码消灭解释者，结果只是把拉比换成了匿名投票人。

真正打破常规的结论：预测市场不会因为更好的合约措辞而成熟，它会因为演化出自己的判例法体系而成熟——这意味着 Polymarket 的下一个关键岗位，不是工程师，不是 BD，而是首席大法官。当那一天到来时，加密圈会发现自己花了十五年，绕了一大圈走回了 14 世纪英国普通法的起点。

四、最后给到一个反直觉的提示

主持人问"参与前该看什么"，嘉宾给的答案非常专业（对手方、规则、信息优势）。但有一件他没说的事：预测市场最大的隐藏风险，不是你输钱，而是你赢钱。 因为一旦你确认自己"看得准"，你会从"看盘但不下注的高价值用户"，退化成"为别人提供数据的散户"。在这个市场里，赢一次小钱的代价，往往是失去那个让你看得清的距离感。这是一个连专业交易员都常掉进去的认知陷阱，值得在每次下单前再想一遍。

📝 转录文本

引子：Strategy 多年来首次卖出 Bitcoin，引发 Polymarket 争议

Let's talk about one other big news item before we start to wrap up here, and that is the controversy surrounding uh Strategy's uh selling of uh of of Bitcoin for the first time. I don't think it was the first time in its history, but the first time in years and years and years.

And uh we'll we'll sidestep, because I know this is not your role, the the impact on the market — which, although this was sort of pre-telegraphed during I think Saylor's Q1 earnings call, the the market is obviously reacting poorly to. Right.

But there is a real debate about how a contract on Polymarket tied to the timing of that sale is resolving, and it creates a whole another — it opens up a whole another suite of issues that we haven't even touched on when it comes to the operation of these platforms. So so why don't you walk us through that really quickly?

争议本身：交易日 vs. 披露日

So I believe the issue is that the — there was a a market on Polymarket where you could place a trade as an expressive view on whether or not, by a certain date um —

through the end of May. It was through the end of May.

— MicroStrategy would sell.

Yeah.

And the — there was a disclosure that they filed, an SEC filing. The SEC filing was ma— was made in June, but it revealed that prior to June, in May, a sale had taken place.

And so there there is a dispute right now, with you know big money at stake, on whether or not who is correct for purposes of this trade. Is is the controlling date the date of the trade, the date of the sale of the Bitcoin, or is it when it was made public through the SEC file?

预言机问题：UMA 与"九个人"的投票权

Yeah, and and like you said, big money. I think um like like tens of millions of dollars have been bet on this market, and uh and yeah, it really comes down again to like how these things — how these contracts are are written, how they're structured, how they're designed to resolve.

And then we didn't even get into the oracles. And then they use a a relatively smaller oracle provider called UMA to actually vote on the outcome of of these events. And I think I've seen some analysis that ultimately nine people control enough voting power on UMA to actually decide — which is not quite as decentralized as I I would imagine many people would want.

预测市场的整体风险图景

And and this is just — I I see it as another risk as it relates to prediction markets. I mean, we're excited about the technology. It it does um — I I can certainly understand the value of it in sort of crowdsourcing intelligence for important events.

Uh I I mean, there are certain vices — I mean, gambling and betting on like like trivial items that maybe we need — may maybe we don't, but I can certainly see the the viability of of certain markets. Uh but at the same time, like, this is another thing people have to account for when it comes to uh participating in these platforms.

And and this doesn't even get into necessarily some of the other issues or risks. Uh and again, I know this is not your purview, Sam, but I mean, there's been a lot of reports — the Wall Street Journal had one, there was an academic study — really showing how the vast majority of participants lose on these platforms. And and just like a casino, the house — the house always wins in a certain way.

Uh, a lot of the people that make money are the the professional market makers, the ones running sophisticated algos, and and they prey on on retail uh traders to a large part, trying to find like long odds that that could pay off and and just fundamentally misunderstanding risk — which frankly we all, I think we all do from from a certain time to time. But that's a lot of — there's a lot of different things for traders to be careful about when it comes to participating in these markets.

实操建议：下注前该做什么尽调？

Um, I'd love for you, because we have to start wrapping up, to just — what to to the extent you are able to share, and from your vantage point as a former prosecutor and just someone who intimately understands the way these markets operate, in a not-official-financial-advice point of view — like what are some of the things that you for instance would look at, look for, try to understand before you might want to put a bet on one of these markets?

Sure. I think what I would look at first — it, as you point out, it's very important to diligence: how is the bet — who determines how the bet is won, what are the rules for that. You you need to understand — that's kind of part of understanding counterparty risk, right? Is understanding the mechanics of it, so that if there — you don't want to — you want to make sure you're playing on a level playing field, right? That's part of understanding the the level playing field.

The second thing is, um, why am I placing this bet? Am I placing this bet because I believe I have better information or better judgment? Is this for fun? What am I — what am I doing here?

If this is a serious uh bet, then I need to to understand whether I really have some kind of asymmetric advantage that is legal. In other words, I'm not — I don't have an asymmetric advantage because I have um inside information, but I have an asymmetric advantage because I am smarter at analyzing the signal than the rest of the market. Or maybe it's a hedge.

But frankly, I personally find that the the the greatest use of prediction markets is the information, not —

not as a better, but as a source of information.

Yeah. Um, and obviously that happens because other people are placing bets, right? But um, it is an entirely new category of data signal. You know, 5 years ago this is not something that was regular, you know, pervasively available. But now when I'm thinking about an issue, I always look at Polymarket or Kalshi to see what are they saying about it, if there is anything.

案例呼应：Zelensky 的"西装之争"

Yeah, I think those are really good points, and and frankly it's — it can be difficult for people to understand the way certain things resolve.

I'm sure you remember, and I'm sure plenty of people listening here remember, the debate over Zelensky's suit — I think when he came to, I guess, meet President Trump another time, because of what happened when he went to the Oval Office. Was it in February of of this year? Was that — or was it last year? This point I I can't remember.

Um, but there was a whole debate as to whether or not what he wore was actually a suit. And it came down to, again, like, um, these people, nameless people, behind an oracle to kind of decide. And I remember that um that resolution was also unsatisfactory to a lot of people. And it's hard sometimes to due-diligence those types of things, because these are very esoteric topics that um, that, that very few folks are are experts in.

给交易员的总结

But I think your point is — is very well taken. And and again, because this is a a trader show, this was a tremendous kind of walk-through all the different issues pertaining to these markets, uh, for people participating, retail people participating — because there is a lot of incentive to participate in these markets given how altcoins and crypto in general are are are going down a little bit. And uh, if you're not going to put money into AI stocks, which are which are booming, prediction markets is an area where you can kind of get that risk-on uh fix, for for lack of a better term.

So yeah, really understanding like, what is the advantage that that you have, and yeah, is it — is it legal? There are plenty of legal ways to get — for these types of markets — to get um, to to get um like an informational advantage, and in that way it's very different and perhaps a little superior to securities markets.

But um, you need to understand kind of what that um — what your advantage really is, or if nothing else, make sure you don't bet money that that you can't afford to lose. Because uh especially as some of these markets are now going to start offering like leverage trades and and so on and so forth, um, it — there's a rule of possibility to um, to to really go up or or go down depending on what happens.

合约措辞与"二元裁决"的本质

Yeah, for sure. And I think one other point — which, in addition to the rules, I think you're hitting on this with the suit example, suit example — is what exactly is the phrasing, right?

I went to Hebrew school. We can debate the Talmud both ways, all right? And litigators, I make a living splitting hairs on what the the meaning of words is. What does it mean? Is this ambiguous? Is it not ambiguous?

Um, and so you definitely have to be: is this clearly a binary? Is there going to be a clear winner to this?

节目通告：Bits + Bips 频道迁移

Heads up everyone: we'll now be fully transitioning Bits and Bips to its new dedicated Bits and Bips channels starting next week.

So if you're not yet subscribed to the Bits and Bips channels on X, YouTube, Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or wherever you get your podcasts, then go there now and subscribe. After that transition, for a few weeks we'll play segments of the full interviews on Unchained as a reminder. But head there now and subscribe so you don't miss an episode.

You can get links to all of the platforms on unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips. Again, that's unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips, spelled "bits."

item.description:
## 一、表面是"披露日 vs 交易日"的争议，本质是**预测市场把自己活成了一家"私设法院"**
大多数人把这次 Polymarket 争议看作合约条款写得不严谨的小事故。但更深一层的真相是：预测市场正在不知不觉中扮演**司法系统**的角色——它需要对"事实何时发生"这种本属于法庭和监管者的认定问题做出裁决。当 UMA 上仅 9 个人的投票权就能决定数千万美元的归属，这已经不是"去中心化预言机"，而是一个**没有上诉机制、没有证据规则、没有回避制度的小型陪审团**。
讽刺的是：加密世界诞生于对"中心化机构武断裁决"的反抗，最终却复刻出一个更不透明的版本——传统法院至少有公开判例、回避制度和上诉权。这是一个加密原教旨主义者大概率从未在 KPI 里量化过的"治理回归"。
## 二、一个出人意料的观察：**预测市场最有价值的产品，根本不是赌博，而是它的"副产品"**
嘉宾说出了一句几乎所有平台运营者都不愿大声讲的话——"我从不在 Polymarket 下注，但我每天看它。"
这句话的真正含义是：**Polymarket 在商业上靠赌徒维生，但在社会价值上靠不下注的人维生**。换句

item.title: [All-In Podcast] The IPO Comeback: Why Tech Giants Are Finally Going Public | All-In Liquidity IPO Panel
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：IPO 本质上是一场"什么都没发生"的烟花秀

听完 Andrew Feldman 的描述，我捕捉到一个被所有人忽略的悖论：IPO 是科技行业最被神化、却最缺乏实质内容的"成人礼"。他说的那句话值得反复咀嚼——"第二天早晨你没有多卖出一件产品，工程项目没有任何进展"。

更深一层的洞见是：IPO 的真正功能不是融资，而是集体心理仪式。Andrew 提到工程师们第一次穿西装、移民父母含泪出席——这暴露了一个被低估的真相：在科技公司里，IPO 是中产阶级移民家庭的"现代成人礼"，是一种社会身份合法化的工具。它和资本市场无关，和宗族认同有关。这也解释了为什么 OpenAI、SpaceX 这类公司即使不缺钱、不缺名声，最终也很难抗拒上市的引力——因为员工和他们的父母需要那个仪式。

二、跨领域连接：太空数据中心 = 大型机到 PC 革命的"反向重演"

Will 谈卫星小型化时类比"大型机到桌面电脑"，但他自己可能没意识到一个更深的悖论——太空数据中心是 IT 历史的反向回放：

  • 1970s-80s：算力从中心化（大型机）走向分布式（PC）
  • 2010s-20s：算力又从分布式（PC）回到中心化（云）
  • 2030s（如果 Will 对）：算力将从地球中心走向轨道中心——但这次的"中心"是太阳同步轨道上 24/7 接受阳光的太阳能板阵列

这里隐藏着一个跨领域的连接：太空数据中心本质上是"能源套利"，而不是"算力革命"。它和加密货币矿场迁徙到冰岛、阿拉伯沙漠的逻辑同构——计算正在变成一种游牧动物，跟着最便宜的瓦特跑。轨道不是终点，只是当前的"最廉价能源边疆"。这也意味着：未来真正的护城河不在芯片，而在能源-发射成本-散热这三者的耦合方程里。

三、被掩盖的真相：Cerebras 的"晚一年上市"和私募市场的"价值掠夺"

Brad 提到 Cerebras "如果18个月前以100亿上市，那 5 倍的涨幅会归公开市场投资者所有"——这句话表面上是在为公开市场叫好，但它暴露了一个惊人的财富分配机制：

过去十年，硅谷"延迟上市"的范式（Andreessen 主导的"Stay Private Forever"）实际上是一次有组织的财富再分配运动——把本应由公众分享的 10-100 倍涨幅，截留在了少数 LP 和后期成长基金手里。SpaceX、OpenAI、Anthropic 的天价估值不是市场效率的体现，而是流动性垄断的副产品。

更让人意外的连接是：这和美国劳动力市场"非竞争协议"问题、和中国"内卷"现象共享同一个底层结构——信息和流动性被人为限制在小圈层里，导致大多数参与者无法捕获价值创造的主要部分。Brad 说的"钟摆要回摆了"，本质上是说硅谷正在经历一次"金融民主化运动"——而这个运动的真正受益者将是那些在公司估值 5 亿到 50 亿之间敢于上市的创业者，而不是那些等到 5000 亿的"超级独角兽"。

四、隐藏的系统性真相：Andrew 关于"时机"的话是整场访谈的钻石

Andrew 说："我们怎么把握住时机？答案是把它做错了十年。"

这句话表面是凡尔赛，但它揭示了一个反复出现却很少被言说的系统性规律：在指数增长的领域里，"早"和"晚"不是时间问题，而是认知问题。你不能"等到时机成熟"，因为当你能看到时机时，时机已经过去了。你只能通过持续做错事，让自己处在正确事情发生时仍然在场的位置。

这与生物进化、与冲浪、与禅宗"不立文字"是同一个结构——你无法瞄准你看不见的东西，但你可以持续待在它可能出现的地方。

📝 转录文本

The IPO Comeback: Tech Giants Going Public — All-In Liquidity IPO Panel

开场介绍

Hey, 2026 could be an all-time record for IPOs.

The AI IPO of the year so far. That company is Cerebras. Cerebras Systems founder and CEO Andrew Bubman. We are participating in something extraordinary on everything we do. We are the fastest bar none. Well, Marshall is the co-founder and CEO of Planet Labs. Space and AI are really um a match made in heaven. They're getting married. In fact, just like Google figured out how to index the internet and make it searchable, we are indexing the earth and making it searchable.

He's got his glasses, the famous red glasses. Brad Gersner is here, founder and CEO of Autoimmet Capital, a leading tech investment firm.

I believe that the wave is the biggest wave in the history of technology, will be incredibly beneficial for America. I'm rooting for all of them because I'm rooting for America.

Ladies and gentlemen, please welcome Brad Gersonner, Will Marshall, and Andrew Feldman.

寒暄环节：Davos 的回忆

On the couch, we switch on the couch. Nice to see you, my friend. Hey, big boy. Nice to see you. Last last time I saw you, we were in Davos. Yes, we were in Davos causing another drop. Another JL. Do you hear that little Davos? We were just, you know, it was preipo. We're chopping it up with Davos. We're in Davos. Hanging out at Davos.

Well, no. Listen, I was Everybody knows the story. I'm supposed to go on my yearly Japan ski trip. Sax calls me with Tucker. Yeah. Well, anyway, we don't drop that name, but I'll pick it up for you. Put it over here. Tucker.

Anyway, so I cancel on Tucker. I cancel because Sax calls me. He says, "Listen, Pus needs you, the world's greatest moderator in Davos." I said, "No problem."

I said, "Saxs, POTUS, and Davos." So I said, "When?" He says, "In 3 days." I say, "You got it." I go and they give me a badge. And it's like the special green badge and they buzz you through the security and I look at the monitor and it says Jason McCabe Calacanis with Donald J. Trump. Oh wow. How did you feel? I thought it was hilarious. So then I went and we did a great interview there and we did like six or seven of these great all-in interviews and it was fun.

进入正题：上市后的感受

Let's start this because uh the two of you guys run two of the most interesting and consequential newly public companies in the stock market. Andrew Feland is the founder and CEO of Cerebrus. Will Marshall is the founder and CEO of Planet Labs. But you are also the insight and a gateway for all of us to understand these two big trends. One is in AI silicon, the other one is in space data centers. I think it would be a really interesting thing to and emerging. And emerging. Yeah. Um but let's just take one step back. Uh you just heard the last conversation about being public, going public early. Let's just talk about that cuz I'm just very curious. How's it been? It's been 3 weeks or so for you. It's been about a year and a half or two years for you. Uh it's more fresh. Was it Was it everything that you thought it would be? Like

Andrew Feldman：IPO 当天什么都没改变

what's clear so far is I need to upgrade my namerop game. I mean that that was a tour to force. But by the way, you were you were in Davos with J. I was I was there but that um uh tour to force uh look I I I think you do all this work and I I think it's really difficult to to overestimate the amount of garbage that's involved in in going public.

The number of meetings where you you look on the the Zoom and there are 130 attendees and the amount of times you review these documents and the commas move and and just no value added.

You go there and you have this enormous event and the next morning you've sold no more stuff. your engineering projects have made no progress since the day you weren't public. And you go back to work and um you you have some new constituents that that you have to to to address and communicate with, but the core parts of your business, you have more money in the bank. Um but not a damn thing changes in the important parts of your business.

Um if you still if you need new supply or if your relationships with your vendors are bad, they're still bad. If they're good, they're still good. And and so I I think what what we've seen is um you your employees have a party, everybody's really excited, and you put your head back down, you high five, and you go back to work.

Brad 补充：Cerebras 上市的艰难路径

Can >> can I can I just give a little context and then I want to hear from Will. You know, if if I can, Andrew, you know, we were investors in Cerebrus. I was on the board a year earlier where we were trying to go public. Um, and you know, aside from just being a warrior who weathered a decade worth of storms that would have taken out any normal human being, the path to going public for Cerebras was a particularly challenging one. One of their investors was the UAE. So there was questions about CPHAS, you know, in in the prior under the Biden administration challenging to get public. My observation outside looking in is everything was really hard until it got really easy like n 9 and a half years of really hard and then 12 months you know of of of really easy where everybody wanted to get in.

They priced the IPO at 185 which was up the range was taken up two times. Okay. The stock opened at $320 a share I think. Today it's at 230 bucks a share, 5060 billion dollars in market cap for a business like you know and andrew is just one of these people. Let's let's get back to work and build

But my my just add-on question to that is from an employee morale perspective like distraction perspective etc. has the last 3 weeks you got a lot more capital you got a lot more profile presumably it's easier to sell to enterprise customers today. net net if you were advising me if I was in a similar position would you say go public?

Andrew：把握时机的方式，就是错了十年

I I I think the first thing is a lot of people asked us about how we got the timing right. Right. And I I think the answer is by getting it wrong for a decade. I mean that's really the right way to get timing right.

Um I I think um first I we we we've been at this for for more than a decade and and we we brought everybody who'd been with the company more than nine years to share and we brought their families. And first I learned that engineers owned ties. I didn't actually know that. Um and they didn't die when they wore them. And second I was surprised at how big a deal it was for them and their family. they they were really proud in a way that sort of their parents might have heard of it or uh that that somehow this was like a a bar mitzvah or kinera or something.

Um and then you you had these sort of the the children of immigrants, one of our uh one of our leaders, her father, Chinese immigrants said, "I thought it would have happened faster."

Right. And but I I think um we are sort of by nature uh sort of in the uh in the trenches people. And so um we we love solving hard problems. And so when when we had this excitement, everybody went and they were so excited and we had a party and um I I think it it gave external validation. And then everybody turned around and said, "Now what are we now back to work?" And and so I I I think um

Will Marshall：Planet Labs 的公开市场之旅

and so you you started off kind of bang right out of the gates. Will you had a little bit different experience in terms of you know the entry to the public markets, but over the last 12 months your stock has gone from five bucks a share to 50 bucks a share, some 10x move in the public markets. So talk us through the other side of this where you come public, nobody really notices until they notice.

Well, we were one of the first space stocks and and I think people just had no idea what earth is going on in space, how it was changing everything and they were just like what the heck is that?

And and but but you know, I have similar opinions. I mean, in the end, you've just got to get on with executing the business. uh the going public gives you access to liquidity uh for early shareholders whether that's the employ early employees or early investors and that's great it gives you cash for the company that's great and I do think um it helps your business as well because the maturing event gives you more credibility to various customers and for us we work with biggest agricultural customers big governments civil governments defense and intelligence all of those sort of actors they want to know you're going to be around.

Exactly. And not going to disappear. I mean, we have countries that are fully dependent on us giving them information. They don't want to just disappear. So, they really care that we're going to be around and and being a public company gives you the kind of force in the world that people go, "Okay, you're here to stay and you have access to capital if you need it and so on, right? It's legitimizing and you know, you you know where the stock is at any one day. You know, we're not focused on that dayto-day. We're focused on how we build long-term value for our shareholders, right?

And um you know the market is I think started to really understand where space is going, why it is changing the the the world. You know people forget how space is part of your everyday life. Every time you use a phone you're using communications using satellites or GPS using satellites or satellite data in some way or another that's sort of integrated in your lives. you may not realize it um but it's just booming now there's there's a

and the the story's changed as well obviously with SpaceX going public but has the framing of planet gone from like a data source for people who need data from space and maps to hey this is a tool to accomplish tasks and military like post Andrew's success like you probably would have been bucketed into Andrew as a military tech company. So is that framing what's driving a lot of

Planet Labs 的业务详解

I think it's a bit more nuance than that. I mean firstly for the audience's benefit what planet does we have satellites doing earth imaging. We have the largest earth imaging fleet about 200 satellites. They image the entire earth every day. So think of it like the Google uh satellite lay on Google maps that you can look at except it's today's date rather than 3 years old and we have every day going back. So it's a time series analysis of everything going on on the earth.

That's useful for farmers. It's useful for energy companies. It's useful for civil governments, flooding and fires. It's useful for security applications like you're getting at. And it's a wide variety of use cases.

Um, I think that where we're seeing this is that AI is now enabling the it's basically reducing the barrier to entry so that more people can get access to this, right? And uh, you know, there's a lot more to say on that, but AI is only as good as the data it's trained upon. percentage is military. I'm curious. Sorry.

军事用途与地缘政治

What percentage of revenue customer bas um uh security is part of the initial thing that we said we would do um out of the gate, but it's true there's a bigger fraction today than perhaps we would have guessed. Um but the needs of the geopolitical situation right now demand what we're doing.

Um you know, just as an example, what this does is enable them to see threats around the corner.

Yes.

And then uh you know give them weeks or months advanced warning of things and then that enables them to more likely do things that stop conflict. So we believe this is you know really better for the world.

Are you reticent to be perceived as a military company?

Not really. I but I wouldn't say we're limited to being perceived like that, right? um we we are helping farmers, we are helping uh you know uh energy companies, civil governments, we work with NASA, we work with what have you and and so um it's a it's a bigger it's a bigger play than that.

卫星小型化革命

But back to the space uh piece of it what has changed obviously rocket costs have come down about four or 5x over the last uh 10 years which has helped tremendously but a thing that people don't know that is actually perhaps more important is that we've had a miniaturization of satellites. So that the same satellite that used to cost a billion dollars and weigh 20 tons now cost a few kg or a few tens or hundreds of kg

and can do just as much stuff if not more. It's it's the same as the sort of mainframe computer to desktop revol computer revolution for space and it's unlocking just like mainframes to desktops unlock loads of applications. This is unlocking loads of applications and it's so both go in combo the launch cost coming down and this

太空数据中心与硅片重生

let's build let's build on this. So I think I I'd like first you maybe take a few minutes and then I I want to talk to Andrew the same question. Both of you guys are at the foot of what are probably huge secular trends in technology. How I would frame this is we are rebuilding the data processing infrastructure that has existed on the earth in the sky. And first you do the satellites, but I would love for you to explain space-based data centers because I think everybody's hearing about that.

Are they really viable? What are they? How will they work? etc. And then Andrew, this is the rebirth of silicon. We're going to find the next version of Moore's law, which I think is more timebounded, not transistor density bounded. We now hear a lot about domain specific architectures. we hear I mean your chip was just a complete transformation in terms of the design principles that you know like at Grock we took a very different approach Nvidia's taken a very different approach you took a big pizza shaped die and said it yolo this is it and you were right just explain where we're going in silicon so maybe will you start and then Andrew you start

Will：空间数据应用与太空数据中心

I mean what we're seeing firstly in space is is all these new applications based on data and AI so you know we we're collecting vastly more data about the planet. And with SpaceX and Starlink and One Webb, they're they're transporting far more data around the planet. As you say, we're sort of changing um uh the nature of data using satellites. And that's basically doing what was once the province of governments only and giving everyone else access to uh satellite capabilities.

And that's going to I I mean I I estimate there's a 75 to$100 billion market just on Earth observation. this kind of data we collect and AI on top of that unleashing all that application. So that's the near-term thing. Applying large language models to earth imagery data, unlocking agriculture, you know, um energy, civil government applications, permitting, you name it. This is going to make everything more efficient.

Um and then where we're going is indeed space is is we did a study with uh our partners at Google about eight or nine years ago uh looking at what are the costs of data centers on the ground, what are the costs that it would take to put them in space and when might it make sense to uh do it non-aterrestrially. And we figured out that when launch costs come down to about $200 to $300 a kilogram um uh it would be cheaper, just simply cheaper to put the data centers in space. Now we're about $1,000 a kilogram, just over that in right today. Uh but that's come down about 10x in the last 10 years.

Um on the current trajectory with Starship in particular, I would expect it the launch cost come down there in 2 to 3 years. Elon might say it's next week, but at least realistically a couple of years. So we're not far away from it literally just being cheaper.

Then in addition and the intuition there that is that helps people understand that is you would naturally use solar panels for doing uh the the data centers are a power problem. It's a power game and you would normally use solar panels. That's the cheapest way to get a watt uh today by far. But you you don't want intermittent power. So then you have to have batteries or you then you have to have gas or then you have to have nuclear and then it gets really expensive.

Um in space you can put a solar panel in a suns synchronous uh dawn dusk orbit where you're 24/7 looking at the sun. So you can have a solar panel that collects and gathers five times more energy per solar panel than on the ground and you don't have to have batteries or anything else. Uh so the infrastructure for comput in space is literally just solar panels and the chips and then the RF signals up and down. So it's actually really quite simple. It was just a question of when it's going to be cheaper to launch all those solar panels and chips into space than putting on the ground.

And it turns out that's going to be in a few years. So we're partnering uh with Google to launch some of their TPUs into space. We've already launched Nvidia's uh uh some of Nvidia's GPUs into space. We're launching Google's TPUs into space on an early test. There's lots of technology to figure out.

Let's have a conversation. Um, but it's an early it's early days, but I think no question within 10 years most compute will be putting in space, which to give you a sense is a lot of money, like trillions, and um will be bigger than any of the other space businesses today. comes Earth imaging. This is why we're getting into this.

Andrew 的怀疑：太空集群难度更大

Do you believe this? Do you believe sending data centers to space makes more sense or is it just the regular Can you have him explain the business first and then Oh, yeah, of course. Yeah.

So, I think they're, you know, with all due respect, one or two hard problems still left be beyond putting putting GPUs in in in space right now. I I think um we we we're not super good yet at building the clusters in space necessary for the communication

between between Exactly. between We're not good at doing it on the ground. We're not good at doing it on the ground. We're really not good at doing it in space.

I I think this is an extraordinarily important and interesting problem and one we should be spending money and attacking. I've got it in a slightly different time frame, but one that certainly will occur. And the the hard part is is it is it one of those problems where uh the last 10% is 80% of the time.

Now, self-driving was a problem like that, right? Where the last 10% proved to be a decade's worth of work and just now we're over the hump. And we don't know yet, but I think the interesting work they're doing uh at Planet is really important. And I think the fundamental driver to experiment to even get insight into whether I'm right or not is to get down the cost of launch vehicles. Then you can start doing experiments and getting it wrong and fixing it and figuring it out. And until then it was unpaid.

Cerebras 与 AI 芯片的未来

For the foreseeable future, you're going to be terrestrial. explain your business and how you made these critical decisions that kind of took you on a different path and you know you versus Nvidia versus AMD and what you think the future of AI silicon looks like.

AI 如何打开了计算的边界

I I I I think there were two parts your your first question was around sort of the rise of silicon in general and I I think what AI did and it's it's rarely sort of framed this way but it allowed computers to address a class of problems that before AI computers were bad at.

We were bad at images for almost the entire history of compute. We could store them and that's about it. We were bad at language. We could store it but that's about it. We could transform numbers. We were magical with numbers.

And what AI did starting in about 2015 16 is it opened the door the aperture to say maybe we could use computers on images. All right. Maybe we could find insight in images. Maybe not only could we store language but we could generate it. All right. maybe we could understand it rather than storing it and regurgitating it.

And what this did is it it opened up sort of to compute huge areas that were previously foreclosed and at the same time we were adding to those areas. we were taking vastly more images, all right, terrestrially in satellites. And what this did is it it simultaneously opened up this entire area and allowed compute to attack it. And this is what's underpinning both Nvidia's growth and and sort of all the growth you're hearing about in in AI compute is as a as a processor builder as a hardware builder suddenly our tools could attack more and different parts of knowledge and and that was sort of the first part to to to answer your question.

历史的规律：新工作负载带来份额变化

Now how you do that there are lots of different strategies tons of different ways to skin cats. What we saw in 2015 were several things. First, we saw that AI would be an enormous consumer of compute. All right.

And historically for computer architects, new workloads were the opportunity for share to change, right? Share changed when the rise of graphics emerged and you got the dedicated GPU. That's how Nvidia was born. Share changed when cell phone compute emerged and Intel and AMD who had fabs and the best architects got zero share and it all moved to ARM. Right? Share changed in the late '9s when Nortell and all these companies we've forgotten about couldn't build chips and couldn't do uh uh data networking and and what you got with Cisco and Juniper and Arist and this collection of new companies.

So we knew that um this new problem would present an opportunity for massive change. So we saw that we we made two bets. Um the first was dedicated silicon would be the answer. The second was it couldn't look like a GPU. And our view as computer architects is if you want to be 20 times better than somebody, right? Your architecture can't look like them, right? It it it can't. They have they have enjoyed and and eaten all the lowhanging fruit. So, if you build a GPU, the odds that you're better than Nvidia in our view are approximately zero.

Cerebras 的核心架构选择

That led us to a fundamentally different architecture. All right. The hard part here, the hard part is moving data from memory to compute.

This is the fundamental problem in AI. And we solved it with a way that that very few others had even attempted, which was to build a very big chip and to put memory right next to compute. And by building a big chip, a chip the size of a dinner plate, whereas most chips are the size of a postage stamp, we could use a different type of memory. And by using a different type of memory, a memory that was vastly faster, we opened up all sorts of opportunity.

So when OpenAI uses us, we're 15 or 18 times faster than a GPU. That means your answers are delivered more quickly. It means your engagement with with the AI is more enjoyable. It means you can use the AI to solve harder problems and not wait.

And the way to think about this is sort of to ask yourself the the counter factual question. How big is the market for slow search today? Right. Right. Is zero. How big is the market for dialup? It's zero. H how long do you wait for a website to resolve before you click away? 3 seconds, 5 seconds. You will not wait for AI. We have to deliver it to you in a in in real time. And that's what we saw. That's what we built.

投资者旅程与流动性话题

So the panel's on going public. A lot of LPs in the room. They need to get liquid. I'm curious about the journey for your investors. Yeah.

Okay. So, well, you guys went public what year? Uh 2021. 2021 by way of a spa. Correct. Okay. And your VCs were who? Um Drake Professor Jverson was one of the earliest Capricorn. Um Peter Te's founders fund. Then we got uh Yuri Milner's DST. Okay. So your your investors come in, you go public at 2 billion via a spa. Now we're four years later. Really, it wasn't until year three or four that 90% of the value was created. Okay. So did those early investors capture this 90% move? Did they stay in it?

早期投资者的持仓策略

Most of them did. Yeah, most of them did, which is really smart on their part. Obviously, I think they should hold on even more. Uh, if I didn't think that, you should. I'm a little bit self-interested. What's interesting about this is No, but really they did. And and I mean, Google hasn't sold a share. They're our largest single investor. Um, uh, Capricorn didn't until very recently. So, basically, most of them stayed really well in and they got all of that upside. And good for them. And the reason I think this is so important is that there are a lot of LPs in this room who they're like when a company goes public give us the shares. No no give us give us the shares. This is a counter example right? This happened to us in 10 years ago. We invested preipo at a billion dollars. We distributed the shares I think at three or four billion and then it went to 50 billion over you know the course of the next 24 months. And we had people who called us who said well why didn't you hold on to the shares? And we're like because you're pounding on us, right, to distribute to the shares. So you're an example. Now

Dribble Lockup 创新

in your case, Andrew, you have an innovation, right? You're just now uh public. So all of your investors are still under lockup like like Altimeter, but you guys have innovated with the banks on what I call a dribble lockup. So over 6 months the shares can be dribbled out according to a bunch of performance hurdles which SpaceX is going to have a very similar when did we start this process of the of the dribble the dribble concept you started it years ago but yes but u we're all of that with respect to the lockup I think this is the the most innovative and I think SpaceX is going to have a very similar innovation but Andrew for your investors if you were talking to ILPs right in the room. Should Alimter be distributing the shares when they come out of lock? How do you think about you know your VCs holding on to the shares kind of post lock?

IPO 之后才是大头

Well, I I I think historically more money's made after IPO than before. Yeah. I I I think every single study shows that uh there is more money to be made both in percentage and in in what we care about which is absolute. Yes.

And and and so uh the amount of money that it's possible to put to work in most venture companies is very modest. I mean there are two or three or five outliers but for the most part you can only put a relatively little bit of money to work. Um by the time we get public there's a lot more money there if things are going well and the opportunity to make vastly more is after IPO not before.

SpaceX 估值难题

Hey, if I could just add on that, one interesting question is what's going to happen with SpaceX on this because you know a lot of the value is is in the future, right? But I mean most of the big tech companies went public at a few billion,

not a few trillion. Like there's a lot of zeros in between those, right? And you got all this upside afterwards. Now, for the equivalent liftoff, SpaceX would have to be aiming at quadrillion valuations. Now, I know Elon has those sort of ambitions, but you really have to believe in that uh to get, you know, this is this is kind of the point I'm getting to, right? We have three mega IPOs, you know, we keep talking about that are multi- trillion. Yeah. All of that value acrewed to private market investors.

钟摆回摆：公开市场的回归

Planet Labs is a great example of venture capital in the public markets where the 10X has occurred in the public markets. We're all advocates of these companies coming public sooner. Had Andrew had his way, he would have been public 18 months ago, probably at $10 billion rather than $50 billion. And that 5x over the course of the last two years would have gone to public market investors. So go ahead.

Way better to be lucky than good. Yeah.

So, so I think that I hear a lot of people thinking that anthropic open AI and SpaceX are the new normal. I actually think the public markets may be shifting back in this direction and a lot of the companies in our portfolios are now thinking about going public at a billion or three billion or 5 billion.

We had this period of a decade where Andrees was really pushing stay private forever and I see the pendulum swinging back to companies are like man I want to be like Planet Labs and get public, right? and and have to play in the big leagues and do it in the public markets like here's what I'll say maybe just like to the two of you guys both of you guys have had enormous pressure because there's visible competition that's always sort of in your periphery but I do think that getting public sooner having the scrutiny of public markets having the scrutiny of having to deliver sharpens the focus it steel sharpens steel iron sharpens iron and I think innovation tends to get better

And so the idea that you allow everybody to participate but you also put yourself in the spotlight to me is where great things happen.

I agree. Um and so anyways I just wanted to say to both of you um just as we wrap you guys are an incredible testament to entrepreneurship both of you. I mean we've been talking literally since day one me and Andrew because we went in different paths and then we kind of recon converged and then Will same with you. I'm happy it worked out for you Jimoth. Well it's worked out for both of us so it's fine. Um you guys are incredible testament to entrepreneurship and uh I just want to say thank you for everything you guys are doing and the next few years are going to be really spicy.

收尾：行星智能（Planetary Intelligence）

Yeah, if I could just spend a 30 seconds on the next few years because I think it's going to be so exciting with um as I mentioned AI and space merging together. We're going to see a take off our applications.

I like to say like all the cool stuff that we're doing on the with LLMs now is really based on just the text of the internet being absorbed into these models which is incredibly powerful already but they don't know about the real world. I call them blind to you know they don't know about that farm field that flood that security situation around the corner. If you give them real world data, then they can answer real world problems.

And that's going to open up gazillions of applications for these uh AI models. I call them instead of having large language models, large earth models or uh instead of AI, planetary intelligence where you have planetary sensing systems in space, planetary compute systems in space. And we can disagree or agree on on exact time frame, but I think it's going to happen. and and then that's going to enable a huge economy. So, it's an exciting time in the next few years.

Will Andrew, thank you guys very much. Well done. Thanks, guys. Okay. Thanks, buddy. Appreciate you. Really appreciate it. Great seeing you, brother. Congrats.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：IPO 本质上是一场"什么都没发生"的烟花秀
听完 Andrew Feldman 的描述，我捕捉到一个被所有人忽略的悖论：**IPO 是科技行业最被神化、却最缺乏实质内容的"成人礼"**。他说的那句话值得反复咀嚼——"第二天早晨你没有多卖出一件产品，工程项目没有任何进展"。
更深一层的洞见是：IPO 的真正功能不是融资，而是**集体心理仪式**。Andrew 提到工程师们第一次穿西装、移民父母含泪出席——这暴露了一个被低估的真相：**在科技公司里，IPO 是中产阶级移民家庭的"现代成人礼"，是一种社会身份合法化的工具**。它和资本市场无关，和宗族认同有关。这也解释了为什么 OpenAI、SpaceX 这类公司即使不缺钱、不缺名声，最终也很难抗拒上市的引力——因为员工和他们的父母需要那个仪式。
## 二、跨领域连接：太空数据中心 = 大型机到 PC 革命的"反向重演"
Will 谈卫星小型化时类比"大型机到桌面电脑"，但他自己可能没意识到一个更深的悖论——**太空数据中心是 IT 历史的反向回放**：
- 1970s-80s：算力从中心化（大型机）走向

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Will AI Crash the Economy? Debunking Citrini – Alex Imas and Phil Trammell
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的"反常识"：丰裕如何"无法"制造衰退

大多数人读Citrini的预测时，会本能地接受"白领失业 → 消费萎缩 → 经济衰退"这一链条，因为它在情感上太顺滑了。但Imas的反驳揭示了一个反直觉的经济学真相：在技术前沿持续扩张的世界里，要"制造"负增长，其实需要极其苛刻、近乎荒谬的条件——资本持有者必须达到一种"硬性饱和"（hard bound），不仅停止消费，还要停止再投资。

这里隐藏着一个令人意外的观察：我们对"AI造成衰退"的恐惧，本质上是在借用1929年大萧条的心理模板。但大萧条发生在技术前沿停滞期，而AGI情境恰恰相反——技术前沿在爆炸式扩张。把萧条逻辑套到奇点上，就像用马车时代的交通法规来规范火箭。两者的相似只是表层的"失业现象"，底层的物理学完全不同。

二、跨领域连接：这本质上是一个"熵"的问题，不是经济问题

让我做一个出人意料的跨域连接——这场辩论的核心，其实和热力学第二定律的逆问题惊人相似。

在一个能量持续注入的开放系统（持续涌入的算力、数据中心投资、AGI生产力），要让总产出下降，你必须人为构造一个"封闭瓶颈"——即资本所有者集体决定"够了，不再投资"。这相当于在一个被持续加热的房间里，要求温度下降。理论上可能（如果窗户全开向太空），但需要一个极不自然的边界条件。

这也解释了为什么Citrini的叙事在社交媒体上病毒式传播：人类对"系统崩溃"的故事有天然的认知偏好（参见Kahneman的"可得性启发"和叙事经济学Shiller的研究）。一个"AI让大家失业 → 经济崩溃"的故事，比"AI让资本疯狂再投资 → 增长加速但分配恶化"的故事更有故事性，但后者才是更符合系统动力学的预测。

三、最深的洞见：这场争论真正的"真问题"被掩盖了

最让我意外的，是这段对话中没有明说但呼之欲出的一点：Imas和Phil实际上是在用"经济总量"的讨论，掩盖一个更尖锐的分配问题。

注意Imas的措辞："财富和收入从劳动者向科技资本所有者的再分配"。这才是真正的炸弹——经济总量可能不仅不衰退，反而暴涨，但中位数劳动者的处境可能像底特律的汽车工人一样塌方。Citrini错在用"GDP衰退"这个错误指标包装了一个正确的担忧；而Imas技术上正确地反驳了GDP预测，但读者很容易因此误以为"AI对普通人无害"。

这里有个跨领域的类比值得深思：这就像19世纪英国的圈地运动——总财富剧增，技术前沿暴扩，但大量自耕农被抛入贫民窟。当时的经济学家如果只看"国民总产出"，会得出"圈地运动是繁荣"的结论。真正的悲剧从来不在加总指标里，而在加总指标的方差里。

所以这场Dwarkesh的剪辑实际上完成了一次议程的悄悄转移：从"AI会不会让普通人受苦"（难答），转向了"AI会不会让GDP下降"（好答）。辩论的胜负，常常取决于谁能选定问题的形式。

📝 转录文本

引入：Citrini的病毒式预测

There there's this very viral uh scenario planning about the future by uh Citrine where they're predicting as a result of automation, as a result of very powerful AI, there will be a recession because white-collar workers will get automated. Uh their salaries, which were, you know, paying for a bunch of things, will no longer be available and so there will be a slump.

Do you want to recapitulate why this might be implausible?

一、可信与不可信的部分：失业 vs. 负增长

Well, I mean so part of it is plausible, part of it's not not plausible. So like the the part that's kind of like within the — This is something that we started the conversation with — is the idea that there could be unemployment, a lot of unemployment, as if if the speed of automation is quick and things like that. People could get laid off and they may not find work very uh very quickly.

So that part of this Citrine essay about the unemployment, you know, we we can quibble about that, but that's that's not the issue. The issue is that they talked about negative economic growth.

Right.

二、负增长所需的苛刻条件

And so what I did in the in the piece that actually Phil and I had a back and forth on was to say: like let's start with the with the proposition that there's negative economic growth. What conditions do you need on the economy to get negative economic growth?

And it turns out the conditions are pretty improbable. So one thing that you need is like for uh the the holders of capital — like rich people basically — like basically what you have in this in those sorts of scenarios, like you have a reallocation of wealth and income from like lower-income people who are working, who are using their labor, towards tech capital owners.

So what you need is that basically demand to be bound. Like a hard bound. Not even like a soft sort of like diminishing sensitivity. You need for them to eventually say, "I've had enough. I don't want to spend any more money." And for that money to not enter as investment.

Right.

Right. Which is like — And then you can get negative growth.

三、奇点世界中"停止投资"的荒谬性

Which is like — And the crucial thing is even if we don't want more — the the world in which there's a singularity and we don't want to invest more money is crazy, right? Where we're not like, "Let's build more data centers. Let's build more fabs." Even though we have AGI, we're not like investing in more data centers to run the AGI. And that's like driving more economic growth.

Yeah.

四、与Phil的往返：场景规划的真正价值

And so I I sent the essay to Phil, and Phil actually wrote back being like, "This is pretty dumb." Like my essay. Saying like, "You're — You're trying to say that there's going to be negative economic growth, but they're — These are very implausible conditions."

And I was like, "Actually, that's the point of the essay — that these are very implausible economic conditions."

So, that's where I think like scenario planning really shines: you have the central thesis of the essay, which I think is like — I I think it was great that it's written because it kind of started a conversation, but it's just like you need — It — It's so intuitive this idea that like, "Look, if there's demand collapse, we can get the economy to shrink."

五、与大萧条的关键区别：技术前沿的扩张

But it's actually you could get that with a depression, right? Where in a — In the depression, the technological frontier didn't expand.

Right.

Here, the technological frontier is expanding. You actually have abundance. And for abundance to generate negative economic growth, that's really hard to capture.

Right.

结尾

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

## 一、被忽视的"反常识"：丰裕如何"无法"制造衰退
大多数人读Citrini的预测时，会本能地接受"白领失业 → 消费萎缩 → 经济衰退"这一链条，因为它在情感上太顺滑了。但Imas的反驳揭示了一个**反直觉的经济学真相**：在技术前沿持续扩张的世界里，要"制造"负增长，其实需要极其苛刻、近乎荒谬的条件——资本持有者必须达到一种"硬性饱和"（hard bound），不仅停止消费，还要停止再投资。
