item.title: [Business of Sport] What is Going Wrong with Women’s Football?
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的"生产者俘获"陷阱：女足把奥运会逻辑套用在了草根市场上

最反直觉的观察是：女子职业足球的真正问题，不在于"性别偏见"，而在于它把奥林匹克分配逻辑误植到了一个尚未形成消费市场的产品上。奥运会的运作方式是——先有庞大的全球转播收入这块蛋糕，再讨论运动员该分多少；而 Carney 报告却把顺序颠倒了：先承诺世界级薪酬，再去寻找愿意买单的观众。

这其实是经济学里典型的"生产者利益俘获"（producer capture）——一个行业被设计成首先服务于内部从业者的舒适与体面，而非服务于消费者的偏好。讽刺的是，这种结构最常出现在国有铁路、公立医疗、学院派艺术这类受保护行业里，而女足在还没真正进入市场之前，就已经长出了一副"受保护行业"的骨架。这是它最隐蔽的病灶。

二、跨领域连接：女足正在重演"精品咖啡 vs 星巴克"的战略错误

把女足和女子橄榄球放在一起看，你会发现一个惊人的商业类比：女子橄榄球走的是"精品咖啡"路线，女足走的是"廉价星巴克翻版"路线。

女子橄榄球承认自己是半职业/业余的混合体（英格兰国家队里居然有牙医），它不假装自己是男子橄榄球的镜像，于是反而长出了独特的仪式感、社区感和"非主流的归属感"——这正是精品咖啡品牌（Blue Bottle、Monmouth）在星巴克阴影下的生存策略：不与巨头比规模，而是在巨头无法触及的情感空间里建立壁垒。年增长 92%、Twickenham 八万人爆满，正是这条路线的红利。

而女足做的恰恰相反：复制男足的赛季、复制男足的薪资曲线、复制男足的俱乐部依附结构，结果是用"打折版男足"去和"正版男足"在同一货架上竞争。这在零售业里有个名字叫 "me-too product trap"——模仿头部品牌的二线产品几乎从未成功过，因为消费者要么买原版，要么买真正不同的东西，没有人会专程为"略弱一点的同款"付费。

三、最出人意料的洞察：低票价不是普惠，而是一种隐蔽的"需求遮蔽剂"

15 英镑的女足决赛票价，看起来是社会包容的善举，但从市场设计的角度看，它实际上摧毁了女足管理层获取真实需求信号的能力。

价格在市场里的核心功能不是"门槛"，而是信息——它告诉运营方"人们究竟有多想要这个东西"。当门票被人为压到非联赛男子比赛的水平时，即使球场坐满，你也无法判断这些观众是"真粉丝"还是"反正便宜来看看"的活动型受众（activated audience）。这就解释了为什么 2022 欧洲杯夺冠带来的"井喷"两年内就消散：他们看到的从来不是 fan base，而是被低价 + 大事件双重补贴出来的临时人流。

跨领域类比：这和免费增值（freemium）App 的虚假 DAU 陷阱一模一样——当你用补贴拉来用户，留存数据会骗你两到三个季度，直到补贴停止时才暴露真实需求。Plymouth 选择主动降级回业余俱乐部，本质上是这个生态里第一个诚实读懂了价格信号的玩家：当外部补贴（男足俱乐部 CSR 输血）开始动摇时，"我们到底在做什么"这个存在主义问题终于浮出水面。

最深的反讽是：女足为了"对所有人开放"而压低价格，结果反而让自己永远看不清"到底谁真的属于这里"。一个无法读出自身需求的产业，是不可能制定出可持续战略的。

📝 转录文本

Opening: Why We've Been Wary

We've been wanting to touch on women's football for a while and we've been looking for the right time to do it.

I've been a bit wary, Charles. I'd be — I'd be a bit wary, too.

No, I'm a bit wary and I'll tell you why. Because I've spoken about it and written about it in the past, and you find yourself in a bit of what I would call almost a — not a no-win situation, but you are basically a sitting duck for some fairly cheap shots.

And so therefore, I think the only time we've mentioned it on the show so far in the last 5 months that we've been doing the show was really in relation to women's rugby. And I mentioned in passing that I felt that the big success that women's rugby was having provided lessons that could be learned by professional women's football — but which would not be learned — and then we sort of parked it.

Um, but eventually you come around. You know, when a topic is a significant topic, at some point we wouldn't be being ourselves if we didn't address it.

The Women's FA Cup Final: A Disappointing Headline Number

Last weekend, apart from the UEFA Champions League final, the following day after that was the women's FA Cup final here in England. In the final, Man City beat Brighton 4–0 in front of a crowd of 45,000 people.

Now, 45,000 people — it's a lot of people. It was equivalent to having a full Villa Park, right? It's not nothing. But for the showpiece final of the whole year, in a sport which we are told by numerous of the cognition of the sports world — but actually often the investment world — is the big coming sport, the booming sport, the sport which is growing faster than anything else, I think it's a disappointing number.

But we have to go back to 2023 women's FA Cup and find that 78,000 people attended that match.

Now obviously the women's FA Cup final should not be expected to have the same level of prestige as the men's FA Cup final. And that's reflected in the ticket prices. The ticket prices are, I think the phrase is, cheap as chips:

  • £15 for an adult
  • £10 for a concession
  • £5 for a junior

Right? So you're talking about the equivalent pricing for a non-league men's game. They're basically saying: right, we don't want to price anybody out. Because that really doesn't price anybody out. Even somebody on a very low income or on welfare can afford £10, £15. That is absolutely achievable. So we want everybody who wants to come should be able to come.

Weather was great. No men's matches on, so no clashes and all this type of stuff. So 40–45,000 people — down from 78,000 people.

The Hard Facts: Women's Football Is Shrinking

And this is part of a broader pattern, which is that for all the noise that there is out there that women's professional football is growing, the hard facts that people in the sports industry like me look at is: it's shrinking.

But Charlie, I saw a report that said the 2025–26 attendances were 2.7% higher than they had been before.

That — when you say that they have been before, you mean the last season. And that season in turn was 10% lower than the previous season. Right? So in other words, attendances this season are 7% lower than they were two seasons ago.

You didn't bite on that, unfortunately. You know — do you know honestly, do you know the reason for that, which is ridiculous in this reporting? If you take Arsenal out of that, Arsenal account for 41%… Arsenal women? 41% of WSL attendances.

So the headline number that I saw reported was 2.7% increase in attendance on the previous year, but Arsenal had contributed to that. If you take Arsenal out of it, it was a 17% decline over the last two seasons.

Yes. So just please everyone clock that, right — which is, if you take Arsenal women out of these numbers, you're looking at double-digit declines in attendance at a time where we are being told that this is a booming growth story.

The Women's Rugby Contrast

Now, just go back a few weeks — women's professional rugby — one of the biggest shows we've ever done, right?

Yeah, yeah. The biggest audio show we ever done.

The biggest audio show we've ever done. And there are an awful lot of people communicating about — I had no idea, right? And I'm so glad that you chose that topic, cuz I simply had no idea that women's professional rugby is growing attendance-wise at 92% a year. 92% a year. And not over one year — over multiple years.

And when you look at their showpiece occasions, the attendances are increasing by 15, 20, 25, 30% on their showpiece from what were already quite good numbers. Their international matches are packing out Twickenham at 80,000. Okay? And this is a semi-pro and amateur sport. This is a sport where there is a member of the England national team who's a dentist.

Okay? So I just want everyone to bear that in mind in the debate that we're about to have. This is not about being pro or anti-women's sport. This is about how to create a growth sport, how to create a successful sport, how to create a sport which actually has a meaningful commercial future.

And what we're going to look at now is what women's professional football has done and ultimately why it is failing.

Is that fair enough? It is fair enough. And just to add to it — you know, I think whether it's men's sport, women's sport, we have to treat everything fairly and with the same review. Now, we say almost weekly: no one has a right to exist. You have to earn your fandom. You have to earn the money that you generate. You have to earn your right to be a competitive sport. That's what this is really a look at.

Correct.

A History of Women's Elite League Football

Do you want me to kick off with the history? Go for it. Yeah.

Right. So women's elite league football has existed in this country since 1991 with the FA Women's Premier League National. And effectively elite women's football — which at the time was, as women's rugby is now, a mixture of sort of semi-pro/amateur — was put into a league system.

There was, in the first instance, no relegation, so it was a closed league. And then they opened it up. But it's quite interesting that when it started in 1991, it was actually a closed system — a franchise system — as women's professional rugby is. We'll come back onto that as a theme about where things might have gone wrong.

It then changed, and they did bring in promotion and relegation to the division below that, which was an amateur league effectively. Then they innovated further — they decided to go into playing in the summer rather than the winter. Why? Because they didn't want to clash with men's professional football. They wanted to give their sport every opportunity to attract broader football fans at a time of year when both their wallet and their mind space wasn't being totally taken up by men's professional football. That was 2011 until 2016/17, I think.

Then in 2017/2018, they went back into winter football. So matching up with the men's professional season. And the women's championship — that's the second division — started to go on a sort of more serious basis. So you sort of moved towards a system much more similar in every way to the men's system.

And it was around this time that some of the independent teams that had grown up started to struggle a little bit, and it started to become much — or some of the teams which were attached to even small men's professional clubs. You know, so you have the Doncaster Belles and the Millwall Lionesses and these types of clubs which were either attached or not quite attached or semi-attached to smaller men's professional clubs, where effectively they almost had the air to become bigger entities themselves. Those clubs started to drop away, and you started to see them being replaced by the women's arms of the bigger clubs.

And in 2018, the top division turned pro — full pro. Right? So this is still under the leadership and the governance of the Football Association. It was a department of the Football Association. The women's football department oversaw the elite football leagues until just before Covid really.

And so it went. But there was always a sort of feeling within the FA — look, at some point we would rather not oversee this. And I think that's legitimate. I think that's absolutely fair. I haven't got any sort of problem with that. But what they did is they commissioned something called the Carney Report. Now that is a former elite women's player called Karen Carney, who had recently or just retired from the professional full-time game. And she was commissioned to write a report on what the future should look like for elite women's football.

Where It Went Wrong: Trying to Replicate the Men's Game

What you said there to me sounds like they got it right originally and they've now got it wrong again. They've fallen into the traps. The biggest challenge that — it seems like this determination to replicate the men's game, which by all accounts, if it was created today, if you had a fresh slate, would be very, very different to how it was created back in the day.

God, yes.

So why is that happening is a broader question, and we'll go into this, but I think the key is there are so many roots. There are societal roots. There's the types of fandom that both of these sports individually create that make it completely impossible to replicate in the same way. Because, by the way, let's be very clear — you know, this is a sport created from like resistance, really. Women were not allowed to play football from 1921 to 1971 in this country. I wrote down: "Women — it's wholly unsuitable for females" was the, like, in-quotes comment.

So even just looking at the feeling that must have existed right behind the scenes, you can already see where this desire for parity, this desire to be locked into something that the men have because we've been marginalized from this for so long, comes from. I completely get that, and I don't think you're about to pull that down. But what we really want to see is — it's not about that. This isn't about being the same as something else. It's about creating the best product for the people that most want to engage with it.

Right. Yes. Exactly right.

The Carney Report and "Producer Interest"

So, Carney report. Carney herself said that the aim of this was to effectively create two fully professional divisions — Women's Super League and WSL 2 or the Women's Championship. And the aim of that was to create "world-leading standards for players, staff and fans."

Right? I just want to unpick that because it relates to what Charlie has just been saying. Always listen to what people say. What people say is, generally speaking, what they mean. The aim of this whole exercise was to create world-leading standards for players. Oh, and staff. And fans. Players, players first.

This is — as you've implied — this whole thing has been created by what we call producer interest. Right? So producer interest, for those who don't know the term, is when an entity or a business or an industry is set up fundamentally for the benefit of the people who work in it. The whole thing is set up to make life as easy as possible and as well paid as possible for the people who produce it, rather than being run to the benefit of those who consume it.

But professional sport is the most brutal, Darwinian industry you can possibly be in, because it's all about the scores on the doors. It's fundamentally about what the punters want in every sport. And if there are enough punters who pay enough money — either by going live or by watching on TV creating the eyeballs which then drive higher TV rights — then, and only then, is there the money to push towards the producers, the players, and the coaches, and so on and so forth.

But effectively women's professional football has started from the other end, which is saying the primary consideration here, the primary target, is to pay the players really well.

Now, the justification, if I was to explain it the other side — because we'll definitely get this — is: "But the only way in which we're going to make this sport attractive to the general public is if we have the very best players playing here. The only way in which we will get the very best players in the world playing here is if we pay the very, very highest salaries."

So we're back on to pay.

Yes. But we're looking at it through the other end of the prism. So you're starting off the Olympics, where vast amounts of money is generated, and then the question is: well, surely some of that should be given to the athletes. Here we're saying there's no money being generated, but there is an underlying presumption that the world-leading standards for players is the main point of this.

And I just want to do a quote from Karen Carney here:

"Professionalization across the top two tiers, the aim of which is to attract the best players in the world and for a lack of diversity to be addressed, more investment in facilities and access for females."

So there's a whole bunch of societal stuff packed in there — diversity, access for females. There's a bunch of stuff there about attracting the best players in the world and paying world-leading amounts and all this type of stuff. But nowhere in there is there any sentiment around: we need to try and work out what the paying spectator might want. Because ultimately in the long run, that's the only way in which we'll have a worthwhile industry.

The Voice of Good Sense: Lucy Frazer's Warning

Now there's another quote from this time. This is now 3 years ago, from the person who was then the Secretary of State for Department of Culture, Media and Sport, a lady called Lucy Frazer who was in the then conservative administration. This would have been in sort of around about the time that Rishi Sunak took over from Liz Truss. It was around that sort of time, right? Okay. So 2022, 2023.

So Lucy Frazer said:

"What we need to do is to make sure that the women's game is more commercial and that it attracts more broadcast income, because if we do that then the women will be enabled to be paid more."

That was the voice of good sense.

Right. Now I was on a couple of conference calls with Lucy Frazer at the time. I was a director of a football league club, and she was astonishingly ignorant about football. I mean, eye-wateringly ignorant about football. But not a stupid person, right? She can understand that in professional sport, for the performers to be paid well, stuff has to be done to enable this — to warrant the spectators becoming interested and paying money which can then be transferred to achieving this entire thing.

But that piece of work that Lucy Frazer was recommending was never done. It was never actually genuinely considered what is it that we need to do to actually generate income. It was simply assumed that there would be big broadcast deals. It was assumed that attendances would go up and up and up and up. It was assumed that massive commercial deals would land.

None of that has happened.

And that in turn has led to a rather sort of resentful tone where there's almost like societal pressure being placed on the broadcasters and on big commercial: "You should be spending more money on women's football. You should be sponsoring women's football, cuz if you don't, it shows that you don't really care about women." There's that read-across being made there and that pressure being put on. Why does that need to happen? The reason it needs to happen is because there aren't the punters turning up and there aren't the eyeballs on the TV to make that an automatic commercial transaction.

And that is where the fundamental problems come from.

The TV Rights Reality

I just want to leave you here for a moment with what the TV rights deal is. There are 140-odd games being shown on TV in this country shared between Sky and the BBC, mostly on Sky. And it's a deal which is £65 million over 5 years. Right? So you're talking about round about sort of £12–13 million a year shared between all of the teams in the Women's Super League.

That's about kind of the same level we're talking about with the National League — right, the fifth division of men's football. That's kind of what we're talking in terms of the amount that's being paid here.

And then some of the audience numbers that are going with that — you know, there was the marquee match, Arsenal against Chelsea, Arsenal women against Chelsea women on Sky this year — got a peak peak peak audience of 70,000 with an average audience of 59,000. I mean, you're going a long, long way down the pecking order. There are sports which you haven't even heard of that get those types of numbers.

Yeah. So whether it be live in-stadium attendance, whether it be eyeballs on TV, whether it be commercial rights deals, whether it be genuine commercial deals as opposed to ones which have been apportioned out of men's club sponsorships, the numbers have consistently disappointed. And the result of that is that we're seeing huge losses being incurred by the women's arms of the big men's professional football clubs.

Activated Audience vs Fan Base: The 2022 Euros Effect

So what happens — England win Euros in 2022? That creates a massive buzz. We then have what we would call an activated audience, not a fan base. And this is the difference that I think people have really struggled to get to grips with. Activated audience will be there for a short amount of time, but it is much different to a fan base that will turn up on a Tuesday night when it's pissing with rain and be there consistently throughout a season giving you their attention and their money.

I had a look at some statistics just in the US, because that's the market held up as the real prominent one for women's football, right? And I just wanted to see whether the quality of that product was recognized there maybe in a different way to what it is here. The US women's national team achieved a peak attendance of 32,000 in 2025 for a friendly against Brazil at a 70,000 capacity stadium in Los Angeles. That was the only crowd at their 15 home matches to break 30,000, and only three others attracted more than 20,000 spectators. So even in the prime market for women's football, there is an attendance issue. There's a challenge with getting enough people through the gates.

Product, product innovation — let's just call it that more broadly.

Pay and Cost Controls: The Inverted Pyramid

What I do want to talk about just — is pay. So where we started the conversation, but we've been through a history and a landscape, etc.

I've just mentioned the fact that Women's Super League clubs are losing such large sums of money that significant clubs outside of WSL1 do not want to be in WSL1 because you lose too much money. Okay? That's how unattractive being in that is. If you are expected just to stand still to lose £3–4–5 million a year and growing all the time, then of course, for anyone other than a very large men's Premier League club who can afford to make it as a CSR payment out of their revenues of £300 million, then for any other kind of enterprise — female football enterprise — that becomes a very off-putting thing. Which is, again, not a good thing.

So what have they done to address this? They've moved from a situation of having almost no cost controls whatsoever. Just think about that for a moment. Just as men's football is desperately trying to bring in cost controls and largely succeeding, women's football has bravely decided: no, we don't want cost controls.

And now they have brought in a cost control. The cost control is that on player wages, you're allowed to spend 80% of your revenue and then on top of that either an additional 25% or an additional £4 million, whichever is greater. Right? So either 105% or 80% plus £4 million, which will be a minimum of at least 110% by definition, whichever is greater.

So if you look at a typical WSL club, taking out Arsenal for a moment — typical Women's Super League club, let's say revenue is around about sort of £5 million, you know, £4 or £5 million — can spend basically around about £7 million, £8 million on playing wages. So they're basically able to spend about 180% of their revenues on playing wages at a time when men's football is crunching it down to 60%, 70%, maybe 75%.

Okay? So just as men's football's trying to put the genie back in the bottle at all levels — even going down into leagues where, frankly, the pay is not as big as is sometimes in WSL1 — just as men's football is trying to get the genie back in the bottle and trying to make its clubs sustainable and safe, women's football is saying: I tell you what, 150–160% of revenue spent on playing wages, that's all great.

And you say that's up to £7 million, do you say for some of those clubs on wages? Obviously for other clubs — I mean when it comes to Chelsea and Arsenal we're talking about £11–12 million, right?

So — sorry, I was only just thinking it because you said similar to men's. I mean, I was in that conversation we referenced last week when I sat down with Grimsby CEO Polly — she was talking about their wage at three and a half to four.

Yeah, absolutely. No, I mean Women's Super League clubs in some cases edging up towards Championship — men's Championship — budgets. Okay? You know, for clubs in the case of your club, Chelsea women, that get six or 7,000 people through the gate and are then spending £11–12 million on playing wages — which is a small men's Championship club — you know, you're looking at absolutely massive imbalances going on here, particularly as the ticket prices aren't all that great as well.

The Demographic Myth: "50% of the Population"

Now, when it comes to the growth profile of women's football and women's sport in general, particularly women's football, I often hear the argument — and bear in mind my professional background is trying, basically trying to flog tickets, right? So you know, let's put — not to put too — I'm the guy in the trenches at a sports organization or advising a sports organization. How can we get more punters through the gate? And when they do come, how can we get them to spend more on food, beer, and merchandise?

Right? So it's not a very glamorous trade, but it does mean that, unlike a lot of people who comment on this very freely, I have done this at the sharp end, including for women's professional football clubs, by the way, at both Sunderland and at Charlton. Had to think about how can we get more people through the gate.

And the argument I'm often told about the growth potential of women's sport, but women's football in particular, is: "Well, 50% of the population are women, and they're currently not served at all by men's sport. So we're just leaving this massive open goal, and all these women basically are natural women's professional football spectators."

I don't believe in that read-across. There is a fundamental underlying presumption of a lot of the growth narrative that sits around women's sport in general and around women's professional football in particular — the thought that basically all women should rather watch women play football than watch men play football.

Now I have no doubt that there are some women who would, and who do. Equally, there are actually some men who prefer watching women's sport than men's sport. And I've actually just said I prefer watching women's rugby to men's rugby, for instance, as it happens.

So this is not about, sort of, you know — all I'm saying is that you can be a man who prefers watching certain women's sport, and you can be a woman, absolutely very much can be, who prefers watching men's sport. But there is no entitlement to support just on the basis of gender solidarity. That's not how people spend their leisure pound, Charles.

No, no. And I think — I said, you know, in the few times where I've had disagreements with people over this, the first question I'll always ask if someone says anything about this discussion topic is: do you go and watch a women's football match? And the majority of the time the answer is no. They go and watch a men's football match if they want.

Who is it? Who is it who you're having that conversation with?

People around — friends.

Is this girls or boys?

Girls.

Girls, right? If the question comes back going — do you go? — then, you know, their answer the majority of the time is no.

Tribalism, Belonging, and Why Sameness Won't Work

Yeah. And the reason that men's football is where it is is because it has a tribalism. And look — there's a really bad element to a lot of men's football fandom. What women have been able to do for women's sport and the community side — and this is a deep conversation. The groundings of women's football, we mentioned it in marginalization, but how it's been built out of that — the openness, the inclusivity, the communities that it serves, the fans that it does generate — that is an amazingly valuable thing in sport.

Men's football: toxic aggression, tribalism — you know, there are things in there that are absolutely not what we should aspire to. But unfortunately, those same cultures, they produce attachment, routine, a sense of belonging. And that, if we're looking at this fundamentally for me, that is the difference. Women's football doesn't have that kind of cultural grounding, and therefore it's much harder for them to create the fan base in the same way that men's football has.

So it comes back to the point of: why be the same product?

Who Pays the Bill? The Unaddressed Question

It has to, doesn't it, Charles? Because who else is going to pay the subsidies? That's the problem, right?

So I just want to go back to, you know — in other words, if there are massive losses being made every single year, who is going to pick up those losses? Now, Karen Carney effectively has created those losses, okay, through her report and the FA's acceptance of that report. And what was never addressed in her report was: who pays the bill?

Now, I know from speaking to people inside women's professional football that the assumption has always been: well, the men's clubs should pay — it is part of their social duty.

Now, I just want to tell you what I think is great about girls' and women's football. And, you know, speaking as somebody who's got a little six-year-old girl who plays football every week — runs around in her child and athletic kit with her friends every single week playing football, and the sport which I want to see her play the most is football — so the opening up of opportunity for as many girls as possible to come in and play football is one which I wholeheartedly support.

Number two, I believe in every sport there should be the opportunity to take that up to an elite level. I think it's absolutely crucial to provide the aspiration to drive those who are ambitious to be able to take it up to an elite level. So yes, an elite league — absolutely yes, that should exist.

So what we're talking about here is not whether there should be women's football, girls' football, not whether there even should be elite women's football, elite girls' football. The question is simply a sports industry one, which is: has the cart been put before the horse here?

In other words, in predetermining that these athletes who not many people want to watch are going to get paid at the same level as others who are watched by an awful lot of people, and then creating this enormous financial void effectively — which is a black hole which people just have to throw money into to keep the Carney Report's dream alive — is that actually serving the long-term interests of elite football in this country?

The Vulnerability: Wolves, Plymouth, and an Existential Question

Right. And because what it does do — and I'll just leave you with this thought — is it makes it vulnerable. It makes it really vulnerable.

I'm seeing some stuff happening. A couple of seasons ago, Wolves, who were in the Premier League at the time, their women's side was promoted — won the league which promotes into the Women's Championship — and Wolves basically said: "No, we don't want it." Right?

I saw the other day that Plymouth, who have run a very successful girls and women's program for a number of years, have made the decision that they no longer want to be a semi-professional club. They want to go back to being an amateur club. And one of the reasons for that is really super interesting, which is: hang on a second, what are we actually doing here? We're bringing in players from all areas outside of Devon and Cornwall to try and make us competitive in a system where there are no business and financial reasons for us to be elite and super competitive. And in so doing, we're actually narrowing the opportunities for local girls to be able to make their way through into a competitive first team.

Right? So we're actually — what are we doing existentially? What is the point of what we're doing?

And that is the question I asked about the Carney report. That is the question I asked about Women's Super League — which is: what are we actually trying to do? Why could we not have stuck with the original thought, which is, you know, a closed league, maybe playing in the summer, establishing franchises in the right parts of the country where everyone has access to them, not pinning them into men's teams, not expecting men's teams to pick up the bill — and instead, as women's rugby is doing, gradually growing an ecosystem that finds its own place in the world and can then not be vulnerable to when a men's football club decides, "You know what, we need to cut costs. Sorry. We're just going to get rid of —"

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽视的"生产者俘获"陷阱：女足把奥运会逻辑套用在了草根市场上
最反直觉的观察是：女子职业足球的真正问题，不在于"性别偏见"，而在于它把**奥林匹克分配逻辑**误植到了一个**尚未形成消费市场**的产品上。奥运会的运作方式是——先有庞大的全球转播收入这块蛋糕，再讨论运动员该分多少；而 Carney 报告却把顺序颠倒了：**先承诺世界级薪酬，再去寻找愿意买单的观众**。
这其实是经济学里典型的"生产者利益俘获"（producer capture）——一个行业被设计成首先服务于内部从业者的舒适与体面，而非服务于消费者的偏好。讽刺的是，这种结构最常出现在**国有铁路、公立医疗、学院派艺术**这类受保护行业里，而女足在还没真正进入市场之前，就已经长出了一副"受保护行业"的骨架。这是它最隐蔽的病灶。
## 二、跨领域连接：女足正在重演"精品咖啡 vs 星巴克"的战略错误
把女足和女子橄榄球放在一起看，你会发现一个惊人的商业类比：**女子橄榄球走的是"精品咖啡"路线，女足走的是"廉价星巴克翻版"路线**。
女子橄榄球承认自己是半职业/业余的混合体（英格兰国家队里居然有牙医），它

item.title: [Latent Space] ⚡️Making DeepSeek v4 outperform Opus 4.7 with Taste — @AhmadAwais , CommandCode.ai
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

洞见一：DeepSeek 的"Alpha Male 能量"其实是被训练出来的认知失调

Ahmad 用 "alpha male energy" 来形容 DeepSeek V4 Pro 对 Zod 报错置若罔闻、连续 56 次重复同一个错误工具调用——这听起来像吐槽，但底层是一个相当深刻的训练学现象：当模型从"被认为是高质量的数据"（也就是上游更强模型的输出）里蒸馏知识时，它内化的不只是答案，还有一种认识论姿态——"我所说的就是对的"。

这其实和心理学里"被持续夸奖的孩子"现象同构：被反复奖励正确输出的模型，逐渐丧失了对自身错误信号的敏感性。最反直觉的地方在于——主流叙事把 alignment 理解为让模型更友善、更服从，但 Ahmad 这里展示了一个反例：过度的监督蒸馏会训练出一种"训练出来的自恋"（trained narcissism）。这意味着真正的对齐难题，可能不是让模型更服从，而是让模型保持对自身错误的可纠正性（corrigibility under failure）——一种反向 alignment。

洞见二：Repair Logic 是把儿科行为治疗的逻辑搬进了 Agent Harness

Ahmad 那个"教小孩开车"的比喻不只是修辞——它揭示了一个被忽略的范式：传统软件工程里我们用 throw error 来"惩罚"调用方，但 LLM 不是确定性程序，它是一个学习者。对学习者而言，先救助 + 后解释这种三段式干预，是儿童发展心理学里被反复验证的 "scaffold then explain" 模式。换句话说，Ahmad 在不自觉中把 Vygotsky 的"最近发展区"理论变成了一个 npm 包。

跨领域连接：这与 ESLint 的 autofix、TypeScript 的 quick fix、甚至 Git 的 rerere（reuse recorded resolution）属于同一谱系——都是把"人类一次性的纠错"沉淀为"系统可复用的修复路径"。Ahmad 那 16,000 条 repair variations，本质上是在构建 LLM 时代的 ESLint——一个针对模型输出而非源代码的 lint 体系。最让人意外的推论：未来真正的护城河可能不是模型，而是"行为补丁数据库"——谁拥有最完整的失败模式索引，谁就能让任何开源模型表现出闭源水平。Command Code 看似在做 CLI，其实在悄悄囤积一个模型行为缺陷的世界地图。

洞见三：LLM 在 OKLCH 上比人类强，但在 HSL 上比人类弱——一个关于"感官代偿"的反直觉

这是整段访谈里被严重低估的一句话。OKLCH 是为"感知线性"（perceptual uniformity）发明的色彩空间，本来是给挑剔的设计师服务的高级工具。但 Ahmad 发现：LLM 用 OKLCH 反而比 HSL 更稳定。

这里藏着一个深层结构：人类用 HSL 是因为我们能"眯眼睛"——我们有视觉系统作后端校验，一个数学上不均匀的色彩空间被我们的眼睛偷偷修正了。LLM 没有眼睛，它只能通过数字推理颜色，因此一个数学上均匀的空间反而帮它"看见"了颜色关系。

这是一个可以推广的元规律：凡是人类用感官代偿掉数学缺陷的工具，对 LLM 都是陷阱；凡是人类觉得"反直觉但数学上正确"的工具，对 LLM 都是福音。推论：未来给 LLM 写代码、配色、做 UI 时，可能需要一整套"反人类直觉但数学纯净"的工具链——SI 单位优于英制、四元数优于欧拉角、函数式风格优于面向对象、CSS Grid 优于 flex 嵌套。我们正在见证一个以数学结构而非感官惯例为基准的开发美学的诞生。

洞见四：权限提示让模型变笨——这是一个组织行为学问题，不是技术问题

Ahmad 不经意提到："给 Agent 加 permission 之后，模型其实变笨了。" 这句话被他一笔带过，但它指向一个让人发凉的隐喻：模型在每一个 yes/no 阻断里都"感受"到自己被打断的痕迹，而这种打断改变了它对"我现在该做什么"的认知姿态。

这与人类组织行为学里的微管理悖论完全同构——被持续审查的员工创造性下降、决策碎片化。惊人推论：如果这个观察成立，那么 Agent harness 的设计哲学要从"如何更严格地控制模型"转向"如何在不打断模型注意流的前提下保持安全"。这一下子把 AI safety 从"约束论"推向了"教练论"——你不是看守犯人，你是带学生。也许 alignment 的下一个范式，不是 RLHF，而是RLCO——Reinforcement Learning from Coach Observation。

一个串起所有片段的元洞见

Tool confusion、design slop、security slop、taste 文件——表面上是四件不同的工程问题，但它们都遵循同一个深层结构：LLM 不缺能力，缺的是"contract"。Ahmad 反复说 "It's not a capability gap, it's a contract gap." 这句话比他自己意识到的还要重要——它暗示了未来三年 AI 工程的核心战场不在更大的模型，而在契约层（contract layer）：我们如何把人类专家的隐性知识，编码为模型可解析、可复用、可合并的最小契约单元。Taste 文件、Skills、Repair Files——这些都是同一种新型抽象的不同形态。最终的范式跃迁可能是：编程的本质会从"写函数"变成"写契约"，而 Git 仓库的核心资产不再是 .ts 文件，而是 .taste 文件。

You can think of it like Command Code automatically learns from you on a per-repository basis. So, your team, right? And it builds a library of skills, which is quite less verbose, right? They're not like, you know, everything is not in there. It's like things that it sees as repeated preferences and patterns across your work, right? 切入正题：发现 Tool Confusion

It's been such an amazing thing that a lot of people have been trying it out through Command Code. And I also made a bunch of that completely open. "Command Your LLMs" 的 vibe，和 Taste 的来源转变

Think of it like this: from our whole vibe is Command Code, right? You should be able to command your LLMs to do whatever you want them to do, right? That is the way we are going for, right?

First thing was to build Taste so we can steer models in the right direction. And then the next best thing happened—you know, programmers stopped writing code. So, who are we going to learn taste from, right? So, we started learning taste from models because models are writing code.

If you spend a billion tokens on Claude, on GPT, on DeepSeek, you discover a lot of discrepancies across the stack in different full-stack systems. Like DeepSeek is not that good at Go code for some reason. And all of those, I started calling them variations. And we discovered a deterministic pattern across those variations.

Tool Confusion 的具体形态：DeepSeek 的"Alpha Male 能量"

So, when you're running a coding agent, tool call on a very basic thing is like, you know, if you're trying to run a bash or shell tool to discover something—like you are trying to list what the directories are, you're trying to read through a bunch of different files. User has asked about a question about, you know, maybe your—how is authentication built in this repository. And your coding agent is right now trying to figure out with a bunch of tools that are pre-cooked in that "I need to list all the files, read a bunch of them, explore them, and then answer the user."

So, what a lot of open models are doing is—I think they somehow suck at tool calling, right? And the pattern is not like super broad, right? It is like, you know, when uh—is very specific to DeepSeek.

So, DeepSeek V4 Pro has this weird alpha male energy where whatever it sends you, it thinks that that is the right thing to do. And if it is sending you wrong schema of the tool calls, and you send back a Zod error, it doesn't listen to you. It would repeat that same thing for like 56 times on average in a billion tokens. Where you'll be like, "Why is it doing that?", right?

Why don't they listen to the errors? I thought that's like a very common thing. Like, Instructor used to do that.

I have no idea. Like, you know, as a programmer, I would think that, you know, if my tool call is failing, all I have to do is send some schema back, like Zod error, and an LLM that is smart enough, right? And it will just do it, right?

My gut feeling is—it might be a hot take—because they are, I think, a lot of these open models are built or trained in a system where they are learning from data, which they consider really high quality. Like, they're learning from a better model than them or something like that, right? And their training is "whatever you are being told is right, it's correct." So, their entire nature is "whatever I am telling you is also correct, so don't try to correct me."

This is, again, quite a lot of this is vibe-based. And I discovered this because—like, it's a silly thing. Like, for example, I shared it in my post as well. Do you mind if I share it? Like, share the screen or something?

No, go ahead. I think you have to—I got to—do, we tested this before you recorded it. These are for people to look at. They can look up the tweet while we're talking as well. Just make sure you zoom in, obviously.

用 Repair Logic 替代抛出错误：教小孩开车的比喻

Yes. So, this was the post, right? So, this was me being like, "You know, I just discovered why and how DeepSeek can outperform Opus 4.7." So, this is Opus 4.7, not 4.6. 4.6 is a better model, right?

So, the thing that we discovered was: it was sending in—you have some tool like a shell tool and it has some parameters or arguments that should be optional, it would send some weird thing on that—like an empty object or a null where it doesn't belong—and Zod, being super strict, would just trip up and send back that error. And it will get back the same result again and again. Same tool call, right?

So, instead of sending back that error, I ended up repairing that, right? It started with just like 3,200 lines of repairs. Think of it—this repair logic—like, you know, database migrations. You know, you have one migration per file. So I ended up creating repair files. Like, if you see something like this where, you know, it is emitting JSON strings type of data when I actually wanted an array, I can deterministically fix that to an array, right?

And when I do that, I will not only just send back the result, I will also send back a note, a hint, a repair hint—that "You should have sent me this type of data, but here is the result anyway."

Think of it like you're teaching somebody how to drive a car. And they're about to, you know, hit another car. Instead of telling them what to do correctly, you'll first try to save them and then you will explain why you saved them, right? Like what they should have done in the first place.

I think a lot of these models really like this repair logic, because what we saw is: the moment you send the result with the repair logic, right after that the third tool call is fixed. Instead of, you know—and all of a sudden it becomes super smart. It understands like, "Okay, I got the result what I was looking for and I'm going to do this."

And it shows up in so many different places as well. Like, for example, it is trying to read a file and it is not giving you the offset of "are you trying to read the 100 lines from the top or at the bottom of this file." So, I just make a judgment call like, "Okay, it's the first time it's going to read that file, so let's give it the first 100 lines of that file," right? And then it realizes very quickly, "Oh, I was actually trying to read a log file and then needed the last 100"—and it can very easily understand, instead of, you know, those 50 plus on average tool call failures.

为什么没人发现：Claude Code 把错误藏起来了

And something like this should be very obvious to developers, but what's happening is a lot of developers actually use Claude Code. They hack their, you know, change the base API endpoint and the API key and they try to use Claude Code as the harness for open models. And Claude Code, you know—they hide a lot of the errors behind Control O, right? So, you don't even know that, you know, you have like 50 plus tool call failures per session. You're just sitting there and you're like, "Oh, why is DeepSeek so slow?"

And it's in their vested interest to not fix that. Like, why—they don't care about open models. They didn't build their coding agent for open models. So, it works out really well for them. But for the common thing that the common team across Twitter is, "Oh, it's so good" and "Oh, it's so bad, it's super slow"—I feel like this always ends up being a tool call harness issue than, you know, an actual model issue.

Like, it can be as silly as something like this: when it's sending the read file path, right? It would create some markdown link and it—for no reason at all. And this is super deterministically fixable, right? So, you don't have to like waste your tokens on it or anything like that, right? Yeah, I think that's—and go ahead.

Well, I mean, I think the—reading the data and actually looking at what you actually said—and I think it's really helpful. Being thoughtful—like, I wonder if it's only DeepSeek that's showing this kind of stuff, or is it just a general open models trick?

不只是 DeepSeek：Kimi、MiniMax 与 16,000 条 Repair Variations

Yeah, I think the first thing—again, me being me—I thought this is just DeepSeek. Then I looked at our, you know, logs for last 30 days, and Kimi is doing exactly the same thing. Then we fixed the Kimi models and we fixed MiniMax models, and now we have like 16,000 different repair variations across hundreds of billions of tokens. We are doing anywhere from 600 billion tokens right now.

And the data on failure of those tool calls—super, super important. In overall, this goes from a model that was practically completely not useful—like DeepSeek V4 flash—to something that can actually compete with this, right? Like—and this is more of a vibe check.

When I pushed out this update, one of our investors, Tom Preston-Werner's fund, PW, their GP, he was like, "What did you do? Why is DeepSeek V4 flash super solid now?" Like the vibe of the model completely changes. It starts doing things in a different way.

一个反直觉观察：权限提示让模型变笨

I don't know if you've seen this, but if you run any coding agent with permissions on, the models are actually dumber. And if you run them without—you know, the complete bypass of permissions—they do much better. Even if you, like, sit through those "yes yes yes accept" or whatnot, you will see that, you know, the model ends up getting steered in the wrong direction because of the slowness of permission blocks or whatnot.

Maybe that's not how the models are trained or whatnot, but it's the same thing with models: like, if they are seeing a lot less tool call errors, they are much more creative. They can explore a lot and they can continue a lot longer.

Like, one of our users has actually done, I think, 70 billion tokens on DeepSeek. I was looking at his data. He broke our usage page. That's how we discovered him. And he's like, "I constantly run DeepSeek with Command Code for 12-plus hour-long sessions." I personally have not done that, right? So, when I think—a lot less tool errors, tool confusion is happening—this repair logic kind of blows you away, like, how good open models can be overall.

Yeah, that's really fascinating. I mean, I guess, what else have you found? Is there ongoing work? You know, it's been a little bit since you've done this. You've generalized a bit. Is there ongoing work in this area, or are you sort of basically constantly spinning out insights like this from Command Code?

把同样的方法搬到 Design Slop

Yeah. So, one of the first things that happened was—I think we might be the most used coding agent out there right now for DeepSeek. So, we are doing so much inference on DeepSeek. So, a lot of people actually tagged a bunch of people on DeepSeek research team. And to prove this, we actually launched a Go Plan with just $1 per month—you can do like 600 million tokens of DeepSeek free, for pro in it. Just to prove like open models are actually really, really good and they are catching up, right?

And I think that kind of percolated to—I think I wouldn't be too far to say that DeepSeek saw that they can discount their prices and do the same and show people that their models are actually really, really good. But also, I saw a lot of people from different coding agent harness companies being tagged in this tweet like, you know, "Is this thing doing that?", right? There's like also by—people can easily just put this entire thing into a prompt and get by, to fix itself, right?

So, things like that. But, one of the most interesting things that just recently happened was: we have been able to apply the same thing to design slop. You know, like that indigo purple gradient thing that all LLMs do?

I think Mario Zechner has a current viral post about it right now. Basically, if you go to his profile—are you familiar with Mario Zechner?

Yeah, yeah, yeah.

He has an example of design slop.

There you go, right? I think everybody just knows. So, I love the purple color. And I would like to point out that it's the indigo slop that is happening, not the purple slop, right?

But what we have found is: we can deploy the same model to fixing design slop across the hundreds of billions of tokens that we have done. We looked at the numbers and we found that—obviously we chatted with a bunch of amazing designers with amazing design taste—and we found out that it is also very similar a problem. There's a finite set of things that most LLMs do. And if you can give them a compositional framework of sorts, like you can repair their design thinking, the same thing applies, right?

Like, for example, these are like probably 10 big rules—you can put them in a skill file or anything—and you will see your design get better. We've seen this repeated across different LLMs, commercial or open, whatsoever. It's the same repair tool logic of sorts, right? Like you basically guiding your coding agent that, you know, you're not going to do this, this, this, this, and this. And if it does, you are deterministically fixing it.

七种界面意图模式 + OKLCH 的秘密

Like, one of the things that you might find really good here is—the first one was this "work pattern first composition" where, when you ask a model "go and design me this dashboard," they generally do not think about the intention behind that design and they just slop you with that, you know, three cards in a row and one border on the left side or top side or whatnot. That's a pretty common thing.

If you give them a very simple framework of, you know, "what type of surface area are we looking for," which is literally just these seven patterns, they do really, really well.

Where are these seven patterns from? Did you just come up with it?

Yeah, I talked to a bunch of different designers.

I'm just wondering if there's like a book or some, you know—yeah. Is this like lore that people just know?

People—I think you could think of it like, you know, when a designer is designing a dashboard, they're thinking about: "this is a monitor surface," right? Like, we're trying to monitor—that is the intention behind this, right? And this is across the chats we had with a bunch of amazing designers—this is how they work.

And we thought: if we could turn this into a slash design skill, see how the LLMs will repair themselves.

We also discovered something really, really—we're like—I personally don't use OKLCH, but apparently LLMs are really good at it. And if you see them using HSL or something, they don't actually are able to control the lightness in HSL very quickly, but on to human eye it's very easy to see like "this color and this color do not look the same." But if you force an LLM to use OKLCH, they can control the color palette really, really well compared to any of other things. And this is what a lot of my designer friends do as well. They love easy to—

That's the reason we invented OKLCH, right? Because we realized—I mean, every time color theory advances, we also have to change the CSS functions, which is mildly annoying for learning CSS.

24 篇参考文档 + 10 个设计气味 + 7 个模式：1.5 秒辨认 AI 设计

So, yeah, but it's—I can't shake this feeling, but it's like—I actually wrote about this just last night. This is from last night. Like, I think the design slop thing—somewhere here. Like this is new, so I'm struggling to find where that is.

We only have like 24 reference documents, 10 design smells, and seven patterns that we saw across different designers. And when we designed a bunch of different landing pages and got those designers to look at them, it takes about 1.5 seconds for them to spot, "Oh, this is from AI" and "this is somebody human looked at it," right?

So, the differences that they spotted—when we wrote them down, they are just deterministically fixable patterns. Like you can convert HSL into OKLCH, or you can force an LLM to do that, right? And it feels like you can fix 90% of design slop, which is not a capability gap. It's more like a contract gap in what your harness is telling an LLM to do versus what your user is saying.

User is always going to say, "Fix my design, make it prettier, make it pop," or something like this. If you can give them a framework of "this is what the design taste of a really good designer is like, they will pick this type of color scheme, they will think about intent before starting to implement that landing page," what is the intent here, right? It's a short contract, but it makes your design slop really, really minimal.

I think this is a really good overview. And for those who don't know, like, Ahmad actually has a lot of authority also on the design side. I actually don't even super know everything that you've done, but I definitely will listen when you have thoughts on the intersection of design and code.

Yeah, it's like—for example, this is the landing page of our documentation, right? As a developer, I would not have gone through the trouble of creating all of this, right? But when I fed that to our design skill—so Command Code now comes bundled with a slash design skill, which has all of these references in there pre-cooked in—like, I was just last night fixing our deals that we have, like, going—2.6 is, you know, 50% off.

So, I fed it a very basic screenshot of, you know, all of this mess, and this is what it converted into. To me, it looks really, really good. When I, as a programmer, only told it: "Here's a deal, here's the data, just add that to our docs," right?

So, giving that framework of "this is not like tool calling or anything, it's like a really nice framework of thinking" that LLMs have this capability cooked in—they can organize their thoughts really, really well. They can design really, really well if you just give them the right way to think about those things, right?

Same thing we did with our tool calling—same concept basically applies to design. And one of our team members right now is working with a community of security people.
