Like, you look through the logs and you figure out that this is the most common pattern security-wise, this is what is brewing up—and they can apply automatic fixes to your packages or whatnot and make LLMs really, really kind of coherent on following your guidelines of "never write bad secure—bad—or poor secure code."

差异化：Command Code 的定位

Yeah, I think—like, that's something that we all want to get smarter on. Can you give us like an overview of just the differentiators that people should be aware of? Because I think this is one of them that you've been working on. You know, taste is something that you're definitely focusing on owning. Are you still pursuing your own models, or are you mostly just going to be the best CLI for open models?

Yeah, there are like two directions right now that we sort of own. This thing—again, this is like a CLI, right? It is a full-fledged coding agent. Does everything that you can expect any coding agent to do. It has both commercial models or open models. It's just that we have found our PMF of sorts in the open models market more than, you know—Claude is actually really, really lenient with tool calls. So even if your coding agent harness messes up, it can figure out that "Oh, I'm being sent this error" and can fix itself. Not the case with open models.

Taste 是怎么工作的：自动学习、自动管理、完全透明

But the taste thing is still there. Like, it basically sort of works like this. This is a very common site that I have. When I'm using something, "please use TypeScript"—it's using TSE. "I want you to use TS up," right? It's using some different framework of testing. "I prefer Vitest." And it's like a lot of back and forth in getting your coding agent to do what you wanted done in the first place.

But with taste—as taste is like a meta neuro-symbolic model—like, I have a lot of—I tried to, based on the feedback, I've hidden it very well in docs somewhere. So, you know, developers don't have to go and read the really silly things there. [laughter] Right?

The entire KL divergence loop that we look at: like, if an LLM already knows about something, it should not end up in your skill or taste file. That is absolutely useless context, right? So, it basically does all of this weird thing where—once you actually go through—where is the link?—once you actually just go through all of this, once you have built a CLI or an API or a front-end project with Command Code, it actually ends up learning a bunch of those rules that are automatically managed for you.

So, for example, if you are using Commander for building CLIs and now in this particular project you start using Meow, it will replace that for you.

The entire idea is that your skills are being automatically learned and automatically managed, and they're absolutely transparent. They are in your repository, not in our model. So, you're reviewing it in every PR. You're looking at like, "Yeah, I don't want to save something like this or whatnot." And they're never stale.

Rules vs Taste：宏大规则 vs 微观决策

A lot of issues that I feel a lot of people face are because their agent.md or cloud.md has some wrong information when they sat down. This is one thing I discussed when we announced our $5 million seed: that a lot of people, when they sit down, they think of the rules or whatnot. They think in the terms of grandiose things, right?

Rules are like, "you know, like, actually zoom in." So, this is like the difference between our skill or rules file or our TS file, right? This is what you are writing down. And TS is what is continuously being learned from your prompts or your edits, and they're being stored in the same markdown file for you, right?

And updates are when you remember to do it. In most of the times I've seen, we as humans we sit down and we do this grandiose thing. Like, "you know, use this. I always prefer that" or whatnot. And TS are a lot of micro decisions, not too broad.

When you are doing this, when you are running a /pr command, sometimes Ahmad prefers that, you know, you basically fetch the latest from main, rebase it, send a PR on a branch, and then go back to the main branch. That is what my PR workflow is. That is not what my PR file says. So, it automatically goes and fixes it, right? It has seen me go back to the main branch again and again. It's like, "Okay, it's just one liner for it, but improves my workflow without me having to take care of it."

And over time it basically compounds a lot. Like, if you have one of the guys—I was looking at his code, he built one Android app with Command Code. And now he's like, "I'm able to build more Android apps very, very quickly." It's the same thing, but it is learning the skill from you as you build those things, right?

And over time we've seen, you know, it becomes really helpful. Like, somewhere down there, we ran a study with like 70-plus developers, and the number of times that they had to go edit files because their LLM made a different—you know, the scene took a different turn, or steered their LLM like, "Yeah, don't do this. Don't use this. Don't use tRPC or something and use Hono or whatever for this part of API"—they found that their number of edits or steers went down.

Taste vs Skills：澄清三个常见疑问

So let me ask some clarifying questions. Yeah. On the website it says a model, but then you also say it's like portable between systems and stuff. Like, is it a—Is there a system of files? Like obviously this is a form of memory. How does it compare with skills? Should I prefer skills? Can I use them side by side? There's all these questions.

Yeah, yeah, yeah. So, one thing is—I actually got this question so many times. I have written a like a blog post on it. Skills versus Taste, right? What is the difference?

At the very basic layer, Taste is the highest order bit, which is managing your skills and rules. Taste is this automatic engine of sorts that is creating skills for you, making sure they're not stale, and you can obviously go edit them yourself as well.

And overall, it actually looks like this. Like, after looking at—I think I probably have built more than 70 CLIs with Command Code so far. And this is the entirety of my taste for building CLIs. This little thing, right? So, it knows that, for example, I use PNPM only build dependencies thing. It knows that I always prefer starting from 0.0.1. It's like those silly little things.

And this is—all I have to do is, I have to do this npx taste pull. We have the taste package as well, by the way, right?

Nice.

Yep. And it just pulls this particular file, puts it in your repository, and then all you have to do is ask any coding agent and "follow my taste of building CLIs. And build me a CLI that does this, this, this, and that. And then by the end, show the taste compliance." And it would go—any LLM would just go through all this list and be able to figure out "I was supposed to use this. I was supposed to use clack for interactive things" or whatnot. It works out really well.

And then when you start continuously building on top of it, every CLI is different from other CLIs. This taste of sorts changes in that particular repository, right? So, it lives in your Git repo anyway, right? Yeah. And all of this was automatically created, but you can come in and edit it yourself as well. It's a markdown file.

Then the confidence, I guess, is LLM generated as well, right? So, if you—

Yeah, but, like—if you always do it, it's one, you know. Yeah.

Something like this.

Yeah.

为什么不再隐藏学到的规则：合并的人类难题

Like, the problem we actually hit was—the funny story there, we actually tried to hide this early on. Like, if we don't show you the learnings, and we just made you compare Claude Code versus Command Code, and people—developers—had like this wow moment, like, "Oh wow, this is so good." They didn't know what was being learned.

But the problem we hit is: we don't know how to merge different taste files. It is extremely a human endeavor. Think of it like 15 different engineers working on a GitHub repository with a thousand different GitHub branches. And taste files are so different in all those different branches. And when you're merging that back into the main, we cannot judge what you want to keep or what you don't want to keep, right?

So we made it very, very transparent. And this is before their—skills existed, by the way. This is from last October, right? There was no concept of skills. There was APIs, CLIs, and MCPs. And we ended up creating this, and then, you know, the skills thing came around.

So, if you, as a human, are writing something, I think you can just probably write confidence score of one. You know what you're talking about, right? If something is being learned from you, some of it is really good, some of it is not.

Like, for example, I always have this local option which I don't want to show up in my help commands because this is an option for debugging local setup of my APIs. And so basically that's a thing I will never personally go and write this learning in a skill file. It's just too minor, right? For me. But it's really good that, you know, it picks up on small things like this and works it out, right?

社区做法：高质量模型生成 Taste，廉价模型继续构建

One of the things that I've seen in our Discord community—which is blowing up after all of the tool confusion fixer—is a lot of people, what they're doing is they're building one project with a really high quality LLM like Opus or GPT 5.5, right? They're building a taste file, and then they're using super cheap models to continuously build on that more, you know, project with that taste file.

Yeah, I mean, I think that's a system that kind of works—makes sense for me. Cool. I mean, I think it's a great overview. You know, if people are interested, they can look you up and chat more. You guys have been building for a good while in this space, and it's really cool to see that you're sort of honing in on this coding use case. I have also obviously become more interested in coding AI agents for myself as well.

Any final words? Any other things that people can look forward to?

路线图：开源 Command Code 与 "Apple 哲学"

I can probably share just a little bit about what our roadmap looks like, where we are headed next, right?

So, we are going to open source Command Code very, very soon. I'm hoping we can announce that on the AI engineering conference in SF—if I can work out the quirks of six or six years old, you know, repository. The idea behind that is—

Six years old? Wait, hang on. Six years old? Oh, because—you're—it was a CLI before. Yeah, I get stuff. Yeah.

Yeah, I've been building it for like since 2020, right? It became a product last year. And I told everybody and their, you know, mothers and brothers and that this will never be my product, right? So, funny how things work out.

Anyway, so we are open sourcing it. The idea behind that is I want to make Command Code completely hackable. I've been working very, very hard on that. My background is also in this thing—like, I've spent 13 years in WordPress core. Matt Mullenweg is actually one of our angels now. When he heard that we are open sourcing Command Code, he reached out.

The idea is: you should be able to modify any part of Command Code irrespective of where our business model is headed, right?

And the other idea that I'm super subscribed to right now is that we will not turn this into a soup of, you know, 1,500 models that you decide what you do with them, right?

I think of it like, you know, so there are three different philosophies, right?

  • One is, it's like Windows and every game works with it. You know, I think Open Code is like that. Every model works with Open Code.
  • One is like Linux, where you build your own drivers—like Pi. You can build anything with Pi, right?
  • With Command Code, what I'm thinking and what the team kind of is going for or towards is, you know, build it like Apple. It will have the best of the best models, both open and closed. It will not have every model, but it will be hackable in any way—so you would be able to put in your local model if you wanted to or whatever, right?

So, that's where we are headed—like, we're about to open source it very, very quickly. Very, very soon. So, yeah, pretty excited about that.

收尾：DeepSeek Code 与 Qwen 3.7 Max

Okay, excellent. Looking forward to that. Open source is always good. Follow competition, I'll say. You know, I was reminded that actually DeepSeek announced that they're going to do DeepSeek Code at some point. So, but, you know, obviously they'll only do DeepSeek stuff.

So many of the comments on this particular thing were about, you know, they were tagging DeepSeek people, "Why are you not building your own code?"

Yeah, yeah, yeah, they announced it.

And then yeah, they did after that—like, you know, maybe a week later or something, they announced that "we are hiring for that" or whatnot. So, it makes sense, right? But, they will only be for DeepSeek. Qwen 3.7 Max is the second most used model on Command Code right now. It's just two or three days old, right? So yeah.

Cool.

We'll see how it goes.

We'll see how it goes. Okay, well, all the best. It's great to catch up and get a sense of, you know, everything going on. I definitely think there's some good ideas here that people can take regardless of what project they work on. So, I think that's why we do the podcast—is learning from everyone and trying to have a common, I guess, shared knowledge of all these things.

Most definitely. And I'll continue sharing more and more. I think everybody can learn. It's not just, you know, you don't have to use this idea in Command Code. You can just use it in any coding harness that you want, right? As far as we are all improving, right? Yeah.

Okay, that's it. Awesome.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 洞见一：DeepSeek 的"Alpha Male 能量"其实是被训练出来的认知失调
Ahmad 用 "alpha male energy" 来形容 DeepSeek V4 Pro 对 Zod 报错置若罔闻、连续 56 次重复同一个错误工具调用——这听起来像吐槽，但底层是一个相当深刻的训练学现象：当模型从"被认为是高质量的数据"（也就是上游更强模型的输出）里蒸馏知识时，它内化的不只是答案，还有一种**认识论姿态**——"我所说的就是对的"。
这其实和心理学里"被持续夸奖的孩子"现象同构：被反复奖励正确输出的模型，逐渐丧失了对自身错误信号的敏感性。**最反直觉的地方在于**——主流叙事把 alignment 理解为让模型更友善、更服从，但 Ahmad 这里展示了一个反例：过度的监督蒸馏会训练出一种"训练出来的自恋"（trained narcissism）。这意味着真正的对齐难题，可能不是让模型更服从，而是让模型保持对自身错误的可纠正性（corrigibility under failure）——一种**反向 alignment**。
## 洞见二：Repair Logic 是

item.title: [All-In Podcast] Dan Loeb: The Lost Art of Short Selling, and Why Stock Picking is Back
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反常识的观察：Dan Loeb 的"巨魔基因"恰恰是他 alpha 的底层结构

大多数人会把 Loeb 早期在 Silicon Investor、Yahoo 留言板上"羞辱管理层"的历史当作年轻气盛的花絮。但仔细看他的整条职业曲线，你会发现一个隐藏的因果链：他的投资优势从来不是"看得比别人准"，而是"敢比别人公开发声"。

  • 90 年代他在匿名论坛揪出 Aetrade 这类骗局，靠的是"嘲讽 + 揭露"的复合武器；
  • 2000 年代他用"毒笔信"（poison pen letters）攻击 CEO，把私人不满做成公共事件；
  • 今天他在 X 上继续"找到自己的声音"。

这其实是同一个机制在三十年里换了三层皮：社交摩擦力 = 估值修复的催化剂。他自己点破了关键——"Activism without proxy contest is like Catholicism without hell"（没有代理人战争的激进主义，就像没有地狱的天主教）。换句话说，惩罚机制不可信，整个信念体系就崩塌。这是一个被严重低估的金融洞见：在一个流动性泛滥、ETF 化、被动化的市场里，"羞辱"是少数几个还能压缩折价的稀缺能量来源。

二、跨领域连接：Loeb 的演化路径 ≈ 生物学里的"共生进化"，而不是金融教科书里的"策略升级"

主流叙事会说 Loeb"从事件驱动进化到了质量投资 + AI 时代主题投资"。但听他自述细节后，更准确的隐喻是生态位的共生扩张：

  • 对冲基金 → CLO → 私募信贷 → 保险公司（拥有永久资本）
  • 同时：人脉（Eric Mindich、David Tepper、Rob Schwarz 的卡拉特同学）→ 渠道 check → 早期 chip 公司 → VC 基金
  • 每一层新业务不是替代上一层，而是吃掉上一层产生的"代谢废物"：对冲基金的研究剩余 → 灌入信贷；信贷的久期错配 → 灌入保险浮存金；保险的盈余资本 → 灌回对冲基金。

这恰好是巴菲特结构的"复刻版"，但用对冲基金的语法重写。意外的洞察是：Loeb 真正抄的不是 Tepper，而是 Berkshire 的资产负债表拓扑学。他自己没明说，但"我们半拥有一家保险公司，它用我们的结构化信用，盈余资本流回我们做最 alpha 的事"——这就是 Berkshire 的现金机器图，只是规模小、节奏快。

三、被忽略的"政治-金融"耦合：赦免 Ross Ulbricht 和做空房屋建造商，其实是同一种"结构性错配"交易

把对话里两件看似无关的事并置： 1. 做空 homebuilders：他指出整个行业假装自己是 NVR（轻资产期权模式），但实际把巨额资本锁死在土地池里——会计语言与经济实质的错配。 2. Ross Ulbricht 的双重无期 + 40 年：他指出法律惩罚的强度与实际行为的严重性错配。

这两件事在 Loeb 的认知操作系统里其实是同一个交易——他终身的赚钱方式就是定位"标价机制 vs 真实价值"之间的扭曲，无论这个标价机制是市场、是公司管理层的 narrative，还是国家的司法系统。所以他对教育改革（"工会让会计变形"）、慈善（"一次救一个人"，对应他做集中持仓而非指数）、激进主义（公开施压压缩折价）有内在一致的逻辑。

真正的"啊哈时刻"：他对"AI 时代人类还剩什么"的回答——"agent 永远无法直视你的眼睛去评估你"——不是怀旧，而是一个做空者的本能。因为他一辈子靠的就是侦测人脸上、季报字里行间、管理层语气中的细微不一致。AI 让数据竞争 race to zero，而"读人"是最后一个尚未被套利掉的信息边界。这也解释了为什么他在播客末尾突然转向慈善、教育、司法——这些都是高维度的"读人"训练场，是他维持自己最后一块 alpha 来源的方式。

跨领域连接：这非常类似于围棋顶尖棋手在 AlphaGo 出现后的转向——技术层面输给 AI 后，他们把研究重心转向"对手心理状态评估"。Loeb 正在金融界进行同样的迁徙，只是他用"赦免一个 Silk Road 创始人"作为训练数据。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

Legendary activist investor Dan Loeb. He of course is the CEO and CIO of Third Point.

"The lost art of short selling has come back and it's absolutely critical." "Doesn't matter what you do, you have to be really selective. People talk about stock pickers market. This is a bond and credit pickers market." "When we were small, our main tool was a shame and humor."

Dan Loeb turning up the heat on Nestle over the weekend. The shift has really been more towards a "dare to be great" message.

"Activism without proxy contest is like Catholicism without hell."

一、互联网早期的"原始巨魔"岁月

You're very active on the Twitter as well.

Oh wow. You found your voice. Lot a lot of emotion brewing there.

Can we actually start with that? Before Twitter, you were actually quite active, but they were in very different places. I mean, you were in Wall Street Bets before Wall Street Bets existed. Can you just walk us through your evolution as a public persona?

Sure. I mean, there was this brand new technology that came out called the internet. And really shortly thereafter, long before Reddit or any of these other things, there were a series of chat boards. There was, you know, Yahoo, there was something called Silicon Investor, a few other ones. And people would congregate and kibbitz. It was done mostly anonymously and it was an interesting place to exchange ideas.

It was really the wild west. People could pretty much say or do anything, but there was a lot of substance there too. It's not actually that much different than from today.

You did engage at all in any trolling per se?

Well, some people use the term OG. Sometimes I say I was the OT. — the original troll. Yeah. No, I did. I mean, it was fun. You know, I didn't know I was one day going to run institutional money and have a big fund, and I was just having fun and blowing off steam.

Investing is fun, and particularly on the short side. I mean, there's so much humor in it when you detect these companies, especially in the '90s. It was really unsupervised. There were some incredibly fraudulent companies out there and it was just fun to uncover them and kind of taunt the management teams and ultimately prevail.

Aetrade 旧事

You have one story above others that kind of stands out in that era.

I mean, there were a bunch. There was a company called Aetrade that I remember, run by a guy who was like a repeat fraudster. We uncovered it, and we really got under this person's skin. Ultimately it was really just a factoring company trading at five, six — I don't remember what — some large multiple of book value.

They had created a new technology called "TADS." I don't remember what TAD stood for, but they were basically repackaging factory securities and saying that they had some special technology. They were financing refrigerators and things like that.

二、投资生涯的师承与原型

Tell us your evolution as an investor when you started Third Point. You started with very little capital. Now it's almost 30 billion of AUM. You're multistrat, but you learned at Jefferies, I think — like you learned helping people like David Tepper allocate capital. Just walk us through how you learned to invest.

Well, I started really fascinated by investing and wanting to do it. I remember when I was 10 years old, my dad took me — my dad was a notoriously bad investor himself. So he didn't give me any good examples. He was a great lawyer, not a great investor. But he took me to meet a broker and I started investing.

Then in high school in the 11th grade, I got a job at the branch office of Bear Stearns — sorry, of Paine Webber — working for a guy named Allan Crown who let me post his books and make cold calls. I think we broke certain securities laws, but I think the statute of limitations has passed. I would trade options on Occidental Petroleum and Teledyne. There was a lot of volatility. I had flurries of making money and lost all of it a couple of different times, but it was a good lesson.

正规化学习：Warburg → Risk Arb → Jefferies

I continued doing it in college, and then my learning started really formally at Warburg Pincus, where I really learned to value enterprises as my first job — private kind of across the spectrum of private equity and venture capital. I worked at a risk arb firm, which was really invaluable.

Skipping forward — I had way too many jobs in my 20s. But I got really serious at Jefferies. I had an amazing opportunity to work on the distressed debt desk there. I started out as a research analyst and I was just drinking out of a fire hose. There was so much activity. The securities were so cheap coming out of distressed.

It was, you know, the 10,000 hours, 10,000 reps. We would write up different things every day. There were big blocks of debt to move and I really got that — that was my real learning point.

横向学习：同辈与客户

I stress this to people: everyone kind of sees mentorship as this sort of hierarchical thing where you learn from some wise older person. But I learned a ton from my colleagues, from my own cohort, and I learned a ton from my customers.

Eric Mindich was a boy wonder at Goldman. He was the youngest partner — youngest partner at Goldman. Ran the ARB desk there, and he had this triumvirate or quadrumvirate — Amos, Dinakar, and some other guys. I don't want to leave them out. Anyway, they were great and they really kind of brought me into their thought process thinking about event-driven investing.

I covered some of the smartest people in the business, including David Tepper. I got to watch their thought process and I was like a Chinese corporation that was like copying and reverse engineering and taking everything in and creating my database of knowledge and my own operating system — kind of taking the best out of what all these different people did.

三、Third Point 的早期风格：事件驱动

What was that style when you first started Third Point?

We call it event-driven investing. It was really less focused on the quality of business, more focused on very complex transactions — takeovers, spin-offs, risk arbitrage, bankruptcies, privatizations, demutualizations.

These transactions created unbelievable opportunities for alpha because of the confluence of dislocation, opacity, time — but also this goes and nothing changes. I always quote this Jesse Livermore line: there's nothing new under the sun.

A real focus on management incentives. In all these different kinds of transactions, management was incentivized to sandbag their numbers during a time when there was an excess supply of securities where their options were being set. And we as co-investors got to come in with these depressed projections and ride along — greater transparency and understanding of the business, coverage, companies that delivered a topline and margins and ROE and everything else better than expectations. It was really a golden era for that type of investment.

四、风格的演化：从事件驱动到质量与主题

From where that started to what Third Point is today — just describe that and where you go from here.

Stylistically that event approach — it's still something we think about, it's in our framework. But what really happened when technology became a bigger force — but really everything changed — is a greater focus on business quality and innovation and disruption, and more thematic on the one hand: understanding of consumer trends, what's going on in financial services, what's the economic macro backdrop that's supporting all this.

Of course the big topic of this event, AI, is sort of the culmination of that, but all these major technological innovations that have really happened since.

You could make money before by not being technology savvy in the markets?

You could be technologically illiterate, or just say "I don't do it," and you could also — more or less, up until the GFC — be more or less economically illiterate and make a lot of money.

Now you wouldn't want to be either one of those things. The tech through line needs to be understood everywhere.

Even if you're like Blue Owl and you're trading — I mean Blue Owl obviously is very sophisticated in tech now — any pool of capital that used to not be correlated is effectively correlated. Yes.

五、Third Point 今天的平台架构

I just want to fast forward and give people a snapshot of what we do today.

风险投资业务的偶然起点

Rob Schwarz, who's my partner, and we took Kempo Karate together when we were 10 years old. — It was a purple belt. I think I never made it past yellow belt. — We reconnected at our 20-year reunion. I'm aging both of us. Sorry to give up your secret, Rob.

In 1999, it was our 20-year reunion, and he was working as a sales rep for wireless RF components. And I said, "Wow, this guy'd be great to do channel checks for us." Then I asked him a couple years later, "Hey, you meet some smart people. If you ever come across a really savvy engineer, we should invest." We didn't know what we were doing. We weren't venture capitalists, but we were getting behind the person.

There was a guy named Dave Fischer started a company called Radio Communications. They made chips that were ABG compatible for Wi-Fi base stations. Ultimately the company was sold to Texas Instruments.

We started to do venture within the fund. We've done a couple of dedicated funds.

一体化平台

Ultimately what I want you to get to is that all these things are interconnected and come together under the platform that we have today:

  • Main hedge fund — credit, equity long/short. Credit is both structured credit and high yield.
  • CLO business — which we acquired.
  • Private credit business — traditional private credit, direct sponsor financing, direct lending, and workouts (very important — credit solutions, as they call it).
  • Insurance company — we started one a few years ago. Not the first insurance company we did — we did a P&C company — but this one was wholly owned. Now we own half of it.

The insurance company captures basically the investment-grade part of what we do: private credit through structured vehicles, structured credit, whole loans, investment grade both private and public. But we also can use our surplus capital in very interesting ways.

六、AI 时代下"人"的角色

So what's the role of the human? What's the role of Dan Loeb in running Third Point 10 years from now? 10 years before, Dan Loeb was 100% of Third Point. Now there's agents, there's AI, all this learning, all of this data. Where do you see the role of the human?

First of all, my time is spent primarily on managing the hedge fund, which for now is the biggest capital pool and most important business that we're in.

The human element — I think this is true for everyone you have here — the social component, the human network of knowing people, being able to capture opportunities, work with people, interact — that's never going away. Maybe you can theorize that there will be agents that will sit at Andreessen Horowitz and whoever else's funds, but I think the human will always have to be there.

You want to know who's making or losing the money. There is a thing that the agents of AI will never really be able to look in your eye and assess.

七、从"便宜 + 催化剂"到"护城河 + 韧性"

You've expanded your philosophy of investing — from cheap securities with catalysts, as I think you described it on a podcast recently — and now you're very concerned about moats, defensibility, and the quality or the brittleness, as Chamath likes to remind us, of the revenue. Could you tell us how you evolved that core thinking?

Obviously that's everything right now. Chamath talks about the time-bounded value of companies, and I think that's essential. What are the companies that are going to be around 7 to 10 to 20 years? What are the real moats that exist out there?

It is harder now. I don't know that we can really go out 10 or 20 years. By the way, I think we deluded ourselves earlier, because if you asked people about the moat around IBM, or some of the other companies — AOL — well, Yahoo, you'd say the same thing.

We're investing outside of tech into companies that have some great — well, first of all, it also comes back to the management. We can't really just look at a product or a technology and say, "Oh, this is going to be it forever." So we really look for a management team that we think will be adaptable. Just like you guys were saying last night, you don't want to be on boards of companies. These are things that they should be doing.

Finding management teams that you really believe in that have a proven ability to stay ahead — is that quantifiable?

No, it's still very subjective, qualitative. After 30 years, there's like a pattern recognition.

八、做空机会与房屋建造商案例

Let me ask a question on screening. You've said recently publicly that there's a lot of opportunities on the short side in the market right now for the first time in a long time. How do you start — top down? Is that a top-down or is it opportunistic, event-driven? Or do you guys have a systematic top-down approach?

There's no one approach to it. One thing that we've avoided is kind of a solely valuation-based approach. I've seen too many people get run over by shorts that have dumb valuations, but they get captured on Reddit or one of these other things — like some of these space companies right now where there's no rhyme or reason.

Homebuilders 短仓逻辑

We had a really strong view on homebuilders from last year. There were two things going on:

  1. It wasn't just rates and mortgage spreads depressing housing prices.
  2. The home building industry was structurally impaired because of the way they were all pretending to be NVR — pretending to be asset light — but they had massive commitments to these land pools, which they said were options but were really very committed capital.
  3. Post-COVID hangover of inventory disruptions and pricing that really made no sense. Prices went up to unsustainable levels, but so did building costs. Buyers are no longer able to pay those prices in the current financing environment. They've also gotten squeezed by inflation and costs.

So that's been something we've been short on.

九、Sacks 加入讨论：流动性与分销

Let me bring Sacks into the discussion. Sacks, we've learned a little bit about distribution of public securities. You're famous in the All-In theme song of this great quote "let your winners ride." I'm curious when you hear Dan talking about this — how do you think about, as a private market investor, navigating distributing equities?

I'm sure you guys share this — it's one of the most vexing questions.

  • We were private investors in Palantir and I think we sold all our stock in the 20s. Huge mistake. Missed a 10x after going public.
  • We led the B round in Upstart. That was one I think we learned not to go on boards anymore because it restricts your ability to be liquid.
  • Early investors in Enphase — we sold some stock on the IPO and then took a tax hit, and I think sold it under a dollar. Had we stayed on, would have made $4 billion.

I am not claiming to have any great expertise in knowing how to best distribute. Markets are brutal. It's so hard.

Sacks, where have you wound up?

I think it's case by case. There's some companies where I was on a board and you can't sell and you end up regretting that. And then there's others where the best thing to do is just hold on to that stock forever.

I've owned Meta and Palantir as a venture investor, as an angel investor. I sold some and held on to some. Obviously in hindsight — you take Meta. I think Meta IPOs Facebook back then at 50 billion, went down to 18, now it's 400+.

Can you imagine how alternate universe — Chamath never sold his Facebook, how insufferable he'd be. Or if Friedberg never sold his Google, Friedberg would be worth 10 billion. I wouldn't be nearly as good — it's like an analyst. Because it created tension. It's not real. It's not earned.

Back in those days, 10 years ago, we thought a hundred-billion-dollar market cap company was pretty much as big as anything could get. Facebook at 50 — the upside was to 100. Things are just totally different now. We have multi-trillion-dollar companies. The market's so much bigger.

Nvidia 的估值悖论

That's a rub against Nvidia, which is a $5 trillion company, and people feel like there's sort of a ceiling on it. I think we'll look back at some point in time and say that was a foolish way to think about Nvidia given its dominant position and its valuation.

Is it undervalued right now?

Yeah, absolutely. On earnings over the next two or three years.

And is it because people are having a hard time processing the largest entity that's ever existed in human history?

I think that, and the narrative. First of all, technically there's all this other stuff that's growing faster and going up more. The long/short pods are structured such that they have to be short something. So Nvidia feels like a safe short. By the way, Google was a safe short. Amazon was a safe short. So this just happens. Sometimes they'll languish at a valuation, then they break out. I think that'll eventually happen with Nvidia.

There's probably some boundary condition discount to that, right? We've never seen a valuation like this. You can't overbet that.

十、社会、慈善与司法改革

I want to shift topics. I want to talk society and culture before we run out of time. You're very passionate about criminal justice reform, and specifically you were a key person to get the pardon of Ross Ulbricht. Tell us your views on criminal justice, why it hit such a nerve, and why Ross Ulbricht?

慈善的框架：教育是根

Let me take a step back and just talk about my framework for philanthropy, which is not unlike Brad Gerstner and many people in the room here. I care deeply about income inequality. I care deeply about making sure that as many people have opportunities as we've all had here.

My interest in criminal justice reform really started earlier with an interest in education and education reform. I was very lucky to start supporting, get on the board, and ultimately be chairman of Success Academies, a charter school network in New York.

Nobody talks about it, but the thing that's hiding out in plain sight for everybody is that the problem with income inequality isn't that Jeff Bezos is going to be a trillionaire, or all these other people are gaining wealth. It's that we're not equipping children — particularly the most vulnerable children — with the intellectual tools that they need to succeed and compete.

It's not because poverty is this intractable thing that can't be overcome. We've proven that it can be. The problem is that the unions and the basic principles that we all use in business — accountability and merit and cultivating talent — are set aside for the benefit of adults who are part of these unions. It's a systemic thing. It's not a lack of money. It's really a broken structure. Accountability is what I'm hearing. Yeah.

跨越党派的议题

I then became aware — I was looking for issues that conservatives and liberals/progressives can agree on. Hopefully they can agree that we want young people to be better educated. I think we can also agree that whenever you put the government in charge of something, they'll mess it up one way or another.

I want to give you guys a shout-out though for noting up this private/public partnership with the investments in the private sector, because I think this administration has done an enormously good job at backing companies. It's one of the rare instances where I've seen that.

Can you give an example?

We have a company in our portfolio called Atom Computing that, with many other quantum companies, has gotten money from the government. We were super impressed how they contracted with us to engage with them in cryptography and to meet the government's needs, but also in the financial component — they drove a really tough bargain. The taxpayers are going to make a ton of money on this, and their involvement will contribute meaningfully to the value of this business. It's just a win-all. They're an investor and a customer. They're capturing part of that value for the American people, which everyone deserves.

Ross Ulbricht 案

So back to criminal justice reform. First of all, there's a lot of bad people in jail. I think the criminal justice movement has been undermined by folks who see it as an opportunity to not prosecute, not deal with bad people. But there's a lot of people that are rehabilitated.

There are really three different categories: 1. People who are falsely convicted. 2. People who have shown contrition and rehabilitation. 3. Those who just had a really disproportionate sentence relative to what they did.

There's a case right now of a guy named John Grubman who dealt in gray market diapers and formula. He got an 18-year sentence for dealing these goods.

In the case of Ross Ulbricht — I was approached by someone. Ross, as people may know — probably this room knows — he was sort of a folk hero because he had this cat-and-mouse game with the government. He ran Silk Road, one of the first crypto-based exchanges. He acknowledges that he did things that were illegal that he shouldn't have done. He regrets it. Drugs were dealt on the exchange. That's what he was accused of.

The government later said that there were murder-for-hire incidents — that was never... he was never prosecuted for that, and he denies that that ever happened. In any case, he was sentenced to a double life plus 40 years. Who knows how he got the extra 40 years on there and how he would spend that after he'd been there for two lifetimes.

There's a woman I met through Intel named Rivetz who alerted me to this — friends with Olaf Carlson-Wee and the crypto insiders. I thought, this guy's got no way out. There's no recourse through the system to get someone with a life sentence out of jail. This will only work with a presidential pardon.

We worked on it. We had some familiarity with the pardon process. I approached Charlie Kirk about this, and Charlie really embraced this individual as someone who had been unfairly sentenced. He took it to the president.

Charlie also had an attorney named David Warrington, who's currently the White House counsel. I just found out a couple days ago, talking to him, that he was Charlie's lawyer for a decade.

I'm not taking credit for this. I'm not saying Charlie does. It takes a village. But David had been working on it. On the last day of Trump's 45th term, we were certain that he was going to get out. The Justice Department, for whatever reason, said, "If you commute his sentence, we're going to go after you" — to the president. So as I understand, he withdrew the commutation.

Four years went by. Charlie took the lead on this. This was his only ask of the president, and the president had — at a libertarian event and to the crypto community — promised to deal with this. Not only was his sentence commuted, but he was pardoned. Today Ross is married, is having a child, and living a free life after spending a decade — you can argue whether that was the right amount or not.

继续的工作

Do you feel like there's a role for you to play in doing more of this?

I continue to work on cases. There's an organization called Olive, and we work constantly on different people. Look, as philanthropists, it's great to work with organizations — there's a lot of great organizations I work with. I do a lot fighting antisemitism and supporting Jewish identity also. But I also think that we can help people one at a time. It just really nurtures the soul, and it's a good thing to do.

All right, let's give it up for Dan. Dan Loeb.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反常识的观察：Dan Loeb 的"巨魔基因"恰恰是他 alpha 的底层结构
大多数人会把 Loeb 早期在 Silicon Investor、Yahoo 留言板上"羞辱管理层"的历史当作年轻气盛的花絮。但仔细看他的整条职业曲线，你会发现一个隐藏的因果链：**他的投资优势从来不是"看得比别人准"，而是"敢比别人公开发声"。**
- 90 年代他在匿名论坛揪出 Aetrade 这类骗局，靠的是"嘲讽 + 揭露"的复合武器； - 2000 年代他用"毒笔信"（poison pen letters）攻击 CEO，把私人不满做成公共事件； - 今天他在 X 上继续"找到自己的声音"。
这其实是同一个机制在三十年里换了三层皮：**社交摩擦力 = 估值修复的催化剂**。他自己点破了关键——"Activism without proxy contest is like Catholicism without hell"（没有代理人战争的激进主义，就像没有地狱的天主教）。换句话说，**惩罚机制不可信，整个信念体系就崩塌**。这是一个被严重低估的金融洞见：在一个流动性泛滥、ETF

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The 200 Year Old AI Forecast – Alex Imas and Phil Trammell
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽视的"幽灵": 李嘉图其实赢了一半

最反直觉的发现藏在这段访谈的褶皱里: 李嘉图200年前的悲观预测在"事实层面"几乎全对——他点名的那些工作确实都被自动化了。 我们却习惯性地把他作为"被历史证伪的反面教材"。这是一种典型的幸存者叙事偏差: 我们记住了"工业革命让大家更富有"的结论, 却抹掉了过程中那条被吞噬的具体职业链。换句话说, 李嘉图并不是预测错了, 他只是没预测到人类会发明出"服务"这个逃生通道。今天关于AI的乐观派, 其实是在赌同一张牌——赌人类总能在被吃掉的旧职业之外, 长出我们现在还想象不到的新需求形态。

跨领域连接: 这是经济学的"暗物质问题"

Imas说"我们没有数据"——这句话表面平淡, 但它实际上把宏观经济学拖到了和天体物理学同样的尴尬位置: 我们能观测到星系的运动(GDP、就业率), 却测不到驱动它的暗物质(消费需求弹性、任务级别的创造与毁灭)。ONET数据库年久失修, 就像哈勃望远镜镜片有划痕还没人修。更深一层的隐喻是: AI讨论中最大的争论, 其实不是"AI会做什么", 而是"人类的欲望边界在哪里"。而欲望弹性正是我们最缺数据的领域。这就解释了为什么AI辩论永远收敛不了——双方都在用未经测量的人性假设*互相投掷。

一个让人不安的方法论翻转

注意Imas提出的研究路径有多激进: 不要从"AI会怎样"出发去预测就业, 而是反过来——先假设一个终局(劳动收入份额为零 / 全就业), 再倒推什么模型能产生它, 再倒推需要什么数据。 这本质上是把预测学从"前向推理"切换成了"反向工程未来"。这种思路在AI安全社区其实有同名兄弟: backcasting——从可能的结局回溯路径。但被一位经济学家用在劳动市场上, 暗示了一个更深的转向: 当系统复杂度超过预测能力时, "想象多个未来并为每一个准备测量工具"比"押注一个未来"更理性。这也许是李嘉图真正的遗产: 不是他错了, 而是他只准备了一个剧本。

📝 转录文本

我们一直不擅长预测

We have been famously terrible at forecasting. And so let's take let's go all the way back to 1820.

这场争论其实有 200 年历史

Um, this sort of debate that we've been having actually is like 200 years old.

So David Ricardo is one of the classic economists — not neoclassical, classical economists. And when the industrial revolution started happening, he wrote a bunch of stuff saying like, "Look, this is going to be great for everybody, prices are going to come down." But then he turned around and he's like, "Wait, I can actually see all of these jobs that are creating value, they're going to be automated by these machines. This is going to be really bad. Everybody's going to become unemployed and there's going to be political unrest," and things like that.

李嘉图其实"猜对了"

And if you look at Ricardo's predictions, they're actually right. If you look at all those jobs that made money in Ricardo's time, they got automated.

So if I was David Ricardo and I woke up and somebody told me all those jobs did get automated, and you asked me, David Ricardo, "Like, what do you think the prime age employment rate is in 2026?" — I think he would be surprised if you told him it was the highest it's ever been other than 2000. We have the highest number of employed people that could potentially be employed since 2000. That was like the peak, and now it's like the second peak basically.

他错过了什么: 结构性变迁

So what David Ricardo ended up missing is the fact that, you know, essentially you have these economics of structural change where basically everything that got automated became cheap, people had more money to spend on things, and then they started spending money on services.

And, you know, this is kind of like the lump of labor fallacy — that's what they call it. David Ricardo didn't think, "Hey, I should have, you know, considered the fact that new jobs would be created." But it's kind of not obvious that like money would go to services. Like, why wouldn't they go to more automated goods or something like that?

And I'm not saying that — I'm not using this anecdote as to say like this is what's going to happen now, we're going to have full employment. I'm using that anecdote as to say it's really hard to make predictions.

经济学家的工具: 从终局反推

And what I think maybe a really useful tool that economists have is, instead, start with a premise. Like, maybe we'll start it today: "Look, labor share is zero. Like labor share has gone down. What could possibly explain this? Let's write down an economic model of what happened." Phil will talk about this later today. Or you can start — write down a model to say, "Hey, what if labor share just stays the same? What can make that happen?"

真正的核心: 我们没有数据

And here's my main — here's, if you don't take anything out of this conversation, for me, we don't have any data. I've been kind of saying we need a Manhattan Project for data.

We don't have data on basically consumer demand elasticities. We don't know what they are. We don't know — um, we're not really tracking what jobs are getting created or destroyed. Like the O*NET database with all of the tasks and different jobs, that's been rarely updated. It's super low quality.

方法论提议: 用情景推导该测什么

And so what I think is really useful is to think about like, what are the potential scenarios — and we'll be talking about a lot of scenarios — mapping them out, and to say, "What dimension of scarcity will generate that scenario?"

So, if there's full employment, we could talk about the relational sector or something like that. If there's, you know, labor share collapses, we can talk about other sorts of scenarios. And then that will tell us what data we should be collecting.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

最反直觉的发现藏在这段访谈的褶皱里: **李嘉图200年前的悲观预测在"事实层面"几乎全对——他点名的那些工作确实都被自动化了。** 我们却习惯性地把他作为"被历史证伪的反面教材"。这是一种典型的**幸存者叙事偏差**: 我们记住了"工业革命让大家更富有"的结论, 却抹掉了过程中那条被吞噬的具体职业链。换句话说, 李嘉图并不是预测错了, 他只是**没预测到人类会发明出"服务"这个逃生通道**。今天关于AI的乐观派, 其实是在赌同一张牌——赌人类总能在被吃掉的旧职业之外, 长出我们现在还想象不到的新需求形态。

item.title: [Business of Sport] Should the Olympics Pay Athletes, The Problem Facing Women’s Football, & F1 Lands Another Mega Deal
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：女足"生产者利益"陷阱与奥运"生产者利益"悖论是镜像的两极

主持人在节目里同时讨论了两件看似无关的事——Kirsty Coventry 拒绝向奥运选手付奖金，与英国女足超级联赛过度向球员倾斜薪资。表面看，一个是"该付而不付"，一个是"付得太多"，恰好相反。但它们本质上是同一种结构性病灶：决策权与消费者之间的链条断裂。

  • 奥运：IOC 总裁靠各国小型联合会的投票当选 → 钱必须流向"选票"而不是"运动员"或"观众"。
  • 女足：Karen Carney 报告把"为球员创造世界级条件"放在第一位，"球迷"在排序末尾 → 钱流向球员而非产品打磨。

两者都是 producer interest 凌驾于 consumer interest 之上，区别只是"生产者"是谁——选票生产者 vs. 球员生产者。这暴露了一个被人忽视的系统真相：任何运动的崩坏，最早可观察到的信号都是"它的预算结构里，最终消费者排第几"。

二、跨领域连接：F1 的复活与 Liberty Media 的"望远镜反转"，其实和女足的失败、奥运的傲慢用的是同一把尺子

节目里有一句话被埋得很深但极其关键——Charles 说，Liberty Media 接手 F1 时"把望远镜倒过来看了"。Bernie 时代是先问"我们怎么赚钱"，Liberty 时代是先问"消费者要什么，再回推到生产者怎么受益"。

把这个望远镜放到节目里讨论的每一个案例上，结果惊人地一致： - 阿森纳 vs. PSG：阿尔特塔的防守体系是"生产者控制"——他控制结构，让球员服从；PSG 则是"消费者驱动"——速度、个性、解谜过程让观众上瘾。所以即便阿森纳赢得联赛，他们仍卡在"无法松开缰绳"的莫里尼奥困境里。 - 南安普顿：Dragan Solak 的声明完全是写给球迷看的"围城心态动员"，但忽略了一件事——客场球迷下赛季会让 Tonda Rekord 无法在替补席执教。他在用生产者的逻辑预判消费者的容忍度，必败。 - Enhanced Games：它能威胁奥运，不是因为"嗑药更刺激"，而是因为它直接把"运动员"当成消费者来对待——付钱、给舞台、给世界纪录奖金。Kirsty 还在用百年前生产者叙事时，Enhanced Games 已经在用 Liberty 的望远镜了。

意外的洞见：F1 不是被 Drive to Survive 救活的。Drive to Survive 只是结果。真正的转折点是 Liberty 进 paddock 那一天，他们决定把"摘下头盔、让 Günther Steiner 上镜"放在"保护车队尊严"之前。那一刻，F1 从一项运动变成了一个产品。 奥运、女足、南安普顿都还没有自己的"摘头盔时刻"。

三、被埋藏的系统性真相："激活的受众" ≠ "粉丝基础"——这是体育产业最致命的指标谎言

Charlie 提到一个被绝大多数体育分析师混用的概念区分： - Activated audience（被激活的受众）：欧洲杯夺冠后涌入女足赛场的人群；Drive to Survive 上线后突然关心 F1 的人；奥运四年一次盯着电视的人。 - Fan base（真正的粉丝基础）：周二晚上下雨还会买票、会因为输球难过一整周、会跟同事吵架捍卫球队的人。

令人意外的连接：女足的 41% 上座率依赖阿森纳 → 不是女足在增长，而是阿森纳借了男队的部落主义遗产把"被激活的受众"暂时拉进了女足场馆。也就是说，女足的"增长"其实是男足部落主义的外溢，一旦阿森纳成绩波动，整个数据立刻塌方。

这同样解释了： - 为什么奥运运动员是奥运选手中最危险的群体——他们是"被激活的受众"产物，每四年才有一次价值变现窗口，所以 Enhanced Games 一出价就动摇。 - 为什么 F1 能把 Gucci、Revolut、Anthropic 这些非传统赞助商收入囊中——它在过去 10 年系统地把"被激活的受众"转化成了"会在每个周日定时关心积分榜"的粉丝基础。赞助商不是为受众数量付费，而是为受众的"重复行为概率"付费。

关键的反常识结论：今天所有声称"高速增长"的运动（女足、女篮、电竞、padel），如果不能在 5 年内把"激活的受众"转化为"基础粉丝"，它们的崩塌曲线会比上升曲线更陡峭——因为投资逻辑是按"激活"定价的，但运营成本是按"基础"积累的。女足现在所处的位置，就是这条剪刀差的起点。

📝 转录文本

开场：Kirsty Coventry 再次"语出惊人"

One of our favourite breakdown contributors, IOC president Kirsty Coventry has been back at it. It's a very simple quote actually: "I don't believe in paying athletes." It's so good isn't it?

It's come to something where the greatest joy that football brings you is a club that you don't like not winning. It's horrible, that's a horrible side of me.

Meanwhile, Arsenal fans just aren't thinking about Chelsea at all. I know. It's just like Chelsea what? Chelsea whom? The road to irrelevance is a painful one for you to travel.

What we're going to look at now is what women's professional football has done and ultimately why it is failing. This is not about being pro or anti women's sport. This is about how to create a sport which actually has a meaningful commercial future.

Gucci and Formula One — not as a minority partner on a car, but actually taking full control. What we're talking about here is something much more sustainable, which is that global…

But first, big announcement alert: We are launching on Substack.

一、寒暄与 PSG 帽子的"复仇"

Salut Charlie, comment s'est passé ton weekend? Très bien monsieur, merci.

And you, Charlie, are looking, I'd say, full of the joys of spring. You're looking very trim. And after my rather unfortunate comments last week about your beach garb, you're looking quite suave today — until you put on your Paris Saint-Germain hat.

My God, what a weekend. Yeah, I mean, it's come to something where the greatest joy that football brings you is a club that you don't like not winning.

Do you know what? I don't know why this one in particular got so deep into me. Firstly, for context, I haven't gone out and bought a hat. I was given it in our secret Santa in the office by one of our French investors who knows I hate Paris Saint-Germain — and as a Chelsea fan, was obviously reveling in the Club World Cup win for far too long over him. Now he's getting his payback.

But it did come in handy when I could come into the office on Monday and put this on with a few Arsenal fans in the office.

I don't know what it is. They brought out a really nasty side. I've never really supported the international team over an English team in the final. I think it's the fact that once someone does it once — like now Arsenal have won the Premier League — I don't really mind that much if they go and win it again, because I lose that kind of "oh, it's been 22 years." It's the same with Liverpool.

The fact that Arsenal have never won the Champions League, and as a Chelsea fan, you know, you say you're the only team in London to win a European Cup. I think that has a huge impact on it.

So it's keeping you going during these dark nights of Chelsea's demise. I know — it's just these little things you're clinging on to to try to sort of stay relevant, stay part of the conversation. It's horrible. It's a horrible side.

Meanwhile, Arsenal fans just aren't thinking about Chelsea at all. I know. They're just — it's just like, Chelsea what? Chelsea whom? Why would they?

Yeah, the road to irrelevance is a painful one for you to travel. Oh, completely. But it's good for your soul. It's good for my soul. It is good for your soul. And I'm glad you're looking so perky. Thank you. I've got to have fun in some way.

You got a haircut? No. That's probably just the beanie that's… Oh, right. It's been flattened out by the Paris Saint-Germain beanie. Exactly.

二、PSG vs Arsenal：欧冠决赛复盘

What did you think of the game?

I thought it was a really good football match for what it's worth. That kind of clash of styles, seeing one team willing to sort of organize themselves to resist what had looked like an unstoppable force, but still have to pose some sort of threat going in the opposite direction — which I think they did periodically.

And then Paris Saint-Germain — watching them work out the puzzle, watching them work out just how fast they were going to need to move the ball to really unlock Arsenal. And they did. And that was, I think, overall, I thought it was a deserved victory.

If somebody had said "that game is a 1-0 to Paris Saint-Germain or 2-1 to Paris Saint-Germain," I think that was probably about right. Arsenal didn't create enough in the game to really be able to honestly stand up and say "we deserve to win it." But compared to a lot of recent matches in the Champions League where other teams had come up against PSG, it was by no means a walkover. So there was no shame in it.

I thought it was a grim game, to be honest, and I actually quite like the attritional ones, but that's brilliant. That, in as many words, I think says what a great job Arsenal did.

Everyone criticising the team and the match really just has to look at what happened last year, when Inter Milan — who won a breakneck semi-final against Barcelona, if you remember, where they just went toe-to-toe — then went into the final with a similar approach and got done 5-0.

And Declan Rice, after the game, said, "Genuinely, what do you expect us to do? We come out here and play like you want us to play and they'll go and score five like they did against Bayern Munich." So Arsenal did exactly what they had to do.

From the structural side it was unbelievable discipline that they showed throughout that whole match. And you know what, penalties — they can go any way. Gabriel I felt really bad for. What a season, what a game he had. He made a couple of outrageous blocks to be the one that carries that. It was just really unfortunate.

But it was nice to see them go straight into their parade the day after and kind of keep the vibe high.

阿森纳的"莫里尼奥困境"

Yeah, I think the vibe is high. Speaking to a lot of Arsenal-supporting friends and family over the last few days — it's still been a fantastic season for them. And there's also this sense from them: it's still a youngish team. "We can now use this as a springboard to go forward."

And that's really the question that I have. I expressed it last week and I saw in the various comments from Arsenal fans — they almost take it as read that they are going to improve as an attacking force. But that's actually more of a challenge than people think. Because if you are fundamentally effective defensively, primarily because you are so structured and organized, loosening those shackles is not quite as easy as one might think.

It's effectively what you call a sort of Mourinho problem. Right — that's how Jose used to be. And quite often, rather than Mourinho's teams progressing, quite often by the second or third season, they were actually starting to regress. Because actually, every single match is based on discipline, structure, concentration. It's quite hard to have those.

The real champion teams that really do build long-lasting dynasties have protracted periods. City have had this in recent years, and Liverpool had it one or two seasons, where for five or six games in a row you're just winning three or four nil. With 25 minutes to go, you're taking your best players off. You're then able to just rest easy, just go into sort of nice rest football, and then sort of think about the next game.

Whereas it still feels to me at the moment like every game that Arsenal play against anyone in the Premier League — as we saw against Wolves recently, Wolves being the worst team in the Premier League — Arsenal didn't find it all that straightforward, because they don't have that break-off way of playing.

I suspect that Arteta partially is a reflection of his own personality, but it's also a partial reflection of "what would happen if I did let the break off? That might be a dangerous thing to do, because it doesn't mean that we'll necessarily immediately become free-scoring, but it might make us easy to score against." But now the monkey's off the back, and it'll be interesting to see if that being achieved allows him to maybe be a little bit more free and test what happens next.

Look, they're going to have the money. They're going to have the money. It looks like they're going to do record revenues for the Premier League era this year. They'll go into the summer well-oiled, well-backed, and if they can get anything in that attacking line that does make them stronger, then…

But it probably is going to be a matter of individuals, isn't it? Because I don't see him changing the structure. I don't see it suddenly becoming a PSG — fast-moving players rotating in all sorts of different areas. I just don't see him doing that.

Anyhow, suffice to say it has been a fantastic season for Arsenal. And when you looked at the celebrations afterwards — the parade and all that sort of stuff — Arsenal is a very big club. It's a huge club. And it's perhaps finally a mixture of getting things right off the pitch, which has been a decade's worth of strategy, combined with eventually getting things right on the pitch, which took probably a bit longer than it should have done but nonetheless they got there in the end.

Suddenly now you're looking at them going, "Wow, they've really diversified their fan base in the last 10 to 15 years." Does that translate through into, on the playing side, really having a protracted period of dominance? Let's see.

三、英超在欧战的统治与"意甲式衰败"的警告

There was an element of relief to UEFA that we didn't have three English winners. You and I spoke briefly about this this week — the dominance that England has shown in the Conference League and the Europa League.

At Champions League level, what you've got is a very small number of clubs who are financially able to compete with the very biggest English clubs — primarily by screwing over their own domestic league. In those leagues, they're the ones taking the lion's share of the revenue, which then makes their own league less competitive, which means over time their own leagues are losing TV money, which means in the end it'll be harder and harder for them to keep up with the English Premier League clubs.

But obviously Barça, Real, PSG and Bayern, and occasionally Inter, are able to compete with that rank of top English Premier League clubs.

I think probably one of the reasons is that in their own domestic leagues, they're not challenged quite so seriously week in, week out, so they can rest a lot of players. I saw some amazing statistics about how often some of the PSG starters actually do start in Ligue 1 — nine times Dembélé had this season.

Imagine that in the English Premier League. Imagine Arsenal saying, "Well, actually, we sort of think Declan probably shouldn't start more than 15 matches this season because we know we've got that title locked down already, and what we really want Declan fresh for is the Champions League." Chance would be a fine thing. That's just simply not going to happen.

But I think the bigger story is outside of what might be called the top 10 clubs in Europe — which we've mentioned before in terms of the money list, the power list. That top 10 is spread quite equally between English clubs and non-English clubs. The issue, and I remember us covering the topic at the time, is when you then go from 10th to 40th, those 30 clubs include the entire of the English Premier League outside of the big ones who are in the top 10.

So it really is very, very dramatic when you see the spending capacity of a club like Villa, and compare it against what might be thought to be peer-group clubs in other major European leagues — Villa are able to spend two to three times as much. It's really, really dramatic.

If Villa hadn't won the Europa League, Forest would have done, and that's true. And frankly, if you look at the Conference League — the Conference League could be won probably by any other club in the Premier League, going right down to the clubs that are relegated. Chuck Wolves in the Conference League, arguably, they'd have a decent chance. They'd have a good shot.

To answer your original question — yes, I'm sure that UEFA were relieved that the English Premier League teams didn't win all the…

1989 年的意甲幽灵

Do you know the last time that a country supplied all three of the winning teams in European competition? Would it be Italy? Yes. Take a guess. Which year? Think about their era of dominance was pre-that. 2003? That was way before that.

Yeah, it's 1989, which is right in the middle of Serie A's real era of dominance, which by the turn of the century was already dissipating. I think AC Milan won the European Cup that year. I couldn't tell you who the other two were. Answered on a postcard.

But what's interesting about that is that at that moment of absolute dominance, Italian football was still only a decade away from losing it all.

So I think — and I hope — that English football is better prepared for really building off the back of it, rather than seeing itself diminish. And as long as it doesn't dent the quality of our Premier League… which is a bit of a challenge when you've got half the division — I think nine out of the 20 teams in the Premier League are playing European football next year. Will that lead to more Premier League matches where there is a bit of a drop-off in quality, certainly in entertainment terms, because some of the players are a bit more tired? Based on what we've been saying for the last few weeks, you'd imagine so, wouldn't you? Possibly, probably, yes. But anyway, a story for another day.

四、南安普顿、Dragan Solak 与"我很抱歉你不开心"式声明

Quick look at the Southampton situation. As of this morning, Tuesday morning, Dragan Solak, the owner, released a statement yesterday on the state of the last few weeks and how the club's looking to move forward. He said some interesting words, so I'll read them out:

"I'd like to address the EFL decision one final time before we close this chapter. We accept that mistakes have been made at the club level, rules have been broken, there must be consequences, and I'll take responsibility for that. But I also believe the sanction imposed is grossly disproportionate. The level of breach combined with the outcome of previous cases makes this feel incredibly harsh. It's the fans as well as the players who suffer the most, many of whom have already made arrangements for Wembley once the semi-finals went ahead. I also believe that the competition itself loses credibility. Suggesting that decisions made off the pitch can see results on the field simply wiped out cannot be good for the game."

Do you know there's a situation where people say, "I'm sorry you're upset"? Yeah, that's exactly it, isn't it? Right? "I'm sorry you're upset." And you sort of actually — I'd rather you hadn't said anything at all. I'd rather you just kept your mouth shut, because that makes me feel both simultaneously unheard but also patronized. So please shut up.

In this situation, I think honestly, he is speaking there — and I'm not saying this is wrong, by the way; as a former communications advisor I might very well say this to him — that is addressed to his fan base. That's what it's addressed to. It's basically saying, "Right, come on guys, we're all one, we're all here, we've been harshly treated, let's get a siege mentality going."

He subsequently goes on to brief that he's not going to be firing or dismissing Tonda Ekert, which is massively surprising, because clearly what happened was gross misconduct. No one can debate that.

Now, Tonda Rekord might be dealt with separately by the FA, and Southampton as a football club may be put in a situation where Tonda Rekord effectively is unable to exercise his duties. That might be preferable financially for Southampton than actually firing him and going through that process.

But putting all that to one side, what that message is, is basically saying: "Right, we've taken the punishment, and there's nothing we're going to do to change that. But I need to use this moment to reunify the club behind its leadership."

Events of the next weeks and months — I wouldn't want to be an executive in Southampton right now, because when these things hang over clubs, they just occupy vast amounts of mind space, negative energy, meetings, lawyers, etc. It's just a horrible, horrible time.

Whatever is said externally, internally those finger-pointing — obviously from people who've suffered, particularly if you're a player who's seen what could have been an enormous increase in pay then not happen — "I've suffered because of that guy, that guy, and that guy, and they're still here." So there's a big internal job that they've got to do to try to get everybody back on the same page.

He's basically highlighted all the reasons why the fans would have a right to be upset with the club and tried to put it on the EFL, saying that it's the fans that suffer. Well, yeah — they suffer because of your actions, not because of the EFL punishment. "I also believe the competition itself loses credibility." Yes — it loses credibility because of your actions, not because of how you were punished.

All for him going "we want to draw a line in the sand and move on from this" and then say "we're going to keep our manager" — basically, we know what football is like. If he isn't banned, let's say by the FA, and he is in the dugout next season, every single game, away fan bases will be doing something that will not allow this to be parked. So it seems like it's at odds with each other.

A quite extraordinary decision if they do stick by. And I suspect, as you've said, that there's an ulterior motive to doing that structurally from a business perspective.

There might be. I don't know the terms of the contract. I don't know what legal advice they've had, but it may well be that he's hoping that the FA does his dirty work for him.

But don't forget, the slight challenge here, if you are the Southampton owner, is that effectively this is a head coach who, after a significant period of underachievement at Southampton, had over a period of six months brought a high level of performance, very high level of performance — because they knew what their opponents were doing.

Very good, Charles. Over that period, they were probably the best side in the division. And you can feel him not wanting to let go of that bit of alchemy, right? Because however much we say that managers only make so much difference, however much we say that everything behind the scenes matters more — I've been there before as an owner and chief executive when you think: "We've got everything right here. The players are fit. We've got things sorted out behind the scenes. We think we've got a squad that should be doing better than this." And you're waiting for that moment of alchemy to happen when it all comes together and you then fly off like this. And that is exactly how promotions happen.

He's reached that point where he's gone, "Ooh, it's happening. It's happening. Every week, you're winning 2-0, 3-0. It's happening. We're on the magic carpet right now." And suddenly, this happens. It's like, well, you are going to get rid of your manager, aren't you? And there'll be a big bit of him thinking, "God, do I have to? I finally got this right. This finally feels right."

So we shall see what the FA does. We shall see what Southampton do in response. As you say, if he's still there next season in the Championship, then there'll be no end of jollity. But there we are. Maybe Southampton take the view that siege mentality, "let's fight together" — and that, as we said earlier on about Liverpool, that can be a very powerful thing. Completely.

五、Kirsty Coventry 与"我不相信付钱给运动员"

Charlie, my phone blew up last week. Not again. Yeah, it blew up because one of our favourite breakdown contributors, IOC president, Kirsty Coventry, has been back at it.

Oh, no. Yes. The gift that keeps on giving.

Kirsty Coventry, the head of the IOC, the president of the IOC, the lady who was so brilliant and competent that she actually beat Sebastian Coe to that job, having never had a senior executive job in her life before.

If viewers and listeners remember, early on in our tenure as breakdown co-hosts, in a press conference, when she was asked a question that she didn't know the answer to, she said that she was going to fire her press officer because they hadn't predicted the question. So since then, we've coined this phrase, "pleading a Kirsty" — which is when you haven't got a clue what you're talking about, just fire a junior member of staff. And I can tell viewers and listeners, as the ever-diminishing group of Charlie's staff demonstrates, that we've got a few things wrong and been a bit ignorant at times recently.

But yes, Kirsty has continued on her merry way. And her most recent contribution to the sports industry discourse has been to put her size… I don't know what, she's a swimmer, probably quite large feet. Size eight or nine feet. Right in another mess.

那句话：I don't believe in paying athletes

It's a very simple quote, actually. Unlike the bible I just read out, it very simply says:

"I don't believe in paying athletes. I come from a small country. I came from a sport that doesn't necessarily pay athletes very well, and I still don't think we should be paying athletes at the Olympic Games."

This was said in an interview at Eden Park in New Zealand. It was captured on a video. It went all over Twitter. It was a short conversation. And it needed more context, which was later provided — she gave clarity. She said she opposes prize money specifically for just the medals rather than broader athlete support. She's basically saying she doesn't want it to only benefit a small number of athletes.

A slightly more defensible position. That was her clarifying quote. So basically, having put a foot in it by saying that she doesn't want to pay athletes — we'll come on to the whys and wherefores of that in a moment — a big storm blew up with lots of athletes going, "Sorry, what? You really, really don't want to pay us. Okay, we thought before there was a conversation going on, but you've just literally said there's no point in the conversation. I don't want to pay athletes."

She then came up with a clarifying quote where she attempted, under heavy guidance from those communications officials that she hadn't yet fired, to put a little bit more context around it.

But what it does is it brings a spotlight back onto — Kirsty makes you laugh so much, doesn't she? It's the blithering incompetence. I just can't believe it. And also, you're looking at a comms strategy. We talked about comms strategies. When Keely Hodgkinson comes out, she goes "Eesh, this is going to age well." You've got your athletes who are massively impacted by this.

为什么这是个"活的"议题

Let's just revisit why it's a live topic. It's a live topic because the IOC, over a four-year cycle — that's the gap between the two main summer games, which also includes a winter games halfway through that cycle — over that cycle, the IOC makes around about $8 billion.

Obviously, they have some head office costs and so on, and they have a commercial department who helps make that money. The whole thing is totally legitimate. But nonetheless, $8 billion, which if you then divide it by the number of years in which it's earned, is $2 billion a year.

I think I said on a recent breakdown — put a finger in the air and say: of that money, how much of it is attributable to basically the main Summer Olympic sports, mostly being athletics, but also being Kirsty's own home sports — swimming, and one or two other of the big sports — which really command the vast majority of the attention at the Summer Olympic Games, the Summer Olympic Games in turn being miles bigger than the Winter Olympics.

Just put a finger in the air and say: let's just call it half. We're probably being a bit conservative there. It's probably more than that, but let's just call it half. So let's just say that $1 billion a year is being generated by the athletics and the swimming, the main Summer Olympic sports.

And currently, they are giving no prize money to those athletes who then create those special moments at the Olympics.

与网球大满贯对比

Some weeks ago, we were discussing the putative strike by the tennis players at the French Open. What happened to that, by the way? Anyway, that's a story for another day. Funny, I seem to watch Aryna Sabalenka an awful lot on TV for somebody who's meant to be on strike.

But we were discussing it in that context because the tennis players were saying, "We are currently given about 17.5% of Grand Slam tournament revenues, and we want to see that being more like 25%." And I discussed why I felt the Grand Slam organisers should hold firm on that and say, "You're already unbelievably well paid, and these days even down to a hundred in the world earning $600,000–$700,000 a year — how much further down that do we owe people a living? It's arguably not a lot further than that."

I'm not somebody who is one of these people that, "OK, the athletes basically deserve everything and everyone else can go hang." Because it's absolutely true that the IOC does have a duty to the broader Olympic movement. It does have all sorts of federations in parts of the world which don't necessarily produce Olympic champions, so where they put money to try and help foster athletic development. So that's all absolutely fair. And Kirsty could have made those points, I think, with a bit of clarity.

But really, instead of asking to go from 17.5% to 25%, the athletes — the runners and the swimmers and co. — are basically currently in the position of saying, "Can we just have something? Can we have something which helps us through the years after a Summer Olympics? Because we don't earn much in our year-to-year sport. This is our one moment in the sun. This is what we're all aiming for. And we're very happy to come to the Olympics. We want to be at the Olympics. But this is the moment when the event that we're taking part in collars in vast amounts of revenue, and currently we get zero."

But Kirsty tried to weaponize that in part of that conversation as well, by saying "if they want to play in nice stadiums or feature in nice stadiums and be part of these amazing events, that's what a lot of the money goes towards." But don't forget — those stadiums aren't paid for by the IOC. They're paid for by the host city. Sorry, Kirsty, I hate to keep on picking you up on your own blithering incompetence, but that is just a total red herring.

90% 分配的迷雾

Their comeback will be that they distribute 90% of that revenue to international federations and governing bodies. Now, while that may be well and good, there is very little system of accountability within those systems as to how the athletes then get paid from that. To put it mildly.

So you don't have a situation where your athletes are then really benefiting from that. That will go into so many different areas. So you're still getting a group of people expected to dedicate an entire — well, a young — life to featuring in a games that will leverage them as brands. Their name, image, likeness will be completely exposed by the organization, and they will receive no money for competing in it.

The money that they may receive — World Athletics led the charge on this in Paris in 2024. They said that they will pay, and they did pay, $50,000 to a gold medal in athletics. That's good. It's a good start. But the reality, again, of what $50,000 does for you in the context of how much you've had to put into your training and career to that date is very, very small.

Matt Richards 的 15,000 英镑

Matt Richards — British swimmer, gold medalist, brilliant guy. Had him on the show. I picked out a comment that he said when I spoke with him a few months ago. He said:

"I was on £15,000 when I won gold. And you have to be —"

And when he says "I was on £15,000," that is the allowance that was being given to him by the British Olympic Association.

"I was on £15,000 when I won gold. And you have to be a multi-gold medal winner in multiple years to get to the top band of funding, which is £28,000."

That's what Adam Peaty level. This is 23 years old, double Olympic gold medalist. He'll be going again in LA. He's been very vocal actually since. He's actually been very prominent this week. Super smart, really invested in all of this. And there's no inflammatory comments coming out around it. It's very much baked in the theory.

历史背景：从 1000 万到 80 亿

You can't live off the legacy of the values the Olympics was built on in the early 1900s. I actually had a look and saw how the revenue-generating commercial entity of the Olympics has changed throughout that time.

In 1950, the budget for the Olympic Games was $10 million, which is the equivalent of $140 million today. From the 70s, 80s onwards, you started to build a commercial powerhouse. The level of production that goes into it, the stadia that goes into it, the sponsorship, all of this — the minute you create that, you have to start paying the key contributors to that, which is still the athletes. Whatever that percentage is to make that make sense, fine, you guys go and have that discussion. But with that being zero, in a sport where this is the pinnacle of your athletic prowess…

People have thrown this back recently. They said, "Yeah, but the Ryder Cup — we have this issue every time." It's like — ah, but it's different, because do you know what McIlroy and DeChambeau and all of that lot are getting alongside it? $5 million paychecks pretty much every time they turn up on a golf course or at least win a golf tournament. So this, for the athletes, is their opportunity to make money. That's their cycle. And outside of that cycle, it's few and far between.

Enhanced Games 的阴影

What happens — and the reason, Charlie, finally why I find this even more extraordinary — is a timing perspective. Because what did we talk about last week in trying to defend the purity of athletics? We talked about the Enhanced Games. The Enhanced Games happened. Kirsty makes these comments.

If I'm an athlete and I'm looking at this — she's sitting here at the pinnacle of my elite performance, and these guys are sitting here going, "I'll pay you a million dollars to break a world record, 250 grand to win an event, and some, and some." I'm starting to see this come a little bit closer together. If I'm an athlete who is sitting here going, "I cannot make this work under current circumstances" — and that's the threat.

"如果我是 Kirsty 的公关顾问"

Let me try and imagine myself to be in the unfortunate position of being one of Kirsty's communications people. I'm just asking you to imagine that for a moment. So you're in some office at IOC Central, knowing that if any point in time your boss makes a mistake, you're probably going to get fired. And in any case, even if it isn't your mistake, she's going to go and say something stupid anyway.

But nonetheless, you are basically having to give advice. So I think this is what I would say:

"I don't think it's appropriate for the Olympics to itself pay prize money, because this is the pinnacle of the sport, and we want people to come here for the greater glory. And furthermore, if we put all the prize money into that one event, those athletes who happen to be injured at that particular time are basically losing out on that money for the entire cycle. And that just makes it massively disproportionate. Just one mistake in one event and suddenly they don't earn anything. So instead, what we're going to do is distribute portions of the IOC's earnings to the governing bodies of those sports, and make sure that they spread that as prize money in the intervening years in between our Summer Olympics, to make sure that our athletes are able to put bread on the table during that period, to make sure they're able to come back as good as they possibly can be."

I think that would be a totally logical thing. If they said, "Right, OK, who have we got here? We've got the IAAF — sorry, World Athletics. Is it called World Athletics? World Athletics. So we are going to work with them on how that is apportioned throughout the cycle. We're going to work with these various different bodies on how they use IOC money to make sure the athletes are earning okay in between the Olympic Games."

I think that would be a perfectly logical and reasonable thing to do. And they could then still keep the Olympics itself as something which is unpaid.

What is not tenable is for them to say — remember that the Olympic Games does not exist in a commercial vacuum.
