As my colleague CTO Shubo said in the little launch video of the Series A, it lets us broaden our dreams.

市场质疑：$200M 估值的合理性

Brandon: But still, $200 million and I guess a $1.6 billion valuation — how is there a market for that? I mean, obviously you're not doing this just for the fun of proving things, although I'm sure there's a lot of that.

Carina: So let's bring us back to 2024. When O1 recently models like just came out, what was Anthropic kind of like secretly working on back then? It was coding.

And everyone knows they're working on coding. Like OpenAI, Meta, X — everyone has full knowledge that Anthropic was working on coding, and they just like overlooked it. They thought, oh, there are a B2B place. They just want one vertical. People think of coding as one vertical.

And now look at where we are today: coding kind of like strong transfer learning from coding to reasoning, to basically a monopoly in the future of reasoning. And I think that's really really shocking. The people who are working on coding back then believe in something that we believe — similarly with math and Lean now — which is that if you have more structured and formal data, it's going to be a lot more horizontal than the specific vertical we are tackling.

So if today we are doing math in informal way like the standard chain-of-thought data, train a math model based on human preference, then I would say, well, perhaps we are just a math startup, right? But while we are pursuing math, we are also doing things that do have transfer learning to other domains.

So I think that's the broader picture: while the DNA of the company remains math, and all of us are math nerds — this is a very strong culture statement, everyone has a great mission of having AI be a superhuman mathematician, like we are seeing on Putnam, on a batch of research conjectures (in fact, we have another batch coming) — we're also thinking that this is going to be fundamental to verified reasoning.

关于 verified AI 与前沿实验室

Brandon: I have several things I want to — so I want to hear about the verified AI. I do want to dig in a little bit. So do we know that Anthropic and OpenAI and everyone — they're not doing formal verification and using that for their rollouts and whatever?

Carina: I think I have a lot of like rumor mill that I probably shouldn't put on the record. Like researchers talk, they play card games.

But there are really interesting reasons if they are or not doing it. I think that's the takeaway I have: if you're at a frontier lab, the direction actually does change a lot for a lot of reasons beyond your control.

AlphaProof 时刻与组合数学的瓶颈

So I want to bring us back to the AlphaProof moment. AlphaProof was such an amazing — really the 2024, 28 out of 42 performance was the IMO moment for me. It was not gold in 2025 because across 2024 and 2025, AI models could solve all the problems that are not combinatorics. The only difference is that if you get all the problems that are not combinatorics, you get 28 in 2024 and 35 in 2025 because there's only one combinatorics question in 2025.

After AlphaProof, we didn't see a lot of the formal math results or progress from Google DeepMind. And that's actually because of reasons that are not necessarily technical. But if you're at a startup and you have very singular focus that is formal math and verified AI, then you get to work on really cool problems for a long time and you have a lot higher likelihood to get to where you want to be in terms of progress and breakthrough unlock.

形式化验证：历史与误解

Brandon: So just define that for us.

Carina: A lot of people think about formal verification as an ancient subject. It existed as long as — way before deep learning. It existed in the time of rule-based computer science.

There's this really strong push of formal verification since 1980s. Really interesting historic anecdotes such as: I think the Paris trade union demanded that the automatic switching of the subway system needs to be formally verified for safety purpose. So quite interesting trade union for technology.

Around the time of Challenger, both before and after, European Space Agency was using formal verification for the Ariane spacecraft. It's also interesting — Boeing, Airbus, for verification.

And then more recent years, there's a lot of push about automated reasoning at AWS because they have a lot of enterprise customers that really requires things to be 100% verified, and there's no edge cases missed. Just general testing doesn't satisfy the need.

对验证本质的重新定义

So a lot of people think about verification as something that's annoying because it's like tax and compliance — like it's making sure that we are good to go. And that's really not the [point].

We talked about verification — I think our competitor when they launched, they talked about formal verification pre-reasoning, they talked about it in the time of hallucination. And maybe for them, formal verification is about the lousiness, the hallucination. For us, no — for us, verified AI is about the brilliance. It's about scaling and compounding super intelligence.

Ramanujan 的例子：验证如何放大天才

So this is quite a deep point, and sometimes it takes a little bit of explanation. If you think about the place of brilliance — for example, Ramanujan, he's a brilliant mathematician. He was able to find a lot of interesting formulas just by intuition before he knew how to do proofs. So he went to Cambridge, worked with Hardy and Littlewood. In the famous movie The Man Who Knew Infinity, there's this storyline of how hard it was for Hardy to force him to no longer rely on intuitions and do proofs.

After he learned proof writing, he came out as a much more powerful mathematician whose intuitions turn into theorems, and future generations of mathematicians build on those theorems. So it is a way to scale and compound the intelligence that we already have.

数学家其实一直在"写代码"

Another example: mathematicians kind of have been writing code in English or their respective countries' natural language for thousands of years. And why do I call it writing code? Because there's this sort of community standard of rigorous logical deduction. Everything has to be step-by-step correct. Otherwise you will get outcasted by your math community.

Brandon: The law... well, rules in the community.

Carina: So it's interesting because that is human mathematician enforced. It's a peer review process. Peer review of a paper currently takes two years.

为什么数学家需要 Lean

But proof assistant and formal proof checkers like Lean still found its place. Why? If I'm a mathematician and any of my work can be peer reviewed by other humans, why do we even — why do mathematicians even play with Lean? Why do we even talk about Lean-based assisted theorem proving?

It's because it handles a low level. For example, we're not even talking about AI, we're talking about the grind tactic in Lean. It can currently handle a lot of math proofs at a very low level. And this is pretty shocking because I have seen actually another company working in the same space — some of their demo — and I look at the demo, like it can actually completely be handled by grind, which is a tactic in Lean.

Brandon: Can you explain what Lean is to non-experts?

Carina: Okay. Yeah. I think our order is a little wrong.

So Lean is a computer program for math proofs. It is a formal language, just like its cousin Isabelle, CoQ, or Rocq, and some other further cousins like Dafny, Agda. Like these formal languages whole sector.

Brandon: And what does it do?

Carina: It basically — if you have a proof written in a program in Lean, and assuming there's no weird things happening (like use of sorry, which is a tactic that lets you take things for granted), assuming everything is safe — hence people have tools like Compare Tour, Safe Verify, and Axiom recently rolled out Verify Proof that's like 100 times faster than Compare Tour — then once you execute that program, once it compiles and it tells you that it's correct, then the proof is actually correct.

Brandon: So just like a type checker.

Carina: Yeah, that is based on this result called Curry-Howard correspondence, which turns proofs into programs.

Lean 的魔力：编程 + 数学 二合一

So I want to talk about the magic of Lean. Why I think it's a really good programming language: because on one hand, if you don't care about the formal part at all, if you don't care about the logic part, you just want to use Lean to write code, you can. We have had candidates — currently the person is working at the Lean FRO — he wrote autograd in Lean in our interview process.

Brandon: So it's a Turing-complete language.

Carina: That's right. You can do a lot of things with Lean. It's a functional programming language. You can use it to do coding, you can use it to do math — two in one.

And going back to what I was getting at: if mathematicians are already enforcing that most proofs — say maybe not all mathematicians, but by the ivory tower and people in academia, all proofs are correct — why do we even need Lean, the theorem checker? It's because Lean has tactics that help them handle the low-level calculation or proof or deduction (not calculation), then for them to be able to navigate in a high-level intuition space.

TAM：所有代码都是市场

So this is my point: that it is not about formal verification or verified AI to us — it's not just about handling or kicking out the lousiness, the hallucinations, the mistakes. It's about scaling brilliance. It's about super intelligence.

Terence Tao has a great video also about using Lean as a way you can collaborate. That's another point I want to talk about. A lot of people think about — what is our market? It has to be some really niche industrial societies area that is mission critical, safety critical. No, that's not the TAM. The TAM is all code.

The TAM is a right of first refusal on all AI-generated code. Right of first refusal meaning you get to choose whether you want to verify it.

So this is the important part I want to come across: people talk about formal verification as almost like painful because it has all these stringent requirements —

Brandon: Up until now it has been.

Carina: Yes. And to us, it's actually verified generation means performance gain. It means higher sample efficiency. It means a startup like us — with still, we raise some money, but lesser compute budget, lesser data budget than a frontier lab — will be able to match, even exceed, performance on superhuman tasks.

Putnam 成绩：120/120 vs 人类最高 110

In fact, for the Putnam exam that we just competed in December 2025, which we did in real time — MathArena, which is this organization that evaluates a lot of LLMs, found the best LLM, DeepSeek, got 103 points out of a 120-point exam. The best human (obviously we now know is a student from either MIT or Chicago — we don't know which one because they don't announce the top five winner score) got 110, and we got 120.

So it's the first time — actually, I remember when we were starting this, people were like, "is it even possible that a formal math system with so much orders of magnitude less data can match or beat an informal LLM?" And Putnam is the first time it beat.

So we're not thinking about it just about the painfulness, the challenges it pose. We are thinking about the verified generation performance gain, the improvement — the fact that you can, just like you would expect RL for Lean to have improvement because of seeing evidence of our own coding.

与前沿实验室的技术差异

Brandon: So maybe we can talk a little bit about — can you describe what is different about what you do versus what the frontier labs do when they're building their standard RL-enhanced LLMs? What's different about what you do?

Carina: Yeah. So we heavily rely on a kind of data called Lean data. We talked about Lean is all the data that we have — that's Lean proofs. You know it's correct or not, and that's quite important.

So we have a system of models. These models are post-trained using RL or SFT.

Brandon: So LLM, some sort of foundation model that you get off the shelf, and you post-train it.

Carina: Yeah. And there's obviously an inclination for open-source base models.

Brandon: So it does speak English, probably knows how to code, but you fine-tune it or continue.

Carina: And the base model might be similar to what everyone else is saying as well. If they're not pre-training their model. And then we basically do this RL for formal math. There's a standard pipeline or tricks of the trade that people use. We try to innovate really on top of it as much as we can.

I think that we found scaling inference to have almost no wall, recursively decomposing a proof goal into many subgoals, and then learning to backtrack as well.

分布偏移与领域覆盖

Brandon: Is there a risk that you start out with what you know in a certain domain of datasets, and then you start rolling out recursively in a space, but now all of your training data is localized in some domain that you — it still is only so, like maybe logarithmically in some large space growing from your initial training data. So you could get trapped essentially in that — you could be really good at this, but you just created a big jagged frontier where some other domains are just far from them.

Carina: Distribution shift, we're talking about. So yes, it is an open question whether a system that can do really well in number theory can do well in another field of math.

Well, actually I think the way we think about it is: it depends. It depends on whether topology has a lot of the existing definitions as almost like the math infrastructure existing. Because what people have found in the past is when people were building out math, algebra, bookwork — they can just —

Brandon: So Mathlib being the Lean undergraduate library kind of stuff. So it's like all the proofs that you learn in undergraduate math, and they're all sort of in Lean.

Carina: Yeah. So for example, some of my friends who currently are at Axiom — it's crazy, like full circle back moment. Kenny — we're friends for like five, six years — and he was the first one to tell me about Lean. He was working with Kevin Buzzard to build out Mathlib.

It's a lot easier to codify algebra in Mathlib than for analysis. For analysis, a lot of the definitions around convergence, limits, etc. becomes tricky. And so I don't think there's a lot of topology in Mathlib today, in terms of differential topology, differential geometry kind of stuff. So our system likely will not do very well on those domains because it doesn't even have definitions to build off on top of.

For the places where the definitions are in, we actually are doing quite okay in terms of distribution diversity. We have good performance — having solved open research questions in number theory, commutative algebra, algebraic geometry, some discrete math that comes, and probability.

组合数学：AI 的薄弱环节

Brandon: So earlier you said that with the Putnam exam, the 2024 version — when all of the questions that AlphaProof did not get right, for the IMO, all of the ones it got wrong were in combinatorics. Is there a weakness there in that specific domain?

Carina: I would say so. For Olympiad math, people are seeing combinatorics being a little bit more tricky. Seems like the steps are quite creative.

For — I'm a human, and when I have friends who are really good at combinatorics (which I never consider myself really the top of combinatorics — I'm kind of better at number theory), but I know some people who are just — they're IMO gold perfect score, Putnam Fellow perfect score, and like all the way — and then when they do tricks in combinatorics, I'm like, "I don't know how you thought of that." But after you give me that construction, it actually becomes a lot more tractable.

I think a Lean-based system will struggle in those very creative places, which is why we at Axiom actually also invest on something called mathematical discovery.

开源数学发现工具

It's not used, and we have some major news in the coming weeks — basically open-sourcing entire code bases of mathematical discovery coming up.

Brandon: You want to tell us a little bit?

Carina: Yeah, sure. We are currently having two code bases being open-sourced. So the goal is for if you're a mathematician or you're a theoretical physicist and you have a problem that you would like to solve — for example, you want to find a construction that is a very complicated graph construction — then we would suggest you follow the very detailed manual intended for mathematicians to run the code that we write. It's a tool for mathematicians to make mathematical discoveries.

什么是 mathematical discovery

Mathematical discoveries is this idea that proof is not enough for math. In fact, before you start proving something, you don't know where you want to start. So you will try to construct some interesting examples. These can usually be sequences — if you want to understand the property of a sequence, you will write out a few of the first terms. This can also be graphs. So if you want to figure out what the graph that you're looking for should have, say, a certain property, then you will start by doing some simpler version of the graph.

Now, constructions cannot be done by Lean. So we believe in having AI for math discovery. And we have one of the OGs in that field — Charton, member of technical staff at Axiom — and he previously have done PatternBoost and end-to-end, settled, disproved a 30-year-old conjecture by finding a counter example. Found the solution to 130-year-old problem, the global Lyapunov function that is a kind of mathematical object showing up in the three-body problem.

So we are thinking that mathematical discovery tools should be open to the math community. So we are open-sourcing entire code bases for that.

Brandon: So discovery meaning it makes new conjectures?

Carina: That's a — yeah, it's a pre-conjecturing step actually.

Brandon: Oh, I see.

Carina: You start to form intuitions. If you're a mathematician and your goal is to solve a really hard conjecture X — improver can't just solve it for you. You might want to try to formulate some sort of lemmas, conjectures, that you want to then give to Axiom Prover.

If you're a human mathematician, you will start by wanting to formulate that conjecture. You don't know where to go. You want to find constructions. Now, the code that we're going to open source is going to help you hopefully significantly.

Rice 定理与可判定性的边界

Brandon: So one thing that maybe — there's a lot of computer scientists listening, and one of the things that will immediately come up especially when you're talking about formal verification and so forth, is Rice's theorem and decidability and incompleteness theorem and some arguments about computational complexity and LLMs. So I'm curious to hear — Rice's theorem says you cannot prove non-trivial things about programs for all programs. So how are you navigating this space? Obviously formal verification is able to do some things.

Carina: Yeah. I think it's very clear that there's a theoretical result telling you you cannot formally verify all programs. But it's good to formally verify the majority of the useful programs.

I remember there's this MIT little documentary — not a documentary, like an advertisement for people who are admitted students — and then there's this famous line by Tim the Beaver, the mascot of MIT, saying, "What a theory give you." Which is kind of like, it doesn't stop us from trying to push it as much as possible.

未来愿景：vibe coding + 形式化验证

So the goal that we have for the future is: suppose you are doing the coding, you want to vibe code a really complex task. Currently it's front-end websites, but in the future we might want to vibe code much more complicated things — whole distributed systems even — then we want to be able to decompose it. There's maybe a high-level kind of sketch plan. We can make, other people can make — but say you have Claude give you, kind of break it down into 10 things, and at one point it will decide to call Axiom. And Axiom will give you a computer program that is formally verified, or it will say "this is still too hard for us."

Brandon: So you write the program, you give it to Axiom, it makes changes to it maybe?

Carina: So we're talking about two sort of phases.

It is possible that we are the verification partner. So you already have a computer program and you want us to verify it. In fact, GPT found a proof to an unsolved Erdős problem and our competitor Harmonic — Aristotle — verified it.

But we want to do verify-generation. We might want to say, hey, this little component, everything that we generate and provide for you is formally verified.

Brandon: So the idea would be you generate both. And I can imagine this fitting into the idea of a sorry...

Carina: A Lean sorry.

Brandon: A Lean sorry meaning it's a lemma that is unproven, but you're just taking it as given until you have the time to prove it. Is that a good way to think about a sorry?

Carina: That is a good way to think about a sorry, but not necessarily in the coding context.

Brandon: I can imagine you can say: assuming that this module is verified, then this module is correct. And so you can decompose a problem small enough that you can verify. Is this kind of —

Carina: So let's say we want to vibe code control flows. That's quite hard. You will likely break that down into multiple steps, and then it will continue to break down these steps into more fine-grained steps. And at one point you want something that is absolutely correct.

And then this is also something that is likely within reach. We want to generate both: a piece of computer program, and underlying is a guarantee that there is also the proof that has been generated, which tells you that the thing that you specify, this programmer can solve for you.

核心公式：anything that can be specified can be proven

So the vision we have is: anything that can be — which "anything" is, you know, it's a little bit marketing, because as you said, theoretical bound, but mostly, well, almost surely hopefully — anything that can be defined can be executed, anything that can be specified can be proven.

So the way I think about it is: if you have a program times a statement or problem, it maps to verifiability conditions times a proof. So while the program verification community has given you say the verifiability conditions (and we're trying to recruit a really strong team to help us do that), Axiom Prover is going to give you the proof.

程序如何映射到证明

Brandon: So just help me map from the program to the proof. Because I could say, you know, this two-line Lean program verifies whatever I claim it solves. How do I know that it actually verifies the thing that I think it verifies?

Carina: So for example, there's this currently this benchmark called CodeMarina. It's a code verification benchmark that's supposed to be LLM friendly. So every problem is a coding problem and the goal is to generate — there's a code part and there's a proof part, two different computer programs. And then the goal is to generate code with proof.

So the code that supposedly solves this problem and then the proof that this program indeed does solve the problem.

各家在 CodeMarina 上的成绩

Now, how do people do on this benchmark? It was rolled out, I think, by Berkeley and Meta researchers in 2025. And they found, I think, whatever version of GPT they evaluated does, like, pass@1 like 3.6%, iterative something like 22%.

Now, how do the formal math systems models do? Copra, which is a system (because in a system you iterate and define, so pass@1 doesn't quite work), but still they evaluated pass@1 of the system about 11-12%. And then also DeepSeek Prover and Goedel Prover model 11-12%.

And I think our competitor has released last year on the only-proof part 96%. And we actually recently with no modification to the Putnam system, we saw a 99% — out of the 189 problems we solved 187, we missed only two — code with proof.

为什么 Lean 适合 RL

So if you want to train something to do code with proof and you want to do reinforcement learning, it's actually quite annoying because — look, it's mixed. If you want proof to be informal math, it's very annoying because then that's like just mixed objective function. Your code is something like Python, your proof is, say, natural language math proof. You will not have very strong RL performance.

But if you have proof as Lean and you have code, you can choose Rust, which is a strongly typed language. It's more conversion. So you're going to have much better performance.

如何把程序对应到证明（追问）

Brandon: I can't wrap my head around — how do I tie... So I can say that this proof solves for math theorem. But I don't know that — like, but it's two lines in Lean, obviously it doesn't. So how do I know that the program that I wrote matches the proof that I generated?

Carina: You will basically look at the coding problem, and you look at the program, and then you try to see if it satisfies the verifiability conditions.

Brandon: But like how do I know? If I read it — I can just eyeball it. And traditionally how mathematicians have done this is they take the paper and they read it and they say, "I agree that this proof solves the problem." And then this other person says, "No wait, it doesn't, for, you know, look at this." And then people disagree and eventually there's consensus that this proof solves this problem.

So how are you —

Carina: But you check it step by step, right? Yeah. So you basically will look at the verifiability conditions and see if it does actually satisfy that.

So suppose we're looking at a piece of computer program. And then whether it does actually solve the coding problem — you will have a judgment about that.

So you will not solely rely on testing, even though that is a way.

Brandon: So somebody looks at the proof and says, "Yeah, that actually solves the problem that we think it's supposed to."

Carina: But then now you're producing a formal verification program that satisfies the verifiability conditions about this program and this statement. So again, the function is taking you from the program and the statement to verifiability conditions and proof.

规范的难题：specification problem

Brandon: Okay, so I can see how this works in a benchmark. Then if I have, let's say, a flight control system that is very —

Carina: Then the problem becomes very annoyingly — the specification, I think the word, is going to — even if we say successful, like, anything that we will have a specification problem.

So like here comes a bank saying that "please, do I have a really safe financial audit?" Sorry — "prove the financial audit for me."

Brandon: What does that mean? We can't specify. Humans are bad at specifying everything that we want.

Carina: There's always like some sort of saying that we are not specified, and if it's not specified, it's not proven.

Brandon: Okay. So what do you do about that?

Carina: Yeah. So we're not there yet.

Currently the vision as of currently is: anything that can be specified can be proven.

Now obviously there are people have been really good at — that's where maybe where that's informal kind of reasoner come in. The informal reasoner can — and this is I want to call the literature of testing. Testing are great because testing is like, "hey, have you thought about that?"

I want to highlight the work mutation-based LLM unit test generation by Axiom CTO Shubo. He was a director of Facebook AI research. The way you think about it is the AI will be like, "hey, have you thought about this case?" And so this is a little bit like conjecture.

Brandon: So the conjecture is going to help with the specification. I see. And then the prover does the proof.

Carina: And so this is an interactive process maybe, so that when we're actually giving good specific —

Brandon: I think this is the future of coding.

Carina: Yes, I think this is the future of coding. And I think this is where, even if we are — suppose, given the assumption that everything can be formally verified — studying sort of automatic task generation is still interesting because it is giving you the specification proposal.

自动形式化（autoformalization）

And then another thing is: let's talk about autoformalization, which is the ability to define it. It is kind of converting something that is more informal into something that is more formal, the formalization.

So suppose I have a coding problem that is written for ICPC, and this problem is written in English — "Alice and Bob blah blah blah." Now I want to convert that into a formal statement, like a formal spec. How do I do the autoformalization step? Right now, this is going to be hard because I have not solved the problem yet. So I don't have any signal. I don't have any grounding.

The test cases — input/output pair — is going to ground my form-most.

Brandon: I have to know I'm going to give this input, I'm going to give this output, it has to have these characteristics. And so I write test cases. And so is there equivalent in Lean of this — where the specification, where you just know the sort of outcomes that you are expecting, so that you the statement of the result — and then the proof is completely —

Carina: Quite annoying because it's like a lot of the times it's proof.

Brandon: So you don't actually have the numerical answers to ground it.

Carina: So autoformalization is quite a hard thing to do. Because what's generally happened is you just — it's hard to ground the autoformalization of a statement. You can obviously ground the autoformalization of a proof, but because you can then just run it — but you need human to eyeball it.

证明规模与扩展性

Brandon: How big is a Lean proof of a formalized program of significant size? Do they grow with the size of the program, or do they grow super-linearly?

Carina: Currently, you know, for each line of code written, there could be like 20 lines of proof.

Brandon: Okay. It's not looking that great. But is that a linear relationship, or is it as the complexity of the program gets greater, then it also grows so that it's —

Carina: That's a good — I don't have a good answer to the scaling law of that.

Brandon: Because I know that that's a problem in formal verification, right? Where you have these huge — you have to have these very very long proofs for even simple things. So are you going to run into limitations in the capabilities of LLMs when you start to get to larger —

Carina: What we believe fundamentally is we are building a reasoning engine. And we have seen Prover deal with really huge trees that are like, you know, tree of a proof. We have seen it scale from 40 nodes to 4,000 nodes.

Brandon: So wait — sorry, actually, improver is the LLM?

Carina: Axiom Prover is an ensemble system of multiple models that we do post training.

Brandon: I see, okay.

Carina: And also it also includes obviously the tools that Axiom that we have open-released, sorry.

So we have seen it being able to deal with more and more complex tasks. We don't think it's potentially bound.

You could ask, is it bounded at one point on the pre-trained base model? I think that's a good question. I think mid-training could be very interesting because it does actually — a lot of the capability gain does come from that part. If you could argue that even if you try to reinforcement learn some person who is not very talented, that person might behave, you be perform a lot less well than an unpost-trained Ramanujan. You can argue that — very very sad reality of things. So at one point we might consider doing that.

But we think there's so much to push.

Brandon: So you just feel like there's so much overhead right now or so much space to grow that you're not running into theoretical constraints at this point.

I just wonder because there's been recent results in the computational complexity of the problems that LLMs can solve fundamentally, and I don't think that they're really a concern when I'm writing code with Claude Code. But I can imagine problems becoming big enough in a system like this where you have a gazillion lines of Lean, you can't get them into the context window so you have to be smart about that, and then you have to summarize, and then you're summarizing and summarizing, and pretty soon you're kind of losing track of what's going on. And it just seems like with a very large system like that you might run into [issues].

Carina: Yeah, I think this is interesting. It's always a problem of abundance. So simple — you just like keep — really the mathematical discovery renaissance has come. Action prover does try to prove everything. You end up with like tens of thousand lines of Lean proof.

So first of all, auto-informalization is a lot easier than autoformalization minus a problem of no grounding. Every model has seen a lot of text and a lot of Lean. So you can always convert that Lean back into informal. And then there's the problem of, well, how do you know if you're correct or not? You can rely on cyclic consistency. So you then formalize again and prove program equivalent, something like that.

Brandon: Oh, so you informalize and then formalize — you can use it to make sure that you still —

Carina: Yeah. And although informalization is, obviously, a less hard problem, so you can always do that. So for a lot of the Lean code that we output, we can have an informal summarizer of big chunks of Lean. It's actually doing okay.

数学家是否会停止理解？

So there's another question of, which I think is very interesting: I think there's a panel at ICML Vancouver last year at AI for Math workshop. There's like Leo de Mura and Jeremy Avigad and Shubo, CTO, was there. And they were talking about: will humans or mathematicians at some point stop trying to understand what's going on?

Because suppose you're a really ambitious mathematician, you're like, "I want to proof read my hypothesis," and bang, here's a Lean proof, and it's actually correct, and it's just like, you know, problem, 1 million lines.

Brandon: Isn't that like a big negative for the community? Because I mean, usually when someone comes up with a big proof of something, oftentimes the process — I was about to get there.

Carina: It's like, well, will that negative outcome happen — was a question the panel was discussing. It's completely hypothetical. No one's model system can prove my hypothesis right. So the disclaimer, please don't cut that part. Just standalone.

But like, will people still trying to understand what's going on? And I think the answer is usually always yes. I think curiosity and the desire to understand what is going on, mathematically or in other domains as well, is a basic human need.

And I think that is — I think a dose of optimism in an era of, I think, verified super intelligence (suppose we get there) is that even if all the outputs are going to be produced at a much faster pace and much more exponential volume compared to what humans could possibly consume, they're still going to try to consume it. And they're still going to try to consume the ones that they deem important.

So then basically attention is the bottleneck. And if attention is the bottleneck, then really intuition and taste — of which statement is probably worth the consumption of human, and also maybe in a finite computer resource, worth the consumption, worth the spending of compute resources — that's where human mathematicians' taste will always guide us. And I think that's incredibly beautiful.

优雅证明与正交路径

Brandon: Is it worth internally taking results — so you can prove one way, and then trying to send your system at many different routes to get conceptually orthogonal proofs, so you kind of get a diverse set of different ways of reasoning about the same thing? Because I think it could be very valuable. If you give it a problem to say, "oh well, like here's kind of the brute force natural way that maybe some humans would do it, and then there's like a really much shorter elegant way of doing it." So have you essentially thought about training your models to be elegant in some ways?

Carina: Yeah, at one point we're going to get to there. Because I think the conjecture will probably depend on what we mean by taste. Elegance feels like an alignment problem to me. Who gets to say what is elegant? Humans get to say what is elegant. That's what makes human.

There's something about hard work, right? What you work on hard is what you're going to be good at. And we're going to have a problem about that, I think, in a lot of the domains as well, not just math. Like, how do you be that senior programmer with really good high-level understanding? Well, I guess full-stack understanding, high-level and low-level, if you haven't spent the year of training.

Brandon: I mean, I would argue that you don't... This is very philosophical, but like, I don't need to be good at assembly language programming. Not many people are good at that. A few people are because it's important for their job.

Carina: It's not experience but curiosity.

Brandon: But it feels to me a little different because not being good at like proving things, for example — that seems like a fundamental gap in like, maybe my mind doesn't develop in the same way if I am not doing that. Whereas if I'm just not good at assembly language programming, but I'm good at higher-level programming, so maybe that doesn't matter.

Carina: I think that's probably because of how the education system, the pipeline, works. Which is that if you do not show early signs of brilliance, you don't sometimes go through the process of pre-training in math.

So maybe you can argue that you don't need to learn everything to develop a sense of taste, but there's like a threshold you kind of need to meet. So for example, you probably need to be able to code, even if you don't need to understand assembly language. And that thing might transfer.

My intuition might transfer from the Olympiad I tried to pursue. And combinatorics transfer is more direct, it's very similar. And number theory could be further but still okay. And then when it gets to something that's a lot more different than Olympiad math, transfer is that strong, but kind of, you know, you need to diligent, as you said. You need to diligently go through some amount of training. And if people over-rely on strong AI and that doesn't happen [problem].

商业模式与估值合理性

Brandon: I want to switch gears. You mentioned software verification. What are the domains — how are you going to make enough money to justify the valuation that — and congratulations, by the way.

Carina: Thank you.

Brandon: So how, what's the — give us the high-level summary of what is the vision that you show, you put in front of investors, about why does this actually make a lot of money?

Carina: Yeah. So first of all, this round is kind of preemptive. So it's, I think, a lot of the investors have pretty high interest about Axiom.

In terms of what we believe in: we believe the future of coding is going to be somewhat constrained by verification capability. And we believe in solving formal math is a very natural starting point. And then by extension, you can increase the verification capability across hardware and software.

硬件验证：全有或全无

And for hardware, for example, that's quite revolutionary. There is no — as we know, there's no partial credit for a mostly verified GPU.

Brandon: No.

Carina: It is all or nothing. And you do need a perfect prover. Like, I want to stress this point: suppose I am someone who loves solving math. I think there are a lot of Twitter users who enjoy Pokemon-like hunting, Erdős problems, and then I just try to use a non-deterministic LLM like GPT to try to get the full proof for that. Now I can do that many many times. And I might succeed and I might not, and I might not have a problem with whether I actually succeed or not.

This absolutely does not work for hardware verification. So for those kind of domains, which I call hardcore verification needed, it is a painpoint. It is a current painpoint. There are hundreds of humans and thousands of licenses being dedicated to solve one local grid problem verification.

Brandon: Just as an aside, my understanding is that the industry standard for design-to-verification in an ASIC project is like 1 to 3 or 1 to 4 — 3 to 4.

Carina: Correct. Both in say team size and then duration.

Brandon: Right.

Carina: So if you multiply that, square — so that's a "must cover."

软件验证：选择的自由

And now for software verification, it is interesting. As probably we all realize, my nephew vibe-codes a lovable website. There is absolutely no need to formally verify that piece of code. Why would you?

Now I heard of a story from Cass actually — New York Times reporter — who told me the story which is like: however, if you think about it, in the time of agents, my open Claude can probably do all sorts of things and probably can do some bad things. Like my open Claude can decide to tag something bad to my professor. And you can say that perhaps is that a problem of formal verification? Probably still not. You can change something about the action space and make it more limited. So you don't need to rely on formal verification.

So you can have a lot of cases. But you can think about maybe an enterprise that is dealing with a lot of regulatory kind of stuff using agents — they might want to do something like — it's their choice. But I will argue that the improvement of verification capability, both in latency and in accuracy — all these stuff, the performance holistically — is going to determine whether people rely on formal verification or not.

So in a way, we want to make it so good that basically we can make that a choice.

投资人为什么愿意下注

Brandon: So why did the investors think that you could do this? Because I mean, people have been working on verification for so long, and I think everyone agrees it's an important problem. And certainly, if I can just have a verification proof for every program that I write — "hey Claude, like give me the proof also, and then it just produces it and yep, looks good to me" — I would absolutely do that.

But so why, what was it that the investors saw, in your opinion, that persuaded them that, okay, this is the moment I'm going to put in my $200 million or whatever?

Carina: I think when it comes to faith, you either have it or you don't. So you either dream the dream with us or you don't, and that's okay. Because when we realize the dream, the company is going to be worth $10 billion.

So I think that's the feeling that I have, which is that we believe verification is the critical part to super intelligence. Our version of super intelligence is absolutely verified. We don't think there's any other possible future.

We do not believe that — I'm going to say on the record — we do not believe that an informal math system is going to be the math AGI solution.

Brandon: Why not?

Carina: We just don't believe that.

Brandon: The counterargument is, oh, we just do a lot of good RL, and we've seen GPT solving I think some problem and whatever. So why do you think that runs out of gas?

Carina: So you can say that if you are a frontier — sorry, frontier lab — and you have infinite resources, by definition there is no running out of gas. If you think infinite means there's no running out of gas. I don't think it's going to scale to super intelligence.

Brandon: So you think that you run out of money basically, or run out of power?

Carina: Sure. So we as a startup first of all cannot do that. We first of all as a startup cannot do that.

But we generally think that formal math, then by converting math proofs to programs to code, gives us much better performance.

Brandon: So it's your sample efficiency argument and so forth — that you just can't bend the curve enough if you don't use formal.

Carina: The thing is, the informal stuff is also available to us in a way. If you really like, you can have a both informal and formal system, and that is going to be very strong.

The thing that I think, my suspicion about whether we can scale to math AGI just by the informal approach, is you're going to keep having LLMs judges solution, or you have human experts who grade. And it's just human experts doesn't scale that well. And then if you really argue infinite — then sure, then you also have infinite money, and you can pay infinite. There are so many — is there really infinite number of people who can understand and prove about, like, a result, a non-trivial result, in Langlands program? I think, good luck finding those people.

And in fact, I think how Frontier Math came together is because they couldn't assemble a benchmark by their expert pool. So they have to collaborate with EpochAI to do it. And I think that's what I worry about: having the human part. So they have LLMs judges and then stochastic judging. The problem is whether something is impossible to achieve versus something is incredibly expensive — and like really incredibly expensive — and "incredibly expensive to achieve" get kind of mixed in the end.

个人背景与团队

Brandon: And then of course investors always want to know "why you." So I've read a little bit about your background, and I think it — we would do a disservice to the audience if we didn't hear a little bit just about your personal story. Do you want to talk just a little bit about — you've done some really interesting stuff. So I'd love to hear, like, you and then your team. What makes Axiom special?

Carina: Yeah. I think Axiom is very special because they're really expert mathematicians. Basically they are users of the system we are developing, and that iteration loop is very fast. It's extremely fast.

You have some of the strongest mathematicians, both in research and Olympiad contest, and you also have people who are Mathlib contributors, maintainers, developers, Lean gurus, really. And combine them with people who come from applied ML, really strong organizations like Meta FAIR and Golden Age, as well as people who have codegen expertise who work with compiler like Colonel Jam — have these backgrounds of people together. I think that sort of interdisciplinary way of thinking about things is quite helpful.

We think AI for math has traditionally been quite interdisciplinary. People are borrowing techniques from even AI for science, pure tech, borrowing techniques from the codegen literature, and people are borrowing techniques from obviously the broader frontier applied ML, to try to apply on the niche problem AI for math. So we also think having this sort of very special team is a differentiation.

We also think that, as you say, there's no permanent moat. The proprietary data that we generate, and a little bit of a flywheel we are seeing, is a time moat.

Carina 个人经历

Well, me personally — I love math. I think I kind of have been doing math since I was very young, and like math sometimes gets really hard when the problem you are solving is just a little bit out of reach, and it gets a bit depressing. And times to times I wonder if I can just have an AI help me. And yeah, I think — why I figure, why not build such a thing?

Brandon: You did a master's at Oxford in neuroscience. Has that informed your thinking here?

Carina: That's a great question. I think my experience with neuroscience is — you learn very well but what's hard. What's impossible. I mean, it's very interesting. I think that year of neuroscience gave me some feelings about what's hard and almost no feeling about what might work.

But I think I was kind of under the pretense of neuroscience like hanging out at the UCL Gatsby Institute and was fortunate to do AI research with some really cool faculties. And so I think that was a very productive year of AI study, non-neural study.

Brandon: So you were — it was mostly for you studying AI.

Carina: That's right. I think in the UK back in the 20th century, if you call something AI, you would not get the donation. But if you call something brain science, you might have a chance. So the UCL Gatsby, which is a premier AI hub where a lot of people actually go from there to DeepMind, including Demis himself, it's a very wonderful research environment.

I remember those tea time talks were very amazing, and people were basically just doing AI. It's called the Gatsby Computational Neuroscience Institute.

I think how that happened was because I was in the master of neuroscience program, and then quickly realized that you need to kill rats. And kind of don't want to do that. And computational neuroscience sounds more appealing. And when you look at the project and you see "transformer," you're like, you absolutely want to do that.

We're all excited about that.

法学院的一年

Brandon: So after the Gatsby, you started a math PhD program at Stanford?

Carina: I started actually one year full-time at the law school.

Brandon: Oh.

Carina: Because the JD-PhD program is structured in a way where you have to spend full one full residency year. So that was also a very fun year of learning things that are just quite fascinating — like criminal law, looking at homicide cases. Exciting. No.

Brandon: Do you ever feel like the legal system is under- or over-specified in some way that maybe you could access and improve?

Carina: That's a great question. I think for a lot of things it's definitely underspecified. For some other things, I was actually quite excited about sort of transfer learning from mathematical reasoning to those specific fields. I think appellate litigation — the legal gymnastics — you see some really good appellate scholars and lawyers that just come from math training. Not many, but like Lawrence Tribe for one — Harvard law professor, one of the strongest appellate litigation and SCOTUS briefs, brains on the left, Democratic party.

And I think there's a lot of other domains such as antitrust that's incredibly flowcharty, contract law sometimes also flowcharty, bankruptcy, tax — more on the corporate side. I just love litigation side.

Erdős 问题事件与文献检索

Brandon: Yeah, so actually — I do, just because we're talking about litigation. It's not the same thing, but there was an Erdős problem that Axiom saw — I don't know if it was Axiom Prover or whatever — is that right? There was a controversy about it because it had represented that it had solved the problem, when in fact the proof had been — it had discovered the proof and then just formalized it.

Carina: Yeah. So actually what happened was: our competitor Harmonic decided to publicize that they have solved unsolved problems, Erdős number 124 and 481. And then we trusted their literature review, believing that these problems are really truly unsolved. And we were really young company at the time. We wanted to test if our system can attempt to try the problems that our competitor can. We fully did not expect that actually solved them. But turns out that we were both wrong — that in fact the problem has been solved before.

Brandon: I see.

Carina: So then it's not the only time that we relied on others' literature search. And we should own it.

The other time was this paper called "Dead Ends in Square-Free Walks." Professor Miller had this problem that actually turns out to have been solved. But we really should have done our part.

That is — you know — there's this Japanese advertisement of like a whole company, like hundreds and thousands of people apologizing in the advertisement. And it's like, "sorry, we raised our price by like 5 cents," and that's the advertisement. I was like thinking that maybe I should just do that. It's so embarrassing.

Brandon: No, but I think the question of provenance of information and sort of like, how do you — it goes back to the question I was asking before about how do I connect the answer to the question.

Carina: Yeah, this is a great question. I think after Erdős we're like extremely careful. So we didn't really look at the other Erdős problems. I believe that Harmonic still continues to claim they have solved Erdős problem that might might not — I don't know. There's a, I think Terence Tao and a lot of other people have a database all the Erdős problems and the status.

It is really, by the way, a really easy mistake to make because there are so many Erdős problems that actually have been solved. And that's kind of indeed — I think search and retrieval is a hard problem. You don't know if that argument or an equivalent version of that — in fact, I think the most interesting part about that entire database is, there are a lot of problems that are not directly solved but can be just a trivial extension of another result that has been solved. Or not, sometimes not even resolved.

Sometimes — I think in this "Dead End Square-Free Walks" case, which is nothing to do with Harmonic — that we actually didn't realize, and then Professor Conander actually pointed to us and to Professor Miller, is that it was actually from a stack math overflow or stack overflow post. A user pointed out that there's a 1936 result. It's fascinating. I think it's hard — search is a hard problem.

知识图谱与合成数据

Brandon: I guess that means that — does the conjecture engine or whatever, does that use search as part of its process, or is that something that you the human does and then feeds?

Carina: I think knowledge graph or knowledge base is a very important component of any company. And I think I don't think it's talked about enough.

Brandon: And so you guys, it sounds like you don't want to give us too many details, but it sounds like you guys have a knowledge graph. I mean, that brings up also — I read somewhere that you guys have a really massive database of Lean proofs that you've generated. So synthetic data in some sense — but this maybe is a competitive advantage for you.

Carina: I think everyone is trying to accumulate data, which is not a moat, it's just time. Time moat. It's all about whether you can execute fast enough to make sure that you have like a certain buffer because of your dataset accumulation. But that is only just a buffer.

AlphaZero for Math？

Brandon: Have you ever thought about doing something like an AlphaZero for math, where you start from nothing and let it just make up axioms and see what happens?

Carina: Ah, this is a wonderful question. I think that's a very interesting approach actually.

I think we believe in something which is that — suppose Axiom Prover can be a really strong mathematician, and then really the thing that it is proving every day should hopefully help it improve. I think this sort of self-improvement design is extremely valuable.

I think there are other people in the AI for math community. I think Professor Gabriel Pesra's work is very interesting. There are some of the more conjecturing type of exploration. Suppose we change a lot of the — there are specific things you can do in certain ways that can try to see if your system can learn to conjecture and build theories.

I think the topic is really interesting and important. Because you're claiming that the — to get to super intelligence, there's sort of this — it's just not going to be possible.

Brandon: Maybe if you had infinite resources, you could just RL and it would work, maybe. But the reality is that you just can't be sample-efficient enough or whatever it is to do that. So you need some sort of verifier in the loop with the inference process. Rather than — because you do have verifiers during the training process, and you just don't have them during the inference process.

Carina: Yeah, I think a lot of them are just secretly trying to use this to ground their reasoning.

Brandon: Yes. I was surprised that when O1 was — everyone knew O1 was coming, but it hadn't come out. I was sure they're going to announce that they're using Lean to do formal verification of proofs and actually generate proofs and then verify them so that they're grounding and reasoning. That was my [thought].

Carina: When I was there, there was 3PF that was a great piece of work. There's also miniF2F. These are all formal math work at OpenAI.

Brandon: Okay, so presumably those guys are doing something.

Carina: No, no, they all left.

Brandon: Oh, they all left.

Carina: So that's my point: which is that if you're an intern — I guess you can't be an intern forever. So let's say you're like a junior member technical staff and you want to work on something for as long as it takes to solve it. Weirdly, people think about startup as this sort of "your runway can just run out and it can just all fall apart" thing. You might have a better chance of staying focused on the same problem for as long as it takes at, say, a startup like Axiom or one of the other new labs.

Brandon: Yeah, if you're aligned to the mission of the big company, rather than somebody decided that what you're doing is no longer [interesting].

Carina: Yeah. It can be your VP lost some political fight, and so —

Brandon: Absolutely.

Carina: So obviously if we succeed, then they're all going to start doing that again. And then as a talent, then there are more potential places to choose from as well. So then your job is to go fast. So they're struggling.

发布 Axel：Lean 验证 API

Brandon: So actually we haven't talked about it, but you actually also just released an API for doing Lean verification. And I actually tried it with Claude Code because it's easier than setting up your own Lean tool chain. Tried to get Lean to prove some stuff. And the infrastructure is maybe non-trivial, especially at scale. So you want to talk a little bit?

Carina: Yeah, we just released AXEL. AXEL stands for "Axiom Lean Engine," and it's really a set of proof validation and manipulation tools that are built for Lean in the language of Lean. So it's a bunch of meta-programming tools.

Now meta-programming talents are extremely hard to find, and we're so grateful to have a really crack team working on that. And we want to release it to the community for use for free because we think that there are probably other people doing also large-scale Lean operations, and these tools going to make their stuff go a lot more robust and faster and do so at scale.

AXEL 的功能

AXEL is currently I think 14 such tools, starting from verify proof, which is to make sure that there's nothing weird going on — no sort of cheating by Lean code, you don't aim something out, you don't assume weird things. If you ask N plus N equals N, you can prove 2 plus 2 equals 2, which you're for sure not — that's not the right answer.

There are also other generation tools. For example, you can try different repair attempts: broken Lean in and good Lean out. And there are currently other repair methods by LLM. So hopefully this what we provide can be just a lot cheaper and more straightforward. And it's just strong and better engineering can get you to a place that's quite far.

A lot of the people from the Lean community has been using AXEL, even if it's just been a week, to do all sorts of different interesting things. We have seen people from the blockchain community use it as actor, and we have heard from a lot of the people that "Claude plus AXEL" is kind of their go-to setup for now.

We think that these are really interesting tools. I think famously today there's this mathematician who said he formalized the Donald News using Claude to prove a Ramsey result, and then to formalize the Lean proof — and that is also using AXEL tool. So we are really glad to see people kind of already using it.

数学协作的未来与 blueprint

Brandon: I feel like this is a great opportunity for the collaborations that Terence Tao was talking about as well, where once people have access to the common tools and it becomes easy to do — and if you have intuition, even not a strong mathematician like myself, you might be able to participate in an effort to prove a larger theorem or something like that.

Carina: Yeah, I think that's a very interesting view, which is that — if you think about, mathematics has been not as collaborative as software engineering. You don't have hundreds and thousands of people working on something together. I think Polymath was an instance when that happened. That was fantastic.

So if you have a lot of really good sort of setup, indeed commoditized access, and people can all participate — in fact, that's how I think some of the large formalization projects have been done. Things are divided into subtasks. But really the blueprint writing process by, say, Terence Tao and Alex Kontorovich — of assigning the task to different people and how things kind of fit together — that blueprint writing part is extremely important.

And there has been, I think, result about sphere packing, I think by one of the other companies out there. And the blueprint part for the 8 dimension is still pretty much built on what the sphere packing community, the Lean community, the humans, blueprint. And similar with some of their other results as well — the blueprint part has still been human-generated. And I think autogenerated blueprint is going to be a technical bottleneck that many people are trying to solve around the same time.

Brandon: So is there value in me as a Claude Code user trying to attempt some small lemma or whatever, where I don't have a great understanding of the math, maybe I have a high-level understanding?

Carina: Depends. Are you trying to formalize or are you trying to prove?

Brandon: Uh, to prove new things.

Carina: That's a good point. Yeah, so for formalization, you would obviously probably start with formalization, you know the proof and you just can't get — nobody has been able to get the formalization correct.

I have actually seen people use Lean in formalization, and they try to do it by hand, not using AI, as a way to learn mathematics. No, it's auto-formalization, you don't have that process.

It's interesting because I think a lot of my friends who started working on Lean and Mathlib was because they are in PhD and this problem's really hard. We get stuck all the time and we want to review some of the undergrad classes a time where we still understand what the math was about. And we do so by doing Lean. And I think that's very beautiful — the material.

But if you have access to Axiom Prover that also can formalize, you don't have — you lose that part of the learning process.

But I do think that for you and I we can set up AXEL and try to see what results we might be able to prove. And I think that's quite interesting. And thanks to AXEL sort of making the speed a lot faster, you don't have to wait very long.

Putnam 当天的实战

I remember the Putnam exam day. We were all in the war room. It was a Saturday. We're all really excited and we just got the exam paper from the official organization, the proctor of the Putnam exam. We just were looking at how much workout AXEL is getting. And without it we couldn't have solved with — I think the 8 problems within the time limit, definitely not within the time limit.

And I think one thing about these tools is, it's very interesting in that potentially you can have interesting reward for RL as well.

Brandon: What do you mean by that?

Carina: So for example, verify proof can be a reward for just basically a proof is completely correct and validated.

Brandon: I see.

Carina: I think formal verification tooling can be interesting direction to pursue with RL.

Brandon: So you mean, for example, you auto-formalize the informal proof and then verify what, and then use that as a reward?

Carina: No, as in like you pass Lean programs in these formal tools, and you will have some sort of score.

Brandon: Okay.

Carina: I think if I were to build O1 or something, I would have in my mind I would have used what I just described.

与前沿实验室合作的价值主张

Brandon: But you're saying just to learn how to do Lean.

Carina: So the value proposition which is interesting about frontier lab is: suppose you are a B2C business, then sure you can just not do what we are doing. And we have, since for example DeepSeek, originally having a formal team and then later dissolve that team because of strategic direction change — that's all completely reasonable.

Now suppose you are focused on coding, and you have talent who want to work on what we're doing — it makes a lot more sense for you to do code generation, further your strength and moat.

You can partner with Axiom. Just like how, for example, frontier labs partner with startups that work on search, such as Exa and Parallel — right, just call Exa API for search. And potentially, if you're frontier lab, I think you should call Axiom API for verification.

Better proposition. But spinning up your own — it doesn't make sense. The talent, the finickiness of Lean, the sort of data, code — there's no reason to.

Brandon: I mean, it took me five minutes to set up.

创业起点：法学院 + 数学痴迷

Brandon: Why did you decide to start? You were a grad student at Stanford in math. So what made you decide to —

Carina: I wasn't in math for a while at all. Almost as soon as I started the PhD, I just started fundraising. So it wasn't like —

Brandon: Oh really?

Carina: Yeah.

Brandon: Was that the plan, or did you start there and you almost immediately realized that this is —

Carina: Right. The year of law school was very interesting to me on an intellectual level. But it's also the first year where I had no science, technology, math whatsoever in my life. It's a weird year. I'm reading a lot. I'm practicing — well, I'm learning how to write, I'm learning how to read.

But I'm just kind of — I want to be obsessed about something in technology. That was also what's going on that year. The year of law school. And it was very interesting to me because — okay, I just need to be obsessed with a technical thing because otherwise I get — I don't think I'm bored because I really love everything about law. I really really loved it. It was something that's incredibly interesting to study.

But I've been very excited about the progress of reasoning. I was looking at a lot of the post-training papers. I was learning all of these by myself. And then at one point it got to a point where I'm like, I think this is for sure happening. And like, I think talking to Shubo at Verve every weekend also didn't help soothing this thought.

So I got more and more obsessed. And at a point I'm like — okay, if I'm doing this literally every minute, and I can't think about something else, I need to do something about it. I mean, it's like, I fall madly in love with the idea that AI is going to do math. And like, okay, now do I do math? It's really really crazy. At a time, the obsession was quite — I just couldn't get out of it.

And then I went to this Ni-Hennessy event — Scholar Dining House like host all sorts of free lunch events. And those are great because you get free food and you get interesting intellectual exposure to things. And I remember Julie Drow, who was I think a Facebook, first Facebook PM, came to speak. And then after that I just basically walked up to her and I said, "what do you do if you want to do a startup and you really wanted to do academia because you love math?"

And then she's like, "well, what's your time spent on these two different things?" And I'm like, "100% / 0%." And then she's like, "well, you kind of have to follow your energy."

I mean, if you are completely obsessed with it. I was completely obsessed with it. But I thought it's going to be big. And I thought it just has to be a for-profit startup because it's so much broader than making mathematical breakthroughs.

If you think about recursive self-improvement and like really the more high-level concept of you really want to have this AI AI scientist — the math reasoner is going to be a pretty big part of it. And now trying — I think the sort of belief by Cursor and Claude and other folks is, okay, like just like math transfer to coding, coding transfer to math as well. I think that's true. It's just that, why not push it directly? I don't get it. You need to push that directly.

三个核心论点：关于 verified AI

And then there's this other thought which is — and maybe kind of going back to the collaboration point — verification has traditionally been thought of as: okay, well, there are some industry where there's a lot of guardrails. So if you're working in defense, military use, okay, you need to basically satisfy a lot of barriers to entry to meet those stringent requirements. So verification is for the industries that are closed.

第一点：开放协作

But it's for the first time now I think verified AI is to open up collaboration. Either it's human-AI collaboration, well before — blueprinting, that's human-human collaboration. And Lean was a grounding, was a verification formal language. And then human-AI collaboration, like we're seeing now, future AI agent-agent-agent like collaboration.

So I think verified AI is for openness. It's not for meeting the requirements of closed industries.

第二点：放大才华，而非消除错误

And I think — just like I think verification should not be about: oh, I remember there's article like "chatbots mixed stuff up, is AI the solution" — sorry, "is math solution to hallucination."

Verification to me is not about lousiness. Verification to me is about scaling brilliance, compounding brilliance. It's like, just kind of going back to the collaboration point. It's about Ramanujan being a much stronger mathematician. He was already a really strong one, but verification helps him extend the brilliance, like both kind of scale up and scale out.

So verification — rigorous verification to me is not about erasing the mistakes, the lousiness. About scaling brilliance.

第三点：性能增益，而非合规负担

And the third point is that verification to me is not about just talking about rigor, it's actually about performance gain. It's not just about the stringent requirements, the hurdles that you need to overcome. It is about, like, actual verified generation is going to make it so much better.

And I think these three points — I think the last point is that a lot of the people think that you work on verification because of your distrust for technology. It sells really well to, I think, the general public, including my parents. Like, "oh, why are we doing verification? Because technology make mistakes." No, we don't think verification is because of the distrust for technology. It's because that's what expected rapid exponential scale up and the deployment and the creation of technology and technological progress is what that compels and demands.

数学如何驱动其他科学

Brandon: It's a very mathematical perspective, right? Because you're saying proofs drive math. A lot of math is about proofs. And math drives a lot of science and innovation in the world. And the innovations in math drive innovation in the world.

Carina: But it doesn't need to even go through. Like, in terms of, the "solve everything," like obviously stands. My point is, transfer learning is about pushing math reasoning.

So there are a couple narratives here. For some people it's like you solve math and then maths are the fundamentals of sciences, so that's actually the — from AI for math like take this radical layer of AI for science. That is narrative we actually believe in. Just like general transfer learning. I think Axiom is on the infrastructure stack.

Brandon: And you think that this is just a first step to basically unlocking capabilities in many domains in science and law, for example.

Carina: Yes. So again, there are multiple beliefs. One belief is that there's math and there is the formal verification. Suppose we actually solve math and have a really strong informal math reasoning engine. We do not expect that TAM to be as large as solving math through the formal way.

Brandon: Why?

Carina: I mean, code is language, but it is indeed on the more structured end. It bridges informal and formal.

What we are doing is — it's not informal versus formal. We're not taking the sort of completely formal of a proof approach. It's bridging between informal and formal. It is bridging between high level and low level. It is a direct sort of direct improvement through reasoning, through transfer learning. And it's also indirect in that, okay, well, math is going to unlock all science. And that is really what we're seeing.

Brandon: So you think that it enables transfer learning.

Carina: Yeah. I think that is pretty much a consensus.

I think it is a consensus, and this is a bet that has been pretty much overlooked by others because math sounds pure and it doesn't sound like there's any commercial value. Well, I do obviously understand the opportunity cost if you're like a frontier lab of solving this problem. But I definitely think this is a problem that if you're a well-resourced startup, you should be doing.

Brandon: That's an interesting perspective. Did you get everything out that you wanted?

Carina: Yeah, I think the question of "is math, or is verification?" — the DNA of the company is math. We think verification is the best first market.

And we think that sort of solving math, and especially formal math, is going to help us tackle the really ambitious quest of verified AI. Now when we are done with that, we might have other second markets including AI for science we just talked about. But on the theoretical layer — I think real-world testing is important, and potentially we can stay in the digital world and software stuff, and for other things to be getting reward, like physical world signals.

Brandon: But do you think that the capability of doing really powerful reasoning — once you have that powerful verified reasoning engine, that's the moment when, okay, now we've unlocked that for software verification and hardware, or whatever — but okay, so now what about biology, what about chemistry?

Carina: So that could be one. Then the other one is then like really, how far are you to recursive self-improvement?

Brandon: Okay, so just AGI.

Carina: Yeah, I think there is this sort of question. And different people because of their probably different backgrounds have different — it's really where your energy and your passion leads you. For some people — actually I've heard this with my friends — they want to work on AGI because they believe solve AGI, solve death. There are other people who come from a more medicine background, they really believe they can solve death and they don't solve AGI and then solve death. They just solve AI for science.

Now, which way is correct? I don't know.

Brandon: And so the recursive self-improvement angle — it sounds to me like you're saying that the combination of verification plus the sort of language which is informal, it's that combination that enables really good recursive [self-improvement].

Carina: I think recursive self-improvement is going to happen anyway. We're trying to have formal verification's earnest place. So we like — again, whether formal verification can be welcomed and deployed and become a consensus depends on how well we execute. And I think when you boil down that problem into an execution problem, you should just go for it.

最大瓶颈：碎片化

Brandon: Looking forward, what's the biggest bottleneck that you see in the field for both Axiom and maybe just the field abroad?

Carina: Fragmentation. So I think we're in a market where people like to start a thousand people — they don't join force, they start a thousand things. I think that's actually the biggest bubble indicator.

I think there are categorical bubble, and there are other categories where there are moonshots — it's not bubble, it just looks a little bubbly in the field. If people who are like really legit backgrounds decides to join force and work in a team for the mission rather than for ego, for kind of the status of new lab founder, I think those categories I'm really bullish on. And vice versa.

So I think the bottleneck actually is — it's annoying because, if you believe we are in an age of research, if you believe deep tech are the interesting directions to go after, the market sort of conditions currently is good and bad. Good in that it enables these sort of long-term, long-horizon bets to be funded. Bad because there's too much noise in the market and some other irrational players.

We try to work with really incredible venture firms. They are our partners. They are our intellectual partners, and there's a lot of alignment, and we really bounce very cool ideas, technical and non-technical, each other for long long hours. And we spend a lot of time off work and weekends together to really intensely build the company.

There are also other people who just want to park capital somewhere. And while we don't work with them, these are market conditions that encourage fragmentation. And when things get fragmented, no one gets there.

I think every category, regardless of how right the idea is, it's pretty much in a sort of "earning the right to exist" stage. And if that is the case, then — for example, great deep tech company SpaceX — people do actually join force to work on that dream, and potentially in that case also a very charismatic founder.

I think a really concerning thing for me personally is that for other probably some categories that I'm personally quite bullish about — just looking at things generally, fragmentation is a problem. We see stop pulling professors from university to work on something when it really is a really interesting situation.

Brandon: Maybe this is a naive question, but right now when you were talking about players in let's say AI for math, where Harmonic, and then the big labs — am I missing someone? Is that actually fragmented really?

Carina: I guess fragmentation, I think, is a bottleneck for the entire AI landscape. I think AI for math is a category that is actually not a bubble because it is not fragmented because people who are really amazing talents do like to join force.

So for example, the fact to get Kanu and Francois Chollet on one team — like this is fantastic. You have someone who's a core contributor, Frontier Math tier-4 really great benchmark setter, Francois who's on the AI for math discovery, have proving and discovery, they work together — then you are suddenly a player with both proving capability and construction capability. And that's fantastic.

And I believe, as you said, Harmonic probably also have some really great talents joining force together. I think AI for math is a good category because of the absence of fragmentation.

But even from our perspective, the sort of — for example, RL right? Being — I don't think that's like a category per se — but RL talents currently, it's quite hard to attract and retain for literally everyone. And there are a lot of companies being started then sold like three months later. And just each month where you could have worked on a technical problem and you're instead working on deals — it's a month that is wasted. And I say that with some amount of pain and suffering because of having gone through two fundraises.

AI for Math 的长期瓶颈：短期 vs 长期

Brandon: So what's the biggest bottleneck in AI for math — not Axiom, but just the community? Where is it going? What is the thing that everyone just really wants to break?

Carina: I expect fragmentation to start to happen as Axiom and Harmonic establish category leadership.

So I expect people kind of, you know, that's one thing. But I also think that another bottleneck could be the pressure of short-term versus long-term.

I think that we are doing things in a very sort of fast-paced manner. But that does not mean we can always — or it does not mean it is always correct to do things in the most fast-paced manner.
