item.title: [Lex Clips] A billion particle collisions a second: How large particle colliders work | Don Lincoln
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、隐藏的悖论：最伟大的发现，正在变成最讨厌的噪音

最让人意外的观察藏在 Don Lincoln 的一句轻描淡写里：1995 年费米实验室发现顶夸克时，全世界顶尖物理学家忙活半年到一年才攒到 38 个候选事件，其中一半还是"垃圾"。三十年后的今天，LHC 每秒钟生产一个顶夸克——它从诺贝尔奖级别的稀世珍宝，变成了"碍事的背景噪音，我们要想办法把它过滤掉"。

这是科学史上一个被低估的母题：昨日的奇迹是今日的污染。哈勃望远镜刚启用时拍到的每一个遥远星系都是论文，今天的巡天项目要把"普通星系"剔除以寻找异常。早期 GPS 信号的米级精度曾是军事机密，现在你的外卖 App 觉得它"不够准"。知识的代谢速度比我们想象的快得多——前沿的本质不是积累，而是不断把昨天的圣杯踩在脚下当垫脚石。

二、跨领域连接：LHC 的触发器系统 = 注意力机制 = 你大脑此刻正在做的事

Lincoln 描述的数据漏斗令人震撼：每秒 10 亿次碰撞 → 4000 万次"快门" → 硬件触发器筛出 10 万 → 软件触发器精选出 1000 → 研究生手工挑出几个。这是一个 99.9999% 的丢弃率。

但请把这个架构画出来看看——它和你大脑视觉皮层的处理流程几乎是同构的：视网膜每秒接收约 10^9 比特信息，丘脑过滤，V1 检测边缘特征，更高层皮层做语义筛选，最终进入意识的只有几个"对象"。它也和 Transformer 的多头注意力机制同构：海量 token 在前几层被弱激活，关键 query-key 匹配在深层放大。

真正令人毛骨悚然的洞见是：宇宙中所有处理"压倒性数据洪流"的系统，无论是物理仪器、生物大脑还是人工智能，最终都会收敛到同一种架构——分层级联的有损压缩 + 学习而来的"什么算有趣"。所谓"科学发现"，本质上是人类把自己注意力机制的判断权委托给一台 14000 吨重的硅基与超导构成的"外置丘脑"。

三、最颠覆性的系统性真相：我们其实从未"看见"过粒子

注意 Lincoln 那个看似随意的比喻——"探测器基本就是相机"。但仔细想想他描述的过程：触发器是学习出来的"什么算有趣"，丢弃的 99.9999% 是基于我们已有理论认为不重要的事件。

这意味着一个令人不安的事实：LHC 永远不可能发现一种"不符合我们触发器预期的物理"。如果新物理躲在我们认为"无聊"的那 99.9999% 里——比如它表现为"看起来非常普通的对撞事件之间的细微统计相关性"——我们的数据采集系统会直接把它扔进字节坟墓，连进入分析阶段的机会都没有。

这与认知科学中的"理论负载性观察"（Theory-ladenness of observation, Norwood Hanson, 1958）形成了惊人的回响：没有"中立的观察"，所有实验数据都是用我们旧理论的筛子过滤过的。我们以为自己在"探索未知"，但其实只在"已知所允许的奇异性边界上"游走。

更妙的连接：这和你刷推荐算法时遇到的"回音壁"是同一个数学结构。Spotify 永远不会推荐你"听起来不像你会喜欢的歌"，LHC 永远不会触发记录"看起来不像可能有新物理的事件"。人类最昂贵的科学仪器和最廉价的消费 App，在认识论上犯着同一种病。这或许才是为什么过去十年 LHC 之后基础物理停滞不前——不是因为没有新物理，而是因为我们的触发器系统是用旧物理训练出来的。

📝 转录文本

一、Higgs 玻色子的搜寻与 Tevatron

The Higgs boson idea was predicted in '64, it became useful in '67, and then scientists started looking for it. So, in the early 2000s, people were starting to think that we had built part of particle accelerators more powerful — or powerful enough — to actually to be able to create these vibrations and detect them.

So, the accelerator that was working at the time was a large particle accelerator outside Chicago at Fermilab called the Tevatron. Mhm. And we were colliding protons and antiprotons at near the speed of light at very high energy. And that was the accelerator at which the top quark was discovered in '95. But we had upgraded our apparatus, we had 10 times the number of collisions per second, we had slightly more energy, and we were banging the protons and the antiprotons together hoping that we would actually find the Higgs boson.

二、为什么造加速器？E = mc² 的真正含义

Can you actually back up a little bit and look at the bigger picture? So, Fermilab has this legendary accelerator that there's also a personal story with you connected to it because — I mean, there's a million questions I want to ask you and will ask you about some aspects of that. So, this idea of an accelerator, the design and the physics of an accelerator, how is that productive for understanding and discovering different aspects of particle physics?

Well, I'm so glad you asked. [laughter] I mean, this is fascinating. All right.

Everybody has heard Einstein's equation E equals mc squared. Nobody knows what it means — maybe they heard that energy equals mass and mass equals energy, I don't know, you know, but they heard the equation, the most famous equation in all of science. But buried inside that equation is a really thoroughly fascinating concept: that energy and matter are equivalent. And you can in fact convert movement energy into mass.

And so this is something that we've known for a long time. This was predicted back in basically 1928. So a long time ago, actually almost 100 years ago. And it is not in the slightest bit controversial. We can do this all the time.

对撞的基本图景

So the simplest thing is to take two particles that have no structure. So you know, the closest thing you can have to BBs that are just true mathematical BBs. If you smash those two things together, it's coming in with a huge amount of energy from one direction, a huge amount of energy from the other direction. The directions cancel, so the net momentum, the net energy of this has no motion.

So you have these two things coming in with a, you know, exactly balanced energy. And if they collide, they could stop. Well, that energy has to go somewhere. And that energy can literally create mass, create particles.

Now, there are special rules about what happens: if you have two things coming together and it creates a particle, it has to create an anti-matter particle to balance it. That's just kind of the rules of the laws of nature. Why is that the case? Well, we have some ideas, but in many respects the answer is because those are the laws of the universe. And that's the things that we try to understand.

But this is absolutely true. So what particle accelerators do, among other things, is simply transform energy into particles. And so basically any particle that doesn't exist in nature, we can make in this way. You can make the anti-matter electron by taking two particles, smashing them together. The energy sits there and it will make an electron and an antimatter electron, and it just does, and we know that.

The antimatter electron was discovered in 1932. This is all pretty easy. The antimatter proton was discovered in 1955 at the Berkeley Bevatron. And so this is just what you do. You can convert energy into a matter–antimatter particle.

Now the converse goes through, and that's something we might talk about. You can take matter and antimatter and bring it together and it'll make energy. It's the — the process can go both ways. Energy can make matter and antimatter. Matter and antimatter can make energy. And this is just true. We do it all the time. There's no question that this is the case.

三、副产品：反物质的生产

should also mention that this is the reason why Fermilab had a nice stash of antimatter particles. So as a side effect you can also collect antimatter in this kind of way.

Sure. You can produce antimatter, but is it extremely costly? Oh, very, very costly. In order, at the Fermilab machine, we would have to smash 100,000 protons into something to make one antimatter proton. So I mean, it took some work.

Is there some extremely precise recipe of being able to produce particular kinds of particles and all this kind of stuff when you smash two things together? Is there like — how can you control accurately which kind of particles you're trying to produce?

If you want to make antimatter electrons, you smash together energy at a certain — it's just easier with electrons, because the electrons, to the best of our knowledge, have nothing inside them. So they're simple. They have a certain mass and that's that. So if you smash particles together with the right energy, you can make them very, very easily.

It's like an old-style radio back in the day where you had to dial it in. You could get right on the station and you could hear the signal, and if you were off a little it didn't work. The problem for things like protons and so forth is they're not point-like particles. They're kind of like garbage cans full of stuff, and so it's very difficult to make antimatter protons.

Now, you can get more of them by increasing the energy at which you collide two particles together. If you're below a certain energy, and you collide — say you collide two protons together at kind of low energy — you just don't have enough energy to make an antiproton. Mhm. And so it doesn't happen. You get to a certain energy and you can just barely make them. The more energy you collide them together, the more you make. So that is just sort of how it works. More is better.

四、CERN vs Fermilab：两种前沿哲学

And then with CERN, if you compare — it's maybe CERN and Fermilab, going to complexity here. CERN's accelerator, the Large Hadron Collider, LHC, is the world's highest energy proton collider, while Fermilab's current and planned accelerators focus on intense proton beams for neutrino physics, rather than pushing the absolute energy frontier.

Absolute energy frontier meaning highest possible energy smashing of protons together.

Correct.

反质子生产的对比

So when we were talking about like accumulating antimatter, all right? And so there, that is typically making antiprotons as opposed to making all particles in general. So let's focus on the antiproton side to begin with, all right?

So Fermilab doesn't make antiprotons anymore. We stopped making them in 2011. And it's because we shut our big accelerator down to concentrate on a different facet of particle physics. However, at the time we would smash protons with an energy of 120 GeV. And in that we would make antiprotons.

So, that's a ton of energy. It's true that the CERN accelerator, the big accelerator, is now much higher energy than the Fermilab accelerator was. No problem. But that's not how they make antiprotons.

All of these big beam, big laboratories — it's not one accelerator. At Fermilab, there were five distinct accelerators, and it was basically like shifting an old standard car, cuz you couldn't just go zero to super speed in one accelerator. You had to go from one to another, getting higher and higher. Well, at CERN, they use one of the — basically their second gear — in their very big accelerator complex to make antimatter protons. And their accelerator is only 26 GeV, compared to the 120 GeV at Fermilab.

Fermilab's not operating, but when it was operating, it operated at an energy about four times higher than what CERN is doing now. And why is that? Well, it's because what CERN needs to do is to not make as many antiprotons as Fermilab did. They are doing a very different current experimental program. They're doing a fascinating experimental program, including trying to figure out: does antigravity fall up or down? — which is kind of neat. And we sort of know the answer to that. Different — that's separate.

But anyways, so getting back to the antiproton business: Fermilab doesn't do it now. It was top dog. It's not anymore. The only really big antiproton accelerator creator is a small accelerator at CERN. Okay. So that's the antiproton thing. And if we get back to antimatter, we could talk about that, cuz that is way cool.

Yeah, super cool.

五、能量前沿：LHC 为什么赢了

Now, the other side of your thing about making high-energy unknown particles: bigger is better. And it is true that the LHC is a very high energy machine. It is about seven times more powerful in terms of energy per collision. It is also about a hundred times more collisions per second than the Fermilab machine. So, it is true that the LHC can make bigger, heavier particles that the old Fermilab — the Tevatron — ever could. And that is true.

So if you want to look at high energy stuff, yeah, you go to CERN now. Which is why many of my colleagues, including myself, once we had measured all of the sort of frontier measurements we thought we could make with the Fermilab accelerator, we saw this bigger, more powerful machine with seven times the energy and a hundred times more collisions per second, we said, "Heck yeah, let's go work on that."

顶夸克：从诺奖事件到背景噪音

And to give you a sense of scale: the top quark, which is the heaviest particle ever discovered, discovered at Fermilab in 1995. There were two discovery papers, and the one on which I was a co-author, we had worked for a good chunk of between six months and a year of collecting collisions, and there were a lot of collisions, and our paper had 38 top quark candidates. 38. And we knew that half of them were crap, because when you make a detector like that, there's what you call background. So you have the background and the good stuff, and we know it was about 50/50. So we had maybe 19 top quarks after working for between six months and a year of collecting data.

[gasps]

But now, at the LHC, we make a top quark every second. And that's what higher energy and more collisions per second will do for you. That extra energy, you're above threshold, you make a ton of them. And when you compare the 1995 Fermilab accelerator to the current CERN accelerator, it's probably a thousand times of collisions per second.

So it went from painstaking pulling teeth, to: yeah, now top quarks are a background. We try to get rid of them. There's just too many of them. They get in the way of searching for the stuff we really want to search. They are like, so 30 years ago.

六、信号与噪声：每秒十亿次对撞

By the way, is there something to be said about the kind of signal processing here? How you remove the noise, how you remove the background, how you determine which particle is which? There's probably some incredible nuance there even outside of the scope of this conversation.

So let me just throw some numbers out, all right? So at the CERN accelerator when it's operating, the collisions occur at a prodigious rate. We get about a billion — with a B — collisions per second. Now, each one of those —

Yeah, yeah, that's what I said. Wow.

Now, it turns out some of them are happening at the same time, so there's about of order 40 million moments in time per second where you would take a shot, and inside that moment there might be 20 collisions. So that's why where we get to the billion.

Yeah, but can you individually pinpoint the collision?

束流的真实形状

Sort of. To a degree. So the beams are — you know, when people think of beams, they think of like laser beams, but that's not really what particle beams look like. Particle beams look like little tiny sticks of spaghetti, except they're much thinner. They're not as fat as a stick of spaghetti. And they're at the LHC — different accelerators are different — they're about this long.

And so you have one of them going one way full of protons, and another one going the other way full of protons, and they pass through each other. And as they pass through each other, you should think of this is like a swarm of bees, and this is like a swarm of bees, and mostly the bees pass through each other and don't do anything. But every so often, some of the bees hit nose on, and — pieces and stripes and wings and everything everywhere.

That's awesome.

And so as they collide through each other, you know, one collision's here, and one's here, and one's here. And you can't tell too much side to side because the beams are really small. They're sort of the thickness of a human hair. But you can see along the direction, and they're, you know, this is about the right size. And so we have detectors around them, and we can actually see, "Oh, particles came from here, and particles came from here." And that's amazing.

七、CMS 与 ATLAS：相亲相杀的两个巨型探测器

All right. So at any one crossing, there's maybe 20 collisions. Now, most collisions are absolutely boring. They're boring because they exemplify physics that we know a great deal about already. We've tested it for decades. We know all about it. We don't care. I mean, it's kind of blasé that we can say, "Oh, yeah, yeah, we're making a billion subatomic particles every second, but who cares?" Because, you know — but that's just the way of, you know, frontier scientists.

So what you need is you need to pick out the cool ones, the weird ones, the ones that nobody's seen before. And so what happens is these beams collide, and we surround the collision point with an enormous detector.

There are two absolutely ginormous detectors at the LHC. One of them called CMS, which is the one I'm on, and the other one is called ATLAS. Mhm. — which is the other one that we don't speak of, because —

No, they're both really amazing. Okay, it's good to know that there's friendly competition even inside this—

It's awesome.

That's awesome.

The fact is they are both amazing, absolutely amazing detectors.

But CMS is just a little cooler than ATLAS.

Yeah. I mean, [laughter] you know, in particle physics, we really absolutely want our competitors to do extremely well. Just not quite as well as we do.

Got it.

巨兽的尺寸

All right. So these two giant detectors —

Right.

So, one of them, our detector, the CMS detector — it's the small one. It is 70 ft long, 50 ft high, 50 ft wide. It's five stories tall. It weighs 14,000 tons. Small one. Yeah. Small.

ATLAS experiment is 150 ft long, 80 ft across. It weighs only 7,000 tons. Just piece of cake. You could take the ATLAS detector and you could put four of them on a soccer or football field, and it would fill the field up with just enough room on the sidelines for the cheerleaders and the water boy and the coaches and stuff. That's how big they are.

Nice.

八、触发器系统：每秒 4000 万张快门里挑出 1000 张

So these are absolutely ginormous detectors, and basically they're cameras. And what they can do is they can take pictures 40 million times per second. Now, all the data comes streaming off that detector and we can't record it all. It would just fill up all of our tapes, and it would be full of all these boring things we don't care about.

So what we do is, as the beams pass through one another, we teach our detectors to say, "We only want one where there are certain configurations that might be interesting. Like there's gobs of energy in the detector, or there's a gob of energy on one side and nothing on the other side, or there's four gobs of energy, or whatever." These are called triggers.

三级过滤的级联

And so what we have is fast electronics that take the 40 million possible pictures per second, and it says, "You know, about 100,000 of those are really cool. We should think about them." And then it passes — not all of the 40 million, but those 100,000 — to the next level, which are commercial processors that have basically our final analysis code, but optimized to run very, very quickly. And what they do is they do a really quick and dirty analysis to further refine what's good and what's not.

And that computer farm then accepts about a thousand collisions per second, and we record those for further analysis. So that's what's really happening: of the 50 million possible collisions per second, the fast electronics and then the computers pick the thousand, and then we pass those through analysis software and hand them to the graduate students, and they pick through them looking and finding the handful that are the next Nobel Prize.

So that's how that works. And that is truly astonishing. I'm hats off to the accelerator builders, the detector builders, the people who make the software work, the people who make the — not gigabytes, not terabytes, but petabytes of data flow around the world seamlessly. It's really amazing. I'm very grateful.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、隐藏的悖论：**最伟大的发现，正在变成最讨厌的噪音**
最让人意外的观察藏在 Don Lincoln 的一句轻描淡写里：1995 年费米实验室发现顶夸克时，全世界顶尖物理学家忙活半年到一年才攒到 38 个候选事件，其中一半还是"垃圾"。三十年后的今天，LHC 每秒钟生产一个顶夸克——**它从诺贝尔奖级别的稀世珍宝，变成了"碍事的背景噪音，我们要想办法把它过滤掉"**。
这是科学史上一个被低估的母题：**昨日的奇迹是今日的污染**。哈勃望远镜刚启用时拍到的每一个遥远星系都是论文，今天的巡天项目要把"普通星系"剔除以寻找异常。早期 GPS 信号的米级精度曾是军事机密，现在你的外卖 App 觉得它"不够准"。**知识的代谢速度比我们想象的快得多——前沿的本质不是积累，而是不断把昨天的圣杯踩在脚下当垫脚石。**
## 二、跨领域连接：**LHC 的触发器系统 = 注意力机制 = 你大脑此刻正在做的事**
Lincoln 描述的数据漏斗令人震撼：每秒 10 亿次碰撞 → 4000 万次"快门" → 硬件触发器筛出 10 万 → 软件触发器精选出 1000 → 研究生手工挑出几个

item.title: [Latent Space] GitHub’s Agent Era: 14x Commits, 200M Developers, Copilot’s Next Act — Kyle Daigle
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：高管的 commit 曲线在"退休"后反弹

Kyle 这条 commit 曲线读起来像一个职业讣告写错了结局——2013–2014 高峰，进入管理层后跌入谷底，然后在他成为 COO 同时 兼任 Microsoft 全体开发者 CMO 的最忙时刻，反而陡然回升。这违背了所有人对 "senior leader 越升越远离代码" 的预设。

更深层的发现是：他用代码解决的，是"人的问题"。 他写的不是产品功能，而是把 Obsidian 笔记、Teams 会议转录、Slack 历史、PR 列表串成一个"回溯引擎"，让他能在周一早上知道上周组织发生了什么。这是一个跨领域的悖论——他在用工程师的工具完成 CHRO/COO 的工作。AI 没有让他更像 CEO，反而让他能重新成为 IC，但服务对象是 3,000 人的组织而不是一段代码。

二、AI 的真正杀手锏不是创造未来，而是回看过去

整个对话里他反复强调一句话："It's actually a recursive loop backwards."

这是被严重低估的一个洞见。整个行业都在卖 "AI 帮你生成、帮你预测、帮你规划"，但 Kyle 说真正高价值的是 retrospection——LLM 对模式抽取的能力远强于对未来的推断能力。这暗合了认知科学里的一个事实：人类自己也极其不擅长复盘，会被近因效应、自我归因偏差污染。AI 在这里其实在补一个人类组织行为学的盲点，而非补一个生产力的盲点。

跨领域类比：这和 post-mortem 文化 在 SRE 圈的兴起是同构的——Google SRE 手册里最被神化的不是预测系统故障，而是无指责复盘。Kyle 把 SRE 对系统的复盘哲学，平移到了对组织本身的复盘上。AI 在这里扮演的是"组织级 SRE"。

三、Mega Skills 时代结束了，但没人公开承认

这是访谈里最容易被忽略却最反共识的论断。过去两年，AI 社区的英雄叙事是"完美打磨的万能 prompt/skill"——越长越好，越通用越好，分享到 GitHub 一夜万星。

Kyle 说："我们正在结束这个时代。"

他的团队转向了"原子化技能 + Lego 化组合"。这与 Postel's Law（输入宽容、输出严格）的内核是一致的，但更深的连接在于：这其实是 Unix 哲学的第三次复活——第一次是 Unix 工具链本身，第二次是微服务，第三次是 AI skills。每一代人都要重新发现 "do one thing well" 的代价是写的人爽，组合的人骂；而 mega 化的代价是组合的人爽，维护的人哭。

四、最容易被忽略的炸弹：GitHub 在用"对角线扩容"对抗物理

"垂直扩容不再够用，水平扩容也不够，因为我们在打 CPU/GPU 的物理墙。" ——这句话其实是整集播客里最严重的行业信号。

GitHub 在 12 个月里增长 14 倍 commits，但他们正在拆 15 年前用 MySQL 1 写的权限系统。这不只是 GitHub 的问题——这是整个互联网假设"上游流量增长比基础设施增速慢"的契约第一次失效。AI agents 让"管道粗细"本身在变（一次 git push 现在比以往大得多），不只是"管道里的人多了"。

把这件事和工业革命做个跨域对比：纺织业蒸汽机的瓶颈最终不在动力，而在纺锭本身的物理速度——飞梭和环锭机的发明才解锁了真正的产能。GitHub 现在正在找它的"环锭机"。

五、隐藏的范式漂移：单体仓库的钟摆回摆

行业 10 年来都在讲 monorepo→multi-repo→微服务。Kyle 顺口讲了一句颠覆性的话："现在 repo 又变大了，monorepos 在回潮。"

这意味着什么？AI agents 需要完整上下文才能工作得好，而碎片化的多仓架构对人类协作友好，对 agent 协作是地狱。软件架构正在被 AI agent 的认知带宽偷偷重塑——这是没人在台面上讲的趋势。

跨领域类比：城市规划里有一个反复出现的循环——"集中—郊区化—再集中（urbanization 2.0）"。每一轮回归的理由都不同。这一轮 repo 重新集中的驱动力不是工程师方便，是机器的可读性。这是"为机器优化"的代码栖息地的第一次显形。

六、最被忽略的产品哲学：信任无法被技术解决，只能被社交编码

Kyle 反复说"PR 本质上是 codified trust"。当 80% 的 PR 来自 agent 时，trust 不能再由"是 Sean 点的同意"这种社会信号承载。但他坚决不接受任何单一的技术解（stars、vouch、proofs）。

这其实是哈耶克意义上的认知论立场——信任是分布式的、上下文相关的、不可被中央集权地标准化的。GitHub 选择的策略是给你乐高，让你自己拼出你的信任启发式。这与 web of trust 在 PGP 时代失败的教训一致：试图全球统一信任图谱的都死了，自愿、局部、嵌入工作流的活下来了。

最让我意外的连接： Kyle 在结尾问 Satya 的"未来两三年什么是真的"——他真正想问的不是预测，而是信念。在一个所有人都在追逐当下的行业里，CEO 的真正稀缺品是 conviction。这和他自己用 AI 做"recursive loop backwards"形成了完美对称——底层员工用 AI 看过去，顶层 CEO 用直觉看未来，中间的所有人都困在现在。这才是 AI 时代组织的真正分层。

Through all those years, GitHub has always operated pretty uniquely. Post-pandemic, working remotely was not as novel as it was when GitHub started in 2008. Kyle 的 Commit 曲线：AI 让他回归编码

Sean: Apparently your commits have gone up. What's going on?

Kyle: Yeah, you called me out pretty aggressively. As you can imagine — you can see my normal era of being a dev in the 2013–2014 era, and then moving into management, and then ultimately the COO role. What you see there is me really getting back to coding thanks to AI.

Similar to attaching problems between how to market and how to operate a business and how to code, I find building agents and workflows that are connecting very disparate problems to be what's driving this. Some of it's writing software, a lot of it is connecting a ton of different data sources to help me out.

But that is completely me really diving in on the AI side, trying out our tools, trying out everyone's tools. Building for me — building for the non-technical leader (though I'm technical) — and how we're able to use these tools more than just the simple call-and-response that a lot of the non-technical employers say "you have to use AI."

So everyone uses ChatGPT or Copilot or Claude or whatever to really get into: how is this going to help me out? I find that it's not "I need to write a blog post." Those simple examples — helping people find the workflows of:

Okay, I need you to go through all the PRs today. I need you to go through everything that we've posted online. I need you to go through what we've did the last three months. Go through all of my Obsidian notes for any mentions of this. Then go through my transcripts at work — we use Teams. So using Work IQ, go call that MCP server, grab all the transcripts, go through all of Slack, and then build me out the plan of what this week's messaging actually was.

That's something that was impossible. Because for me, I find AI in what most of this launch here is — is actually less building forward. It's actually a recursive loop backwards. I'm always looking at what had happened first. Like, go back through the week and tell me what we did, what worked, what didn't work — and then tell me in the next three or four days, what would you tweak based on this sort of looking backwards and then looking ahead a little bit.

I find that to be so much more valuable, especially for non-technical, because that retrospection is actually very LLM — they are very good at finding all the patterns, pulling them out, and then applying that retrospection to just a couple of days or just a short period of time.

It's all a bunch of apps that I've built and launched, a bunch of internal tools. I use the new GitHub Copilot desktop app with workflows. Every time I crack open my laptop it's running workflows for me. It's just a ton of different stuff. And of course it all ends up on GitHub.

Sean: Of course, that's where stuff is hosted.

如何用 AI 运行一家 3,000 人的公司

Sean: Man, there's so much to ask you. I was going to leave the "how do you run a company with AI" thing at the end. I have to ask one double-click thing. You said you're looking back at the week, you're understanding what happens — when you say "we," that's 3,000 people. How?

Kyle: When we started rolling out AI internally beyond engineering — one of the things that I was really passionate about is: we have to do this in a way where no one has to change how they work. I don't want to have to teach you a tool. I don't want to have to teach you something new.

So we tried out a few tools — most of them don't work because I've got to get you on board, I've got to teach you how to use it. What we've actually ended up doing is we've built a set of skills internally. We each have our set of skills, and we've been distributing — even to the non-technical folks — the CLI. And then effectively we're just giving it access to read about everything that we're writing. That's usually GitHub, Teams email, and Slack.

Sean: Teams for video chat generally speaking?

Kyle: Teams and Slack. We use Teams for video communication, but we don't use it for chat. We — GitHub for a long, long history — we always talk about ChatOps. Everything is built into Slack: every command, every flow.

Sean: Even though you've been acquired for like, I don't know, eight years now.

Kyle: Yeah, we still use that. It's a purpose-built tool for us. The reality is that moving off of it would be so bluntly expensive simply because all the tooling is baked in with that paradigm. They both have their pros and cons — but they don't work the same way at all. We still use a bunch of different tools because of the purpose-built tools that we need.

Sean: But the same doesn't go for the rest of Microsoft presumably. The various teams operate in various ways.

Kyle: It just matters what you're trying to do. We do work across kind of every tool that we use, and by giving everyone access to all of that context in the new Work IQ MCP server — which is quite cool if you do live in the M365 world — I can ask it all these backwards-facing questions. And it's incredibly important for our teams that are working remotely. There's a lot of stuff you miss when you're not in an office, and we are spread out all over the world.

So most of that is looking back. And then we post either automatically into GitHub issues or discussions, these sorts of findings, or our industry reports — what's happening this morning, today, yesterday. A little automation gets run. We'll use the app. We might use GitHub Actions with our agentic workflows just to go do that run. And then we push it into GitHub and we keep having a conversation.

For us it's about that sort of looking back, looking forward on the non-technical side. And then of course for a lot of those folks it's also building an app, pushing it to GitHub Pages or pushing it somewhere to host it. It's enabling everyone with that power: "It's going to take me a week to figure this out" — instead we're going, "Okay, I built a skill. Let's put it into a repo. We'll all share that skill together, and then we'll use the CLI or now the app just to run it."

技能蔓延与"大技能"时代的终结

Sean: I think we're going straight into the team management and productivity thing. A lot of people are getting various levels of LLM psychosis. How do you manage the bloat of skills? Everyone has their thing and they're trying to promote it to the rest of their peers in your org. And obviously whoever becomes a skill influencer internally becomes an AI leader of sorts. I assume you have those. And I assume it's a mess.

Kyle: There's two pieces. First is, I think we're ending the era of these massive, beautiful, perfect skills that are just not any of those things. For a while, every tweet every day is "go download the skills, the perfectly managed thing to do this entire workflow."

What we found — I was just with my team this week and we were talking about the skill side — we're really talking about these incredibly micro skills that are just doing one thing for us very, very well. Versus a skill that's going to do that full report — that doesn't really exist on our side anymore. It's usually a single skill that's going to identify the most important marketing information given any MCP server. Less about stitching a bunch of tools together and having it produce this mega output, because then weeks go by, months go by, things change, and you want to tweak your mega skill and you're screwed.

Now we're really just talking about the Legos we're using, and just letting the instruction book be something we're all putting together. Whereas a lot of AI skills for a while have been that mega instruction-book style.

Sean: I've thought a lot about Postel's Law. I don't know if that's a term that means things to folks — it's the idea that you should be liberal in what you accept and strict in what you output. I think that's a good framing principle for skills.

This is my skills, obviously. I feel like everyone should have — you know how some repos in GitHub are special repos? I feel like we should sort of reify the .skills and everyone give it as some kind of special presentation.

Anyway, this is one of those "download anything, transcribe anything," and then you can string together the atomic skills that do one thing well into some kind of orchestration skill that calls other skills. Does that match?

Kyle: I think so. Summarizing something for me — I do communications and PR and analyst relations and marketing and customer activities. So my "summarize everything" is very different for each one of those contexts. If I'm summarizing something for an analyst, that's a very different thing than how I'm going to summarize something for a customer meeting or an engagement.

That's the difference. When we're talking about the tools I might use on Saturday — or the skills I might use on a Saturday when it's just for Kyle — those have an atomic actual tool underneath, or maybe skill, and then "Kyle cares about X."

But when we're talking about work and enabling the marketers, communicators — there it's the atomic "this is what good summarization is," and then "this is what I care about as marketing, as communications, whatever." That, I think, is the interesting matrix problem when we go from a developer set of concerns to all kinds of different professions. What that word means to me is different than it means to you, is different than it means to the analyst or the salesperson.

That's where I think the matrix mess is that we're still starting to find. It's not these mega skills. They're all just slight permutations — but those permutations are really important. It's the difference between someone reading this and going, "Did AI make this?" Or, "This makes total sense, and I would expect this when I'm giving a briefing to Gartner."

Sean: I think the beauty of it maybe is that you don't have to be that careful about what goes in there. It doesn't have to exactly fit, as long as it roughly is contained in there. I used to complain about plug-in hell — when you have a framework and then you have 100 things that you need to integrate. GitHub used to be bloated full of these things, and now we don't need them anymore. Now you just use skills.

Kyle: Oh, and the most magical thing is that I can just also crack it open. Yes, I could go change how the plugin is coded, or I could go do that now with AI. But there's just something more magical about getting a response back and being like, "That's not right." And then you just crack the skill open. You just type English words, you know — and it's different. That building block is very unique once I get everyone to understand how to best make those changes to get the most power out of them.

前开发者转管理者的黄金时代

Sean: Is there a common framing for the something I'm feeling — which is true — that this is a golden age for former developers who are now in leadership? Because you can wield the tools, you would know the right words, you're maybe not too close to the details, doesn't matter. But like, you're more effective than someone who doesn't come from that background.

Kyle: The secret has always been your ability to identify patterns and solve problems. For folks like myself that don't code day-to-day anymore, that has made me successful as a developer, made me successful as COO, now CMO.

So now that I have access to Git and write code, I'm applying that pattern-finding and problem-solving — and I know enough still about how to then go and say, "Oh, I want to make an app, and I don't want to break into jail or create something that's not going to be able to work or to be deployed at scale or whatever." That ability to apply all that additional business knowledge and still code, I think, is what makes that so interesting to me. Slightly different than some of the other technical leaders that became business leaders and now are going back to their apps and updating them. Good for them. But the much more interesting thing is, "Well, now I have this whole new set of expertise over 10-plus years. Why not take that and use that as a developer with these AI tools?"

So I definitely think that makes me more powerful. But I think that's true for every dev as well. Most of the dev friends I still have also have some other underlying skill and passion. There's really talented very linear computer science software devs — absolutely. I just find that the folks that came from a different career, went to school for something else, went off and did this random thing and then became a software dev — or were a dev, did a random thing, came back — learning that extra set of information, those extra skills, and now having the power of an AI where I can crank up 15 agents on Saturday while my kids are doing lacrosse. That's really powerful.

It gets me back to that feeling of creation, and it's very hard to replicate that in most other senses. That first time you build an app and you click it and you show someone — that's magical. Being able to do that not just in code but across all kinds of different assets — that's huge.

一个完全用 AI 做的演示文稿（但故意做得不像 AI）

Kyle: We're doing our every-year revenue planning. We talk about, "Okay, what is it going to look like for next year?" Of course as you imagine there's slideshows everywhere, talking about what are we going to talk about, what's the narrative, etc.

I'm like, "Okay, well, I could probably just build something to build this, and then that way I don't have to go build the whole spreadsheet where I have to pass it to my team." We went through this process and I got all the information and used the skills I mentioned. I built a little app just to make it so I could look at some of the information in a SQLite database more easily. And I ultimately built this entire presentation without touching any of it.

I was like, "Okay, I'm just going to present this to our CRO, the CFO, their teams, without mentioning I built it with AI." I built a skill to make it look very much not AI-driven. Not pretty — but just very clearly not AI. Like, "Don't do anything interesting. Just go exactly..."

Did the whole thing through. It used my notes from Obsidian. It used all the context I mentioned before. The plans. And it never came up once that it was AI generated. Never once. It didn't matter.

So now I can take that tool and go, "Look, I don't want you to go build slideshows. They're just helping us share information with each other. If this thing can do it with a little bit of crafting from you, and then we can look at it together, awesome. There's no value in all that extra work."

The ability to make it look humanly bad, and to build a little app to manipulate the data — I think is part of that upside for devs that are now in leadership roles. Because the thing I feel like — like I said before this — that's all a people problem.

Sean: I know if you've used Copilot or not to build a slide deck unless you spent a bunch of time to not. There's a certain charm to just being blatantly AI. You're just honest about "there may be mistakes here that I cannot vouch for." So how much value is there?

But the real question I want to ask is: there's — you were a chief of staff to Thomas — and in the pre-AI world, that job would have been a chief-of-staff job of "can you prep me these slides and all that," and now you do it yourself?

Kyle: I still have a chief of staff. The difference is — it's the discussion every time we have some sort of technology evolution: it's not that the jobs, the roles, all go away. They just change. I don't have someone spending all their time building out slides for me in presentations because I don't need that anymore. But now I need that person who is able to go and find all the different connections between humans in those discussions, to help me find out, "Okay, I should be meeting with this group and this team, and they have an opportunity, and I'm going to be in San Francisco today, I'm going to be in Seattle tomorrow."

Those human connection aspects are still incredibly valuable and have always been a big part of that chief-of-staff role. Chiefs of staff are not opening up letters to process — they're doing email, you know what I mean? It's the same thing. And now they're not building out as many of these presentations because they have the ability to have AI take it on for them. And share that with me — great, let's keep moving — because it's allowing us to go faster and make better decisions more quickly.

GitHub 简史：从 Web Hooks 到 Actions 到收购 NPM

Sean: Awesome. We can dive into more sort of productivity insights as you go. I did want to do a little bit of a brief history of GitHub. Because we started here — and then you also were involved in the NPM acquisition. I did want to touch up on that. And then more recently I just want to bring up to present day where we're having uptime issues, which transparently you've already addressed publicly, but we'll discuss in the pod. Did I miss anything?

Kyle: It's a lot of years to cover. One highlight was, right before that acquisition closed in 2018, I got to launch the first version of Actions at GitHub Universe.

Sean: They're that young?

Kyle: It was October of 2018 I think. I was an engineering leader on that project and got to launch that. And then we did acquisitions of NPM, like you said, Semmle, Dependabot, Pendo, like a whole bunch of things — that was a big one. Pendo, right? Abby (Obie) is doing well, did well on DX.

That was the big shift after the acquisition: I joined the business side.

Sean: So I need to hit you on some of these things because you were there, right? How often do I get to talk to someone? But Actions — is that the number one source of security issues on GitHub?

Kyle: Oh sh— I mean, the number one source of security issues is probably the literal code in everyone's underlying repositories. I would say back further than that — if you remember, like in this graph — this is ultimately web hooks. Like, back then it says "GitHub Services." Do you see — it says Hookshot, Hookshot FE for front-end, and then it says "GitHub Services."

GitHub Services back in the old days, right — we had a repository that was Ruby code, and you could write any Ruby code in there, and then we would execute that on your behalf as a service. That way if you were trying to integrate with something, we would run it for you.

Sean: And of course no containers.

Kyle: No, because it was 2014. There was some isolation obviously, but it was mostly the separations on the server level. That's an example, as long as the very old version of Pages, which ran on its own containerization infrastructure not on Actions — which is an all-time great product, Pages powers the internet at this point, to some degree.

Those were places where, to my knowledge, but it was those things where I'm looking at and going, "Okay, well, we can't be running arbitrary Ruby code on everyone's behalf," then containerizing all of that up into Actions, now where, yeah, the containerization is really good. The pinning — most folks aren't pinning to a particular SHA — their workflows, and so that's a big place of pain for folks. Similar to any dependency management — v1 or newest or latest.

But that journey from that day — "Okay, we're just going to run all this arbitrary code and it'll basically be okay" — to now: we have really good containerization. We have a new underlying agent containerization service through — we're using it under the hood. It's through Azure. They recently announced it: Azure Dev Compute. Very fast compute to be able to spin up your own cloud agents. We're using it under the hood for some parts of the new — Box? No, no, no. Dev Compute.

Sean: Not finding it just yet.

Kyle: Oh, it's in there somewhere.

Sean: All right, well, we'll cut that out.

Kyle: Sorry. But with Dev Compute, you can run really, really fast — spin up really small VMs really, really quickly. So you're doing a tool call, just do it containerized. Exactly. We're using that. So definitely moving in that direction to protect us from every piece of code that we're ultimately running.

That grows into the full SDLC. Code hosting was just the start, and then it's grown beyond that.

NPM 收购：拯救互联网的供应链

Sean: Let's talk about NPM maybe, because that's also a very major point in the industry. It was looking for a home, kind of struggling as a business, right? I don't know how you characterize that whole acquisition.

Kyle: When we were talking to the team, the big thing for both of us was to find a way to keep NPM — which was basically powering the internet then, and way more so now to some degree — running. Keep it going, continue to scale. It was having scaling problems if I recall, back at that time they were doing some rewrites.

Sean: That's cute compared to now.

Kyle: Yeah, well, that's the thing. When I'm talking to folks now, there's so many more underlying uses of NPM than there were when we had them join in with GitHub.

But that was ultimately the goal: we used to have Pages, we have the world's code — let's make sure that we can keep NPM running well for the world. We put a bunch of time and investment into fixing some of the underlying backend changes, some of which we talked about, like some of the manifest work, etc. And then now really trying to bring the security posture of NPM up to speed.

It is a unique challenge in that every move that we make to make it more secure will break a lot of people. Security is paramount, and we take it very seriously, where anytime that we have a problem with GitHub where we make a change that makes us more secure but hurts — there's like a snow day for developers, or a really bad fire that they have to go put out.

So we've changed the 2FA policies, we've changed the way the tokens work. When we find tokens that have been exposed or potentially exposed, we invalidate them.

Sean: I love that feature of GitHub that creates issues.

Kyle: We're trying to push the community forward without necessarily doing something that is going to break the contract that's been for 15 years — or close to, some amount of years — on NPM.

"Slop Forks" 与开源安全的未来

Sean: Now we're talking about open source and publishing. I think there's something here with what people are calling slop forks — which I think Malta from Vercel is doing. And part of me thinks, well, the way to get past any vulnerability is: let's just get rid of the concept of NPM, and we only publish source code. Anytime you want to import it, you have your coding agent look at it and then adapt whatever subset you're going to use into your — like vendor it. But the AI vendors it. Is that realistic? Will that solve all our security issues?

Kyle: I don't think it will solve — Mitchell was just talking about this today. Mitch Hashimoto was just talking about this today. In some ways it's all things "old or new again." Like, vendoring everything — I do remember 2013, 2014.

Sean: Yeah, we must return.

Kyle: We were vendoring everything. We were having actual discussions around, "Should we take this full thing? Why is this so big? We only need this one file." So I do think there's something true there, where having either taking only what you need or the dependencies just getting incredibly small over time will help to some degree.

But it's not going to solve the fundamental problem, I don't think. Because the vulnerabilities — in an agent looking at them, time and time again, there's a million different ways in which we can convince an agent that this thing is secure or not, and pull it in. Or we can do static code analysis or runtime testing to say whether the code works or not. That is, I think, the step that needs to continue to be invested in. The question is just on how much scope: should it be this enormous project that I'm pulling down, or should it be this piece?

Either way, most companies are running some amount of security checking on the packages that they're bringing in or vendoring. That I think won't change. That's what Advanced Security does, to some degree. Socket does some degree. Everyone is doing a piece of that. How we each do that — especially when we're talking to enterprise customers — is just very, very different. No one wants one single way to do it.

That's always been GitHub's unique position in the world. I talk a lot to maintainers. I talk a lot to folks about this. We rarely start a process and a practice and push it onto the community. We usually wait for the sort of RFC process — socially or literally — everyone agreeing, and then we'll cement something in.

Sean: It's your role in GitHub — yeah, we don't want to shape the whole thing. We want it to be figured out.

Kyle: How do you balance that role in the industry to keep everything as secure as possible, and make sure that you're not going to be compromised as a human — because that's usually how it all happens — and not create a process or lock us into a flow that you're not going to like, or Mitchell's not going to like, or other open source projects aren't going to like? That's always been a tricky balance for us.

That's something that we haven't talked about enough: we're not going to be able to fix everything for everyone in a way that everyone is going to like. So help us — tell us what is working.

When Mitchell was talking about — when he's talking to us, I was chatting with him and talking to him about this, and I put it on Twitter, and we talked over DM. We're going to keep working. But the important thing is, "Keep — I do actually want to hear what isn't working for you, and be as specific and clear for your project as is possible." And to every piece of credit, over the many years that we've known each other through the industry, he's always done that, and I appreciate that. Because there are places that we need to fix up, and we hear from him and we'll fix up, just like we do all other kinds of maintainers.

That process between you making those types of improvements and being more secure — and creating, I forget what he calls it, it's not the proof process, not the claims process. Do you know what I'm talking about? He has that — his projects have a way for you to kind of like vouch.

Sean: He has like the vouch system, for saying, "Hey, you should accept my PRs."

Kyle: I just built this into GitHub.

Sean: I don't know.

Kyle: You say that, and he and his community really likes this — and then I'll go talk to other maintainers, and other maintainers globally, and they're like, "No, this doesn't work for me." That is the tension. But also the kind of beauty of GitHub, depending on which way you look at it, is: we want to help maintainers. So we create all these tools to let you have more control over how much you take in from AI and PRs. But you can also use this — you can go use this project, and if it takes off and becomes the mostly standard, then yeah, we probably wouldn't enforce it, but we would add it in, because that's the flow that we tend to do.

Sean: A lot of people don't know the history of the pull request. That's something that GitHub standardized, basically.

Kyle: Yeah, it was a very messy process beforehand. And now we have the benefit of it being the process. Now we have to go and figure out the next best process, or what adaptations change, or what does a pull request look like when 80% of your PRs are just coming from your agents and not from other devs?

Pull Request → Prompt Request？信任作为社会问题

Sean: Do you like the prompt request idea from Peter?

Kyle: I think for each idea — I think each has its merits. I'm not avoiding saying anything good or bad. But I feel like I've seen a version of, "We have that in entirety," in Thomas's startup — "take all the assets of what you've built and put that in." I think that's got great ideas. There's all these various permutations of the PR flow.

But I think the reason why there's not a single answer is: ultimately, we're trying to codify trust. We're trying to say, "Okay, if Sean reviews this, I'm going to trust it, because you're Sean, or you're the senior dev, or you're the whatever."

Right now, when we are working in a flow where an agent writes code and another agent reviews code, and then Kyle goes and looks at it — the trust is kind of diffuse. Most of the tools that we're talking about are talking more about verification flows. We have more assets to look at. So I can probably say whether this is a good PR or not. But that still doesn't solve, I think, the human problem of: I'm looking at a PR and I want to know if I can trust it. And we still tend to use human signals for that — Mitchell approving it, or Kyle approving it, or whatever.

I think that's why most of these options haven't really solved it: because it's a social problem ultimately. It's a human problem to review it and agree, or you fully trust the tool and you're imbuing that tool with full trust — which in some cases that absolutely exists.

Sean: So in the same way that there will be a tipping point in society when we don't allow humans to drive anymore — because machines are measurably better than humans — I'm looking for that tipping point. Mythos is ridiculously expensive. Someday we'll have Mythos on a desktop. I don't know. What does that change the equation?

Kyle: I took a Waymo here, and I was on my phone and not looking around at all. There are other self-driving vehicles that I would not trust while staring at the road.

Sean: Is this a Zoox?

Kyle: I think that is both — depending on what level of self-driving. My point is sort of: I think part of that is, I strongly believe that's a mixture of verifiable proof — how many accidents, how much data, and so on — and the human aspect of how I feel when I'm in this car, what it tells me, etc.

That's why I think some of our AI tools tend to imbue me with more of that feeling of trust, even if the data says this is 100% accurate. I feel like it takes more time for us to go, "Should I trust this or not?" And that's in the soft sense of startups with high agency, weekend projects, and open source. And then there's enterprises and regulated industries and everything else. That is an even harder problem to go solve. Because even when it is fully verified, not only do you have to have trust from the humans on the team, you probably have to have trust from multinational, multi-governments around the world, regulating agencies.

So that's where I feel like, until we tip over to your point on the sort of human EQ side — like, "I feel okay, this feels okay, I've been proven enough" — then the ball will start to roll a lot faster, where we'll end up getting to "okay, we can trust this and feel good about it" in the most difficult of cases.

Sean: If human trust is the thing that matters, I feel like GitHub as the developer social network could maybe do more there. Like, Voucher is one system, but we have star counts, and then we have contributor rights — and that's it. I feel like there should be more in that space. I don't know if there's any other design decisions that—

Kyle: I think one of the places that we don't really expose right now in this sort of way is some degree of hard trust and support — which for me is like Sponsors is a good example of that. It costs you something to prove that, "I believe in your project and I trust you to some degree." Or, "I want to support you, at the very least."

Sean: Self-payments for open source. So why not?

Kyle: As we keep moving forward, there's more and more projects where I'm adding more and more dollars into Sponsors personally because I want to support them. But I also know — I've probably never met them in person, but I know enough of their work that I want to support them.

The thing that I don't love about stars or commit counts or anything else is: ultimately, even with all of the various abuse and de-spamming and de-duplication work that we do — these are all not active social signals, they're passive ones, that are ultimately gameable. And you may trust me, but another open source maintainer may not — and on what heuristic should you be trusting me? That, I think, is kind of where some of our thinking is right now. What signal from me is most important to you, if you can define that? Potentially, honestly, like in an agentic workflow.

That's what we see some of these open source projects do, where you have GitHub Actions and then you have an agentic workflow that's calling AI, and you're setting these rules: if Kyle has submitted and gotten accepted PRs across any given project, and has a social handle tied to his account in GitHub, and that social account's older than a certain amount — really complex measures that matter to you. Because most open source projects have that heuristic built into their heads, if not written down in the contributing guidelines.

You could take that and apply it, and then just say, "Oh, we're not going to accept this PR." Building something that is malleable to everyone's needs is a little bit better, rather than going, "Hmm, this account's too young." Because what happens? The attackers just go and build, create a multitude of accounts, and they wait until it ages up. "Needs to have a certain amount of stars" — that's how star inflation happens. "Need to have a certain amount of repos with PRs" — they all just create repos and submit PRs to each other, and then they come in and do something nefarious.

It's hard to find the measure. So we're looking more at: how can we provide you tools so you can choose what's best for you? And of course we'll give you some standards. But the trust vector gets down to — I don't know — some version of like human digital ID, like everyone's been talking about. How do I prove that it's me on the internet?

Sean: Give me your eyeballs.

Kyle: Exactly.

Stars 真的破了吗？以及 GitHub 的 2 亿"开发者"

Sean: I got to keep moving on topics, but obviously I could go all day on this. I've been involved in GitHub and open source my entire professional career. Stars — very superficial, everyone knows it. But time to 100,000 stars is the fastest I've ever seen. People just reached that in I don't know, months. And then at the same time, I don't trust it, right? How many of these are real or bought or whatever? I don't know how to ask this, but what can we do about it? Is stars broken? Is stars fine?

Kyle: There are kind of two pieces. Obviously, we're constantly trying to find ways in which your users are producing spam — which I would include like only doing star gamification. When we find them, we pluck them out.

Sean: As like whack-a-mole.

Kyle: It's 100% like whack-a-mole, now like powered by AI to be helpful.

But more so what I'm seeing — a lot of the "fastest time to X" tends to be because we're now inviting so many more people into software development on GitHub, that the zeitgeist is just swarming. It's not just developers. And it's not you and I, you know, however you want to say what a developer is. It's not just folks that have been coding for a very long time. It's folks that have maybe started coding, or only joined in since the AI era.

Sean: What's the latest Octo number? I know 80 million was my last memory of the number of developers on GitHub.

Kyle: Oh, we're over 200 million now.

Sean: Yeah, okay.

Kyle: Over 200 million developers now.

Sean: But it's not developers, right? It's people with a GitHub account.

Kyle: This is the biggest debate that everyone loves to have at GitHub at this point. From my perspective, there's clearly a difference between professional enterprise developer and developers. But the idea that we should be splitting hairs or segmenting developers in the early era of software development is not worth our time.

Sean: You get into gatekeeping.

Kyle: 100%. I wasn't a developer when I started writing code. I made — I cloned the thing seven years before I learned to code. And then I wrote about my learning-to-code journey, and people called me a fraud because I had a GitHub account.

Sean: I just use GitHub but I don't know — I didn't know what.

Kyle: I remember those sets of posts. I fight very clearly on the line of: if you create code, if you have an idea and you create it into some way of "I'm going to run it and use the app right now" — you may still use AI in that moment, but that's okay. At some point you're going to do the next thing. You're going to create — you're going to have to learn about this database. You're going to fix a bug. We're all on some same journey.

And those people are also hearing about the great new agent skill package, or a new CLI tool, or a new whatever. Those projects are going up because you want to be a part of this moment, just like I wanted to be a part of the Ruby community when Ruby was popping off when I started becoming a developer. And now I can just click the star button.

So yes, there's clearly some amount of spamming and gamification that we're working against. But I really think we're just seeing this whole new cohort of folks that are moving from technology to technology because they're not working on a 20-year-old software application. They're working on a side app that they built on the weekend for their friends, or for their new idea, or whatever. That's how you see these enormous charts going up and to the right with stars.

Sean: Something that's remarkable is the persistence — that GitHub extends to those folks. Usually when I see platforms go into a new audience, they usually have to have a second platform with a different name that wraps the main platform. But somehow GitHub has been able to persist and extend, and it's friendly and whatever. It's nice.

Kyle: That's partially why, as we've tried to move into more low-code things — we started working on Spark as a way to build an app and run it. The reality is, anytime we try to put even a veneer on top of it without — when we put a veneer on top of something, we still always show you the code. That's kind of like a tenet. We're never going to hide the code from you, ever.

However, what we learned with things like Spark is that really the value of Spark for most devs is easy runtime. You may have a runtime or a host that you're going to use for that, or you just build something and run it. But the package of making that even more simple isn't really needed for folks that are trying to build software — and not just trying to build an app, which is a slightly different goal.

I want to get you in. I want to get you comfortable. The best thing for me, as someone that did not traditionally come into software dev way, way back — I want anyone to be able to breach that chasm and not be in — I don't know, I feel like we're still in an era of like "STEM, STEM, STEM." I've got a 12-year-old and an 8-year-old, and it's like, we got to get them into STEM, over and over. I do the things that good parents do — I was like, "Oh, we want to do coding, yes, I want to do coding, do coding classes."

But now they're just not afraid of doing software. And that's, I think, the thing that's honestly kept me at GitHub for so long. Anyone should be able to go and build a thing, just like I can go change a light switch in my house. I'm not going to go into the breaker box because I'll probably kill myself. But I can go change that light switch. Everyone should be able to go and say, "This freaking app doesn't do what I want. I want it to work like this."

That's what's kept us all connected with GitHub through the years — and some, during the easiest of times, during the hard times, because of that opportunity of "we're the home for all developers, and we want everyone to be able to have that feeling that we've had of: I had an idea, I created it, and holy s—, here it is."

GitHub 的"史上最难也最激动人心"时刻：14x 增长

Sean: All right, I'm going to try to do more spicy questions. Is it an easy time now or a hard time at GitHub?

Kyle: It's a hard time. And also, I was just with my team and I said, "This is also the best and most exciting time that I think I can remember at GitHub."

Sean: Best of times, worst of times. Never?

Kyle: We've — we're talking about Octoverse reports, and usually we do an Octoverse report once a year and we look at the numbers and we say, "Oh my goodness." I was at Universe in October saying, "This is the fastest year of growth that we've ever had." And now we're doing more in a month than we did in a year last year.

Sean: You're talking about PRs, commits—

Kyle: PRs, kind of like — you name it. By roughly every measure that we're looking at, there's some amount of growth that is much, much bigger. And that is breaking our system in new ways, not old ways.

Web hooks were always notoriously unreliable over the years.

Sean: Whose fault is that?

Kyle: Not anymore mine. But for a period of time, I'm sure you could pull up a tweet that was like, "It was me, I'm sorry."

But now that got rewritten at a scale level that is still working and is not having problems today. Now what we're finding isn't just the simple stuff that folks are on Twitter or on the internet are like, "Hey, why is this like this?" Sure, there's absolutely silly problems that shouldn't exist. But now we're talking about unique novel permission problems that happen only at scale across all different objects or whatever, that now we have to go rewrite this underlying system.

There are problems that caught us off-guard, which I think — like I said, the growth is astronomical, but also we're making such material progress that I'm excited. Once we've reimagined the underlying foundation layer or pieces of it at least, what's going to be possible when it's not just all of us and all the new people that are becoming developers and all of their agents in all the tools, like working together — that'll still happen in that GitHub tool, that GitHub community.

But it's a hard day anytime we can't give you what you're looking for. We have the same problem internally. We operate through GitHub.com, of course. We have backups when things go down and whatnot for our own operations. But we feel it too. If it's not working, it's not working for us. And that's the promise of dogfooding for GitHub — it's always been true. We're using the same tool you're using. We're not using a super secret version. So we also need it to be great for us, for our customers, of course, for open source. And now an exponential growth of agents doing it too.

14x 增长的具体含义，与扩容的物理墙

Sean: I wanted to load — for audio listeners who maybe haven't seen your tweets — 1 billion commits in 2025. Now it's 275 million per week, on pace for 14 billion this year. If growth remains linear, is that still the pace?

Kyle: It's still speeding up. This was in April.

Sean: All right. So basically you have 14x growth year on year. I think that's a scaling issue. I'm going to try to really steel-man this thing. People have experienced 14x growth — they haven't had your downtime. And that's like — can we go dig into that? Like, why? What broke? What are we doing to fix it? Anything for the community to reassure them.

Kyle: There are a couple different places that we've seen the growth issues. Some of them — which is why I was talking about pushing hard on more CPUs — is in Actions in particular. More tools, more agents, more PRs mean more builds. More builds need more CPUs. So we are expanding through not just our data center, but obviously we were talking about moving to Azure and adding additional cloud compute — because we simply need more CPUs. Not as much GPUs. We definitely need GPUs too, but now CPUs are becoming a factor underneath the hood when it comes to some of the underlying services.

We've been breaking up our database infrastructure over the years so that we have more cognitive separation between the various services. The place that we continue to have pain is in permissioning. Right now many of our permissioning layers sit into a database that we internally call "MySQL 1." Old hovers will know what I'm talking about. We've been pulling things out of MySQL 1 for many, many years — because we use Vitess, and we use other technologies.

Sean: Vitess was born from this?

Kyle: 100%. Sam — old hover and friend.

Finding these opportunities to break this out and then do that globally — the other thing that I think is interesting in both a unique opportunity and tricky: we also run everything I just talked about in a black-box container with GitHub Enterprise Server for people that work on-prem. So we take everything I just said and we also do it on-prem, and we also do all of that in a data-resident setup for customers that need to have their data in a single location.

Each of these has the unique characteristic around how we're storing that data in MySQL or in a permissioning setup. That's where some of these outages have occurred, where you're seeing it more across the board rather than just the one piece isn't quite working.

Part of it is — there's been some other places where agents are much more, or more projects appear to be moving towards monorepo. Versus we were going the other direction for many, many years in the industry. Repos were smaller but there were more of them. And now we're seeing the opposite. Repos are bigger, and there's not fewer of them per se, because there's new growth, but we're just seeing many more big repos.

Big monorepos have always had a unique performance problem. Each one is slightly different, particularly if the underlying blobs are incredibly big inside the repos. We've put a ton of work — that most people probably haven't experienced unless you're in the case of the monorepo — into Git infrastructure layer improvement. That does help the overall system, because many of the improvements that make monorepos work better make all repo infrastructure work better.

I can kind of keep going down the line. Another thing — we're moving out of, we're changing how we do job queuing for lack of a better explanation, changing the underlying technologies there.

Sean: I spent two years being a job queuing guy.

Kyle: It's a little bit of piece by piece. And it's mostly because as it was built, we built everything in a way that assumed in some ways that the size of the pipe of work was going to remain the same. There's just going to be more people coming through each of those pipes. But instead, now in places where a Git push was generally a certain size, for example, is no longer true.

Sean: Oh yeah.

Kyle: Or on the average online commits — same thing with PRs. We've talked about optimizing that and making changes where there were technology choices that did not work. It got slow, did not do what users wanted. We've been reeling that all out, going, "Okay, that's just not right. Let's stop putting good money after bad and do it the right way."

When I've experienced scale at GitHub historically, it's almost always two options that we've used. We go vertical scaling, particularly with databases. And we go horizontal scaling — "Oh, we just have more people using the service, great, we're going to add more servers and rack them in our data center, we use it in a cloud."

Now we're sort of in a diagonal, where vertical doesn't really work anymore, horizontal isn't working either — because we all have some CPU or GPU constraints in the world now. And now we have to go crack open services that have been running for 10 or 15 years and go, "Okay, the rules of this service have legitimately changed, and now we have to rewrite them."

None of this is an excuse. We have to do the work. We have to make it better.

信任与透明：GitHub 的可用性合同

Sean: As an infra guy, I'm like, this is one of the most fascinating scaling challenges I've ever seen.

Kyle: That's the thing — when we weren't talking about it publicly, and I was like, "Hey, I just want to explain what's going on" — part of it comes from a very old GitHub ethos, which is, "It's our uptime, it's down."

I know you're a developer, so you're inclined to want to understand more what's going on. But at the same time, us going, "Hey, this service didn't perform the way we expected, and now we have to go change it" — we weren't trying to hide anything from you. It's just, that's our problem, because you expect us to be up. I think that's really baked into the core origins of GitHub.

What we're trying to do as a team now is do all that work, and just tell — talk about it more. Just share you more technical details. Write these blogs, write the posts, get the engineers who built it after they finished the work, just tell you, "Okay, this is what we did." That's the contract we want to bring back to the community: "Hey, we're still very serious about what we're doing. We haven't been telling you about each piece. So let's do that." And we're going to keep building this and scaling it in a way to support — if it's not 14x, then it's 30x or 50x, or whatever the next exponential growth is going to be.

Sean: Fantastic answer.

Kyle: I apologize in advance if any of that is slightly incorrect, simply because I'm still in the weeds with this, but it's not my day-to-day. But that's the thing — we're all looking at it to that level. If people want to help, they can join.

Sean: I think people also just want to know — when are you through the thick of it? Have we identified all the issues? Is this just never ending? Is Git broken? Do we have to change the Git protocol? How much is breaking? It's been a while. People do want to know what's the path back to the reliability that everyone expects out of GitHub.

Kyle: Our availability in recent few weeks has been much better than the three weeks before that or the three weeks before that, and so forth. A lot of these improvements are still very much paying off for us. We're still working on that database piece that I mentioned, and that's just a little bit physics — a little bit of time to get it fixed up.

Sean: My answer I had in my head was "call YouTube."

Kyle: YouTube ultimately—

Sean: They also use Vitess.

Kyle: They also use Vitess, but — like whoever was the scaling guy at YouTube — I believe went to PlanetScale and was a part of PlanetScale too.

Sean: Oh, you mean—

Kyle: I think so. Yeah.

Sean: He's super based now. The whole Postgres drama.

Kyle: Yeah, totally. Some of it's that. The other piece is, our move to get additional compute will alleviate a fair amount of this, particularly on the Action side, because a lot of the underlying outages is actually related to—

Sean: Telling you, Actions — it's the root of all evil.

Kyle: It's the core compute layer for either CI, side project — Actions.

Sean: I pay a lot for compute.

Kyle: Actions is definitely a piece of the overall business, but we ultimately also give away so many minutes as part of our entitlements. As I was saying, everyone's using it. We talk about it as CI/CD, but the reality is people use it for CI/CD and various processing and automation.

Sean: Exactly.

Kyle: Part of it is also that compute piece. My abuse of Actions — I have been scraping for every day.

Sean: Thank you for your service.

Kyle: But this is also how I track Actions and time. So anyway — each month I expect, in the next three months you're going to see fewer and fewer moments where we have an availability problem, where things are going to go down. And that's not just "it stopped" — it's that we're still experiencing faster growth than ever before. It's just that those underlying improvements that we've been hard at work on are finally paying off. It's less about these incremental improvements where you make a small change and you get this big output. It's now material change that takes a bit of time, and then you see a step change in our availability.

Sean: There's the thing we used to do at Amazon — I don't know if it's like a thing — but you know, automated software verification or simulation of load testing and all that. At this point you have a whole map of GitHub. You can assume whatever growth rates on whatever dimensions that you care about, and just run it through the system. I feel like there's a way to have a systems model of GitHub and see what breaks. But obviously I'm not that close to problems.

Kyle: Totally. That's been the journey and work that's been happening since I would say November to now. Because October was the time where we even said, "Oh, look at the growth," and then you start to see the chart really pick up. It's like, "Oh, we tested it at n amount of scale, and now it's at n-cubed maybe, in some vectors." So now we have to go build it that way and make sure it can handle all of that scale.

Copilot 的现状：从代码补全到 Agent 编排

Sean: Let's talk Copilot. How many original creators of Copilot are there? I count like 12.

Kyle: I forget — all joking aside, I forget the number of people that were on the original GitHub Copilot team, but there was a bigger group.

Sean: It's Alex—

Kyle: Alex worked on it. Hugo worked on it. There's a bunch of people, and their entire management line.

Sean: So — enormously successful in its day. I think the last number — I think Mario came to my conference and talked about the hundred million mark. I think most recently 300. I might be out of date as well there.

Kyle: I don't think we've shared the dollar amounts.

Sean: All right, cool. Just like, what's the state of Copilot? It's obviously, as a concept, brought into more of Microsoft. But just at GitHub.

Kyle: One of the challenges that we had with Copilot is that we came out the gate with code completion. It was super great, powerful, etc. And then what we initially worked on after that initial year, year and a half, was going after fine-tuning. Because our customers, the industry on the whole, was really talking about, "Okay, well, how do we get more correct or performant out of this?" So we were working on a whole bunch of efforts to do fine-tuning on larger and larger code completions, or like next-edit suggestions with fine-tuning.

Sean: Let me clarify — is this fine-tuning one model, or per-customer fine-tuned model?

Kyle: Both. Fine-tuning one model for the overall use, and fine-tuning per customer that wants this as a service.

Around that time is when the next generation of models came. And that's around the same time that all these other AI coding tools came to be — because the models really, really sped up.

Everyone will ask, "Well, what happened to GitHub Copilot? There's all this time." I would say that we were on an era of going, "Okay, we want to improve everyone's results, so let's focus in on fine-tuning, because that'll give us these better results." And then the models got better.

Ever since, we've been really on this journey to go, "Okay, of course we have this great code completion, and we've done a ton of investment in the better underlying models that we have, post-trained, better next-edit suggestions, post-train language-specific models" — all the stuff that sits in the ether of GitHub Copilot is code completion — but also now have a single underlying SDK and harness for our coding agent: Copilot, ultimately, the new CLI, the new desktop app, cloud agents that use the same SDK.

There was this moment of really, really trying to figure out what our customers want — models Sherlocking us a little bit, and then going and saying, "Okay, what does everyone ultimately need?" And what we think is that it's not solely about the code generation. It's really about having the ability to use these coding agent harnesses or runtimes across not just the coding experience — where I'm going to send a bunch of tasks out, or I'm going to use Fleet to break up a single task, or autopilot similar to Goal, all this stuff — but also: how do I do that for all of my security remediation? How do I do that for every GitHub issue that comes in? Just stick a coding agent on it just to say if it's possible. How do you go through my repository and see all of my documentation and extract out, "Okay, this doesn't actually match"?

That amount of AI coding agent automation, I think, is a big part of what we see when we're looking at, "Okay, we're still going through a similar but very different flow." It's just all happening at the same time. There's not really the same, "I'm going to create an issue to track my idea of building this." You're probably just going to go do it. You're going to say, "Hey, just build this."

And there are still tons of open issues and projects, etc., that are using issues — like Peter and OpenClaw — to be able to sic all of his agent on that infrastructure layer. A really, really great coding experience that allows you to handle the multiplexing aspect is what we've built — are still building — with GitHub Copilot.

For folks that haven't really used GitHub Copilot since the thing that got them excited about this — I really encourage you to look at, especially, the GitHub Copilot app. That's my new daily driver. If you prefer the CLI, also the CLI. Be able to use all the models. The bring-your-own-key side of it. We're still improving our own models and using those too.

It's a very different experience. But that broader sense of software development, and how coding agents can help throughout — not just writing the code, or even verifying it, or deploying it — is where we have this unique angle.

The other side is the context piece.

Sean: Oh god.

Kyle: We're still — it's one of those things where the final thing that will let me feel complete at GitHub is when we have this ability for GitHub to act like Kyle wants it to act, or Sean, or whatever. And we all codify that in rules and in memory and everything else.

Sean: That's an open research problem, right?

Kyle: 100%. But if we can even just do it where my team — without me having to codify everything — and as our methods shift on purpose, to be able to have that full experience and all the understanding of what's happening in my dependencies or open source. That feels like a big place for us to be able to continue to provide something really unique and valuable with GitHub Copilot.

还有什么形态？Ambient AI 与软件开发的真正终点

Sean: Is there a form factor that we haven't explored? We did code completion. Then we did, broadly call it, agentic IDE — which Cursor famously popularized.
