And then now it's all about the sort of agent orchestration, background agent, whatever, whatever. And then there's the security review. I feel like everyone has just thrown agents at everything. The entire SDLC has been covered with agents. Are we at the end of history here, basically? Is it just refinements from here on out?

Kyle: I think we're all still in this hyper-myopic era of AI, where the reality is that — for various boring security and governance reasons, at least for most people's work — why is my coding agent, even if it's all background agents, not losing all the context that's available to it across everything that I'm doing outside of coding?

The most interesting thing to me in AI is actual ambient AI. Not "insert assistant name thing." I've tried just about every pin and tool and whatever, and they don't work the way that I'm looking for them to work. Because they are just trying to capture, and then they are trying to codify, and then recall.

The thing that I'm looking for is, back to the very beginning: I'm looking to be building out the next version of web hooks, or like, implementing a new feature, and for it to know every spec doc, every email, the conversations that I've had online, everything about how this could be implemented — and be able to use that as part of its decision-making.

And none of these tools are ultimately doing this. It's as if software development was a single-lane task: "It only needs a developer. Once I write the perfect code, we'll be done here." But that's just never been true. It's all the context of the other team members, what the business is doing, what's popular right now.

That's this huge opportunity for us to go much broader than really, really excellent coding agents. That is honestly why I think OpenClaw has been so interesting — sure, it's connecting to all the data sources that Kyle the human cares about. And now my question is, "Okay, how can I take all that and use that every day as a software dev, connected together?" Not just have a new way to kick off a coding agent.

That's where we're at. We're saying, "Okay, I'm going to go use this CLI under the hood, or this SDK." But that's not what I'm talking about. I'm talking about: I'm having a conversation with you. It downloads the podcast and it realizes, "Oh, Kyle sounds like Kyle needs this app, or this thing, or this — that level." Exactly. That level of connectivity is where we still have a ton of ways to go in software. Because then, when we have that red thread, we want to pull that idea — it can not only use the perfect way to write that code, but instead, all of the taste and judgment calls and expertise that I've earned, or that we've earned as a group, and use it as part of the actual implementation.

Sean: The extreme of it is AI runs your life. And I think there's a scary inversion of control — in the way that developers mean it in terms of frameworks, like the Hollywood principle, "Don't call me, I'll call you." At some point there's an inversion of control where you should stop telling the AI what to do. AI tells you what to do. That's a little bit scary, but also maybe better.

Kyle: Nat Friedman shared this in like a Stripe event, talking about his OpenClaw — he connected OpenClaw to his cameras, and it was like watching—

Sean: Directed his Uber.

Kyle: There's a degree of this where I was like, "I actually would love OpenClaw to tell me to drink water." I don't know that I want it to be changing where my car goes. But I do think that's what I'm talking about, which is: it needs to have so much more information at its disposal for it to be helpful to me.

I still don't think we're anywhere near talking about AGI. I'm just talking about: every time I have to tell you something I care about that I've ever said, or I've said a dozen times — it should be able to know that, codify that, or gain access to it.

The Dreaming Ideas-like stuff are an attempt to do some version of this, but I think there's a much more proactive angle that will help software devs if we can test that out a bit more.

为什么 Microsoft 有一位 OpenClaw 的 CVP？

Sean: Well, the other thing about OpenClaw that reminded me — Microsoft has a CVP dedicated to OpenClaw. Why?

Kyle: Because you don't think they should?

Sean: I don't know. I think CVP is a high title.

Kyle: Yeah.

Sean: Why is this so important? Microsoft doesn't even own OpenClaw. What's—

Kyle: We're talking a lot more about this at Microsoft Build this year too. The main thing is that what OpenClaw has done is, it has made this connection for people to have access to the resources that you have access to, and be able to do things for you in a way that previously people were trying to codify into their own agents.

When you think about it in the work context — wouldn't it be great to have a Claw-like object that I could actually run on my work device, that had access to my work assets, worked well on Windows? What that would look like.

OpenClaw has become a personification of a valuable agent that understands me because it has access to all of my information, and it can use a computer — and thus it can do a lot more than just a task-oriented process or a chat tool.

That's a bunch of the goal of Build, right? We're at Build this year trying to take a very different approach. It's unapologetically aimed at developers. We're trying to show the bigger investment to not just say, "Hey, like you said, why do you have a CVP of OpenClaw?" Well, because one of the problems that we have is, if you install our agents on a — not on a Mac mini, or not on a hosted device — you install them on a personal device or a work device, we need better sandboxing at the OS level. I need to be able to use that Claw and not get fired.

So Microsoft is like, "Okay, great, let's do that too." And then it's, "Okay, well, where should I be able to talk to this agent? Should each of us just have a Claw available to us at work?" Probably. So there you go.

Continuing to contribute a ton to the open source project too. Microsoft, as I've gotten more information, there's so much investment into the open source projects themselves that — for whatever reason, I think those teams don't want to come off as taking any credit or getting any recognition. But so many of these core contributors of teams are full-time just pushing into open source projects.

That kind of shows the difference between — "Well, why are we looking so hard at something like Claw? Why are we looking at sandboxing on Windows? Why are we looking at cloud versions of sandboxing?" Because ultimately, we need more platform components. We don't need everyone to be building the same exact topline product. If we're building for builders, that requires us to give you all these components and tell you what they are, how they work, and why you should be interested — versus only delivering that single vertical, over and over and over again.

Sean: My maybe one way of framing it: Microsoft is the original operating systems company. And here's the new operating system for AI.

Kyle: We are also in an era where we need to help build that bridge. All joking aside, operating systems need to look different than they looked five years ago, because it's not just you using them anymore. And that's changed the whole idea. It's not "OK, my Claw is going to create a user account" — doesn't work like that.

Just like all of us, we all have to look much, much more deeply in the stack, all the way down to the silicon layer in Azure, to be like, "OK, well, what do we need now?" Because the workloads are different. It's not just, "OK, we need more inference." It's, "OK, well, what type of inference do we need? What type of compute do we need to run these agents or run these agentic flows?"

It's a really interesting multi-layer problem. Versus, I would say software in the last five or six years — we're all going to our events and we're saying a version of the same thing: "SaaS product has new SaaS thing, it's the best SaaS thing ever."

Sean: It was boring for a while.

Kyle: Now it's like, "Oh my goodness, we're at physics."

Sean: Yeah.

Kyle: We're at physics problems. And that's exciting.

Sean: We're now trying to make semiconductor room-temperature superconductors still.

Kyle: Yep. That's never going away.

Build 上的发布、Work IQ 与给 Satya 的问题

Sean: That's a really good overview of everything. Have we left anything unsaid that you wanted to really get out there?

Kyle: I'm really excited for folks checking out the announcements that we have at Build. You can go look at them online. I'm hoping that it's driving a degree of curiosity and interest, because there's such a big shift that we're making at Microsoft for developers.

If you're a daily driver of a Mac device or a Linux device and you're like, "Okay, I don't use Windows" — there's improvements that are being made that are going to surprise folks. Just be like, "Oh, that's — they really want to do that." I'm talking for developers. Not "I play video games on the weekends on my Windows computer" — I'm talking my daily driver.

All the way from that to: what is it like to build an agent or build an app and deploy it and run it at work in particular? I talk all the time with the team — how I build on the weekend should be how I build at work. But if you're working in a Fortune 100 or Fortune 500, you're probably not vibing an app and then shipping it to some service. You've got to go through security and compliance. How can we move just as fast at work? We have a bunch of different offerings for to give you that same agility and power, but in the work context.

And then I'll tell you — I've mentioned it a couple times, and it's very freaking cool — if you are in the M365 land in any way, check out Work IQ. Check out Foundry IQ. These little — I'm oversimplifying it — context engines are wild good. We've given them to our developers at GitHub. We've given to employees at GitHub. We've used these tools to be able to just ask questions around everything that you have in your work context. And with Foundry IQ, be able to do the same exact thing across all your existing stores. Not move to new tools — just connect them in. It's surprisingly powerful. And your boss is still not going to get fired, and it's not going to turn it off, because it's leaking all this private information.

That is the trick that I think is sometimes getting lost when we're talking about all these great new platforms. Because I can use them and I'm like, "Oh, this is super powerful." And then I can't use it. And it's not because I'm at work at GitHub — it's not because I'm not allowed — because they can't do all the things that large complicated companies need.

Whether it's the kind of interesting daily-driver curiosity, all the way through to, "Oh my gosh, I can go use this at work tomorrow potentially," and have that context layer, have that intelligence — it's a huge shift. So check it out. I'd love to hear — I'm not shy on social — I'd love to hear feedback, what's working, what's not. But hopefully surprise folks a little bit.

Sean: What I'm hearing — first of all, that's a great pitch — what I'm hearing actually is that you should put the Work IQ people next to the Copilot people. Because the exact context problem that you named, they've solved enough for you to do your job, which is nuts.

Kyle: That's literally what has been happening the last several months.

Sean: I already forecast you.

Kyle: Totally — because you're totally right, the code and the code-asset problem is a little bit unique, but otherwise yeah, we're all working with each other now. It's all just context.

Sean: Exactly. Amazing. I'm going to be there. I'm going to be doing a couple sessions there. I'm going to be interviewing Satya. When I first started the pod, I had Jeff Dean on. Jeff is hall of fame of "I want to meet someday." Satya's on there. What should I ask Satya?

Kyle: I think the best question to ask is what he thinks is true in two or three years from now. It seems like such a throwaway question, but ultimately, the way that he is looking at this AI problem, inference problem, token problem, and how we're actually going to be working — I think you can see some of the recent shifts that have been happening inside of Microsoft to drive us to a place where it's not four, five, six, seven, eight different things. It's not a lack of context everywhere. Why is this approach in two years going to pay off? Because that I think—

Sean: Wow, that's a bold — okay, I'll ask it. I'll say "prompted by you." But it's a bold question because there's a lot of doubts to be honest, externally. So I want a straight answer from him on that. I think would reassure a lot of people, and honestly give me a lot of food for writing.

Thank you so much for spending your time. Thank you for doing what you do. As a COO, you don't need to be the external face. But because you are authoritative, because you have so much background with GitHub, and it's so authentic — we on the outside feel it. So thank you for that.

Kyle: Of course, I appreciate it. Thank you so much, Sean.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：高管的 commit 曲线在"退休"后反弹
Kyle 这条 commit 曲线读起来像一个职业讣告写错了结局——2013–2014 高峰，进入管理层后跌入谷底，然后在他成为 COO **同时** 兼任 Microsoft 全体开发者 CMO 的最忙时刻，反而陡然回升。这违背了所有人对 "senior leader 越升越远离代码" 的预设。
更深层的发现是：**他用代码解决的，是"人的问题"。** 他写的不是产品功能，而是把 Obsidian 笔记、Teams 会议转录、Slack 历史、PR 列表串成一个"回溯引擎"，让他能在周一早上知道上周组织发生了什么。这是一个跨领域的悖论——**他在用工程师的工具完成 CHRO/COO 的工作**。AI 没有让他更像 CEO，反而让他能重新成为 IC，但服务对象是 3,000 人的组织而不是一段代码。
## 二、AI 的真正杀手锏不是创造未来，而是回看过去
整个对话里他反复强调一句话：**"It's actually a recursive loop backwards."**
这是被严重低估的一个洞见。整个

item.title: [Lex Clips] Dark matter explained by physicist | Don Lincoln and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

当"不存在"成为最强的证据：暗物质的认识论悖论

最让人意外的是 Dragonfly 2/4 这个观察：一个不含暗物质的星系，反而成了暗物质存在的最有力证据。这是科学史上罕见的"缺席论证"——如果暗物质只是引力定律的错觉，那么定律应该到处错；但如果它是真实的"物质"，那么就可以被剥离、被减去、被局部抽空。Don Lincoln 实际上揭示了一个深刻的认识论原则：当一个东西可以"被拿走"时，它才真正"存在"。这与佛学中"破我执"的思辨结构惊人相似——只有能被剥离的东西，才不是本质属性。

跨领域连接：暗物质搜索 ≈ 早期互联网的"暗网搜索悖论"

暗物质质量范围跨越"小行星到比电子还轻"——这是 60 个数量级的搜索空间。Don 说"你做一个实验只能扫描一小段质量范围，可能恰好不是那一段"。这个困境在结构上和密码学暴力破解、新药筛选、深海生物搜索完全同构：当搜索空间巨大、信号稀疏时，单点深耕和广撒网都是错的，唯一的解是"分布式异构搜索"。Don 自己选择不做暗物质实验恰恰是博弈论上的最优策略——当一个领域已经被多个团队覆盖，边际贡献最大的人是去做别人不做的事。这暗示了科学共同体本身在无意识地执行一个类似生物多样性的搜索算法。

隐藏的系统性真相：物理学正在经历"测量极限的哲学转向"

注意 Don 反复强调的一个时间线："今天的实验比我做学生时灵敏一百万倍，但什么也没看到"。这不是失败，而是物理学正在悄悄进入一个前所未有的认识论新阶段——历史上第一次，我们用"看不见"作为科学结论本身。粒子物理过去 100 年是"发现的科学"（每一代加速器都有新粒子），但暗物质搜索可能开启了"排除的科学"时代。这与金融市场的"有效市场假说"、生物学的"基因组黑暗物质"（非编码DNA）、AI 领域的"对齐失败的不可观测性"形成奇妙的镜像：当一个系统的核心驱动力是"看不见的大多数"时，研究方法必须从"找东西"转向"画出我们不知道什么的轮廓"。Don 说"我知道它不是什么"——这句话本身就是一种新的科学语言。它和苏格拉底"我知道我不知道"相隔两千多年，但在结构上完全同构：真正的进步往往是更精确地描绘无知的形状。

一个额外的意外观察：Don 承认 25 年前他认为修改引力理论更可能，现在改为相信暗物质——这是一个公开的"贝叶斯更新"。绝大多数科学家不会在公开场合承认自己改变了立场，因为这被默认为软弱。但 Don 的叙述揭示了一个反直觉的真相：愿意公开记录自己认知转变轨迹的科学家，往往是最接近真相的人，因为他们的信念是被证据驱动的，而非身份驱动的。

📝 转录文本

为什么暗物质比暗能量更神秘？

Lex: You mentioned dark matter is perhaps even more mysterious than dark energy. Okay. Can you build up the intuition why it's more mysterious? What is dark matter?

Don: Oh gosh. What is dark matter? A — I don't know. B — is terribly fascinating.

我们为什么相信暗物质存在？三条独立证据

All right. So, first thing — and the most important thing, because I'm an experimentalist by God — the first thing is: why do we believe there's dark matter? And the reason is that astronomical measurements do not agree with predictions by Newtonian or relativity theory.

  • Galaxies spin too fast.
  • Clusters of galaxies move too quickly.
  • And the distortion of very distant galaxies due to the gravitational field of near galaxies disagrees with the prediction from what we see from the observed matter.

So there are three very distinct reasons why we are predicting that something is wrong in our understanding of either the laws of physics or the matter budget of the universe.

旋转星系：最简单的切入口

The easiest one to talk about is the spinning galaxies. Now, what I'm saying is not unique to spinning galaxies — just easiest to talk about.

So, galaxies are observed to spin more quickly than they should if we add up the gravity we see. By all rights, galaxies spinning that fast should blow themselves apart, and they don't.

So, what can be the answer? Well, you have the force required for a star to orbit, to move in a circle, and you have the force due to gravity, and they're connected by an equal sign. And the prediction is wrong. So either:

  1. The force due to gravity is wrong,
  2. The force needed to move in a circle is wrong, or
  3. The equal sign is wrong.

I mean, this is really simple. One of those things is wrong. So one possibility is simply that Newton's law of gravity — mass times the mass over r² times a constant — that's just wrong. Another possibility is Newton's F = ma, that we are taught in introductory physics, is wrong. Both of those are eminently possible. Maybe we don't understand gravity, or maybe there's more mass than we can see.

排除法：不是氢气，也不是流浪黑洞

So, you can look at this really simply and come up with a list — cookbook things we can test. And so we've done that.

The most obvious possibility is that there is more mass than we can see — there's black holes, there's hydrogen gas that we can't see, whatever. There's something out there.

  • You go and you look, and there's no hydrogen gas, because we can see that through radio waves. That's not it.
  • In the '90s, we went looking for black holes, rogue planets, things like that. Those exist, but not enough of them. That's not it.

And so now we're left with: there's some sort of matter that we can't see, or we don't understand gravity, or we don't understand inertia.

我的立场转变：从"修改引力"到"暗物质是真实的"

Now I personally, if you asked me this — oh, I don't know — 25 years ago, I would have said the most likely answer is that we don't understand inertia or gravity. 20, 25 years ago, that's what I would have said. No problem.

However, there have been a couple of observations that have caused me to change my thinking, and I think that dark matter is more likely.

证据一：子弹星系团 (Bullet Cluster)

One of them is called the bullet cluster. There are two large clusters of galaxies. Any galaxy consists of a couple of components:

  • The galaxies themselves,
  • The hydrogen gas that surrounds the galaxies,
  • And maybe dark matter.

If dark matter is real or not real, you will get different answers if those two galaxies pass through one another. The galaxies themselves should pass through one another basically not interacting. But the big thing is the gas clouds. As the galaxies pass through one another, the clouds should interact, and the gas cloud should stop in the middle and be really really hot.

  • If there were no dark matter: you would see a cluster of galaxies, cluster of galaxies, a big gas cloud in the middle. And because the gas cloud is much more massive than the galaxies themselves, you would expect to see distortions — what we call dark matter distortions — in the middle.
  • If dark matter is real: the galaxies pass through one another. The cloud stops. Dark matter doesn't interact with the clouds, so it passes through. In that case, you would expect to see the distortions where the galaxies are.

And that's what we see. So that, in my mind, is strong evidence that dark matter is a real thing.

证据二：蜻蜓星系 (Dragonfly 2 and 4)

And there is another example which is much more recent — the bullet cluster was a while ago — called the dragonfly galaxies. There's Dragonfly 2 and Dragonfly 4. These are galaxies that rotate exactly according to Newton's laws.

The fact that they rotate exactly according to Newton's laws says that whatever is causing galaxies to rotate too fast is not a property of matter. But if you had a galaxy where there was no dark matter — for whatever reason, it got stripped off or something — this is one of those lovely ironies: the existence of a galaxy with no dark matter is very strong evidence that dark matter is real, because you can take the dark matter out.

So DF2 and DF4 also suggest to me that dark matter is real.

Now, it remains possible that we need to modify the laws of inertia, or we need to modify the laws of gravity. Those are possible still — in my opinion, and now this is Don's opinion, but it's probably the opinion of most of the scientific community — dark matter is likely a real thing.

那么，暗物质究竟是什么？

Now that's great. I've taken you all the way to dark matter. So now you're going to ask me: "Don, what is dark matter?"

I don't know. But I know what it isn't.

  • I know it is not black holes.
  • I know it is not rogue planets.
  • We've done the measurements. We've looked across nearly every mass range for compact objects and ruled them out.

So if dark matter is real, it can't be made of those. Then you're left with the idea that dark matter is a particle. And that's what we've thought about. The name for the dark matter particle that we've called for a long time is a WIMP — a Weakly Interacting Massive Particle.

寻找暗物质的三种方法

We have spent the last — god — 30 years looking for them in various ways. There are three ways that we might see dark matter:

1. 直接探测

The direct way says dark matter exists literally everywhere in this room, in our laboratory, and the dark matter is passing through the Earth like a wind. We put up detectors trying to see those. We have done that, and we've seen nothing.

Lex: So we should say — we have done that for neutrinos.

Don: We've done that for many different types of dark matter. We just simply put detectors in labs deep underground, and we can see neutrinos in them, it's true. But dark matter — especially heavy dark matter, these WIMPs — they have a different signature, and we've seen no evidence of dark matter interaction in these detectors.

Lex: So neutrinos are also weakly interacting and also have mass.

Don: They are. But not enough mass. So WIMPs are heavy. Right? Neutrinos are indeed WIMPs of a sort. Now we have to be careful what we mean by WIMPs — they are weakly interacting massive particles. But we can calculate, and there's just not enough mass in them. It's not it.

So we need another form. And we have seen zero evidence of this wind of dark matter through the Earth.

2. 间接探测（湮灭信号）

Another possibility is you look where you think dark matter might be concentrated — at the center of galaxies. If dark matter exists and there's antimatter dark matter, maybe they annihilate and make photons. So we look for gamma rays and various other signatures of annihilating dark matter.

There are always constantly announcements of "oh we saw it, oh we didn't." The problem is, that way of looking for dark matter is hard, because there are other ways of making, for instance, gamma rays — like neutron stars and stuff — and you really need to understand the details of galaxies really really well to believe that.

3. 对撞机制造

The final option is what I do — where we smash particles together at high energy and try to make dark matter particles. If you make dark matter particles, because they don't interact except via gravity, they escape your detector.

So what you hope to see is an event where you collide particles, a dark matter particle escapes and you don't see it, but the recoil you see on the other side — because momentum is conserved. So you see a blob of energy on this side, nothing on the other side. Maybe that's dark matter.

That also happens with neutrinos. So you need to understand everything about neutrinos and calculate how many of those you see, and then hope you see more — and then that might be dark matter.

Again, that hasn't worked.

巨大的参数空间难题

So we've ruled out some dark matter particles. But the problem is the range of possible mass. If dark matter is of a particulate form, the range of viable dark matter ranges from something like the mass of an asteroid to far lighter than an electron — and everywhere in between. We have looked, we've ruled out some little spots in that phase space, but that's a big range.

Lex: Is it really possible to miss a particle the size of an asteroid?

Don: The astronomical searches were not sensitive to that level of dark matter. You would expect that there would be some of those in the solar system. If they're what we think, like asteroids or something, then we'd heat them up and we'd eventually see them. But if they're truly dark matter and don't interact with matter, which means they wouldn't absorb energy from the sun, they'd be really dark.

微引力透镜的灵敏度极限

The only way we searched for them was a thing called microlensing. If a massive object — a distant star and a massive object pass between that star and your eye — that star will momentarily brighten. So you look for these microlensing events and you count them.

We did see some. Black holes pass in front of stars, and we've seen them, but we just haven't seen enough. For very low mass particles like asteroids, they wouldn't make enough brightening effect to see. So there's a minimum sensitivity of brightening — about a third the mass of a moon. Our moon is about the sensitivity we had.

Nobody, I think, really thought that these low mass guys were likely. What they thought was more likely was just unseen black holes — which I think is completely reasonable. Then when that got ruled out, I thought, okay, modified gravity or inertia. Well, now bullet cluster and dragonfly seem to have ruled that out. So I'm stuck in my head with: dark matter seems to be real, and we don't know what it is.

占宇宙物质的巨大比例

Lex: And it makes up a giant percentage of matter in the universe.

Don: It is five times more prevalent than ordinary matter.

Lex: Wow, this is incredible. This is so fascinating.

Don: And that's why it's cool. If someone out there is a young person who wants to get into this, understanding dark matter is a big deal. It's five times more prevalent.

The problem is, as I told you, if the mass is ranging from an asteroid to far lighter than an electron — if you get on an experiment that looks at one little range of mass, maybe you weren't the lucky guy that measured the right place. That's one of the reasons why, as fascinating as I think it is, I'm not doing dark matter experiments. If you make an experiment that searches one mass range, it'll be blind to another mass range.

So what you need is many groups doing all sorts of radically different experiments, exploring all sorts of parameter space. And with all that said, until you see it, there still is the possibility that maybe we don't understand gravity or inertia. You can't rule that out.

对直接观测的渴望

Lex: If there is dark matter out there, you're hoping it's actually somehow detectable?

Don: I don't know what it is. I think it's cool. It's very, very fascinating. That is one thing I really do hope in my lifetime is understood, because I'd like to know the answer to that. That's the thing where, legitimately, you can see a discovery.

Lex: You got to get lucky though. I mean, you got to look in the right place, whatever it is.

Don: Or you have to come up with that really cool theoretical idea that everybody's overlooked — which is another possibility.

There are people who are really, really religiously hating dark matter, largely because we've looked so hard for so many years, and the experiments in today's world are a million times more sensitive than when I was a starting student, and they still haven't seen anything. That's why people really hate dark matter — some of them because they think we should have seen it by now. But I don't know.

Lex: I mean, I'm a sucker for direct observation. Not — indirect is obviously also really great — but direct. Just imagine pointing your telescope in a certain direction and, because of some artifact of cosmology, being able to directly detect a giant amount of a thing that you could say is dark matter.

Don: Yeah. You would see it — orbit things, orbit it, or it would eclipse things in front of it.

Lex: In an obvious way. Because some of the stuff you mentioned with DF2 and DF4 — those are brilliant indirect deductions that there should be something like dark matter. But some obvious blocking, occluding, this kind of thing.

Don: We did that in the '90s — experiments called MACHO, OGLE, and some others. They looked for a black hole that you just can't see. A black hole — you can't see it, it's a perfect candidate for dark matter. And if there's enough of them out there — remember, there's five times the number of stars, which means there's a whole lot of freaking black holes out there. We should have seen them. And we didn't.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 当"不存在"成为最强的证据：暗物质的认识论悖论
最让人意外的是 Dragonfly 2/4 这个观察：**一个不含暗物质的星系，反而成了暗物质存在的最有力证据**。这是科学史上罕见的"缺席论证"——如果暗物质只是引力定律的错觉，那么定律应该到处错；但如果它是真实的"物质"，那么就可以被剥离、被减去、被局部抽空。Don Lincoln 实际上揭示了一个深刻的认识论原则：**当一个东西可以"被拿走"时，它才真正"存在"**。这与佛学中"破我执"的思辨结构惊人相似——只有能被剥离的东西，才不是本质属性。
## 跨领域连接：暗物质搜索 ≈ 早期互联网的"暗网搜索悖论"
暗物质质量范围跨越"小行星到比电子还轻"——这是 60 个数量级的搜索空间。Don 说"你做一个实验只能扫描一小段质量范围，可能恰好不是那一段"。这个困境在结构上和**密码学暴力破解、新药筛选、深海生物搜索**完全同构：**当搜索空间巨大、信号稀疏时，单点深耕和广撒网都是错的，唯一的解是"分布式异构搜索"**。Don 自己选择不做暗物质实验恰恰是博弈论上的最优策略——当一个领域已经被多个团队覆盖，边际贡献最大的人是去
